### Translation Li Zhi Human and Fox Exchange Marriage (Draft Version: "Xu Haifang") Wu Liniang Gu Yihui Volume Five Cui Yu Liu Weng Lin Guiying Hu Erjie A Certain Young Gentleman White Ape A Certain Person (Yi) Ajiao Righteous Ghost Biyu Invoking the Oracle Debt of a Future Life Drought-Footed Fish Pan Zeyuan Volume Six Qin Lijuan Zhan Ruxian Cui Jie Ox Repaying Debt (Draft Version: "Changshan Someone") Feng Guantun (Draft Version: "Tian Sizhi") Volume Seven Yue Xian Lu An Gong Sheng Bi Cheng Xun Niang Water Mother San Niang Qiong Xian Zhang Chunjiao Zhang Yisheng Wang Wei Three Cases of Longzhou Wang Fu Duan Cheng Volume Eight Gu Qinggao Jiang Zai Xin Beauty's Picture A Certain County Case Rui Xue Zhao Cheng Bad Dream Xu Cui'e Qi Shi Jiao Niang Niu Ziliang Volume Nine Su Cheng Yan Xingli Prostitute of Taiyuan (Draft Version: "Feng Yun") He Mengling

### Volume Ten **Geng Rumei** **Lin Fang** **Li Sishun** **Opium** ### Volume Eleven **Jiang Lian** **Yu Mu** **Hehuan Tou** **Yan Sanfeng** **Dong Er Yun** **Long Zhen Xuedi** **Jiang Yun** **Kai Lai** **Dai Chanchu Ji** **Gui Hu Yi Fang** **Zha Xiwen** ### Appendix One: Preface, Postscript, Inscription ### The Book of Wisdom Volume One **He Fu** **He Fu**, courtesy name **Deyou**, was a scholar selected from the district of Wuan. He lived in the northern outskirts of the county and was a man of pure sincerity. He went from being wealthy to poor, losing almost all of his property, but still had many taxes to pay. Every time he paid them, it felt like crossing a fiery mountain. This year, it was again the time for tax payment. He borrowed money and managed to pay half. He thought that paying it all in advance would be better than having an outstanding debt. Early in the morning, he set out for the capital, arriving at dusk. As soon as he entered the Haidai Gate, someone approached and said, "Are you not the scholar **He Fu**?" He replied, "Yes." He looked at the man closely and said, "We have never met before. Could I be mistaken?" The man replied, "You are not mistaken. My master has a request for you." "Who is your master?" He asked. "You will know when you meet him." Upon arrival, they found a grand and deep gate, and a venerable old man in fine attire emerged to greet them. After three bows, they entered. Once seated, He Fu asked, "What is your esteemed surname?" The man replied, "I am surnamed **Hu**."

Fang was about to speak again, but Hu said, "Are you surnamed He, given name Fu, courtesy name De You, and a selected scholar from the Xin You (1891) examination?" Fang replied, "How did you know this?" Hu said, "Not only that, you are thirty-two years old, born on the first day of the New Year in the Jia Yin (甲寅) year at the hour of Yin?" Fang, upon hearing this, could not help but be astonished and said, "I wish to hear the reason for your prior knowledge." Hu said, "I was shown by a divine being, so I know. Let me slowly explain the details: I have reached the age of seventy and have only one daughter, who is of marriageable age but unmarried. The divine being spoke of your family background and said that you have a fateful connection, and now wishes to offer her to you for service." Fang was taken aback by Hu's sudden proposal and, not knowing what her daughter was like, felt doubtful and fearful. He declined, saying, "I have a wife who is content with simple fare, and I dare not follow your command." Hu said, "Your wife, surnamed Zhou, has a gentle nature, and I am well aware of this. If we can tolerate each other, we will live together; if not, we will live separately. What harm is there? And it will actually be very beneficial for you." Fang remained silent. Hu said, "Today is a good time, and the ceremony can be held. It is not a formal marriage, so the ceremonial music and decorations can be omitted, but only the formal attire and ceremony are necessary." After saying this, Hu entered and, after some time, came out with a servant carrying a top hat, a ceremonial robe, and a string of prayer beads. Hu said, "You may wear these to perform the ceremony."

He saw it, it was a fourth-rank official's insignia. He said, "I am of lowly rank and reputation, how could I overstep my bounds?" The host replied, "This was my old possession. You haven't had time to prepare proper attire, so wear it for the ceremony. After the ceremony, you can remove it; who would see it?" He initially refused but was eventually persuaded to wear it. As soon as he finished dressing, he was led out to perform the ceremony. He then entered and worshipped heaven and earth with the bride; after removing the veil, they both worshipped her parents. He saw her, and she was a woman of unparalleled beauty, around twenty years old. Soon after, a feast was set up in the courtyard, with all the delicacies prepared. He enjoyed the feast with her, completely forgetting his earlier hardships. As night fell, the feast was cleared away, and a young maid carried a candle to guide him to the bridal chamber. He seemed dazed and lost, saying to himself, "Am I not dreaming?" The bride said, "Indeed, this is beyond your wildest dreams." He replied, "That's true." She then said, "Virtue has it, but the vessel is small!" He said, "I am not small in capacity, but my heart truly doubts this."

"Your noble family marrying a poor scholar, one; your beauty and willingness to be a concubine, two; despite never having met your father, my name, age, and other details are all correct, three." The woman said, "There is something more suspicious, please look at yourself." She then handed a piece of silk to He. He opened it and saw that it was his name-donation certificate for the position of governor. He was deeply moved and began to understand that the clothes and hat he used for the ceremony were all prepared in advance. In the warmth and comfort, they stayed for ten days without realizing it. He said to the woman, "I want to return home temporarily, is that possible?" She replied, "Yes. Otherwise, your wife will think you have abandoned her to go to the east to fulfill your duties. But when you return, you must wear the clothes you wore when you came to avoid gossip. I have prepared several hundred pieces of silver for the repair costs, maintain the old ways, do not change. The delicate items in the room, I will take care of, do not trouble yourself." He said, "Why don't you come back with me?" She replied, "I cannot do that now. The broken mirror flies to the sky, I will come on my own, do not wait anxiously." As He was about to leave, he said, "I have an item that is missing." The woman said, "Is it not the receipt for the grain tax? It has already been paid into the treasury yesterday."

When He returned home, his wife was surprised by his absence for ten days. She saw carriages and horses at the door and attendants moving his possessions, but he did not feel it necessary to inquire. After the moving was completed, He then told Zhou about the strange encounter. Zhou heard this and deeply appreciated the woman, and he was eager to meet her, considering going to greet her. He said, "It's not necessary; she will come in half a month." Zhou smiled and asked, "Can you wait?" He replied, "Whether you can or not, you already know, so don't ask me." He quickly prepared the house, and it was completed in ten days. When the time came, the woman indeed arrived. She wore plain clothes and light makeup, without any extravagant decorations. When she saw Zhou, she wanted to perform the formalities of a wife, but Zhou stopped her, and they greeted each other with simple courtesy. Zhou smiled at her and said, "Her beauty is so captivating, it's no wonder the young man spoke of her constantly." She only smiled in response. She brought clothes, bedding, and other items, all in pairs, and they were used by both of them. Later, the woman gave birth to two sons. Zhou could not have children, so he treated the woman's sons as his own, cherishing them as if they were his daughters. The woman would visit home every month, and her visits lasted half a month. Even when she went with He, she never missed her schedule. However, every time she returned home, she would bring back thousands of gold pieces, which made He become extremely wealthy.

One day, the woman and her husband played with their child for amusement. Suddenly, strangers sent by the trapper arrived to fetch the woman and invited her husband to come along. Upon arrival, they found the banquet already prepared. After taking their seats, the trapper said, "The wealth I have accumulated, aside from what the woman is carrying, still amounts to over ten thousand in this place." He then presented the deeds for the properties he had purchased, one by one. The husband was shocked and asked why. The trapper replied, "Let me tell you the truth. I am not a human; I am a fox. I accumulated wealth and bought property for the sake of the woman, but it is truly due to your couple's good fortune. How could I act without reason? Since the woman married you, I have visited your home once or twice each month. Now that she has given birth to a child, and the three of you live together in harmony, my wish is fulfilled. I am now going to retreat to the mountains to cultivate the Dao and will not return." Upon hearing this, the woman's tears welled up. The trapper said, "The departure date is not yet set, perhaps it's not too late in half a year." Despite this, the woman remained unhappy. After the banquet, they went to bed. The next morning, the woman got up early to visit her relatives but did not know where the elders had gone.

The Daoist Master Xu Bai said: "To encounter such circumstances is something that all people desire. If not with pure sincerity, how can there be a fortune to enjoy without effort? Though a fox can bring good fortune to people, it is also due to one's own efforts; how could there be any selfish motives involved?" The matter seems to involve illusion, but the text is extremely clear and explicit, this is a masterful stroke. —Hou Zhonglin Those with virtue will surely have good fortune; the matter is strange but the principle is correct, this is called strange yet lawful. —Shangyuan Li Yu, respectfully annotated Ghostly Couplet In a certain county office, every night at midnight, people constantly hear a recitation sound saying, "Midnight of the second watch." It has been passed down for a long time, and no one finds it strange. Governor Qian, who had newly taken office, heard it and found it unusual. Until dawn, he gathered the officials in the office to ask about it. About ten years ago, the wife of a certain governor was deeply versed in literature and writing. At midnight, she happened to compose this couplet, but was unable to find a matching line, and because of this, she fell ill and died. After her death, people began to hear the ghostly voice as before. The governor sighed for a long time. Coincidentally, during a Mid-Autumn night gathering, after the guests had left, he was still fond of the moonlight and lingered by the steps, when he heard the ghostly voice as before. The governor said, "Today, I have found the matching line! 'Mid-Autumn in the middle of August,' can it be perfectly matched?" From then on, the ghostly voice ceased. The Daoist Master Xu Bai said: To die because of a single couplet, this is foolish! Yet, people in the world think themselves superior, and are content with their inability, unwilling to deeply use their minds, instead claiming that their learning is high and beautiful, and that others cannot match them, therefore, their lives are without progress. Thus, those who are not foolish have much to be ashamed of. As for her, she can be called a good learner and deep thinker.

Literature is naturally formed, and a skilled hand occasionally captures it, making it worthy of inclusion in the *Talented Ghosts*. —— Annotated by Li Yu of Shangyuan Tragic Suicide Jia studied at home, with Yi as his companion. In that summer, Jia married, and on the night of their wedding, the bride said, "I have heard that you have been studying for many years. I would like to propose a couplet for you to match. If you succeed, we will spend the night together; if not, you may go to the study to read. We can meet again after you achieve fame." Jia replied, "Agreed." The bride then said, "As a woman, I cannot outdo you, so I will propose a lower line." She said, "Wait for the lamp, climb the tower, and read together." Jia pondered for a long time but could not come up with a good answer, feeling ashamed and leaving. He eventually entered the study. Yi asked, "This night is precious, why did you go out to sleep?" Jia explained in detail, feeling ashamed and went to bed. Yi had long heard that Jia's wife was beautiful and impersonated Jia to enter. Seeing Jia's wife, he said, "Your thoughts are ingenious, but I cannot match your couplet. Please allow me to think about it tomorrow. Do not dismiss me as a failure." Jia's wife smiled slightly. They then went to bed, and he left early the next morning. The next night, Jia returned and said, "The couplet you proposed, I truly cannot match." The bride said, "Why do you speak so much?" Jia said, "Last night, I felt ashamed to stay in the study without saying a word. What is there to speak of?" The bride realized that the previous guest was not her husband and that she had truly lost her virtue. She then committed suicide by hanging.

After 甲 and 乙 entered the school in the same year and both took the provincial examination, there was a paulownia tree in the examination hall. On the night of the Mid-Autumn Festival, the examiner sat under the tree, gazing at the autumn moon, and spontaneously came up with a couplet: "Move the chair, lean against the paulownia, and enjoy the moon together." He thought of no exact match, so he immediately displayed it to all the candidates in the examination hall, offering a reward to anyone who could provide a fitting response. The responses were written on separate sheets, with the names and places of origin of the candidates recorded. After displaying the couplet, he sat and slept under the tree, and in a dream, a beautiful woman said, "I have a couplet: 'Wait for the lamp, climb the tower, and each read a book.' Do you think it can be a match?" The examiner saw the beautiful woman with a rope around her neck and her tongue hanging out, and was startled awake, realizing it was a ghost of someone who had been wrongfully strangled. He immediately sent an official into the examination hall to collect the answers, and among them, the responses of 甲 and 乙 were identical, which was actually the couplet of the strangled ghost. He thought that the ghost's death must have been caused by this couplet, and the ghost's injustice must also be resolved by this couplet. He secretly recorded the names and places of origin of 甲 and 乙. After the results were announced, he summoned 甲 and 乙 and had an official question them.

The official first asked A about the origin of his response. A truthfully said it came from his deceased wife and described in detail the circumstances of her hanging. The official then asked, "Did you tell others about the literary situation when you stayed in the study?" A replied, "I told my companion reader B about it." The official followed up, "Was B staying in the study that night?" A explained, "During the day we read together in the same room, but at night we each stayed in our own room. Because of the summer heat, I couldn't sleep and got up at night to cool off. I called out to B but got no response. Seeing that B's door was locked, I realized he wasn't there. He returned at dawn." The official then summoned B to the hall and asked who wrote the response. B said, "It came from my own thoughts." The official countered, "The response came from A's wife. A was ashamed and couldn't answer, so he stayed in the study and told you. You say it came from your own thoughts, isn't that shameful?" B did not answer. The official then asked, "Do you know why A's wife hanged herself?" B said, "I don't know." The official continued, "When A stayed in the study, were you also staying there?" B replied, "Yes."

The official said, "That's not true! That night, A got up to cool off and saw your room door locked. You've already confessed clearly, so how can you evade the truth?" B replied, "I truly did not stay in the study that night." The official asked, "Where did you go?" B answered, "I went home to visit my relatives." The official summoned B's father and asked, "How long has your son been studying away from home?" B's father replied, "He has only been studying at A's house for two years." The official asked, "Does he often come home?" B's father answered, "Because he is not yet successful in his studies, he is only allowed to come home during Qingming, Duanwu, and Mid-Autumn Festivals." The official said, "Your son is over twenty years old and has a wife at home. Could there have been times when he came home and you did not know?" B's father replied, "Because he is studying, I am afraid it would distract his mind, so he has not yet been married. We have now decided to marry him in October." The official still feared that B might be wronged, and asked again, "Your son studies with A. Could it be that because A is getting married, it was inconvenient for your son to continue studying, and he temporarily returned home? Do you not remember?" B's father replied, "No, I do not."

The magistrate also entrusted this matter to you before, yet you did not return. The magistrate ordered the attendants to record clearly and brought B up to the hall. The magistrate said, "Your father's confession is clear, and I am concerned that you might be distracted. Therefore, except for the clear days and the three specified times, you are not allowed to return home. On the night when A stayed overnight in the study, since you were not in A's room, nor did you return to your own home, where exactly did you go?" B had no words to answer. The magistrate smiled and said, "I know where you went!" B asked, "Where did I go?" The magistrate replied, "You impersonated A and entered A's room, committing adultery with A's wife, isn't that right?" B refused to admit it. The magistrate ordered the removal of B's hat, indicating that corporal punishment was imminent. Fearing the consequences, B fully confessed. He then said that A's wife hanged herself the next night, and it had nothing to do with him. The magistrate angrily retorted, "When you entered A's room, you must have said that the excellent couplet was hard to match, and when A entered again, he said the same. The woman knew that the previous relationship was not with her husband, and she hanged herself in shame. Can you still say it has nothing to do with you? Because of your adulterous act, she died, and you should be prepared to face the consequences, do you still hope to return alive?" The higher authorities, seeing that A's wife died and he remained unmarried, and B was about to marry but incurred a fault, ordered that the county itself serve as matchmaker, making B's unwed wife return to A as a second wife, as a punishment for the adulterous act.

The Daoist Master Xubai said: "Suffering such injustice, it seems as if there will never be a day of clarity; who would have thought that a clever use of a couplet could gradually and completely vindicate. The vast net of heaven is wide but does not miss its targets; who would say that heaven lacks clear distinctions!" Judgment was so clear and decisive that there was no room for injustice, but it is regrettable that you were not given a chance to prove your innocence. Master Hou Zhonglin Recently, Governor Li Yunsheng published "The Comprehensive Review of Criminal Cases" in dozens of volumes, but this case was not included. Master Ma Zhuwu The clarity and decisiveness in judgment can be inferred from regular inspections. Shangyuan Li Yu respectfully annotates Little Treasure In a certain village, there was a woman named Wang, wife of Li, who in autumn took her young son to the fields. She saw a leather pouch under a tree at the beginning of the field, knowing it was left by a passerby. Picking it up, it was very heavy, and she knew it contained something, but she did not open it to look inside. She covered it with straw leaves and sat on it to wait. Not long after, she saw a man riding a horse come, looking very hurried. Dismounting and asking hurriedly, he said, "Just left something, did you see it?" She replied, "What is it?" The man said, "A leather pouch, containing three hundred pieces of gold." She said, "I did not see the color of the gold, just describe the newness or oldness and style of the leather pouch." The man described it in detail, and she knew it was truly what was left behind. She then got up, removed the straw leaves, and pointed to it.

The man opened it to see that the contents were completely undisturbed, and he was greatly surprised. He said, "I wish to give half of this gold, for my father accidentally caused someone's death and urgently needs this money for settlement, which cannot be short of the amount. What should I do?" The woman replied, "Take it all and go. Although our family is not wealthy, we regard what is not rightfully ours as mere clouds." The man was deeply moved in his heart and asked, "What is your surname, madam?" She replied, "My surname is Li." He then asked, "Who is the young master?" She answered that it was her young son, Xiao Bao. The man said, "I am Liu Da, and I have lived in a certain district of a certain county in Shanxi for generations. If you ever need anything, send someone to me, and I will certainly give a generous gift in return." The woman said, "It is far away, and it is not easy to go back and forth. Even if there is a convenient person, they may not believe you." Liu Da gave her a token of trust and said, "You can use this as proof." After saying this, he left directly. Later, the woman faced family misfortune and lived from day to day, and she had no choice but to tell her son Xiao Bao, "While your father was alive, he had a close friend named Liu Da, who lived in a certain place in Shanxi. If you go to him, you will surely gain much." Xiao Bao said, "We have never met before, how can I expect to be trusted?" The woman said, "There is a token of trust here. Take it and go to visit, and you will not be doubted." Xiao Bao set out on his journey, traveling and begging along the way, proceeding gradually.

After traveling for several days, the beggar arrived at a small village and saw a large mansion with lanterns hanging at the door, realizing there was a celebration. He approached the door to ask for alms. A man came out and examined the beggar closely, asking, "How old are you?" The beggar replied, "Seventeen years old." The man said, "Come, I will feed you." The beggar followed him to his home, where he was fed. After eating, the man said, "There is something I need your help with. Do you agree?" The beggar asked, "What is it?" The man explained, "I am Zhang, and I have a son who is getting married. He has been betrothed to a family named Liu in the northern gate area since childhood. Today is the wedding day, and it is customary for the groom to go and greet the bride. However, my son is not very attractive and is not suitable to go and greet her. Could you go on my behalf? After the wedding, I will give you a generous reward." The beggar said, "I am willing to help." Zhang was pleased, dressed the beggar in new clothes, and sent him to the wedding in a carriage.

After the wedding ceremony, the groom was about to leave in his carriage when a servant reported that the river had risen several feet, making it impossible to cross. There was a dry riverbed on the way back, about several miles wide, and the mountain water had suddenly surged, blocking the path for travelers. Mr. Liu said, "This is a good time, how can we miss it? We can still perform the wedding ceremony here." Although the servant did not want to, he had no choice. At night, the groom sat alone in his formal attire, staying awake all night. The next night, he did the same. The bride asked, "Do you have something on your mind?" The groom did not answer. She repeated, "Is it because I am ugly and unworthy of your service?" The groom replied, "You are as beautiful as a celestial being. Having a wife like you, what regret could there be? I must tell you, we are not actually married." As he spoke, someone was secretly listening from outside the window and quickly informed Mr. Liu. Mr. Liu called the bride and asked her, and also invited the groom to the inner room, saying, "Why did you say you are not married?" The groom denied it. Mr. Liu said, "There is someone in the room, and there are ears outside the window, how can you deny it?" The groom could not hold out and then explained the reason for the substitution.

Liu said, "Your voice sounds as if you are not from here." Bao replied that he was from Shandong. Liu asked, "Why did you come here?" Bao said, "To seek out my late father's close friend, Mr. Liu." Liu asked, "Do you know him personally?" Bao replied, "No." Liu smiled and said, "Since we do not know each other, even if you seek him out, he might not believe you." Bao said, "I have a token of trustworthiness as evidence." Liu said, "Please give me the token to see." Bao handed it to Liu. Liu looked at it and saw that it was the name and title book given by Lady Wang. He then said with visible joy, "Are you the son of Lady Li, with the milk name Xiao Bao?" Bao replied, "Yes." Liu said, "If so, you do not need to seek others; Mr. Liu is here." Bao asked, "Where is he?" Liu replied, "I am here." Liu then recounted the story of the lost gold. He said, "Your mother said to seek out my late father's close friend, not to boast about his good deed of returning the gold; truly, she is a saint among women! I am deeply grateful for your mother's great virtue and never forget it. Now I am happy that heaven has granted this opportunity; since you have married my daughter, you are my son-in-law." He then told his daughter, "Li Lang is your husband, you are no longer an Ong."

Zhang sued in court. The official asked Liu, "Since you have already promised your daughter to Zhang, why did you also marry her to Li?" Liu replied, "Zhang asked Li to act as a proxy for the wedding, and I then married my daughter to him; and I married her to him without knowing that Li was wearing Zhang's hat." The official said, "Do you want your daughter to follow which family?" Liu said, "If the daughter follows Li, she has already been with him for ten days, so there is no reason for her to go to someone else." The official asked Zhang, and Zhang wanted to cut off the daughter and give her to his son. The official said, "Liu's daughter is no longer a virgin, so cutting her off would be unfair to both parties; it would be better to arrange another marriage for your son." Zhang repeatedly pleaded. The official angrily said, "What is the purpose of marriage, and why did you ask someone to act as a proxy? The blame lies entirely with you. If you insist, I will punish you for deceit." Zhang was afraid, and the case was settled. Later, Bao welcomed his mother to live in the west and settled there. The recluse Xubai said: Seeing Xiao Bao's fortunate encounters, one knows that Xiao Bao has a good fate. However, it is not just Xiao Bao's good fate, but rather the noble character and high virtue of his mother that led to this outcome. Seeing a passerby leave something behind, he picks it up and holds it very carefully, knowing there is something inside but not opening it to look. What kind of virtue is this! It is fitting that he enjoys unexpected good fortune!

This piece of writing has three major twists: Liu Da loses his gold, and Wang does not reveal it, one; Xiao Bao, who starts as a beggar, is hired to escort someone to a wedding, two; Liu's daughter marries, and a situation that starts as a joke becomes real, three. The piece has rises and falls, like the source of the Luo River, which appears and reappears three times. The structure is tight and skillful, with no flat or uninteresting parts, truly the work of a capable writer. Annotated by Li Yu from Shangyuan. --- Liu Fengchun A merchant from a neighboring village, Mr. Liu, trades in Dezhou and says there is a villager named Liu Mao in Dezhou, who has some savings and one daughter but no son. He has a cousin named Fengchun, with whom he has lived separately for a long time, and he treats him indifferently, so he gives all his possessions to his daughter, thinking that she will have a lifelong source of food and clothing. However, his daughter has a heart like a viper, and when she first gets the money, she treats her father like a god from heaven; when she becomes lazy, she follows it up with hatred, not unlike a thorn in the eye, treating him like a servant. She works all day, and later she even eats without being full, while Liu Mao is even worse. He leaves his daughter's house in anger and is about to go to his cousin, but since he has not received any money, he feels ashamed and becomes a beggar in the market town. Just as he arrives at a village, he approaches a wealthy household to seek shelter, calls out once, his cheeks turn red, and he cannot speak again. Suddenly, he sees his cousin Fengchun come out from inside and says, "I heard your voice, it's really you! My sister is fickle, she doesn't keep her uncle, but I still have a cousin, how could I let my white-haired uncle beg for a living? Uncle, please wait a moment, I will come out."

Fengchun, as a child, learned carpentry and just happened to be working at this household on carpentry. He then told the owner, "I have some matters to attend to today; please come early tomorrow morning." He went out and invited Mao to return home together. Upon arriving home, he told his wife, Ren Shi, "Uncle is hungry now, please cook quickly!" He also told his uncle, "From now on, do not go to your sister's house. My nephew's property is small, but his skills can still be relied upon, and he should not die of starvation. Even if times are bad and fate is unfavorable, there may be a lack of food and clothing, but he will definitely not let his uncle freeze or starve." Mao heard this and felt a mix of shame and joy, finally realizing that raising a daughter was useless, and it was too late to regret it. Fengchun worked for others and rarely returned home. Ren Shi treated Mao like an old man, just like a filial daughter-in-law. At this time, Mao enjoyed peace and plenty, even forgetting that he had no son and instead regretted having one daughter. He happened to visit the Liu Zhi Temple Fair and saw someone selling potted flowers. The flowers were not extraordinary, but there were two stones in the pot, which were actually diamonds. Mao had once been in the business of repairing leaks, so he recognized them, but the flower seller did not know that these were valuable goods. So he bought them at a low price, sold the stones, and received over a thousand taels of silver, which he deposited in a pawnshop run by someone he knew. He had not yet told Fengchun and his wife about it.

One day, Feng Chun told his uncle, "Someone has invited you, and you can go." Mao asked what the matter was, and Feng Chun said, "There is a piece of land somewhere, and the left neighbor is some land with several mu, yet they did not hear about it. It was sold for more than a thousand yuan to someone for business. On a certain day, the contract was made, so we have made this agreement." He also said, "Go if you want to, don't worry about it, this land originally could not be bought by our family." Mao pretended to agree. On the appointed day, Mao went a bit late, and the land had already been measured and completed, and the contract was about to be finalized. Mao asked, "Who is the mediator?" The buyer said, "It is someone." Mao said, "Not good at being a mediator. Although the land neighbors cannot afford to buy it, it is reasonable to inform them. Can you all think that our family has no such money?" The mediator remained silent. The buyer knew that Mao could not afford to buy the land, so he replied, "If so, it is fine. If you want to buy, I am willing to sell it to you." Mao said, "Is it true? Is it false?" The person said, "I will definitely not break my word." Mao said, "If so, we will definitely finalize the contract the next day. Everyone will be present, please forgive me for not inviting you." Everyone said, "Okay." Mao returned home and told Feng Chun about it.

Fengchun said, "He is indeed hateful, but if we buy it, where will the money come from?" The uncle replied, "No worry!" Fengchun still feared that buying the property would leave them without funds and bring ridicule, so he strongly advised against it. Ren Shi, watching from the side, knew that the uncle must have hidden money, and then said to her husband, "If the uncle wants to buy, then let him buy. If there is no money, say that the uncle is old and has become senile. What does it have to do with you?" The next morning, the uncle and nephew went to the city to buy vegetables. The uncle led Fengchun straight to the pawnshop. As soon as they entered the shop, the shopkeepers greeted each other with respect, their feelings extremely close. Fengchun thought to himself, "Why is my uncle so close with the shopkeepers?" Soon after, a shopkeeper asked the uncle, "What are you here for today?" The uncle replied, "I want to buy a few acres of land, so I came to buy vegetables." The shopkeeper asked, "How much money are you spending today?" The uncle said, "Let's spend a thousand or so." The shopkeeper asked, "What about the remaining amount?" The uncle replied, "There will be a remaining amount of a couple thousand, and I will let my nephew Fengchun take it gradually later." The uncle and nephew went out together to buy vegetables. Upon returning, the contract was completed and the price was paid. Fengchun asked about the source of the money, and the uncle then mentioned the matter of the gold. Therefore, Fengchun's family became wealthy, and they were the richest in the village. The uncle's daughter heard that her father had become rich again and harbored covetous thoughts. She returned to visit her father. The uncle did not allow her to enter, and Fengchun advised her repeatedly but she did not listen. He then arranged for her to stay in a separate room, treated her with courtesy, and gave her generous gifts before sending her back. Until the uncle's death, the two families had no further contact.

The White Void Hermit said: How great is the unreliability of women! Though women are not entirely unreliable, the unreliable ones are eight or nine out of ten. And the world's practice of favoring daughters while treating nephews and nieces poorly—how foolish is that! The case of Mr. Mao's daughter can serve as a mirror for reflection. Some might say that Mr. Mao lost his wealth to his daughter but then regained it through external means, and Creation seems to be particularly generous to Mr. Mao. However, it is not particularly generous to Mr. Mao. If Mr. Mao had just lost his wealth and then immediately gained this wealth, it could be considered particularly generous; if Mr. Mao wanted to separate from his daughter and start his own household and then gained this wealth, it could be considered particularly generous; if his nephew treated him poorly, preventing him from settling down and then gained this wealth, it could also be considered particularly generous. Only when Feng Chun treats Mr. Mao like a father and Ren Shi treats him like an old man, leading Mr. Mao to forget he had no sons and instead regret having this one daughter, does he finally gain this wealth. Thus, Creation's generosity to Mr. Mao is actually generosity to Feng Chun. From this, we know that if Mr. Mao gains this wealth and still gives it to his daughter, Creation does not approve; if Mr. Mao gains this wealth and keeps it for himself, Creation also does not approve. Mr. Mao can be said to have the ability to correct his mistakes, and Feng Chun is indeed a person of proper conduct! Mr. Mao has nothing commendable, but Feng Chun truly sets an example, and this piece can be called a text to exhort the world. In short, Good deeds bring blessings, and it is the natural order of things, do not view it as a mirage. Shangyuan Li Yu respectfully annotates.

Chen Ruoyu East Chang Chen Ruoyu, a scholar of Confucianism, was free-spirited and unrestrained. He once read Pu Songling's *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio*, which included tales of fox spirits resembling immortals and heroes. He often wished to make a friend among these fox spirits. He tried various methods to invoke fox talismans and spells, but they never worked. He heard that a certain mountain was full of fox spirits, so he took food and wine there and placed them on the hillside, drinking as if in the presence of a guest, but still received no response. Whenever he prayed silently in serene settings under the moon and among flowers, there was still no effect. He then said, "There are no fox spirits in the world. Otherwise, why have I invoked them for so many days without ever meeting one?" One day, while alone in his study, a visiting scholar came and sat down without being asked. When Chen asked his name, the man replied that his surname was Gan and his given name was Lu, with the courtesy name Xue Chun. The visitor spoke in a refined and elegant manner, and Chen was quite fond of him. Later, it rained heavily, and Chen took out some hidden wine and shared it with the visitor, but he did not change seats. After talking for a long time, Chen mentioned his long-held wish to make a fox spirit friend, but he had not yet succeeded. Gan said, "What is your intention in wanting to be friends with a fox spirit?" Chen replied, "Fox spirits have foresight but are not difficult to approach. Being friends with them would mean having someone to consult and a companion for travel; when facing avoidable misfortunes, there would be no fear, and when facing unavoidable ones, one could step into a sword; when encountering deserved blessings, there would be no侥幸, and when not, one could decline titles and rewards; food and wine could be shared immediately; under the moon and among flowers, one could enjoy them right away. There would be no worries or troubles in the heart."

I am the fox friend you seek! Do I have any intention of seeking profit and avoiding harm, coveting wealth and aspiring to high status, or earnestly seeking help from a fox?" Lu said, "If so, your intention has changed, then I am the fox friend you seek." Chen, upon hearing this, was overjoyed, stood up, and bowed, first humbly asking for forgiveness, and then invited Lu to his seat. By then, the rain had stopped, and they set out more wine and food for a hearty drinking session. At the time, Chen was just coming of age, while Lu was five years older, so Chen treated Lu as an elder brother. Their conversation flowed smoothly, and Chen regretted not meeting him earlier. Chen said, "In the future, if I wish to respectfully listen to your refined instruction, where should I welcome you?" The fox said, "There is no need. Write my name Xue Chun in red ink, draw eight circles around it, and below write your name Ruo Yu, drawing four circles around it. Burn it with fire, and I will come." Chen was a good drinker, and the fox could also hold his liquor. They took turns toasting each other, and Chen soon became drunk. When he woke up, the fox had already disappeared. The next day, Chen bought fine wine and delicacies. By evening, he wrote as the fox had instructed, burned it, and the fox indeed arrived. "What brought you here after last night's drinking?" Chen said, "Yesterday was rushed, and it was far from a respectful guest's courtesy. Today, I have prepared wine and food to express my sincerity, and from now on, I will not be so casual." After that, they sat down and drank merrily all night, dispersing only at dawn. From then on, they would gather to drink every ten days.

One day, the fox said, "Your household is not very prosperous, further burdened by alcohol expenses, and income does not match expenses. What should we do? I have a method that can lead to wealth, but I only wish it and never dare to act on it." Chen asked for the strategy. The fox replied, "There are tens of thousands of white strong coins in the stone room of Mount Nan. We can borrow them as capital, use them to generate more capital, and the profit will be enough to return the principal, without violating the natural order." Chen said, "That's acceptable." The fox then transported the silver to Chen's home. For every item Chen bought with the silver, the profit was doubled. Within five or six years, Chen became wealthy. Chen said, "The profit is sufficient for daily use, and the principal should be returned." The fox said, "You are a trustworthy person!" And so, the fox returned the capital silver as agreed. A man named Jiang from a neighboring village, who had a long-standing grudge against Chen, secretly harbored resentment and planned to take advantage of an opportunity to harm Chen. One day, Chen was drinking with the fox when Jiang sent a letter. The fox said, "This is dangerous, and you must not go!" Chen returned the letter unopened. The gatekeeper suddenly reported, "Jiang has come in person." Chen said, "What should we do about this?" The fox said, "Temporarily agree, and we will think of another plan." Chen asked the fox to temporarily avoid the situation, and the fox said, "No need. Even if he comes, he won't see me." Chen then went out to greet Jiang and invited him into the study. Jiang took the letter and presented it to Chen, saying, "I have prepared tea and snacks, hoping you would come to chat. Why did you ignore me and not honor my invitation?" Chen said, "It would be presumptuous to disturb you without cause. Since you are so kind, how could I refuse?" Jiang was pleased and left with the letter.

Tchen said, "If I do not leave, I cannot avoid being unfeeling; if I leave, I will just suffer harm. What then can be done?" The fox replied, "If you pretend illness halfway and return, you can achieve both." On the appointed day, Zhang sent two people to hurry the guest along, waiting to go together. Tchen, filled with suspicion and fear, gradually departed. When they reached the midpoint, Tchen suddenly clutched his abdomen and sat down, saying, "My old illness has recurred; I truly cannot go. Please convey my apologies." The two people did not listen and forcibly supported him to continue. In the midst of this critical moment, a man suddenly appeared from the opposite direction and said, "To invite a guest who is just ill and forcibly support him to continue, must not be with good intentions! And what business do you two have with this? You just say the guest fell ill and returned halfway, and that is enough. Why go to such lengths?" The two people nodded and left. Tchen looked at him and saw it was the fox brother, so he talked and laughed and returned. Zhang had a grandson named Sheng, who always loved drinking, and that day came without an invitation. Upon arriving at the guesthouse, it was quiet and empty. Just as he sat down, he saw a wine set on the window, took it down, and the wine was not full, but smelled very fragrant, so he drank several mouthfuls. Soon, he felt severe abdominal pain like tearing and cried out loudly. Zhang's mother happened to be there, heard the noise, and hurried out to ask what was wrong. Sheng explained the reason for drinking, and after speaking, he died. Zhang was immediately called over, and he knew it was poisoned but dared not speak. It was the poisonous wine Zhang had bought, which, if drunk after a full meal, would take effect over two or three days, but if taken on an empty stomach, could kill instantly. Zhang's father sued in court, and the judge asked Zhang why he kept poisonous wine. Zhang could not hide it and then revealed the truth. The judge sentenced him for murder.

Chen enjoyed banquets and feasts, and for many years he had been engaged in trade, long neglecting his duties related to the imperial examinations. When the provincial examiner arrived for inspection, the examination for candidates was still over a month away. The fox said, "On the battlefield, one must sharpen one's weapons." Chen laughed and replied, "Yes. To me, academic success is as trivial as straw or chaff; can I achieve it in this life?" The fox said, "You will definitely succeed." Chen asked, "When?" The fox replied, "Soon, in the upcoming examination sessions." The fox then said, "I can distinguish quite well between good and bad compositions. I wish to draft several questions for you to compose, and I will judge your work. Would you agree?" The fox then presented two essay topics and one poetry topic. After Chen finished composing, he recorded his work and presented it to the fox. The fox said, "Your work does not stand out; you should write another." After three attempts, the fox said, "This is acceptable. If you have such work in the examination, you are sure to succeed." The fox then instructed Chen to thoroughly review his work. Chen asked for more topics, and the fox said, "The examination period is approaching soon; you do not need to write many compositions. Instead, immerse yourself in literature to nurture your writing ability." After entering the examination hall, all three questions were drafted by the fox, and Chen passed the examination.

Tchen's wife suddenly contracted an epidemic disease and did not recover after several days. Tchen asked, "How is my wife's illness?" The fox replied, "I fear there is no hope of recovery." Tchen did not fully believe this. Various medicines were ineffective, and she died after ten days. After the burial, Tchen said, "I wish to remarry. I have heard that ancient people married fox wives, and I deeply admire this. Would you be willing to act as my matchmaker?" The fox said, "To be honored as a friend, how can I refuse the responsibility? But for matters in the past, I have always supported them, and they were all in line with your fortune and destiny. This matter depends entirely on human effort, and its success or failure cannot be predicted. I have a cousin named Feixia, who has reached the age of marriage but is not yet married. She possesses both virtue and beauty and is truly a good match. I will immediately act as your matchmaker." Tchen said, "Can I see her first?" The fox said, "Yes. Wait quietly tomorrow morning, and I will invite you to go together." She then left. The next day, Tchen got up early and dressed in fine clothes to wait. By noon, the fox had not arrived. Tchen had been thinking all night and did not sleep much, and unconsciously fell asleep sitting. The fox suddenly appeared and invited him to go immediately, but the road was very unfamiliar. They suddenly saw a village with only one big gate. The fox entered first, and Tchen followed. They arrived at a guesthouse, which was well-equipped with books, scrolls, and ritual vessels, seeming like a noble family. Tchen asked, "Whose residence is this?" The fox replied, "It is my uncle's, the surname Hu. Please wait a moment, and I will go to visit my uncle." The fox entered and greeted her mother, then said, "Why hasn't my cousin come out to see you?" Her mother said, "She went to pick flowers in the east garden." The fox left and invited Tchen to go into the east garden together. Tchen said, "What are we doing here?" The fox said, "The beautiful lady is here."

Upon entering, they saw a path of flowers and trees, with red and purple blossoms revealing a pavilion faintly in the midst. Two young ladies and two maids were playing among them. As they drew near, the fox said, "Sister Xie, how old are you? You didn't greet your elder brother?" The girl was about to speak, but suddenly saw Tchen and lowered her head in silence. The fox said, "This is my dear friend." He instructed Tchen to bow to her. The girl, blushing, returned the bow. Tchen saw the young lady with sparkling eyes and graceful demeanor, truly a sight he had never seen before. He fixed his gaze on her, completely forgetting his reservations. The fox observed Tchen's reaction and deliberately started talking with Feixia, saying, "How old are you, sister?" She replied, "Sixteen years old." The fox asked, "What is your family name?" Feixia's cheeks turned red. The older maid laughed and said, "I have never seen a cousin ask such questions to an unmarried cousin. If you tell the matriarch, scolding and humiliation are unavoidable."

The fox looked at Chen with a fixed, intent gaze and then recited a couplet from a Tang poem: "We look at each other and never tire of it; only the Jingting Mountain remains." Chen smiled in understanding, and they then went out together. The fox said, "Please go back first, and I will come shortly." Chen woke up, realizing that the fox had invited him to meet in a dream. Recalling the beauty of the young lady, he regretted the dream ending too soon. Soon after, the fox arrived and said, "I have ground down five parts of my teeth for your sake, but the matter still cannot be accomplished. What should we do?" He then added, "I have an aunt's daughter, who is of similar age and equally beautiful. Why not try to match you with her?" Chen replied, "Since I have seen the mountains of Jingting, the waters of the sea cannot compare." The fox heard this and sighed, then said, "Success is determined by heaven, but planning is up to man. Please give me five days, and I will make another attempt for you."

--- If the matter fails again, I will have no choice but to give up." With these words, he left. Indeed, five days later, he returned, saying, "It has been settled. But there is a slight issue that I must inform you: we can live together, but we cannot be buried together." T said, "My previous wife has passed away, and there is already a plan for joint burial. What is the issue?" The fox said, "If so, please choose a date for the wedding." T said, "In ten days, is that acceptable?" The fox said, "Although you have given me an order, why is it so quick?" T also laughed and said, "I am in a hurry, and I still think it’s too slow." The fox agreed. On the appointed day, Hu indeed brought Fei Xia to T. T looked at her and found her even more beautiful than before. After this, the fox rarely came. Fei Xia gave birth to three sons, all of whom became prominent. T lived past eighty, and Fei Xia gradually handed over the household affairs to her sons and grandchildren, while taking care of T’s funeral arrangements. After the arrangements were completed, T died peacefully. After his burial, Fei Xia also passed away. 虚白道人曰：狐之助陈，可谓至矣，然皆弗求助而狐助之。苟放利为心，谘询仰望，恐狐将厌而弃之矣。黄帝遗元珠于赤水，离朱索之不得，象罔求而得之，如是如是。 上元李瑜谨注

A Man Named An Yanyi A man from the village made a living by renting out carriages. He once carried passengers to Dengzhou and saw a young boy outside a store, about seven or eight years old, intelligent, graceful, and very handsome. The man quickly looked at him and said, "What kind of old woman gave birth to such a child?" The store owner pointed and said, "This is An Gong, the nephew of Zhang Xiangfang, who lives opposite the store. There is a strange tale here. Once the store business is done, I will tell it to you." After closing the door, the man enjoyed discussing things and asked the store owner about the story. The store owner said, "Our village stretches east to west for more than six li, and the store is at the eastern end. At the western end, there is An Yanyi, a scholar in the county, who is good at resolving disputes. The neighboring villages rely on him and do not have legal troubles. He arranged a marriage between his son and the daughter of Zhang Xiangfang, who lives opposite the store. The boy and girl were the same age, both fifteen years old. Two years after the marriage, in October, a play was performed at the eastern part of the village to honor the gods. An Shun, the son of An Yanyi, also came to watch the play. As soon as he arrived at Zhang's door, he saw Zhang's daughter standing inside. The two families were old relatives, and the boy and girl had known each other for a long time. He then asked, 'Is anyone at home?' The girl replied, 'Everyone has gone to watch the play.' Shun entered, followed the girl to her bedroom, and they engaged in intimate relations. Afterward, the two young people, unaware of taboos, did not know the rules. Shun happened to be thirsty and drank cold water from a jar, and then suddenly fell ill. He broke out in a sweat all over and could not move even an inch. The girl was at a loss and especially feared that the people watching the play would return. She hurriedly carried Shun to a small house in the garden. The girl kept coming and going, checking on him without stopping, hoping that his illness would improve. By midnight, his breath had stopped.

On that snowy night, the girl, holding back her tears, carried the body and left it in the ash pool within the garden, covering it with scattered wood. By dawn, the snow had piled up to a foot, and the family scooped up all the remaining snow and piled it into the pool, erasing her tracks completely. Not long after, the girl's belly began to grow larger. Her mother asked, "Are you ill?" The girl replied, "No, I am not." The mother became angry and forced her to commit suicide. The girl said, "I should indeed die, but I should die at An's door, not in my mother's home." The mother heard that the girl's words had a reason and pressed her to reveal it. She told the truth. Zhang wanted to send the girl to An's door, but An said, "My son has run away, and I still don't know where he is; how can I marry him off?" Zhang said, "We have already set the date for the wedding, and it is now due. We can first bring the young girl, and wait for the groom to return and complete the ceremony. It is not inappropriate." An did not want to, but Zhang insisted.

After passing the gate, the mother-in-law saw the daughter-in-law's situation and knew she was pregnant. She asked her, and the daughter-in-law replied, "My husband will know when he returns." The mother-in-law said, "When did my son go, and where did he go again?" The daughter-in-law replied, "In October, he came to my home, but I truly do not know his whereabouts." The mother-in-law was half doubtful and half believing, and she scolded her all day long. The daughter-in-law could not bear it and hanged herself at night. The mother-in-law had a dream in which her son said, "The pregnancy of the daughter-in-law, indeed, is my son's child. She is about to hang herself; can you quickly save her?" The mother-in-law woke up in great fear and called her family to go and see her. The daughter-in-law had already hanged herself, so they quickly untied her, and she soon recovered. From then on, the mother-in-law and daughter-in-law began to reconcile. After a month, she gave birth to a son, and the couple of An were very happy. After a hundred days, they finally asked about the child's parentage, and the daughter-in-law truthfully told the reason. The boy seen outside the shop was actually the grandson of An Yanwei and the son of Shun. Later, it was heard that Zhang trained his children well and passed the local examination at the age of twenty.

The Daoist Master Xubai said: "Fortunate indeed is the An family, which has a single thread of ancestral succession. However, at the outset, I had three deep concerns: An Shun died in the Zhang household, having no sons himself, how could he have grandsons? This is the first reason why the continuation of the lineage seemed hopeless. Although An Shun had a relationship with the Zhang family, the first child might not be a boy, which is the second reason why the lineage might be cut off. The Zhang family, though with a child in the womb, the sex of the child was unknown, and if the mother died by hanging, mother and child would die together, which is the third reason why the lineage would be completely severed. Despite the secret union, the birth of a noble son is a sign of the way of heaven rewarding virtue. Those who resolve difficulties and disputes are very few." The Zhang woman upheld her resolve and cared for the orphan; do not mock her for an unmarried private union. Carefully annotated by Li Yu of Shangyuan. Translated by Xue Weidong.

Xue Weidong, a native of Henan, was a childhood friend of Lou Shangyi, with whom he shared the same desk and inkstone. They both entered the local Confucian school at the same time, passed the local examination in the same year, and thus had similar upbringings and education. However, their fates were vastly different. Lou Shangyi passed the imperial examinations consecutively, was appointed as the governor of Guangxi Liuzhou, and traveled by carriage to take office, a glorious honor indeed. Xue Weidong, on the other hand, toiled on the imperial examination circuit, failing multiple times. His family was not wealthy, and he continued to take the spring imperial examinations, needing travel expenses and lacking a position as a tutor or scholar, leading to extreme poverty. Reflecting on his life, he realized that there was no one he had been closer to than Lou, so he borrowed money to visit him. After two months, he finally arrived. At the beginning of their meeting, Xue Weidong already saw that Lou had no interest in him, though he hoped for a generous farewell gift. One day, Lou suddenly wrote a couplet and asked Xue Weidong to compose a response. The couplet read: "In the warm south, it's hard to keep snow." Xue Weidong thought that "snow" and "Xue" sounded the same, and "hard to keep snow" meant that Lou did not want to keep him. He then left without saying goodbye, determined to achieve success in his career. He passed all the imperial examinations, was later appointed as the commissioner of Tianjin in Zhili, and rose to prominence. Lou Shangyi, however, was disgraced for bribery, stripped of his office, and reduced to poverty. Desperate, he sought refuge at Xue Weidong's residence. Xue Weidong treated him with respect. One day, during a drinking session, Xue Weidong suddenly said, "I have now found a perfect response to Lou's couplet from Liuzhou. Don't be surprised." He then recited: "In the high north, the wind is too strong for a tower." "Lou" and "tower" sound the same. Upon hearing this, Lou felt ashamed, blushing, and was unable to apologize quickly enough. Xue Weidong said, "I did not intend to seek revenge. With this perfect response, I can prove the principle of cause and effect." He continued to treat Lou with courtesy. Feeling ashamed, Lou eventually bid farewell, and Xue Weidong generously provided him with a farewell gift.

The Daoist Master Xubai said: Xue Weidong is a loyal and kind person. Having endured the humiliation from a friend, he immediately emulated the same behavior, who would say it was overly harsh? Yet he set it aside, received him with courtesy, and gave him a generous gift, which is indeed rare in the world. As for Lou Shangyi, how could he be mentioned among the scholars? Good at jesting, a frivolous person naturally deserves to be treated with contempt. —— A note by Li Yu of Shangyuan The Fox Lady Feng Fan, courtesy name Jia Ren, was a son from an old family in Taiyuan. At fifteen, he entered the school and went to study under an external teacher. The school was separated by a lane from the residence of a high-ranking official (Yang Taishi). Every morning, he would get up early to go to school, and on his way, he saw a young lady of marriageable age standing alone inside the gate. He gave her a slight glance, and she seemed like a lotus leaf with dewdrops and a plum blossom with mist, her beauty unparalleled in the world. Feng suspected she was the daughter of the Taishi, hurriedly passed by. Later, whenever he saw her, they exchanged glances that conveyed affection, and he gradually forgot all caution. He then loudly recited: "If there is fate, one will meet even after traveling a thousand miles; if there is no fate, they will go their separate ways even when face to face." Whenever he saw her, he would recite this. One day, after he finished reciting, the young lady held a sealed envelope and threw it onto the street. Feng picked it up and opened it, inside it read: "Do not say that the red thread of fate will bind the matchmaker, who will see the good match in the mulberry grove?" After reciting this, Feng was wildly delighted, seeing no one around, he quickly entered her door, kissed her hand, and the girl showed no sign of anger. He urgently asked for a meeting time, and the girl said, "Come tonight, I will be here."

Fan saw the girl's arms adorned with pairs of gold and jade bracelets, and he then removed one of her jade bracelets and left. By evening, the girl was indeed there. The girl invited Fan to enter together, but Fan hesitated. The girl said, "Just follow me and don't leave my side, even if you meet someone, it will be fine." Fan followed her, passing through corridors and pavilions, and indeed, people came and went as if they didn't see them. They reached the back room, where the curtains were drawn and the quilt was laid out, indicating it was the girl's bedroom. They then became intimate. After a night of pleasure, the girl seemed unable to bear it. She said, "The mad lover only knows himself, not others." Fan replied, "It's also hard to be free." Soon, they both fell asleep. The girl woke up and shook Fan, saying, "You're so greedy for pleasure that you've forgotten it's morning!" Fan got up and saw that the sun was already high in the sky, showing worry on his face. The girl said, "Don't worry." She then gave Fan a red scarf, saying, "Hold this when entering and exiting, and don't talk to anyone." Later, Fan entered whenever the door was open, regardless of morning or evening. After half a year, no one was aware of it.

One day, Fan父 was about to arrange a marriage for his son Fan with a certain family and discussed it with Fan. Fan shook his head without speaking. When asked repeatedly, he said, "I only want the Yang family." Fan父 replied, "Yang is a long-time acquaintance, and if he has a suitable bride, the matchmaker should have no trouble arranging it." Fan said, "I have seen her many times." So Fan父 sent someone to investigate. The messenger reported back, "The Yang family has no eligible daughter, and it is said that there are many foxes in the back building of the Yang family, often transforming into human form. What you saw before might not have been a human." Fan父 was afraid and did not allow Fan to go out, and he quickly arranged to marry the daughter of Dong太守, who was both beautiful and virtuous. After the match was set, they hurriedly completed the wedding. On the night of the wedding, Fan looked at the new bride and found her to be exactly the same as the fox spirit he had previously encountered, and he was greatly startled.

In the blink of an eye, two new people appeared, and the guests who had come to see off the daughter could not distinguish them. The mother of Fan said, "I have only this son in my life; I do not want him to have a fox wife. You can quickly leave." The two new people both laughed but said nothing. She then added, "If things can transform into humans, they should also have a sense of shame and revulsion. Being hated by humans and still showing a guilty face here, are you not ashamed?" After she finished speaking, one of them suddenly disappeared. A voice from the darkness was heard saying, "The old mother does not want to have a fox wife; this wish is hard to fulfill." Later, Dong Shi returned home to visit her mother-in-law, who limited her stay to half a month. She would return every three days. When asked why, she said, "My neighbors are getting married, and I fear seeing the new people, so I send my son back." Her mother-in-law believed her. By evening, she said to Fan, "Myself and the fox sister, can you truly recognize us among them?" Fan replied, "I can recognize them; others cannot." The woman laughed and said, "I fear you might also be judging by appearances."

After half a month, the woman from Dong village arrived with her daughter. It was then known that the one who came ahead of time was the fox wife. On a summer night, Fan's mother occasionally fell ill with a seasonal disease and immediately called for Dong. Dong arrived at the sound of her call and saw Fan's mother vomiting and diarrhea incessantly, with the condition being very serious. Dong said, "My daughter-in-law has a pill specifically for this illness. I wonder if Madam dares to try it?" Fan's mother said, "You can quickly bring it." Dong brought the medicine and gave it to Fan's mother to take, and the illness was immediately cured. By dawn, Dong paid respects to Fan's mother. Fan's mother said, "If you had not given me the pill tonight, I would have already gone to the underworld." Dong said, "I did not know Madam was ill last night, so I did not actually give her the pill." It was then known that the medicine was provided by the fox. From then on, she greatly appreciated the fox. After Dong returned, the fox came. Even though she knew it was the fox, she did not stop it.

By the third year, Dong had a son, but the son died due to illness caused by childbirth, and Dong's mother cried bitterly. An old woman told her, "Do not cry, my daughter is not actually dead; she is currently entertaining guests in the inner court." Dong's mother went to see her daughter and found that she was indeed still alive. She asked, "My daughter is still here, so who is the corpse in the coffin?" The old woman explained the reason. Dong's mother said, "She looks just like my daughter, so she must be my daughter. But will she accept me as her mother?" The fox heard this and crouched on the ground calling her mother, and Dong's mother changed from sadness to joy. Although Fan's mother and son mourned the loss of their daughter-in-law, the fox woman helped with the household duties, so their grief was not deep. So the body was kept in the main hall, the coffin was placed in the wilderness, and no one was crying. Dong's orphaned son was named Xiangru, and the fox raised him with skill, treating him as if he were her own child. Fan broke off the marriage and could not continue it, and sometimes the family referred to the fox as the Fox Lady, but the fox did not find it strange.

Xiangru, as he grew older, was intelligent and attractive, entering the school system at the age of 20. Soon after, Fan also passed away from illness. With his father's death, Xiangru had no fear or respect for authority, wandering aimlessly and neglecting his studies. His mother-in-law and mother scolded and humiliated him, but he paid no heed. He believed that being at home would still subject him to constraints, so he secretly took several taels of silver, along with his clothes, and left home, thinking that with money in his bag, he would not lack for resources during his travels. The fox lady used magic to bring him back. He then pawned his clothes, thinking it would last him a year or so. The fox lady used magic to burn them. Within ten days, Xiangru was in dire poverty, feeling ashamed to return home and embarrassed to seek help from friends, finding himself in a difficult situation with no clear way out. He suddenly had a foolish idea but ultimately could not bring himself to act on it. Walking alone in the fields, he was extremely hungry and decided to hang himself. He tied a belt around a tree, extended his neck, and hanged himself. Afterward, he felt someone untie the rope, and he woke up to see an elderly white-haired person.

After some time, the elderly man stood up to thank him and asked, "What is your respected surname?" The man replied, "My surname is Shi, and my courtesy name is Deren. I am currently over eighty years old." The man said, "You are in your prime, so why did you do this?" The elderly man said, "My home is not far from here. You can come to my humble abode to discuss further." After they arrived, Xiangru introduced his own name and narrated in detail the hardships he has faced. The elderly man said, "The blame does not lie with the stepmother. If you had diligently studied and read, there would have been no scolding or humiliation." Then, he served him wine and food. After that, the elderly man said, "How about sending you back to your old residence? Otherwise, in my idle garden, there is a thatched cottage with three rooms where you can study in seclusion. Can you endure the solitude?" Xiangru said, "I can, but I worry about the cost of oil for lighting." The elderly man said, "This is a trivial matter." He then led Xiangru through the inner courtyard to the garden and into the room. The elderly man said, "This garden originally had a side gate. Because no one was guarding it, the gate was locked. Entry and exit must be through the inner courtyard." After bidding farewell and leaving, Xiangru saw that although there were not many flowers in the garden, the young peach trees, the elegant apricot trees, the dark green cypress, and the towering cedars were all enjoyable to view and appreciate.

In the study room, the windows were bright and the desk clean, with bookshelves filled with books. Upon inspection, it was evident that all the books required for the school were largely available. At noon, food and drink were brought out from inside. By evening, the food and drink remained as before, along with various items of clothing, bedding, and curtains. Xiangru felt uneasy and sat alone in the thatched cottage, with nothing to do. He wished to go out for a walk, but the garden gate was locked, and it was also inconvenient to leave through the inner courtyard, causing him great frustration. Unable to do anything else, he resumed his studies, reading aloud to relieve his boredom. By nightfall, the food and drink were more abundant than usual, and a servant said, "The master of the house is about to come out." Soon after, Shi arrived, and the two drank together, with Feng being the host and Shi the guest. Shi said, "Hearing you read is pleasing and admirable. However, every day when reading, one must follow the curriculum to write essays. Who among the recent scholars can be considered a teacher?" Xiangru replied, "A certain Jinshi (imperial scholar) might be suitable." Upon hearing this, Shi took his leave. On the third day early in the morning, Shi gave him one poetry and one prose topic to write.

Xiangru asked, "Who is the author of this question?" The other person replied, "I am a Jinshi." Xiangru asked, "Why did you choose this question?" The other person said, "I have already acted on your behalf by presenting a card and offering a gift." Xiangru said, "Please hurry up and write, and I will come and pick it up and deliver it to the Jinshi every day and evening." After reading, I will return it. Do not neglect your studies. By evening, the historian was indeed waiting in the garden. Xiangru hurriedly copied it out, handed over the scroll, and the historian left. Later, this became a regular practice. One day, both the poetry and prose questions were difficult, and he deeply pondered them, not realizing he fell asleep. When he woke up, it was already past noon. The draft was still incomplete, and the historian had been waiting in the garden for a long time. Xiangru excused himself and explained the reason. The historian replied, "Okay. Please hurry and write, don't worry about me." Despite repeated refusals and agreements, the historian still did not leave. Xiangru then hurriedly started writing, finished it by midnight, and the historian finally left. Xiangru felt truly reluctant about this. Later, he started early and wrote quickly, handing it over as soon as the historian arrived, without making him wait.

Sima Xiangru never looked out of the garden for three years. One day, the historian said, "The great comparison is near; it is time to prepare for registration and submission of scrolls." Xiangru replied, "Yes." The historian then said, "When you go, do not open the garden gate; you may enter and exit through the inner courtyard, and do not let anyone guard the study." Xiangru agreed to all of this. However, every time he went in or out, he would see a girl of sixteen standing in the courtyard. Upon closer inspection, she was as beautiful as a fairy, graceful and elegant, and he could not help but be captivated. Three days before entering the examination hall, the historian respectfully prepared tea and wine to send Xiangru off, saying, "After entering the hall, take good care of your written work, be cautious with fire and candles, do not think in isolated or obscure ways, and there will be hope for the entire competition." He repeatedly and earnestly advised, as if he were a scholar of the Way, and Xiangru respectfully listened to him. He then said, "There is something I wish to ask; please explain clearly."

I saw in the inner court a young woman of marriageable age. Who exactly is this benefactor?” the historian asked. “He is the son of the Huángfǔ family, my wife’s grandson. Since his parents have both passed away, he was taken in here.” Xiangru said, “I have not yet repaid your great kindness, and now I am causing you further trouble, which I feel is inappropriate. But Xiangru has remained unmarried since childhood, and I wonder if you would agree to arrange a match for me?” The historian replied, “After the examination, you will return to your mother-in-law’s home. If you do not嫌弃 humble origins, there is nothing wrong with it. The decision is entirely up to me. Soon you will enter the examination hall, so your spirit should not be distracted outwardly. Please excuse me.” After the three examinations were completed, the scene was grand and impressive, and Xiangru passed as the top candidate. Dressed in proper attire, he visited the historian to thank him for his great favor. The historian said, “This is all due to your mother’s kindness and benevolence; what merit do I have?” Xiangru was startled by this. The historian continued, “I am not someone else; I am your father-in-law. At the beginning, I followed you from the eastern suburbs to the west. Your mother had been waiting at my residence for a long time, her eyes turned red from anticipation! Afterward, your nephew wrote according to the curriculum, and your mother came for every lesson, leaving only after the exam was submitted.

For a long time, my nephew took a very long time to finish his examination. He only knew that I had been waiting for him in the garden, but he did not know that your mother had cried herself sick at home, thinking that he had given up halfway and was no longer trustworthy. When I showed him his completed exam in the middle of the night and explained the reason, she finally changed from sadness to joy. Not only that, but every time there was an exam, your mother would always seek out past exam papers of successful candidates to review. If she found them good, she would show her joy on her face; if not, tears would well up in her eyes. Her heart was torn between joy and sorrow, and it was hard to say how she felt. Because of my nephew, your mother has become extremely thin and weak. Yesterday, before the results were posted, your mother came here to hear the news. Upon learning that my nephew had passed, she immediately rushed home to report the good news to your grandmother. When Xiangru heard this, he cried with his head in his hands, regretting that he had learned about it too late. He immediately ordered his carriage and hurried home. Upon arriving, he fell to the ground and asked for forgiveness. His mother told him to stand up, and Xiangru stood by her side. Seeing his mother weeping continuously, he comforted her, saying, "Now that I have achieved success, you should be happy, and do not grieve too much."

The lady said, "My son is now honored, but my heart has been worn out! When you stole money and fled, I used my method to retrieve it, leaving you with no money and experiencing all kinds of hardships. I cried alone at night, tears floating on the pillow and mat, and no one knew. When I learned you had a foolish thought, I was in a state of panic and confusion, fearing that a slight delay would harm your life. I urgently asked your grandfather to save you quickly. Seeing you return with your grandfather, my heart was still uneasy. Now that you have succeeded, I have no worries, but still not yet, we haven't completed your marriage." Xiangru, upon hearing his mother's words, said, "I have something to report. The granddaughter of your grandmother, Huangfu, has come of age but is not yet married. I have seen her, and I wonder if we can marry?" The lady replied, "I have had this thought for a long time, but there is a fear of being considered different, and I was reluctant to arrange the match. If you desire it, then it is acceptable." Therefore, both Xiangru and his father obtained the fox lady.

The Daoist Master Xu Bai said: "Regarding the esteemed person, I do not envy him for having a beautiful wife, but I envy him for gaining a supportive partner. Regarding Sima Xiangru, I do not envy him for passing the local examination, but I envy his mother's virtue and wisdom. When Sima Xiangru stole money and fled, she devised a plan to force him onto the path of reading and studying—what wisdom! When he suddenly had a foolish idea, she rescued him to ensure his safety—what kindness! When he repented and changed his mind, she involved his father to guide and encourage him, read through his writings with hope, and made a wayward son into a successful and virtuous person—what compassion and righteousness! If all mothers in the world were like Fox Lady's actions, what distinction would there be between legitimate and illegitimate children?"

**Translation:** Whoever reads this text and does not shed tears must lack compassion. Ma Zhuyu There is such a virtuous stepmother; she can be rebuked with a rod and shout. Can humans be less noble than foxes? Li Yu of Shangyuan respectfully annotates **Yizhi Lu Volume Two** **Meixian** Tang Wu from Taixian, courtesy name Naiwen, lived in a villa and his study faced an open field. Outside the wall was a cemetery with countless graves, often seen with phosphorescent lights at night, and sometimes heard with ghostly cries. Wu was bold and placed these matters outside his concern. One night, he got up to cool off and heard a woman crying outside the wall, her voice sorrowful like that of a girl. He spoke through the wall, saying, "What troubles do you have that make you so sad? If you can tell me, come to my study and speak openly. If I can help, I will do my best to comfort your spirit." After he spoke, the crying stopped. Wu returned to his study, and soon a beautiful woman entered, pulling back the curtain. She was a young woman, graceful and beautiful beyond compare. Knowing she was the ghost who had been crying, he sat down with her and asked about her. She said, "I am the daughter of Judge Liu. After my father retired from office, I died and was temporarily buried on the east side of the wall. Now it has been seven years. The people around here are all young ruffians. After a long time, the wooden coffins will rot, leading to the mixing of bones, which is why I am so sad."
