Wu said, "Relocating to a different place is also an easy matter." The woman replied, "My parents have long returned to their hometown, and there are no other relatives here. How could there be anyone who would benefit the dead?" She finished speaking and tears welled up in her eyes. Wu said, "I want to move it, but I don't know where." The woman said, "West of the green willows there is a small stone tablet, inscribed with 'Tomb of the beloved daughter of Liu Tongpan.' The coffin still exists, so it is easy to identify." Wu then volunteered to say, "I will decide to move it tomorrow morning, and do not cry." Upon hearing this, the woman turned from sadness to joy. Wu wanted to enjoy her company, but the woman said, "I cannot bear to bring misfortune to the gentleman. The bones in the night hall are different from living people, and I fear of shortening one's lifespan." Wu then stopped. As they were about to sleep, the woman left. The next day, Wu indeed moved the woman's coffin to a high place. By evening, the woman came to express her gratitude, with her sleeves gathered and demeanor respectful, she was overwhelmed with gratitude. While Wu was reading, the woman attended to him, brewing tea when he was thirsty and preparing food when he was hungry. Wu highly valued her, and did not investigate where the things came from.

One night, Wu wanted to return home, and the woman said, "It is not possible." Wu replied, "What is wrong with my family?" The woman remained silent, and Wu persisted in asking. She said, "If I speak and you become angry, I will not speak; if I speak and you do not listen, I will still not speak." Wu said, "I will fully follow your command." The woman explained the situation in detail, saying, "Handle it like this, like this." Wu heard this and became very angry, holding a knife and wanting to go. The woman snatched the knife and threw it, saying, "How is my advice? If you are so angry upon hearing it, you will be even more angry upon seeing it. You truly cannot work with me!" Wu apologized. The woman said, "Wait until your anger subsides, and I will go with you." After some time, the woman said, "We can go now, and I will assist you in secret." Upon reaching the main gate, the door opened by itself; upon reaching the bedroom door, it opened by itself. The lamp lit the room, and Wu's wife Zheng was having an affair with someone. The two saw Wu and tried to escape, but as if someone was holding them down, they could not get up. Wu saw the situation and realized that the woman had secretly helped him, so he tied the two naked. Since the father-in-law's house was not far, Wu pretended that his wife was seriously ill and deceived Zheng to come. Zheng saw the woman and her lover tied naked on the bed and told Wu, "Life and death are at your discretion; why do I need to see?" Wu said, "Killing them would soil my knife." He released their bindings and sent the woman back to her family. Later, the woman married another man, and the lover continued to see her, but they were all killed by the new husband.

After Wu divorced his wife, the family's honor was compromised, and he moved to study at home. The woman continued to watch over him as before. Being newly widowed, Wu again wanted to offend her. The woman said, "I truly dare not repay a gentleman with harm; now I will find a good woman for you, to take my place." Wu asked, "Who?" The woman replied, "There is a plum tree on a cliff in a certain mountain. Have you seen it?" Wu said, "Yes, I have seen it. The plum tree grows halfway up the cliff, about three zhang from the ground. It has an ice-like posture and jade-like bones, and no one has picked or broken it. Therefore, its faint fragrance floats in the air and can be smelled for several miles. Previously, while visiting with friends, I gave it a poem: 'Why does the fragrant plum blossom attract poets? Its slender bones and clear spirit claim the early spring. Now, there is me, as distant as the poet He Jing. When we meet, do not say there is no reason.'" The woman said, "It's fitting, there is already a connection in the poem! This plum tree has already become a spirit, but it can be captured. The plum blossom spirit hates firecrackers as much as it fears axes. On New Year's Eve, when firecrackers are set off in each village, the plum blossom spirit hears the sound, panics and at a loss, and hides in rock crevices or buries itself in earth pits, only daring to come out the next day. You should place a clean wine vessel under the plum tree, surround it with soil while leaving an opening, and set off a thousand firecrackers a hundred steps away from the plum tree at midnight. I will observe its movements, and after three nights, we will plan again." Wu followed the woman's instructions. On the fourth evening, the woman suddenly arrived and said, "It can be done. It has been hiding in the vessel for two nights, and only comes out at dawn."

Tonight's actions will be the same as last night. When the firecrackers are half gone, light more to continue. Quickly pick up the pole and rush to the plum tree. A few steps away, throw the firecrackers on the ground. Use a pig bladder to cover the mouth of the wine vessel. Carry it back, place it on the table, burn incense, and make a wish. She will come out on her own. However, it can be sought but not forced." Wu followed the girl's instructions, carried the vessel back, placed it on the table, and performed the ritual. After some time, he felt someone patting his shoulder and said, "You scared me to death!" Wu turned around to see a woman with a beautiful, ethereal appearance, unlike any ordinary beauty, perhaps comparable to the moon goddess Chang'e. She embraced him, and he did not resist much. The woman said, "Don't do that. Please tell me, why did you place me in your room, for wine and chess or for intimacy?" Wu replied, "As for wine, my capacity is limited; as for chess, I am not particularly fond of it."

Upon lying down, the entire body was fragrant. During the embrace, it was no different from the fire of the Bosshan incense burner, rising in one breath to the purple clouds. Using her wrist as a pillow, she asked, "Who taught me the way to recognize and cultivate you?" B said, "I recognized you on my own, with unique ingenuity, why should I wait for others to tell me?" Mei did not believe him and pressed him further, so he revealed the truth. Mei said, "This ghost is quite righteous, do not betray her." B, still desiring more, sought further intimacy. Mei said, "Is this all the pleasure for this night? Greed for pleasure is endless, it greatly damages one's life. If you do not accept my sincere advice, I will leave and never return." B then ceased. In the morning, Mei rose and handled household chores as if she had always been there. Neighbors and women came to see her like ants, and this continued for ten days. Mei found this tiresome and said to B, "I will temporarily leave; I will return in five days." She then departed and did not return, leaving B with no recourse.

One night, the woman arrived, and Wu informed her that Mei had not returned, and also asked for advice from her. The woman said, "If you want her to return, it is easy. Have someone use a cannon and fire it far away; he will be afraid and surely come." Wu followed the woman's advice, and Mei indeed returned. She said, "This is another little demon helping the tyrant; I will certainly repay him." After saying this, the woman arrived, and Mei deeply resented her. The woman said, "I am a strange being, unable to approach people, so I trouble the immortal to substitute for me." Mei said, "The little demon is not your wife; what do you mean by substitution? Moreover, everything else can be substituted, but is there anyone in the world who can substitute as a wife? However, it cannot be done constantly." She then said to Wu, "Have you ever seen a beautiful woman in your life?" Wu replied, "Yes. The daughter of a wealthy man named Wan from a certain village, she is uniquely beautiful." Mei said to the woman, "There is one matter to discuss."

Both went out and did not return. The next evening, Mei arrived. She asked, "Why didn't you come?" Mei replied, "She will come soon." It turned out that Mr. Wan from a wealthy family had a daughter named Ruolan, who was beautiful and virtuous but had not yet been betrothed. One night, the family sat together talking, and Ruolan suddenly fell to the ground and died. After a long time, she regained consciousness and asked the Wan family, "Who are you? Why did you not leave?" The family said, "Are you sick or crazy?" Ruolan replied, "I am not crazy. I am the female ghost Liu, who has a marriage agreement with Tang Naiwen. I regret being an otherworldly being and often feel ashamed." Those who heard laughed and covered their mouths. Looking at her clothes and shoes, she realized she had borrowed a body to live. She then said to Mr. Wan, "Tang Naiwen's string has been broken and not yet mended. You can advise indirectly and arrange a matchmaker. I will not marry anyone but Naiwen." Mr. Wan had always known the Wu family but considered the social status mismatch and set it aside. Ruolan then stopped speaking and eating.

万 greatly feared, and through his intermediaries, he hinted to Wu. Wu discussed the matter with Mei, who said, "Yes, Lan is not someone else; she is the ghost of the lady you love, Liu." Wu was startled upon hearing this. Mei explained, "Last night, I went out with Liu, and I led Lan's soul to another place, allowing Liu to borrow her body and live. Otherwise, you and Wan cannot compete in wealth and poverty, how could you possibly marry into a wealthy family?" Wu then agreed to the marriage. On the wedding night, Wu saw that Lan was even more beautiful than ever before, but all the words she spoke were those of Liu, and when they talked about the affairs of the fairy Mei, they were extremely detailed. Mei did not come for several nights. When Lan returned to her parents' home, Mei came at night, and Wu rebuked her. Mei said, "In your happy new marriage, I am here, where would I put myself?" Thus, they spent several intimate nights together. When Lan was about to return home, Mei also left.

One night, 若兰 suddenly asked, "Who are you? Whose house is this? Why am I here?" 武 laughed and said, "Are you crazy? I am your husband. I passed by the door on a certain day, and it has been two weeks since then." 若兰 remained silent and speechless, and 武 also felt puzzled. Later, 梅 arrived, and 武 recounted the situation to her and asked her about it. 梅 said, "I have transformed her soul. Without transforming it to become 万, there would be no way to deepen the bond between husband and wife; without transforming it to become 刘, there would be no way to repay the love of a ghost. However, you and 万 are husband and wife. My feelings for you are as pure as morning dew, but they will naturally dry up when the sun rises. I will soon transform 刘 into a ghost fairy, and I will no longer come here." 武 sighed, "This is a matter for the future; I cannot do it now." 武 then realized that when his wife spoke like 刘, she was referring to the love ghost's form; when she spoke like 万, she was treating him as if he had two beautiful wives. Thus, 武 had one wife but two beauties. Ten years later, 梅's visits became gradually less frequent, and eventually, she stopped coming altogether. 武 only lived with 若兰 until old age. The Pure White Taoist said: To have a flower fairy as a wife, it might happen, but it is still rare; to have one beautiful woman with two souls, treating her as if she were two beautiful wives, is even more unheard of. 武 ultimately achieved this through the matter of moving the coffin of a woman. Thus, we can understand that the gifts given by Su Dongpo to Li Jian and by Ouyang Xiu to Man Qing were also acts of great virtue, like King Wen of Zhou reviving the dead and Han Yu providing a coffin for a traveler.

The entire text is beautifully crafted. Wang Xuemaofeng Yin Zhongxun It surpasses the entry on "Green Feathers" in Liu Zongyuan's *Dragon City Record* in its novelty and vividness. Ma Zhugu He Jing took a plum blossom as his wife, which is a metaphorical expression, but it is claimed that the story is true. The writing is fresh, elegant, and spirited, adding luster to the subject. Shangyuan Li Yu respectfully annotated **Giant Scorpion** Cui, the wife of Wei Daozhang from the eastern outskirts of Qixia, is a virtuous and upright woman. Her family is extremely poor, and her husband is away. They rely on manual labor for survival, and their basic needs are often not met. She often goes to the fields with her sister-in-law to gather vegetables. A man admires her sister-in-law and sets up a trap to force her into a sexual encounter. Cui and her sister-in-law suspect that someone might have hidden grain in the field and fear being accused of theft. Her sister-in-law suggests hiding the grain in a basket and covering it with vegetables to avoid detection. Cui agrees. Just as they are about to leave, the man arrives, feigns anger, and tells Cui, "The real evidence is here. You and your sister-in-law should be beaten severely and handed over to the landowner for his disposal. If you allow your sister-in-law to have a good time with me, then you can take the grain." Cui refuses, and the man violently beats her. Fearing his strength and seeing no witnesses, Cui reluctantly agrees. The man is delighted, takes the woman into his arms, and eagerly wants to proceed. However, he dislikes the wet and dirty ground and says, "There is a raincoat in the pine forest. We can lay it down and rest there." He then wants to take the woman there.

```markdown The woman said, "Do not do that. You go first, and I will follow shortly." The man then let the woman go ahead. Cui hurriedly sent his sister to follow and watch from a distance. Just as she reached the woods, she suddenly returned. When asked, the woman said, "A certain one was lying on the ground crying out in pain, seemingly unable to get up. You should quickly escape." She then abandoned the vegetables and returned home. Soon after, it was heard that the man had died. His father rushed to see him and found his son's body covered in bruises. He questioned the people present at the scene and learned that his son had just returned from drinking with a merchant named Jia. The officials conducted an investigation and summoned Jia to question him. Jia said, "It is true that we drank together, but there was no act of poisoning." The judge, seeing Jia's youthful and frail appearance, thought he seemed incapable of poisoning someone. When asked what he did, Jia replied that he was studying. When asked about his family, he said, "Only my mother is at home." The judge then summoned Jia's mother to question her. She said, "I have a close relationship with the deceased. My son is weak and timid, and he was often bullied by the deceased. Whenever the deceased came to our home, my son was too frightened to stand up, how dare he harm him?" The judge told Jia, "In reality, he was poisoned to death. You and the deceased drank together, which is true. It must be that you were unable to bear his harassment and poisoned him." Jia, unable to defend himself, was forced to falsely confess. ```

One day, Cui and her sister had a quarrel at home. Her sister spoke disrespectfully, and Cui became angry and said, "In the past, during the incident in the pine forest, fortunately, the person who was watching the slope died immediately, otherwise your chastity would have been lost, and you would have been ashamed to face people, having committed suicide long ago." A neighborly old woman heard her words. The old woman was closely related to Mrs. Jia and ran to inform her. Mrs. Jia reported the matter to the official. The official summoned Cui to interrogate her. Cui recounted the fabricated charges of theft and forced indecency and the circumstances of his death in detail. The official was angry and said, "He was indeed a scoundrel, and he is already dead!" The official then asked, "Did he have the intention to drink during the act of forced indecency?" Cui replied, "Yes." "Did he have the intention to be ill?" Cui replied, "No." The official asked, "Did he go to the forest first, and how long after did your sister go?" Cui replied, "Fearing his strength and violence, she went immediately, not much time later."

The official said, "The person was not poisoned by Jia. How could someone die immediately without showing signs of distress after being poisoned?" The official then returned to the pine forest to carefully examine the situation. He saw a large hole in the forest, deep and bottomless, with signs of something entering and exiting. The official ordered water to be poured into the hole, and a large scorpion, shaped like a lute, emerged. The official told the father of the deceased, "Your son died from this creature. Your son had done evil, and it was fitting that he died a violent death, yet you still falsely accused others to seek retribution?" He then angrily dismissed the man. Returning to his office, the official immediately released Jia from restraints and said, "Your survival is entirely due to Cui. And Cui's sister is still unmarried; you may marry her." Jia remained silent. The official said, "Although the girl has been involved in many lawsuits, she has not appeared in court; despite her misfortunes, she is still pure like a flawless jade. This county magistrate will act as a matchmaker; marrying her would not bring shame." Jia finally agreed. The official gave Cui a generous gift for the purpose of marrying her sister.

The Recluse of Pure White said: This is an instance of divine retribution for immoral acts. Yet, there is indeed such a giant scorpion in the forest, who does not know how many people it has injured? But it only injured a certain person, thus knowing that the scorpion is divinely ordained. Blessing the good and punishing the evil is a constant principle. Yet, people often fail to understand this, why is that? —— Wang Xue Ma Feng The scorpion is a venomous creature. Yet, it can eliminate immoral violence and preserve one's reputation, which is said to be divinely ordained, indeed. —— Ma Zhu Wu In the "Scorpion Demon" section of Ming scholar Lang Ying's *Xu Ji Pian*, it is extremely fantastical, but this story is especially strong in reason. —— Shangyuan Li Yu, careful annotation Shangguan Yong Shangguan De was from Huayin, Shaanxi. He married Ren Shi and had two sons: the elder was called Zhi, and the younger was called Ren. After Ren Shi died, he remarried Ma Shi and had a son named Yong. Zhi was strong-willed and brave, often taking up grievances for others. Despite repeated warnings from his father, he would not listen. One day, Zhi went out and saw two acquaintances beating a man, and after they stopped, they started again. Zhi said, "If you kill someone, you must pay with your life!" The two men angrily asked, "Do you know this person well? If so, why don't you help us?" Zhi replied, "I tried to reason with you, but you responded with harsh words. If I help him, what can you do to me?" The two men were also angry and attacked Zhi. The man being beaten also stood up and fought one of them. Zhi's blows were heavy, and he struck the fatal blow, causing the man to fall dead. Frightened, Zhi immediately fled. After some time, the dead man revived, but Zhi, who had fled, did not know this. Shangguan De sent people to search for him in all directions, but there was no news of him.

Not long after, De died of illness. Ma, suddenly filled with jealousy, ordered Ren to manage household affairs, forbidding him from studying with the young child, and gradually treated him like a servant, with his food being especially inferior. At the age of fourteen, Yong saw his elder brother eating coarse food and felt uneasy in his heart. Every time he ate, he asked to eat with his brother. Ma refused, so Yong refused to eat; Ma had no choice but to allow it. The brothers had different dishes, but Ren paid no heed. Every time they ate, Yong would always exchange his food with his brother, but Ren would not allow it. Yong then ate what Ren had eaten, and Ren had no choice but to eat Yong's food. Ma saw this and her jealousy grew even more. There was a dry well in the garden, and Ma placed some supposed lost items in the well, ordering Ren to go into the well to find them. Ren tied himself with a rope and descended. After descending, Ma cut the rope and left. Yong returned from school and did not see his brother, so he asked about it.

马说：“他出去了还没回来。”勇不相信，四处寻找，到了闲园，听到井里有人声；仔细听，原来是哥哥叫自己的名字求救。勇说：“哥哥先忍耐一下。”于是找来一根长绳，一头系在井旁的树上，一头放入井中，让哥哥抓住绳子爬上来。哥哥上来后，勇问他，仁如实相告。勇说：“母亲有伤害哥哥的意图，你应该好好躲避，不要把服从命令当作孝顺。”仁答应了他。母亲得知后，鞭打了勇。勇毫无悔意。一天，仁去亲戚家庆祝，喝得大醉而归。马见到他非常高兴。勇想起以前母亲见到哥哥必定生气，现在反而高兴，知道一定有原因。于是假装去学堂，不久就回来了，却发现门已经关上了。担心母亲会伤害哥哥，知道家里有一个很大的狗洞，于是从那里进去。

Seeing his mother attempting to strangle his brother with a rope, he hurriedly advised, "No! The brother-in-law of the mother is a cunning man. If he strangles his nephew to death, he will have a way to deal with the mother; if it were a boy, he would also not die easily." The mother, frightened, stopped. 勇 untied the knot from 仁's neck, and 仁 woke up, asking, "Are you going to harm my brother?" 勇 replied, "No, it was the mother who did it, but I saved him!" 勇 saw the mother buy and secretly hide "信石" (a type of poison), and upon finding out where it was hidden, he replaced it with something similar and put it back in the same place. Returning from school, 勇 ate, and the mother told him, "Today's food is different; let your brother eat first." 勇 smiled and agreed. 仁 ate and then left. By evening, 勇 asked the mother, "Did you use '信石' to poison my brother today?" The mother denied it. 勇 asked, "Where is the '信石' now?" The mother did not answer.

Yong said, "Fortunately, I replaced [someone/something] with [someone/something else], otherwise you would have already met the king of hell! I told you yesterday that Uncle Ren is meddling in affairs, stirring up trouble, confusing right and wrong, relying entirely on the skill of litigation; distorting right and wrong, skillfully using the power of the pen. If he harms his own nephew, our family will be exterminated!" Yong then secretly invited Ren to his home, and had his brother accompany him for a drink. Ren said, "Yong is so fat, and Ren is so thin, is it not because they eat differently?" Ren replied, "We eat together every day, there is no difference in our food." Ren said, "Ren is so thin, there must be something troubling his mind. If so, you might as well tell your uncle about it, do not let it fester and lead to illness." Ma heard this and broke out in a cold sweat, and thus his heart to harm Ren ceased, and he saw Ren as a thorn in his eye, even if Qin Yi were to be reborn, a thousand words could not change it. Someone said to Yong, "You and Ren were not born of the same mother, why are you distant from your mother but close to your brother?" Yong replied, "One hundred mothers make one father, brothers are still close. Those who know their mother but not their father are no better than beasts." Those who heard this all sighed in admiration.

When the neighbors were holding a gathering, Yong wanted to go and watch, but Ma refused. Yong pitied him, and Ma said, "Who will go with you?" Yong replied, "My elder brother can also go." Ma said, "I am afraid it will harm you." Yong said, "My two elder brothers are sages. If they really wanted to harm me, I wouldn't be here today!" Ma then agreed. Brother and younger brother went together to play. When they arrived at the gathering, they found a crowded place with both men and women. Ren and Yong suddenly got separated. Brother searched for younger brother, and younger brother also looked for brother. Yong saw a classmate and asked about his brother. The classmate lied to him, saying, "Follow this path to find him!" Yong believed him and walked for many miles, but he didn't see his brother and it was already evening. Yong realized he was at the residence of his uncle Ren, so he went there and told them the reason. Uncle Ren and Aunt Ren were very happy and treated Yong like their own nephew. Yong was afraid his mother would be worried, so he wanted to leave early in the morning. Uncle Ren said, "I have already asked someone to inform your mother that you are here, so she may not worry." But the person I asked forgot about it. Ren searched for his brother at the gathering until evening, but there was no news. He thought his brother had gone home first, but the family hadn't seen him.

Ma said, "You hurt my son and fabricated false words to deceive me. How can you still wish to remain at ease alone?" Ren replied, "My brother is not like your brother, and since he went out with me but did not return with me, how could I bear to stay at home?" Ma cursed him in countless ways, all night without stopping. Ren got up early to search, all day without any news, and did not dare to see his mother, so he stayed overnight at a neighbor's house. Someone who came from Ningqiang said they met a person who looked like Yong. Ren then got up early and hurried on, asking around the way, but there was no news. The money he brought was exhausted in two days, so he slept in a temple at night and begged in villages during the day. There was a traveler from Sichuan Chengdu whose servant died in the inn and could not travel on his own. The innkeeper saw Ren and wanted to hire him for the traveler. Ren thought it was hard to return home, so he agreed. The traveler's name was Zhang, and he had a shop in Suzhou and Hangzhou. Seeing Ren's loyalty, upon returning home, he sent Ren to the shop to make a living, and Ren stayed there. Yong stayed at Ren's house for five days, and Ren sent him back. Ma was extremely happy to see him, as if reborn. Yong said, "Where is my brother?" The family said, "Three days ago, they searched for you but you did not return." Yong then stole his mother's belongings, heard that his brother went to Ningqiang, and also asked the way and left. He met a fellow villager who was a rascal and traveled together for two days. The rascal saw that Yong had plenty of money, so he lured him with medicine on the road, stole everything he had, and left. There was a wealthy merchant from Guizhou named Qiao who was returning home with goods. He saw Yong lying unconscious on the side of the road, shook him but he did not wake up, so he loaded him onto the rear carriage. Two days later, Yong's illness then subsided. Qiao asked him, and he told him everything truthfully. Qiao had no children, so he adopted Yong as his son.

Ren was in a shop in Chengdu, and over ten years, he had risen to become the head of the business, having already accumulated half of the capital. At that time, there was someone who owed goods but tried to defraud and sue Ren. The magistrate Qiao, who had a good reputation in governance, heard of Ren's name and immediately summoned him. Ren's words were straightforward and just, so the magistrate ruled that the debtor must clear the debt within a set period, and if delayed, he would be severely punished. After Ren returned, he praised Magistrate Qiao's fairness with his shop friends. Suddenly, someone reported that the magistrate had arrived. In a hurry, the magistrate had already entered. Ren looked closely and saw it was Yong. It turned out that Yong had taken the surname Qiao, passed the imperial examinations, and was appointed as the county magistrate of Chengdu. The brothers met, and their emotions were a mix of joy and sorrow. They each recounted their hardships, unable to hold back their emotions. Yong said, "Tomorrow morning, I will welcome my brother to the office and we will talk in detail." From then on, the brothers would meet and talk every evening. One day, Ren saw Yong with a worried expression and asked him. Yong said, "There is a military commander who has insulted me with his words."

He was favored by the higher-ups, but frequently faced slander and defamation, and feared being impeached, which is why he was worried. Ren asked about his surname and given name, and Yong replied, "He shares the same surname as my family and has the same name as my elder brother." Ren said, "How can we know he is not my elder brother?" Yong said, "The appearance is somewhat similar, but I wonder how my elder brother could have come here. That is why I dare not recognize him." Ren said, "I will try to visit him." One day, the general riding out on horseback, Ren called out loudly, "Is that not my elder brother?" The general heard and dismounted to meet. After careful examination, it turned out to be true, and so they went together to the government office to catch up. Ren asked, "Do you have a dispute with Magistrate Qiao?" The general replied, "Yes." Ren said, "Magistrate Qiao is not someone else; he is my younger brother." The general said, "He is surnamed Qiao, how could you say that?" Ren explained it in detail. Riding together to the county, the brothers reunited, and the joy is self-evident. Later, Yong requested leave to return to Shaanxi, serving his mother in Sichuan and taking care of her. The three sons were all present, and the Ma family was at peace.

The Taoist recluse Xu Bai said: Upon hearing this story, I could not help but admire it several times. At the age of more than ten, the young boy was able to share his sparse food with his elder brother, demonstrating the virtue of taking the smaller portion of fruit, which is beyond words. I could not help but admire him! He went to great lengths to save his brother, fearless of his mother's labor, showing that brothers are as close as hands and feet, and the injury is so severe that it cannot be healed, indicating his deep understanding of righteousness and courage. I could not help but admire him! Upon hearing that his brother had searched for him and perished, he did not fear hardship, secretly hiding and searching for him, showing that his emotions were purely natural and instinctive. I could not help but admire him! As for Ren, who faced the difficulties from his stepmother, who was in a position to harm him like digging a well or completing a granary, yet he remained silent and without complaint, I could not help but admire him! The brothers reunited, both achieving wealth and status, all unexpected, and I could not help but admire the entire family. I wonder if future generations will also admire this story as much as I do?

Reading it evokes the natural emergence of filial and fraternal love in one's heart. — Wang Xuemafeng The misfortunes of Shangguan Zhi are even more severe than those of Zhang Cheng in "Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio"; the respect and reverence for Shangguan Ren surpass those shown to Zhang Cheng in "Strange Tales from a Chinese Studio." As for Shangguan Zhi encountering Ren and Yong, and Zhang Qianguan meeting Ne and Cheng, all were unexpected, fully demonstrating the effect of brotherly love and respect. This text is especially sincere, simple, and profound, with intense emotions and deep reasoning; its writing and events can indeed be transmitted alongside Zhang Cheng's biography! — Wang Zhisan This text is beneficial to the moral and social order. — Ma Zhuwu The events in this text are similar to those in "Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio" involving Zhang Cheng. The narrative is structured with a methodical alternation, making it suitable for transmission alongside "Zhang Cheng's Biography." — Ye Yunshi Observing the love and care between Ren and Yong, the events of easily eating, hiding poison, and advising the mother, one laments the good fortune of the Wang family and notes that others cannot outdo it. — Yang Zihou

Reading the chapters of *Tang Du* and *Tang Di* from the *Book of Songs*. The scholar must be deeply devoted to friendship and love, hence the language used is so intimate and flavorful. Annotation by Li Yu from Shangyuan. **Centipede** When Mr. Jiao Yinquan from Zhang County was a student, he once taught at a nearby village. At the time, I was teaching at Huangtai Mountain, and we visited each other frequently. We discussed an incident from Zhang County, and although the details of the person's name and place of residence were fully known, I have forgotten them. Here is a brief record of the event: There was a person named Jia who was filial to his mother but extremely poor. He had a strong and impressive physique, and he carried firewood daily on his shoulder to sell and support his mother. One day, while returning home with firewood, he saw a young lady in front of him. Thinking it was just a common sight, he passed by without looking. The young lady called out and asked for directions. Jia stopped to look, and saw that she was a beautiful and quiet girl. Her eyes conveyed affection, and he was captivated by her. She asked, "Is this the right way to reach a certain place?" He replied, "Yes, but the place you are asking about is far and it will be dark, so you will not be able to get there." She said, "I will stay overnight at the village ahead." Jia was about to leave with his firewood, but the girl spoke again, "Do you have an empty room?" He said, "Yes, but my mother is at home, and I cannot make decisions alone." She said, "Please ask your mother first, and I will come later." He agreed. When he returned home, he told his mother. His mother thought it was a convenient thing to let a woman stay, so she agreed. By the time he returned, the girl had already arrived. Seeing that the girl's appearance was unusual and that she spoke to him without any shame, he was suspicious.

The mother then placed the girl in a quiet room and called Jia to her bedroom to give him instructions, saying, "She is a woman, and it is not appropriate to have a long conversation with her." Jia nodded and left. The girl, seeing that her mother was not there, asked Jia, "Where will you be staying?" Jia did not respond. The girl looked at him with a look of fear, and then said, "Live with the mother in the same room, each in their own space." She added, "Do not lock the door at night, I will come to you." Jia agreed. By evening, Jia followed his mother's instructions and locked the door before going to sleep. At midnight, the girl tapped on the window with her finger and called for Jia to open the door. Jia seemed to be afraid to not open the door, so he opened it and saw that it was not the girl, but a monster, shaped like a bag, with equal height from top to bottom, and no distinction between head and feet. Fortunately, a large axe used for chopping wood was nearby, and Jia grabbed it and swung it at the creature, which howled and went away. When Jia burned it, he saw that the creature's lower jaw was shaped like a fan. By dawn, Jia followed the blood trail and found that it led to a certain mountain, where he saw a centipede that was about a zhang long, as thick as a large bowl, still wriggling and not yet dead. He swung the axe at it again, and it died immediately. It turned out that Jia had once been chopping wood in that area and saw a large creature coming and going from a stone hole, fearing that it would cause harm, so he blocked it with a large stone. Two days later, when he checked, the stone had moved again, so Jia blocked it again. The arrival of the monster may have been due to this. Jia's survival was fortunate.

虚白道人曰：孝之必获神佑也，审矣！盖妖物既化女身以惑甲，必令甲死于女身。乃扣关时似女子，而启户视之非女，或妖物仍托为女，惟甲自视非女。不然，甲将死于女，何能执斧伤妖物，而自得不死也？ Translation: The Daoist recluse Xubai said: "Filial piety will surely receive divine protection, indeed! The demonic entity, having transformed into a female form to bewitch Jia, must cause Jia to die as a woman. When Jia knocked on the door, it appeared like a woman, but when he opened the door and looked, it was not a woman. Perhaps the demonic entity still pretended to be a woman, but only Jia saw himself as not a woman. Otherwise, Jia would have died as a woman, so how could he have wielded an axe to injure the demonic entity and survive?"

A Style of Broad and Free Flowing Elegance. The Flowing and Majestic Wind. A large stone blocks a stone hole, fearing that it might cause harm, this is also a heart as firm as burying a snake. Benevolent people are blessed by the divine, how could evil spirits harm them? This is a precaution. The brush has no trace of dust, it is referred to the *Taishi* to establish its purity. Annotated with care by Li Yu of Shangyuan. Shen, the Ritual Master During the reign of Kangxi, the area around Dengzhou suffered from a severe drought, with the summer season still barren and without green grass. The magistrate sincerely prayed, but for ten days there was no response. He then gathered senior officials from the six departments and asked if there were any techniques for summoning rain. One official said, "There is a ritual master named Shen in a certain place who is skilled in summoning rain." The magistrate said, "Quickly invite him to come!" The official replied, "Not only would he not come when invited, but upon hearing this news, he would certainly flee. Only by personally visiting and pleading might he not shirk responsibility." Upon hearing this, the magistrate immediately went to visit him. Without bringing attendants, he directly went to Shen's residence; he entered the door, ascended the hall, and saw Shen reading a book by the window. Shen was greatly surprised and said, "I did not know that Your Excellency had come, I failed to receive you properly." The magistrate said, "I fasted and visited, with respect, I have a request." Shen asked, "What is it?" The magistrate said, "The drought demon is causing harm, the common people are suffering, if you can universally save, how could you stand by and do nothing?" Shen replied, "The scholar truly cannot, the person who reported this was mistaken."

The Governor said, "All people are my fellow beings, all things are my companions. How could a virtuous scholar not have a responsibility? If one cannot, then seek someone who can; if one is capable but claims to be incapable, then what was the purpose of seeking one's ability in the past?" Shen was moved by the Governor's sincerity and agreed, saying, "Is it a literary request or a martial one?" "Is there any difference?" Shen replied, "A literary request involves setting up a platform for prayers, which requires some time; a martial request can bring rain immediately." The Governor said, "In a great drought, waiting for rain feels like a year. A martial request is very good. How does the martial request work?" Shen said, "There is an ancient temple several miles from the sea. A platform can be built in the temple. Additionally, on the beach, a long iron chain, about twenty feet long, can be prepared. Twelve blacksmiths can each set up a forge and heat the chain red-hot, then together lift and throw it into the sea. Also, pre-select two swift horses, and the official must ride alongside the scholar to the platform site without delay. This is the method, and it can be carried out tomorrow morning." The Governor agreed to all of this and returned home, preparing everything as Shen had said.

Early the next morning, the prefect arrived to see Shen using several square tables, having built a three-tiered altar in the open ground. On top of the altar, rain shelters were set up, and talisman water was sprinkled everywhere. Shen said to himself, "I have arrived here, so I have nothing to fear." After the setup was completed, he then went to the coast with the prefect. Each burned incense and bowed three times, and afterward, Shen ordered the prefect to ride ahead and wait for him. When Shen saw that the lock had been heated to a red glow, he drew his sword, chanted a spell, and commanded, "Hurry! Throw it!" The craftsmen each used fire tongs to lift the lock and together threw it into the sea. The sea water surged like boiling water, with a sound like thunder. Shen held his sword and rode a horse, galloping alongside the prefect. Behind him, lightning and thunder raged. Shortly thereafter, the lightning and thunder seemed to swirl around his head. Shen was greatly afraid, holding the prefect's horse reins with one hand, his face pale as chalk, and he cried, "Hurry! Hurry!" When they reached the temple and dismounted, Shen smiled and said, "I have no worries now!" He entered the temple and ordered the prefect to seek shelter from the rain inside, instructing him to signal when the rain was sufficient. Shen ascended the altar and sat cross-legged. Soon, it began to pour heavily, and after some time, the prefect said, "The rain is sufficient!" Shen commanded, "Stop!" Immediately, the rain ceased and the clouds parted. Shen descended from the altar and said, "This is the proper method to subdue dragons, but it inevitably makes one an enemy of the dragon. One can only practice this after maintaining righteousness. If one acts unjustly, the dragon will surely retaliate. My master and my teacher both died at the hands of the dragon, which is why this cannot be done lightly. I will also retreat from this place." The prefect offered gifts of money and goods, but Shen firmly refused and left without accepting.

The Daoist Master Xubai said: "To correct people with correctness is an eternal truth. The technique of praying for rain is a small technique, yet it still carries a sense of caution about not being correct. How much more so for larger matters?" Those throughout history who have ended up both dishonored and with a bad ending are all said to be the result of fate, but it may not be entirely due to their lack of correctness. Words have their significance, not just because of writing skills. - Wang Xue Ma Feng Before the respected Mr. Bao Wen passed away, he bestowed his learned scholarship upon his son and warned him not to practice it. He also said, "If the emperor thinks of me and asks about my last words: I wish to govern with virtue." "The word 'govern' means 'correct.' The master did not forget to correct the ruler even in death, therefore practicing with correctness. He warned his son not to practice, lest his son become incorrect, and to be cautious about the possibility of the technique being incorrect." - Gai Fang Ru Guo Pu was killed by his own technique, having placed himself in an incorrect position. If it were Shen Jun, he would use correctness as a technique, and it could correct the evil techniques of the world. - Ma Zhu Wu

The True Person Sa was moved by the Heavenly Lord Wang because he regulated himself with rectitude. By reading this, one can understand that the master regulated himself with rectitude! Carefully annotated by Li Yu from Shangyuan. --- Yuan Xiuyun My fellow scholar, Sun Xiangyu, was a student in the local school and excelled in poetry and verse. He once set up a lectern at the White Snow Tower of Baotu Spring and, in his spare time from studies, would sometimes go to the spring for leisurely strolls. One day, during a lesson period with his students, after setting a topic, he felt unusually bored and restless, so he took a leisurely walk. Before he knew it, he had arrived at the spring, where he saw a young woman and an elderly woman. Glancing at them, he noticed the young woman was exquisitely dressed and stunningly beautiful, truly the most beautiful maiden he had ever seen in his life. He suspected she was a lady from a wealthy family, so he held back and did not stare openly. But the young woman conveyed emotions through her eyes and eyebrows, as if she had an intention. Soon, the elderly woman and the young woman left together. Sun watched them leave, and the young woman glanced back and smiled, her beauty radiating in a hundred ways. Sun thought to himself, this must be an immortal, for in the world, how could there be such a beautiful and graceful woman with such an enchanting demeanor? He followed them, but soon lost track of where they went. Six days later, he returned to the spring for another leisurely stroll, and the young woman and the elderly woman were there again. The young woman's feelings and demeanor were even more endearing than before, as if they were physically apart but spiritually connected, wanting to speak but hesitating. The elderly woman saw this and hurriedly urged the young woman to leave, and when the young woman glanced back, the elderly woman blocked her with her body. Sun was so entranced that he was disoriented and could not control himself. He hurriedly followed them, but they were only a few steps away when they suddenly disappeared. Sun decided they were not human, and upon returning to his study, he deeply contemplated and admired them.

Again, when the students were not present, the loneliness was unbearable, so he composed five quatrains to express his longing. The first one says: "A glimpse of her divine beauty makes my soul fly, my heart breaks, only known to the mountains of Wu. Who will share this bright moon tonight? Do not be late, with your delicate jade rings." The fourth one, I have forgotten. After recording them, it was already the second drumbeat, and he burned them with a lamp. Soon after, a beautiful woman entered from outside and said, "How extreme is the passion of this madman?" Seeing her, it was the young lady seen during the day. Greatly delighted, he embraced her. Her face turned a deeper shade of red, and under the light, she was even more beautiful than during the day. The woman pushed him away and said, "Do not do it so hastily! Before I have even spoken a word, you act like this, how can your passion not wait?" Sun released her, and asked, "Are you a ghost or a fox?" The woman did not answer. Sun said, "Even if you are a ghost or a fox, it would still satisfy my long-held wish, and it would be no harm to tell me." The woman said, "I am neither a ghost nor a fox. Since you suspect me of being a ghost or a fox, then consider me as such. Why interrogate me further?" Sun asked, "How old are you?" She replied, "I am sixteen years old." "What is your name?" The woman did not answer. Sun said, "How can there be someone who talks and laughs all night without knowing their name?" The woman said, "I am Lady Yuan, with the nickname Xiuyun."

After a while, she said, "I spoke in error! How could I let you know my given name? Please remember not to call me by it!" Sun replied, "I just now composed five seven-character quatrains to express my deep longing and hopes. Do you know about them?" The cloud said, "I just warned you not to call me by my name, and I just heard it and immediately called you. But I cannot prevent you from calling me. I and you first met, how could I know your excellent works?" Sun recounted them in detail, reading and explaining as he went. The cloud said, "Reading them is fine, but do not explain. Do not say that the excellent work has profound meaning and is difficult for others to understand. According to me, they are just plain phrases." Sun lost interest and did not read again. After a while, Sun said, "It is late at night, should we go to sleep?" The cloud said, "We need wine for the wedding ceremony." Sun said, "The wine for tonight can be made up tomorrow night." They went to sleep. When they woke up, the cloud had already left. The next night, Sun sat quietly waiting for the cloud. Suddenly, he heard someone laughing loudly, saying, "Has the poet Sun not yet gone to sleep?" Sun was just about to get up to greet, and the cloud had already arrived in front of him. Sun said, "Do not speak loudly, the student may not be asleep yet." The cloud said, "It does not matter. As soon as I arrive, I will make a lot of noise, ensuring that he does not hear. The previous night's joyful gathering, there was no wine to drink, it was really a regrettable matter. Shall we buy some now?" Sun said, "Being in the same building as the disciples, fearing it might be inconvenient, that is why we did not buy any."

"Cloud said, 'Frugal, indeed! What is there to fear? I have brought wine with me.' Sun asked, 'Where is it?' Cloud replied, 'Is this not it?' She saw the wine utensils flying in from outside, as if carried by someone unseen. The cups, chopsticks, dishes, and fruits all entered in the same manner. Sun was amazed and said, 'The roles have been reversed!' Cloud smiled. At this moment, Sun was drinking with the beautiful lady, leaning close and laughing, finding the joy of a minor success far less than this. He said, 'Yesterday, I saw your face and longed for you with great earnestness. I did not expect you to come as I had hoped. What merit do I have to deserve this?' Cloud said, 'I have a predestined fate with you. Even if you did not long for me, I would still come, to fulfill our destiny. I fear that if we delay, it will be too late.' Her words were filled with sorrow. Sun said, 'You are still in your prime; what harm is there in a slight delay? And what is there to worry about tonight? Let us play a drinking game.' Cloud smiled and agreed. They drank until late into the night, becoming drunk before finally going to sleep. Sun asked for intimacy, and Cloud said, 'Is the greatest pleasure in this?' He replied, 'Without this, there is no true pleasure.' Afterward, they lay together in the same bed and talked. Cloud said, 'You must keep this secret and not tell anyone. If rumors spread, I will no longer be able to come. I urge you, I urge you! And since your body is naturally weak, I should not come too often. When I come, I will come; do not wait anxiously.' From then on, she would come every six or seven days. When she came, they would drink all night, and sometimes she would leave without sleeping. Sun also accepted this."

One day, a close friend named Sun suddenly arrived, and they agreed to meet the following noon in Foshan for a gathering. Subsequently, Sun and his friend went to a place called "Spring Play," where it suddenly started to drizzle. The friend said, "It's a pity we don't have any prepared meat dishes. If we did, we could enjoy the rainy weather together, and wouldn't the fun be even deeper?" Sun laughed and agreed. The friend then stood up to admire a couplet, and Sun followed. As they turned around, they saw food and drink already laid out on the table. Sun knew that "Xiu Yun" had provided the food and drink, so he said to the friend, "Let's drink!" The friend was surprised and asked, "How did this happen?" Sun replied, "The servant brought it." The friend asked, "Why didn't I see the servant?" Sun explained, "While you were admiring the couplet, the servant placed the food and drink and left, so you didn't see him." The guest and host drank together, and the rain stopped just as the friend left. The next day, Sun wanted to meet a friend but was concerned about the hot sun. Suddenly, he remembered a straw hat left behind by a villager, so he put it on and went. As he was about to leave the gate, the wind blew the hat off, and the strap was broken. If he wore it, he would have to hold it, and if he held it, it would be useless, so he decided to leave it at a shop he knew. Soon after, he saw that the strap had been mended. He thought, "It must have been 'Xiu Yun' who mended it, who else could it be?" So he put the hat back on.

Upon arriving, the seven people drank together, all of whom were known for their ability to drink. They took turns following the drinking rules, expressing their feelings and enjoying themselves freely. Later, they used large cups and played a drinking game. Sun felt he had had enough to drink and was very wary of continuing. The other six people were in the same situation, and it was difficult for him to stand out. So, he also followed suit. After losing, he lifted the cup but the wine was already gone, and this happened every time. He greatly appreciated Xiu Yun. Therefore, the six people were all drunk, but Sun returned alone and sober. Sun missed her very much because she had not come for several days. However, Xiu Yun suddenly arrived and said, "I haven't seen you for several days, causing you to worry, and I felt very uneasy." Sun thanked Xiu Yun first for the matters in Quanshang and Foshan upon seeing her. Xiu Yun said, "Although I didn't come openly, I often secretly observed with the old woman, fearing that you might have made some mistakes. Sending wine and replacing the belt are minor matters." Sun was deeply grateful. Xiu Yun asked, "Shall we drink?" Sun replied, "Yes, but I don't know when you would come, so the food and wine are not prepared. What should I do?" Xiu Yun said, "Don't worry, I will bring them myself. What I said about your stinginess the other day was just a joke." Soon, the food and wine were laid out on the table. Sun wanted to play the drinking game again, but after losing, he would drink and win by singing. He said, "Drinking is fine, but I'm not familiar with singing." Sun lowered his head and remained silent. Xiu Yun thought Sun was angry and asked, "Can someone else sing for you?" Sun replied, "Yes." Xiu Yun asked, "Is there a famous courtesan now?" Sun replied, "Yes, there is Lan Jun, who is both beautiful and talented, and I have met her once before."

Cloud rose and faced south, muttering words under her breath. Soon after, a beautiful woman entered carrying a pipa. Upon seeing her, it was Lady Lan. Sun then sat with her and offered her wine, commanding her to sing. Lan tuned her strings and warmed up her voice, then sang a song. Sun said, "The sound coming from the beautiful woman's mouth was especially pleasing to the ear." Cloud said, "The *Xiang Duoqing* song is very good, sing it for us to hear." Lan heard this and was displeased, answering that she didn't know the song. Sun said, "Cloud, since she doesn't know it, let her sing another song instead." Cloud said, "If that's the case, I won't force her." She then pulled out a red scarf and tied it to her collar button. Lan saw this and was startled and fearful, so she changed her mind and said, "I do know it, but I sing it poorly." Cloud said, "It's clearly intentional disrespect; I'll make her stand and sing!" Lan indeed stood and sang. Afterwards, she said, "Master Sun, I've been standing for a long time, why don't you intercede on my behalf?" Sun interceded on her behalf, and Cloud agreed. Lan said, "Thank you, Cloud, for your forgiveness." Cloud became angry again and said, "Am I to be called by lowly people?" Lan was greatly frightened, her teeth chattering loudly. Sun pleaded on her behalf, and Cloud's anger subsided slightly. Cloud wanted to send her away, but Sun wanted to keep her for the night. Cloud said, "I'm not someone who gets jealous; if she can replace me, I'm not unwilling."

He then unfolded the red scarf and quickly put it in his sleeve, and Master Lan had already disappeared. When he asked about it, she said, "There's no need to ask further." She then took out five taels of white silver and said, "Carry this to the courtyard, and it can bring three nights of joy." Later, she gave her red scarf to Sun and said, "The soul of Master Lan is inside. When you meet him, unfold the scarf to show it to him, and he will wake up. Your good fortune is approaching, and I should not stay here. I will come on the twentieth day." She then took her leave, and Sun could not persuade her to stay. In the afternoon, Sun went to the courtyard with the scarf, asked for Master Lan by name, and the madam declined, citing illness. Sun said, "I came specifically because of his illness." The woman replied, "Are you Master Sun, who teaches at the White Snow Tower?" Sun was puzzled and asked her. She said, "Master Lan suddenly fell ill last night, and he would not wake up even when shaken. He said his soul was at the White Snow Tower, and tomorrow, Master Sun will surely bring him there." She then led Sun to see Master Lan. Sun unfolded the scarf and showed it to Master Lan, asking, "Have you seen this?" Master Lan suddenly woke up and quickly stood up to express his gratitude. They then respectfully set up a table with wine and drank together, playing and singing late into the night. As Sun was about to leave, Master Lan offered him money for the wine and other expenses, but he firmly refused. Sun went twice more but found it uninteresting and stopped going, waiting only for the woman to return. On the appointed day, she indeed arrived and said, "How fresh and lovely you are, but I am like a fan discarded in autumn!" Sun apologized, and they resumed their intimacy as before.

The meetings became even rarer, and by the time of the school's closure, Sun was already ill, but he could still manage. When the cloud arrived, she said, "This night's meeting is a lifelong farewell." Sun was surprised and asked why. She replied, "It is difficult to express in one word, but in short, my fate with you is finished!" Sun insisted on a future meeting. She said, "If not, then again at the school in early spring." She left before dawn. In early spring, Sun struggled against his illness to go to the school. The cloud arrived and asked, "How is your illness?" Sun said, "All medicines are ineffective, the path to the netherworld is near!" The cloud said, "Life and death are predestined, just accept it. Our friendship over the years, and yet I never once visited you in your illness, which is ultimately a regret. And now that we are about to part forever, I cannot bear to hide the truth. I am not a ghost or fox spirit, but actually the daughter of a mountain deity. Since things have come to this, there is no harm in telling others. Do you have a scholar friend named [name]?" Sun replied, "Yes, I do." She said, "[Name] wrote the *Yizi Lu*, you can tell him to add this as an entry." They then parted. Sun returned home ill, passing by my study, and told me in great detail. A month later, Sun died of illness. When Sun told me, I still had aspirations that I had not yet achieved. With such a deity, my writing is illuminated.

The Daoist Master Xubai said: "How beautiful the fairy is! Cloud is superior to all kinds of beings!" Indeed, Cloud's interactions with Sun are such that they last for several days, and only those who can restrain their desires and cultivate deep affection can achieve this. She secretly serves food and wine to entertain guests and openly fixes a fallen hat, showing her skillful and considerate behavior. Only those who do not defy their husband's wishes and are good at household duties can do this. She sings on behalf of Lanjun's soul and understands the karmic connection, knowing Sun Lang's lifespan. However, she sees the subtle signs but lacks the power to bring the dead back to life. If one were to blame her for not helping her husband when he was ill, it would be a minor benefit, but it is a pity that my friend did not consider this.

**Translation:** What virtue or merit did Mr. Sun possess to deserve this? — Wang Xue and Ma Feng I was very close to Sun Xiangyu, yet I did not know he had such a favorable encounter. However, since she is the daughter of a mountain deity and possesses such great supernatural powers, she still cannot extend her lifespan with elixirs, which greatly puzzled me. — Hou Baili In the past, Shen Jiao was known for his eloquent speech, and people of his time praised his skillful tongue. I say that the goddess's eloquence is due to the author's skillful writing. — Li Yu of Shangyu respectfully annotated Mr. Wei Prostitutes, actors, and others in eight categories are held in even greater contempt in certain counties. The children of those who fall into these categories are not guaranteed to receive government stipends if they do not form mutual agreements with local children, and scholars consider teaching these people a disgrace. During the reign of Emperor Kangxi, there was a wealthy man named Wei who fell into two of these categories but had a large fortune and three sons. He used a large sum of money to hire a bright child to teach them. Whenever nearby families of scholars had celebratory or mourning events, he generously gave gifts but did not include himself in the guest list. Moreover, when he heard that scholars were having a drinking party, he always respectfully prepared food and wine and sent someone to deliver it, causing scholars to jokingly say that they didn't need to bring much food and wine, as Mr. Wei would definitely provide something. Indeed, this was the case, and such occurrences happened dozens of times. In a certain place, there are many maple trees, and after autumn, the leaves turn red, making it quite beautiful. Scholars agreed on a date to go out together to enjoy the scenery. On the appointed day, about ten or more people attended the gathering, and Mr. Wei's provision was more abundant than usual.

They then said to each other, "Master Wei has prepared feasts more than once. If he has something to trouble us, we will plan for him on our behalf, and it will not be in vain." They then ordered the person who brought the feast to call Master Wei. All said, "You have come, you can sit and drink." Master Wei said, "With all of you here, how could there be a place for a lowly person to sit?" They replied, "If you don't sit, you can stand and drink a few cups." Master Wei drank as commanded and finished. All said, "We have often provided you with abundant food, and it makes us uneasy. If you have something you cannot do, please speak clearly, and we will do our best to help you." Master Wei said, "I have nothing else. My three sons have reached adulthood, and I want to trouble all of you to ask on my behalf for a teacher to teach them, is this possible?" Everyone heard this and all had a difficult expression. One person said, "Is it possible to ask Master Yu?" Although he is a jinshi, his family's finances are difficult, but if we speak gently, he will surely agree. Everyone said, "It can be done."

Then he told me, "Stay here and wait. Mr. Yu's residence is not far from here. We will go together, and you can immediately receive good news." Upon arriving, we engaged in casual conversation, and no one dared to speak about the matter. After a long time, Mr. Yu asked, "You all have nothing to do, and it is rare to come together. Why don't you speak about it and discuss it together?" One person said, "Mr. Yu usually has nothing to do. Setting up a lecture hall could also relieve boredom." Mr. Yu replied, "Regardless of whether there is no place to teach, or if there is, who would be willing to be like Cao Qiu Sheng?" Someone said, "There is." Mr. Yu asked, "Who?" The person hesitated to speak, and everyone said, "Speak to Mr. Yu without angering him." Mr. Yu said, "The group is planning for me. How could I be angry?" Everyone suggested that I speak. Mr. Yu was displeased and said, "Am I to teach the son of someone?" Everyone said, "Wait for the right price before selling, and it is not necessary to be overly selective about the family."

Yu Zaisi said, "If that is the case, a gift of 500 pieces of gold, if he is willing to give it, I will comply." They all said, "Please go and ask about it." When they saw someone, they said, "It is agreed, but the gift of gold is not too much." The person asked, "How much?" They replied, "500 pieces of white gold." The person said, "Not too much, not too much." They all said, "In that case, you go and bring the gift of respect, and we will present it on your behalf today." The person was about to leave, and one person said, "If the gift of respect is generous, if it is simple, we can write it for you." The person agreed and left. The group discussed, "Now, for this person's matter, 10 pieces of gold are not too little." Soon after, the person returned, saying, "Is 50 pieces of gold not too little?" They all said, "Not too little." The person said, "There is still another request, on the day of enrollment next year, may I trouble all of you to accompany me with honor." They all said, "It is our pleasure. Drinking is a small matter, we can present the gift of respect for you." They then left.

In the spring, the students indeed accompanied Mr. Yu into the school, drinking merrily all day, and Mr. Yu was also pleased. Whenever the teacher's food was served, I must personally wait on him, and food must be served with chopsticks. Suddenly forgetting and using my hand, Mr. Yu became angry and said, "You lowly hand, you've contaminated the food, who would eat it?" I responded humbly, "Yes, yes," and quickly picked up the food with chopsticks to serve it. One day, Mr. Yu told the students, "In the evening, it seems someone is performing a ritual inside the garden gate. What could be the reason?" They replied, "My father thanks the teacher for his instruction." Mr. Yu asked, "Is this done every day?" They answered, "From the time I started school until now, without a break." Moved with compassion, Mr. Yu said, "Go tell your father, from now on, there's no need to do so; every day, there's no need for you to personally serve the food." By the time of Qingming Festival, Mr. Yu told me, "I am having difficulties at home and urgently need half of the money." I responded humbly, "Yes, yes." "In three days, I will send a carriage to pick you up, do not delay." I responded humbly again, "Yes, yes."

At Yu Zhi's home, I saw the house completely renewed, looking very much like it had been sold and rebuilt by someone else. I asked the family members, and they told me that after the children went to school, a neighbor had taken care of the repairs, and the work was completed not long ago. Upon inspecting the house thoroughly, I found that even if it had cost over a thousand gold coins, it might not have been as sturdy. Moreover, I heard that someone was providing daily supplies, and there was nothing lacking in the enjoyment. I sighed and said, "The master is treated with such loyalty and respect, it is almost beyond reproach, truly deeply felt!" As the exam period was approaching, I arranged for a feast to be set up, and Yu Zhi sent a brief message inviting nearby scholars and long-time acquaintances who received government stipends. When they arrived, Yu Zhi said, "My students hope to succeed in their studies, so I ask you all to support them." Since these scholars were either his relatives or close friends, none of them would have any objections. Thus, three of his sons passed the examination in the same year; within ten years, two of them passed the imperial examinations, and one was recommended for a higher examination, all due to Yu Zhi's gentle guidance.

虚白道人曰：谚云：“天下无难事，最怕心不专。”诚哉是言也！以犯款之家，转而为绅士宦门，未有不以为难者矣。而某竟以挥金如土得之，可知贱者亦不可自贱也。 The Taoist priest Xubai said: A proverb goes, "There is no difficult task in the world, but the greatest fear is lack of focus." Indeed, this saying is true! For a family that has been punished or disgraced to transform into a family of officials and gentry, it is something that no one would consider easy. Yet, someone achieved this by spending money extravagantly, showing that those of low status should not undervalue themselves. 如规如讽。 汪雪马风 某伟延师训子，行时时之方便；于公烦友左袒，作种种之阴功。师弟显贵，岂非从阴骘中得来哉！ 盖防如 厚德食报亦宜。 黄琴轩 待先生如此其忠且敬，其子之会殿也，宜哉！予尝见世之厚待先生者，其子弟每多发达；待先生刻薄者，其子弟往往不肖。是编可为请先生者作一箴规。 侯百里 敬师如此，宜得美报。且古人有不循资格之说，此事可以恕论。 马竹吾 Someone great invited a teacher to educate his son, always acting with convenience; in public affairs, he supported his friends, performing various good deeds. The teacher and his students became prominent and wealthy, which could not have come from unseen benefits, could it? Gu Fangru Those with great virtue should also be rewarded. Huang Qinxuan Treating the teacher with such loyalty and respect, it is fitting that his son passed the imperial examinations and achieved high office! I have often seen that those who treat their teachers well often have descendants who achieve great success; those who treat their teachers harshly often have descendants who are unworthy. This compilation can serve as a guideline for those who invite teachers. Hou Baili Treating teachers with such respect, one should receive a good reward. Moreover, there is an ancient saying that one should not be bound by formal qualifications, and this matter can be excused. Ma Zhugu

Mr. Yu's various prideful traits, I, a certain Wei, revere him as if he were a deity. Even upright gentlemen find it difficult, let alone petty people? Yu Yunchuan How much prestige they have added to their plain robes and headbands. Ran Xinghang Petty people have never failed to wish to associate with the virtuous. If officials like Wu Cixi and Chen Dingsheng would be somewhat more lenient, the southern crossing of Yuanhai would not have led to the persecution of the partisans! This shows that the high gate of Yuanli is less beneficial than the broad path of Taixiu. Shangyuan Li Yu respectfully annotated Madam Yu In a certain village in the eastern outskirts of the county, there was an old woman named Yu who returned from her daughter's home, carrying several sheng of grain in a basket, inside which were eight hundred wen of capital money. The weather was hot, and the items were heavy; sweat dripped from her forehead like pearls. Behind her, a young woman caught up with her. Yu looked and saw that it was the wife of Wang from the neighboring village, so she asked her to help carry it. Wang agreed. Wang walked quickly, while Madam Yu walked slowly. Wang said, "Can I wait for you in the village ahead?" Madam Yu said, "Yes." Wang then went ahead. When Madam Yu arrived later, the money inside the grain had disappeared. Madam Yu asked her, and Wang replied that she hadn't seen it. These coins were gathered bit by bit by Madam Yu's daughter, secretly from her husband, to support her mother. Although the amount was not large, Madam Yu regarded these coins as worth one hundred wen for one wen and a thousand wen for one hundred wen. Suddenly losing them, what a great sorrow! She then cursed instead of crying, almost ready to use force. Just then, County Magistrate Ye Yuns, coming from the east, heard the argument between the old woman and the young woman and called them over to inquire. Madam Yu cried and recounted the truth, truly deserving pity. Looking at Wang, she was an elegant and beautiful woman of the countryside; her summer clothes were made of coarse linen, and she had a few coins tied around her waist, barely visible.

The officials wanted to have male servants search, but feared causing shame. Nearby was an ancient temple, so they proceeded to interrogate this matter in the temple. They ordered the servant to call the local people, and instructed him to buy four liang of wine, using it as a standard to weigh it. The weight was insufficient. Then they summoned the wine seller, and said, "Did the locals pay you the money for the wine?" The seller replied, "I was paid in full." "The money was given in full, why is the weight insufficient? We wanted to impose a heavier punishment, but pitied you for your rural ignorance. We will impose a fine of eight hundred coins to be paid in the capital, and do not allow shortchanging. Please fetch it quickly!" When it was fetched, they handed the money directly to the old woman, saying, "Your money might have been forgotten by your daughter; do not ask the king for it." They then sent them away. They asked the wine seller, "How long have you been in business?" He replied, "Five years." "Do you have any external debts?" "I have external debts of over two hundred thousand." The county magistrate said, "Recovering it would not be without some benefit." They then reviewed the accounts and collected the debt on behalf of the old woman. They instructed the servant, "In rural households, there is often no ready cash. Those who have money should pay in full, and those who do not should pay in grain. If there is any deliberate violation, they will be severely punished." Soon, all the debts were settled. They said to the wine seller, "By paying the fine, we know you are wronged. Do you still harbor resentment?" The man called out to heaven and left.

**Simple and Clean Writing** Wang Xue Ma Feng Leaf Gong, with a compassionate heart, practiced benevolent governance, and his writings were also passed down along with his deeds. This can be called going beyond the law. Judge Qian Mu once resolved a difficult case, and Su Shi said, "He is truly a 'Thunderbolt Hand.'" Judge Qian replied, "How could I be a 'Thunderbolt Hand,' I am just free from the label of a mediocre official." Mr. Yunshi resolved this case and is truly a 'Thunderbolt Hand,' not to be labeled as a mediocre official. Li Yi Li Yigong from the new city was a farmer. Although his family was not wealthy, they did not lack food and clothing. When he reached adulthood at the age of 20, he had no children, so he adopted a child from a different family as his own son and named him Yi. At the age of 14, Yi was sent to a private school to study. After two years, Yi said, "I am naturally dull and slow-witted, unable to read, and wish to engage in business." Li Yigong, thinking Yi was too young, did not agree. Yi said, "First, use ten thousand coins as capital, and if there is no profit, stop." Li Yigong agreed and gave him twenty thousand coins as capital. Yi went to the market, observing the fluctuating prices, buying in bulk when prices were low and selling when prices were high. Over the course of twenty years, his family's wealth grew several times over.

In the beginning, five years after Li De Yi became a father, he gave birth to a son whom he named Li. As Li grew older, he began to look down upon Yi and constantly excluded him from eating together. Yi would trade and buy food, but Li refused to eat it. Li advised Yi not to buy the food, but Yi did not listen, and Li secretly ate it. After Li's marriage, Li's wife treated Yi's wife like a servant, but Yi's wife showed no sign of anger. Li often comforted her, and Yi and his wife said together, "Our younger brother and his wife are young, so it is reasonable for them to do more work. Even if they do not have a home to manage, do not have morning duties, the father should not interfere." Li heard this and was very pleased. Suddenly, Li wanted to live separately from Yi. Li tested Yi by saying, "All the family wealth was earned by your elder brother, it should be divided equally." Li became angry and said, "He is not my elder brother, how can he share equally with me? Divide the family wealth a bit, I won't object, I have already treated him very well!" Li did not speak. From then on, Li stopped buying property, and Yi advised him, but he did not listen. Yi also did not know what his father's deeper intentions were. Li often spoke of living separately from Yi, and Li hesitated for six or seven years. Li gradually came to view Yi with hostility, and it was difficult for them to live together. Li had no choice but to tell Yi, "Your younger brother wants to live separately from you." Yi said, "If my younger brother wants to, then it is fine." Li wanted to say something but stopped three times. Yi sensed his father's intention and found it hard to speak, saying, "If we are to separate, I will not take any part of the property. I have been accustomed to doing business since childhood, I will not starve to death."

But I beg my younger brother to give me a place to stay, so that my wife and children do not have to sleep in the open. I would already be overwhelmed with gratitude!" Speaking these words, he could not help but feel a pang of sorrow. Li said, "There is no need to be sad; I have my own way of dealing with it." Turning to Li, he said, "Give your elder brother a house and some property; it is still not even one-tenth of what I could give. Do you wish for it?" Li, though hesitant, agreed reluctantly. Li then said, "Since the family property is not divided equally, all matters concerning my upbringing and burial, are they entirely unrelated to your elder brother?" Li replied, "He is not my elder brother, why should I use him?" Li then had them live separately. After several days, Li saw Yi living in peace, so he specially visited Yi's place. Yi did his best to serve, and they were as happy as usual.

Li said, "Li, my younger brother, your misfortunes are all due to my excessive indulgence. Do you have any resentment?" Yi replied, "Marrying and having children, the gratitude is as vast as the sky. How could I dare to harbor resentment?" Li said, "You have been at home for days without capital to conduct business?" Yi said, "I still have many friends who can lend me money to conduct business." Li said, "Even so, you still need capital. I have not been fortunate with my property for many years, and I have accumulated a certain amount of silver, which can rival one-third of your younger brother's estate. It is buried here for safekeeping. Wait until night when it is quiet and no one is around to retrieve and use it." He then pointed out the location. After drinking, Li wept and said, "Li is unworthy, gradually indulging in drinking and gambling. After my death, he will certainly not be able to maintain the family's fortunes. Think of my nurturing kindness, do not let him be reduced to the state of a beggar. Even in death, I can rest in peace!" Yi said resolutely, "Even if the father had not given this instruction, I would not be able to treat my younger brother like a stranger." Thus, Li agreed to visit Yi's house every five or six days, and later, he would go to Li's place once every five or six days.

Not long after, Li died of illness. Li saw Yi's business thriving and asked Ping to share the funeral expenses. Yi agreed. After the funeral, Li had no means of support and had debts from gambling. He then pretended to sell land to Yi, received payment but did not give a written contract, promising to return the money with interest later. Yi did not argue about it, saying, "I know this and did it intentionally; I dared not treat my younger brother without trust." Li was idle and did nothing, and also engaged in excessive drinking and gambling. He again wanted to sell land to Yi. Yi said, "It cannot be done without someone." That person was someone Li feared, but was actually someone Yi was close to. Not ten years later, except for the house, Li's property was completely gone, and most of it belonged to Yi. One day, Yi's wife said to Yi, "Li's family struggles to have one meal a day; can you show some compassion?" Yi agreed, and it became a regular practice.

At the time, Li arrived at Yi's household to manage the household affairs, which he did in an orderly manner, and Yi was very pleased. Yi wanted to hire an old woman for cooking, and Li said, "Your wife would be suitable for the job." Thus, Li and his wife took over Yi's household chores as if they were servants. After several years, during a rainy season, the brothers drank wine and talked about their inner feelings. Yi asked, "If our property were as it was before, would Brother still not be able to stay in the countryside?" Li replied, "That was then, and this is now. Now that Brother has one-tenth of the property, he can sustain himself." Yi said, "If that is the case, all the property of Brother that was sold to me is still here. Now that the harvest is imminent, you and your wife can return immediately to prepare for it. Agricultural tools and livestock can be temporarily taken from here, and gradually acquired over time. Moreover, I have had this thought for a long time. I have the intention but not the action because I feared that Brother's character was not yet stable. Now that he is stable, there is no need for doubt or discussion." Li resumed his old business, lived within his means, and once again became a happy family.
