Now that Yi and Li have both passed away, their nephews and nieces are as close as brothers and sisters.

The White Void Hermit said: How extraordinary! This is righteousness. To show great care for a stepchild, raised by a father, is remarkable; to transfer the entire property acquired from a stepbrother, whom one had previously treated harshly, is even more remarkable. Yet, is this excessive? Only benevolent and virtuous men can do such things. Reading this makes one unable to stop sighing. Wang Xue Ma Feng Mr. Li can be said to have a clear distinction between righteousness and profit. Huang Qin Xuan The "History of the Five Dynasties" has a "Biography of the Righteous Son," where righteousness is not always truly righteous, and many bear the name of righteousness. To fully embody the weight of the character "义" in the case of Li, and to write out the heart and mind of righteousness—there cannot be such an event without this text. Ma Zhu Wu

Righteousness is righteousness, profit is profit, each following its own path. Ultimately, the profit from pursuing profit becomes a form of righteousness, which is due to valuing righteousness as profit rather than profit as profit. The distinction between righteousness and profit is clear, as it is. Qin Zishan Maintaining the norms of society and leading the moral teachings, writings that benefit the world and its ways can be read like the sayings of Song dynasty Confucian scholars. Master Ma of Zhugu evaluates this especially aptly. Li Yu from Shangyuan respectfully annotates Raising a son named Sheng, the righteous elder brother cares for his younger brother, how profound is the satire of the world. Fisherman and woodcutter record Ying You, courtesy name Fu You, is from Quanzhou, Fujian province, a young man selected as a juren (a scholar-official), known far and wide. Living in poverty, he makes a living by teaching. Teaching and seeking a position, if the plan is good, joy shows on the face; if the plan fails, anxiety arises in the heart. Getting a position is like crops encountering rain, losing a position is like autumn grass encountering frost, there is nothing in the world more difficult than this. Yet he has an upright and straightforward nature, not willing to bother others to help find work, therefore, by the end of the year, he still has no position, the house is empty except for the walls standing, the pot is cold and the bag is empty, extremely difficult and hard. One day, sleepless at night, occasionally thinking about having no rice for breakfast, suddenly hearing the rooster crow, hating that it crows too early. His wife, Zong, said, "The rooster has crowed, and the stars are bright, you can get up." He said, "The poetic rhythm on the desk cannot be exchanged for breakfast, why get up early?" Zong said, "The neighbor owes two hundred coins for sewing work, you can take it to relieve the urgent need." He then took it to buy rice.

As the rice was almost cooked, just then the house collapsed, filling the pot with dust, and the pot was also broken by bricks and stones. One laughed heartily and said, "How poor is my fate!" Suddenly, there was a loud knocking at the door. He ran to see it, and it was his cousin Zhao Desheng, leading a large horse. Zhao hurriedly told him, "I am busy, no time to sit and talk. Have you decided on the school?" He replied, "Not yet." Zhao said, "There is a good school, the tuition is five thousand coins. I will come to pick you up next spring, bringing a gift of five taels." He handed over the letter and rode away on the horse. He took the silver and the letter and entered, with a face full of joy. His brother asked, "What good news is there?" He replied, "Heaven does not close all doors." He then told his wife about it. His wife said, "My cousin Zhao has been dead for several years. How could he recommend such a good opportunity?" He was startled and realized, "My uncle's son, how could he lie? Although he is dead, his words are trustworthy. Besides, there is a gift here. There is no worry about lacking funds for the year." By the beginning of spring, friends heard about it and all said that the words of the dead are not to be trusted, but he alone believed it.

On the full moon night, nothing had happened, and even if something had, I would have doubted it. Suddenly, past noon, a carriage came to fetch me, and I only arrived at dusk. I saw an old man with white hair, dressed in fine clothes, waiting at the door. I bowed and entered. Just as I sat down, someone asked, "What is your courtesy name, sir?" I replied, "I wrote a letter yesterday, where my courtesy name is Chen Qingxu. Later, I used my courtesy name as my given name, and a friend gave me another courtesy name, Ban Shi." They then asked, "How old are you, sir?" I replied, "I am ninety-seven years old. This year, I made my disciples adopt my two grandsons." So, I had them come out to perform the ritual of respect. One is fourteen, the other is nine years old. Soon after, a lavish feast was set up, and the serving of wine and dishes was done by beautiful maids, all of whom I had never seen before. The banquet ended by the second drumbeat, and the bedding was new. After going to bed, I pondered what the host had done. I could not understand what kind of family they were.

Afterward, Ying often saw the maid moving back and forth with a young lady of sixteen through the gate of the study. Peeping through the window, he found her more beautiful and charming than the other maids. As the Qingming Festival approached, Zhao suddenly arrived, and Ying distanced himself. Zhao said, "I serve as the registrar under the five King Yamas, and public affairs are numerous; I dare not consider personal feelings." Ying replied, "Since you are the registrar of the underworld, you should know people's lifespans as clearly as if they were in your hand." Zhao said, "I never make mistakes." Ying asked, "How are my family members?" Zhao replied, "They are all safe, except my current wife is ill and will not recover; I should quickly return home to take care of her. I will first inform your master." After saying this, he entered. Soon after, the servant prepared the carriage and set off. Upon arriving home, Zongshi was indeed well. Not long after, she fell gravely ill and died within five days. The funeral arrangements were just completed when the master sent someone to fetch Ying. Ying then entrusted the household items to his neighbors to guard and left by carriage.

In the temple, whenever he thought about the minor matter of his wife's death and the major issue of having no heir, he couldn't help but shed tears. Reflecting on such hardships, being poor and having no one to turn to, he only had his brother Zhao, a close relative, who was deceased. Given the separation between the living and the dead, he had no choice but to burn incense and silently pray at night when no one was around, hoping that Zhao might appear. After three nights, Zhao suddenly appeared and said, "My younger brother has been looking forward to this for days. My older brother was busy with public affairs and couldn't come to meet your thoughts, and even when reflecting on my own heart, I couldn't feel at ease. Your concerns are always on my mind. Do you have any long-standing wish regarding remarriage?" He replied, "I am as poor as washed out, and even if I have a wish, it is difficult to fulfill." Zhao said, "Try to speak, regardless of wealth or poverty." He still found it hard to speak and remained silent. Zhao then asked, "Have you seen the young woman from the east side of the town?" He replied, "I have seen her repeatedly."

"Brother, may I act as a matchmaker for you?" he asked. "To have such a woman as my wife, I wish I had a golden house to keep her in," the younger brother replied. "If the match fails, it might bring shame; I hope you will take this seriously," he added. Slightly annoyed, the elder brother said, "To marry such an extraordinary woman would be the greatest honor. How could the match fail? Besides, since I am acting as a matchmaker, I am sure you will not dare to refuse." Saying this, he left in a huff. A short while later, he returned and said, "It's agreed! Your home is empty, so you can come to his home now. When the teaching period ends, you can bring your family back together." He then asked the younger brother to choose a good date. The younger brother lowered his head and remained silent. "Is there anything else that makes you unhappy?" the elder brother asked. "The matter itself is agreeable, but the rituals and expenses for a wedding are considerable, and I fear we may not have the resources at the moment," the younger brother replied. "Don't worry about that. I will take care of all the rituals and expenses. Not a single coin is necessary," the elder brother said. "In that case, please let me choose a good date."

Zhao said, "The beginning of the month marks the summer, with virtue concentrated on the day of Jia. On the fifth day of the month, which falls on Jia Wu, Wu represents the brightness of the moon, and the stars are aligned in a favorable configuration known as Lihe, making it both auspicious and harmonious. This day is the most auspicious for marriage. By the appointed time, I will arrive shortly before dusk, without disrupting your affairs." On the day, Zhao indeed arrived, taking out 200 taels of silver from his sleeve as a gift. It was already time for the lamps to be lit, and Zhao pointed and said, "Here you can light the lamp." The lamp was lit; "Here you can decorate with colorful ribbons." The decorations were put up. All necessary items were readily available. Within a short time, the interior and exterior spaces were transformed and looked brand new. The formal attire and rituals were carried out, and after sharing a wine cup, the bridegroom went out to thank Zhao. Zhao said, "Your wedding night is tonight, and I am also busy with my own affairs." Then he took his leave and departed.

Ying returned to the bridal chamber and saw the bride, dressed in red and seated in the bridal canopy, with a group of maids standing on either side. Unconsciously, he spoke out, "What virtue have I practiced to attain this?" The bride replied, "The fate of a true man can change dramatically; from a farmer in the morning to a minister of the emperor in the evening. Even eating with a full banquet before you and having hundreds of concubines to serve you is within the realm of possibility, let alone lesser achievements." Upon hearing this, Ying was deeply impressed and said, "I was wrong! I was wrong!" Afterward, the maids all dispersed, and Ying told the bride, "To be honored by marriage, yet unaware of your family lineage, this is a regrettable matter." The bride said, "I mention it, fearing that you might be startled." Ying replied, "Even if the entire family were ghosts or fox spirits, there is no need to hide the truth." The bride said, "As you say, the entire family are fox spirits, but I am not." Ying asked why she was different. The bride explained, "You and I are from the same town, and the former Censor, who was my biological father, is your fellow townsman." Ying said, "I wish to hear more details." The bride responded, "There are many details to share, but the story is long. Perhaps I can tell it in greater detail another day."

Ying taught at Chen's house for five years, during which his wife gave birth to twins in one pregnancy. One day, Chen prepared wine for a toast and toasted together with Ying and his wife. Chen said, "My ancestors lived in Hubei, and there are still two sons and a grandson at home. Because of the daughter, I have stayed here for five years, and now I will return home. Moreover, your son-in-law's bad luck has passed, and good fortune is to follow. The carriage has been hired, and we can leave early tomorrow morning. I have prepared 1,500 taels of white gold as a gift, and 500 taels are given to the daughter. The 5 years' salary and expenses were limited, and additionally, some silver is here. Take it back, and you won't regret waking up early." Ying and his wife were very sad to hear it. Chen said, "Don't act like children! It is already late afternoon, you two should quickly pack your belongings, and don't be in a rush at the last minute." Ying then packed his valuables and other items that could be carried, and just as he finished, the carriage had arrived at the door. On the way out, Chen gave the daughter a five-colored cloth bundle, as long and wide as a quilt, and said, "Wear it, and you can travel thousands of miles in an instant." He then taught her a spell, saying, "Do not use it lightly, do not tell others." After that, they watched as she went up the carriage and parted with reluctance.

In that year, 应 took the provincial examination. Mr. Zeng, a former official, was at home in mourning due to the death of his father. 应's wife, Chen, wished to go and recognize her father, but 应 stopped her. Chen said, "Is there anyone in the world without parents? Why are you so unfeeling in stopping me?" 应 replied, "How can you know that Mr. Zeng is your father?" Chen said, "My biological father, after passing the imperial examination, had a relationship with a nun from a nearby village and had me. He abandoned me by the roadside, and my adoptive father raised me in the lake." 应 asked, "Do you have any proof?" Chen replied, "If I had no proof, how could I dare to claim kinship?" 应 agreed. Chen then went directly to Mr. Zeng's residence and asked to see his wife. The wife asked her purpose, and Chen said, "I have a poem that I don't understand, and I especially ask for your guidance." She then presented the poem to the wife. The wife read it, and it was a white silk handkerchief with the following lines: "Early on, I knew that birth was a burden, so how could I have been born? It was hard to part with me when I was brought back, but letting me go was even more filled with emotion. My dream was in vain, like a snake's omen, but my heart hoped for a bird's covering to come true. In the future, if we meet again, this will serve as clear proof." The poem was signed with Mr. Zeng's name, and the handwriting was unmistakable. The wife recognized the handwriting and the content, and she confirmed that Mr. Zeng was indeed Chen's father. The story ended with the family reunited, and the truth of Chen's lineage was finally established.

After repeatedly pondering and analyzing, it seemed to be the case of a girl being abandoned, but the exact reason remained unknown. He then had a servant girl present the poem to him. Afterwards, the official came with the poem and said, "Where did you get this poem?" Chen replied, "When my little daughter was born, she had it in her arms." The official asked, "Do you still remember your birthdate?" Chen said, "My little daughter is now twenty-three years old. My adoptive father said we took her in on the morning of the seventh day of the seventh month of that year." The official exclaimed, "She is truly my daughter!" He then told his wife, "This is the girl who was abandoned on the roadside." His wife said, "Our daughter has a red mark on her elbow like a coin." Upon seeing it, it was indeed true. It turned out that the nun had returned to secular life and married into the Zeng family, making her Chen's birth mother. The Zeng couple was overjoyed, as if their daughter had been reborn. They changed Chen's surname to Zeng and then asked about the details of the adoption. The Zeng family hesitated to mention that Chen Gong was a fox, but they described the rest in detail. The Zeng couple was overjoyed and told the girl, "Come tomorrow with your husband, as if it were the third morning of a thanksgiving ritual, and do not be careless!" Chen bid them farewell. The next day, the couple came dressed in formal attire, and they performed the ritual of a father-in-law and son-in-law. They stayed for two nights and then sent them back. The gifts were loaded onto several subsequent carriages, and Chen Gong became extremely wealthy as a result.

One day, Zeng's daughter returned home to visit her parents. She noticed her father's worry was evident on his face and asked her mother about it. The mother said, "Your father made a mistake when he was in the capital, and recently he received news from his colleagues that a certain official, who is an enemy, will submit multiple reports to accuse him, which is why he is worried." Zeng's daughter asked, "Is there truly no way to handle this?" The mother replied, "A certain high-ranking official is your father's former teacher and student. If we can convey a message to him, the matter might be stopped. However, it must be done within one or two days; otherwise, it will be too late. Six thousand miles away, how can a letter arrive within one or two days?" Zeng's daughter said, "This is easy. I have learned a secret technique from an immortal and can ride a five-colored auspicious cloud to deliver messages to the capital, ensuring I return before sunset." The mother was extremely delighted and shared Zeng's daughter's proposal with her husband. The husband, though half-believing and half-doubting, decided to try it out for now due to the urgency of the situation and ordered the daughter to prepare her luggage. As soon as the letter was finished, Zeng's daughter also completed her preparations and left immediately.

Then, Zeng gave the letter to his daughter. Seeing her lay a five-colored cloth on the ground and leap upon it, she suddenly transformed into five-colored auspicious clouds, rose lightly into the air, and vanished instantly. Zeng's daughter directly went to the inner residence of a certain Grand Secretary, descending from the air, with the maids and female servants all suspecting her to be a fairy. She said, "I am not a fairy. Please allow me to see the old lady; I have an urgent matter to report." They led her to see the Grand Secretary and his wife, who were sitting together. Zeng's daughter introduced herself as the daughter of Vice-Chancellor Zeng, who had sent her to deliver a letter. The Grand Secretary, seeing that the letter was sealed at the hour of the snake and had arrived by the hour of the horse, was greatly surprised and said, "Your father's letter has not mentioned one matter clearly, making it difficult to handle. Moreover, I have another matter to consult you on. I respectfully respond with a formal letter and await your reply, can I do so?" The girl replied, "Yes." Thus, she took the letter to the south, received her father's letter to the north, and then carried another letter to the south, as the slanting sun was still in the west. Upon receiving the letter, Zeng opened it and read that all matters had been properly handled, transforming his worry into joy. Therefore, Zeng loved his daughter even more. Later, Zeng rose to the position of Grand Secretary, responded to the palace assembly, and was promoted to the position of Military Commissioner, primarily due to Zeng's efforts.

虚白道人曰：Observing Mr. Ying's character as straightforward and upright, yet his family is impoverished, to the point where the house has nothing but walls standing, the steamer is cold and the bag is empty, one almost doubts that there would ever be a time of success or advancement in his life. But if he were to remain poor and destitute for life, people would use Mr. Ying as a pretext, thinking that his straightforwardness is like this, it is appropriate for him to be abandoned by people, and there would be no hope of advancement. However, Mr. Ying made a living by copying books, he did not trouble others to make plans for him; because marriage was not a suitable match, and he advised matchmakers to take themselves seriously. Like the steadfastness of an arrow, his lifelong aspirations were fulfilled on his own, uprightness does not hinder one's fate! What kind of circumstances did Mr. Ying encounter? — Huang Qinxuan

Within the book, there is a beauty like jade; I have heard this saying, and now I see the person. This is a metaphor for the inherent value and beauty found in literature and learning. In the strange and marvelous transformations, one sees the work of nature; in the painstaking efforts, one sees the writer's heart. — Ma Zhugu Fu Changyu of the Jin Dynasty once said: "The killing power of wine and women is greater than that of being straightforward." To die for wine and women, people do not regret it, but they fear that being straightforward will bring disaster; this is because the heart is not upright, so they consider compromise as wisdom. Reading this passage, one knows that upright people are indeed blessed by the gods. Yet, although upright like the master, he was not blessed by the gods. Why is that? — Shangyuan Li Yu respectfully annotates Song Hui娘 In the year 1782 during the reign of the Qianlong Emperor, a certain governor-general, upon imperial command, ascended Mount Tai to worship the Lady of the Azure Clouds. Upon arriving in Taian, he chose a date for the sacrifice. The county magistrate immediately issued an announcement, stating that people from far and near who come to offer incense are not allowed to ascend the mountain on this day. Moreover, he had the temple buildings cleaned, ensuring they were spotless. On the appointed day, the county magistrate went first, then set up elaborate decorations in the main hall, and finally invited the governor-general to light incense. As soon as he entered the main hall, there was a pile of paper offerings right where one would bow. The governor-general said, "How could this not be removed!" The county magistrate was greatly startled, and then saw a single sheet of paper on the altar, which read that a young girl named Song Hui娘 from a certain village in Shanghe had made a remote offering. The governor-general took the paper and said to the county magistrate, "Since it is a remote offering, the person who burned the paper offerings did not come here, and there must be divine protection." In a moment, the paper and ashes disappeared, and the governor-general was greatly astonished. After the worship ceremony, he returned to the province and issued an order to investigate the matter in Shanghe County.

There was a man named Song Menglin, who lived generation after generation in Shanghe. His family was not wealthy, and he made teaching children his profession. His wife suddenly fell ill with a strange disease, and the doctors and shamans were unable to cure it, only watching as she died. His daughter, Huinang, was fourteen years old and prayed every night by kneeling in the courtyard, hoping that if her mother recovered, she would personally climb Mount Tai and offer incense to thank the gods. She prayed for half a month, and her mother's illness gradually improved, fully recovering in a month. Later, the daughter wanted to fulfill her promise, but the family was poor, with no money to spend, and there was no older brother to accompany her. She had an uncle from the father's side who went to Taian every year to burn incense. She asked him, "How many miles is it from here to the summit of Mount Tai?" Her uncle replied, "From here to the provincial capital is 240 miles, from the capital to Taian is 180 miles, and from Taian to the summit is 40 miles, totaling 460 miles." The daughter then asked, "How many steps are there in a mile? How many steps are there in 460 miles?" Her uncle answered, "There are 360 steps in a mile, totaling 165,600 steps."

The girl remembered this advice and took the opportunity to tell her father, saying, "The wish to climb Mount Tai is difficult to fulfill on my own. My uncle said it is X miles to Mount Tai, totaling Y steps. I wish to walk back and forth in the courtyard to reach the number of steps. Once I have walked the full number of steps, it will be as if I have climbed Mount Tai, burned incense, and worshipped, thus fulfilling my wish. Is this possible?" Her father praised her sincerity and creativity and agreed to her plan. The girl then began walking in the courtyard every day, except for her morning and noon meals. However, her steps were slow, and she could only walk about ten thousand steps in a day. She reported her daily step count to her father, who kept a record for her. After six or seven days, her strength was not enough, and the number of steps she could take decreased. On the sixteenth day, her father told her, "If you walk another five thousand steps, you will have reached the total." Hearing this, the girl was very happy and got up early the next morning to walk quickly. She was so eager that she did not even have time for breakfast. By late afternoon, she had completed the required number of steps. Her father then used a red slip of paper to write her hometown, name, and offerings, which he burned together. After the burning, a gentle breeze blew, and the ashes disappeared completely. This was the story of the filial daughter performing a distant sacrifice, which coincided with the day and time when the governor climbed Mount Tai and offered incense.

The magistrate of Shanghe, after investigating and presenting a detailed report, found that the date and time matched, knowing that it was moved by filial piety. He could not help but be delighted and admired, and thus gave 200 taels of silver as a dowry to Hui Niang, and also ordered the magistrate of Shanghe to provide substantial relief. The magistrate also gave an additional 100 taels as a gift. 虚白道人 said: Hui Niang's actions were not extraordinary, but they were extraordinary when performed by a young daughter. Following the command of her parents was not extraordinary, but acting on her own thoughts was extraordinary. Her ideas were beyond the ordinary, and divine silent protection must be seen as exceptional. The matter seemed non-existent, but the principle was actually real. The *Book of Documents* says: "Greatest sincerity moves the gods." By observing this, one believes even more. 张子澄 It is said: "What time does not produce, those who concentrate their thoughts can obtain it; what the earth does not nurture, those with strong will can produce it; what the body cannot reach, those who think sincerely can achieve it." Hui Niang can be said to attract the divine. 盖防如 The *Book of Documents* says: "Greatest sincerity moves the gods." It is evident that sincerity cannot fail to be effective. Especially for a young daughter to have such filial thoughts, her heart truly stems from greatest sincerity, and her being divinely protected is a natural course of events, nothing to be surprised at. 侯百里 Heaven reveals its traces, skillfully opening a window to the world. 马竹吾

A Woman of Sorrow and a Boy of Eight, Together in Legend. An Annotation by Li Yu of Shangyuan Con Gu Daoquan Gu Daoquan was from Lingqiu, Shanxi. He studied Confucianism and took the examination for young scholars, possessing extraordinary talent and a love for reading. At seventeen, his parents wished to arrange his marriage, but he did not desire it, saying, "It is not late to enter the school after the age of 15." His parents were pleased with his ambition and allowed him to continue his studies. He ranked among the top in the county and prefectural examinations but was not admitted in the academy examination, and this pattern continued in the regular and supplementary exams. By the time he had surpassed the age of 20, his parents forced him to complete his marriage, though he was reluctant and dared not speak against it. When the time for the wedding ceremony arrived, Gu left his home in Lingqiu and went to Yizhou in Zhili Province. In Yizhou, there was a wealthy family named Huang Cheng, with whom Gu formed a friendship at first sight and thus went to stay with them. Huang recognized his academic abilities and hired him to tutor his children. That year, during the provincial examination in Yizhou, Huang's son, who was selected, recorded Gu's revised essay, which was chosen as the top entry. Gu wanted to leave and go elsewhere, but Huang refused. Gu said, "A teacher who is younger than his student, ordinary people would find this very unseemly." Huang replied, "Scholarship is scholarship, and fame and fortune are fame and fortune; how can one judge scholarship by fame and fortune?" He insisted on keeping Gu, who then stayed. Huang said, "I have a business in Beijing with many rooms. I wish to send you and your students there to study. Would you consider this?" Gu replied, "I am just about to visit the capital." Huang sent Gu and his students to Beijing and helped him enroll in the Imperial Academy, saying, "Your writing is advantageous for small examinations; it will certainly be advantageous for the major examinations."

Respectfully, I have been admitted as a student, and this year I passed the provincial examination together with your disciple. To consider teacher and student as fellow examinees, isn't this a great event for the gentry?" Gu replied, "If it is as you say, then I will live up to your generous feelings." Huang heard that the items needed for the examination would be prepared and provided. As the examination period approached, Huang instructed someone to bring all the examination supplies. Gu smiled and said, "Such an amount of supplies would require a large cart to carry them." Huang asked, "Is it too much?" Gu replied, "One-tenth would be sufficient." When they entered the examination hall, Gu and Huang had the same examination number. Gu said, "What a coincidence!" After the questions were announced, all six arts and two poems were written by Gu alone. By the second and third sessions, they still had the same examination number, and Gu knew there must be a reason, not just a matter of chance. The papers for the second and third sessions were all written by Gu. After the third session was over, Gu told Huang, "The paper I recorded must have passed. However, if you look closely, it is not as detailed and thorough as mine, and it should be listed after my name." Huang said, "If I pass, I will be very fortunate, and it's the same whether it's before or after." Gu said, "It's just about the quality of the paper." When the results were announced, Huang passed the imperial examination with high distinction, while Gu failed. Huang was happy inside but sad on the outside, and tried very hard to console Gu. Gu said, "To use your good fortune to give my writing a boost, it's enough to fulfill my wish. My failure is my fate, and it has nothing to do with the paper." He then left the teaching position, and Huang tried very hard to keep him. Gu said, "From now on, I will never read or write again, and it would be pointless to keep me." Huang then sent him off by truck.

After leaving the capital for half a day, Cong suddenly felt hungry and thirsty and wanted to stop at a roadside inn to buy food and drink. Upon arriving at the inn and just sitting down, a carriage arrived later. A young man sat in it, with a handsome and elegant appearance. He also got out of the carriage and brushed off the dust. Then he pulled the curtain and helped a young lady, aged sixteen or seventeen, get out. Looking at her, her natural beauty was like that of a national treasure. Cong, usually mature and composed, was also captivated by her beauty. Then the young man spoke to Cong: "Where are you going, sir?" Cong replied that he was returning home. The young man said, "It seems familiar, but I can't remember." Cong replied, "Yes, your name is He Wanfu." The young man asked, "Where did you come from just now?" He pointed to the young girl and said, "This is my younger sister. She went to fetch the bridegroom's carriage for my maternal grandfather's wedding last night and is returning now." Cong asked, "What is her family background?" The young man replied, "Not yet." Then they bought wine and talked.

贺说：“考场上的事情，六艺都是你自己做的，却让别人高升，自己落榜，这难道不是命运吗？”顾说：“学生中也有优秀的人，可见这并不是因为文章不够优秀而无法被推荐。”贺说：“先生还想设馆教书吗？我可以帮助先生成就一番事业。”顾原本不想再重蹈覆辙，但为了女儿的缘故，可以借此机会接近她的芳容，于是说：“既然有美意，那就辛苦你了。我愿意租一间屋子，暂住等待。”贺说：“寒舍可以供您下榻。”顾非常高兴。贺说：“天色已晚，路途遥远，到了我的住所再谈吧。”于是算还了酒食的费用，各自登车，直到日暮才到达。顾想自己买饭吃，贺不同意，一定要一起用餐。一天设酒清谈，邀请邻居学生王某作陪，谈到文章，顾侃侃而谈，贺与王心悦诚服。贺想拜顾为师，顾不愿意，于是结为朋友。凡是顾修改的文章，贺都视为珍宝，说：“真是天下奇才！”一天，贺说：“好消息告诉您，明年恩科已经批准。”顾说：“我发誓不再读书，何况还要参加考试吗？”贺惊讶地问他原因，顾不说；再问，仍然不说，贺便不再追问。

At the beginning, when Gu followed He to the west, it was originally about the beauty of He Wanfu's younger sister. His true feelings had no way to be expressed, so he thought that Wang could act as a matchmaker. However, with their shallow acquaintance, he could not speak of deep matters. Therefore, he often bought delicacies and wine to drink with Wang. Once they became acquainted, he then revealed his feelings. Wang said, "Yes, I will temporarily act as an axe for you to cut the wood." Wang then took the opportunity to inform He. He sent his wife to invite his sister-in-law, saying, "There is a guest named Gu staying at our house. Could you, Wang, act as a matchmaker for my sister-in-law? Regarding Gu's talent and learning, achieving success would be like picking up mustard seeds. However, because he regretted that his fame and achievements came too late, he vowed not to take the exams and would not listen to advice." The sister-in-law remained silent. He then said, "If Gu listens to advice, he has never been unsuitable for marriage." He's sister-in-law replied, "There is no one who does not accept advice; it depends on whether the advice is good or not." Upon hearing this, He realized that she was willing and agreed to marry Gu. They married in He's house. On the wedding night, it was like fish finding water. Gu said, "The joy of the bridal chamber and the red candles far surpasses passing the imperial examinations." He placed his aspirations for fame and achievement outside his considerations. After three, nine, and six days, He's wife said, "Hearing that you do not pursue officialdom, why is that?" Gu replied, "My fate is thin."

The wife said, "A wife follows the status of her husband. Your fate is thin, and so is mine. Yet, if your writings are indeed recorded by Huang Xiu and succeed, while your repeated recordings and failures do not, I will beg with you for a lifetime, why should there be any regret? After just one attempt, you attribute it to fate and suddenly abandon previous efforts. Why do you look down on your own writing but undervalue your own fate?" He remained silent. The wife continued, "When Huang Xiu records your writing and it succeeds, it is not necessarily because your writing is destined to succeed. Perhaps it is due to his good fortune. Why? Because it has not been tested by others. When you yourself write and record it and it fails, it is not credible that your writing should succeed but does not. Perhaps there may still be flaws in the writing. Why? Because you believe it should succeed. After one attempt and failure, you should work harder to achieve success in the future. Instead, you have given up hope and almost abandoned yourself. The saying goes, 'A man should strive to be strong.' Why are you not striving so hard?" He turned red in the face and had no words to answer. The wife said again, "In this matter, you cannot promote your reputation to honor your parents, which means there is no father and son relationship. As a scholar, you cannot wear purple and red robes, which means there is no ruler and subject relationship. If you do not listen to sincere and correct advice, there is no friendship. If you cannot obtain a minor official position to honor your wife, there is no marital relationship."

The Five Bonds of human relationships are five, and the ruler has abandoned four of them. At this time, the ruler should feel ashamed and die, yet he still places himself in a high position! When we were getting married, my brother and I discussed with you, saying that you had no ambition for fame and glory, and you did not listen to our advice. At that time, I thought you had the talent to be a scholar at the Hanlin Academy, so I said, "No one is beyond persuasion." My brother heard what I said and thus gave me to you. Now you persist in your error without realizing it, content to be a mediocre and unambitious person. I truly have no eyes, unworthy to be the wife of a man of the world! Moreover, you say your fate is thin, belittling yourself; my fate is thin because you belittle your own fate. I do not only blame you; who else should I blame?" She finished speaking and wept uncontrollably. By night, I woke up from sleep and saw the lamp lit in the room. My wife had already hanged herself. I quickly got up and released her. Fortunately, she had not been hanged for long, and she soon revived. She said, "Why did you save me? I want to die, not to be the wife of an unfeeling and unresponsive man!" I went back to sleep. She again tied a rope in the dark, and I saw it, saying, "Are you determined to take your own life?" She replied, "Do I take my life as a game with you?" I said, "Do not die, my heart and aspirations have all been moved by you." She asked, "Is this true?" I said, "I will never deceive you!" She then changed from sadness to joy.

Gu said, "When I am engrossed in my studies, I set aside all other matters, and our marital affection may seem distant. Do not harbor deep resentment!" The woman replied, "Not only will I not resent you, but while you read, I will attend to you; if I fail to attend to you properly, I will take the blame." Gu then read in the bedroom, and both he and his wife began to sleep. When they woke up, they also rose. Meals were irregular, but always ready-made; drinking was frequent, and there was no need to wait for drinks. Gu said, "You are truly my virtuous helper!" After several months of reading, Gu suddenly pounded the table and said to himself, "Last year's provincial examination essay was certainly not one that must be selected. The failure lies in the essay, not in fate!" The woman asked, "How is my advice?" The exam period was not far off, so Gu and He went to the capital together. After entering the examination hall, Gu and He were in adjacent examination rooms, making it easy to pass notes. Gu wrote the first essay on behalf of He, and they both passed the exam and ranked first. The following year, they both passed the metropolitan examination, and Gu was selected to serve as a scholar in the Hanlin Academy. Gu returned home to pay respects and visit his family. Upon arriving home, he saw a young woman standing by his mother's side and asked about it. His mother said, "This is your legitimate wife, Zhou. After you went into hiding, your father said that the wedding date could not be changed, so he arranged for the wedding to proceed as planned, waiting for you to return home before completing the ceremony. Who would have thought it would be delayed for four or five years! We can proceed with the wedding tonight." Gu looked at her and found her appearance comparable to He's. Considering his parents' advanced age, Gu decided to stay home and care for them.

The Daoist Master Xubai said: How great is the danger of a person being overly self-assured! Just as Mr. Gu's efforts were arduous, yet his talent went unappreciated, it is indeed appropriate that he could not escape sorrow and lamentation. However, literature has no limits; one should strive to explore its profound mysteries. Success in career and reputation has its time; it cannot be guaranteed in the present moment. He attributed his success to others recording his writings and thus attributed his own failure to fate, simply believing that he was not destined for such success, leading him to abandon all previous efforts and content himself with a life in the mountains and forests—how regrettable! Fortunately, his virtuous wife, by sacrificing herself, persuaded him, and he subsequently achieved success. Otherwise, he would have remained just a momentary madman. How great is the danger of a person being overly self-assured!

**Translation:** Those who fail several examinations and thus burn and discard their writing tools, believing that success in exams is determined by fate rather than one's writing ability, will surely gain a clear understanding upon reading this. — Zhang Zicheng It can relieve restlessness and calm arrogance, serving as a golden rule for scholars. — Ma Zhuwu By reading the phrase "learning is learning," one can understand that those who pass the imperial exams are not necessarily superior to commoners; by observing the words of He Shizhi advising Gu Sheng, one can see that scholars are not necessarily superior to women. Arrogance and indignation are all useless. — Yang Zihou Xue Juzheng failed the imperial exams and wrote an essay called "Dispelling Sorrow" to console himself. The essay is full of free-spirited and unconventional ideas, and wise people believed he had the potential to become a high-ranking official. Reading this essay would certainly make the scholar Kang Le calm and peaceful. — Li Yu of Shangyuan, careful annotation Zhang Qing Zhang Qing, a farmer from the eastern outskirts of Dezhou, had a family that loved beef and had been involved in raising and slaughtering cattle for generations. Those who raise and slaughter cattle buy thin cattle and fatten them, selling them to Hui people when they are fat and then slaughtering and selling their meat. In raising them, they use the cow dung to fertilize the land, with all the profit lying in the land. In selling them, the profit from the fattened cattle is double the original price, even greater than that from lending money. The number of cattle they raise ranges from sixty to seventy to over a hundred, indeed making them very cunning and ruthless in taking advantage of the people.

One day, Qing bought a fat cow at the market. Its price was very cheap. There was a farmer who knew this cow was strong and well-trained, and he wanted to add two thousand to the original price to resell it. Qing did not want to, and the farmer again added two thousand, but Qing still did not want to. The farmer then stopped. Qing sold the cow to a Hui person in this gathering place, earning hundreds of coins more than the farmer. The Hui person thought the pieces of beef in this gathering place were few and the price high, so he immediately killed the cow and sold it. Qing carried the money home, feeling very pleased. Just as he arrived home, two officials came with a warrant to arrest him. Qing asked, "Who accused me?" The official replied sarcastically, "You don't even know what you did?" They then bound his neck and dragged him away. The two officials demanded extra fees on the way. Qing pleaded, "I don't have a single coin, what can I do?" The officials angrily retorted, "Do officials live by breathing air?" Qing promised, "If not, I will double the payment when I return home after the case is settled." So he promised extra, and the officials were pleased. On the way, they passed a mountain and saw several cows at the foot of the mountain, speaking human words and demanding Qing's life. Since you were summoned for this case, you must be innocent. Before the trial, you cannot be disrespectful. The cows then dispersed. Qing then realized he was dead.

Suddenly, he saw the capital city. Upon entering, he saw an official building that looked like a judicial office. Upon entering further, he saw the king seated angrily on the dais, with a cow crouching at the steps. The king ordered a trial between him and the cow. The cow's accusation was true, and the king angrily said, "This person has cleverly taken people's wealth, showing no mercy and harming living beings; he deserves to suffer in the knife mountain!" The myriad spirits joined in agreement, their voices like thunder. Immediately, a horse-faced demon dragged him away. Clear saw a very steep mountain with sharp blades arranged like dense bamboo shoots, and people on the mountain had their intestines cut and bellies pierced. The demon urged Clear to go up, but Clear trembled, cried out in sorrow, and retreated. The demon struck his head with a huge hammer, causing unbearable pain. Suddenly, the king ordered him to be brought back. Clear felt as if reborn. Clear saw the king's anger turn to gentleness, and his heart relaxed a bit. The king said, "Your actions, although they did not allow for a second chance at life, were commendable because you saved the lives of a mother and child when you were alive. I commend your actions and send you back to life. You must deeply repent your past wrongdoings, do not repeat old mistakes, otherwise, you cannot escape the severe punishment in the afterlife." Clear nodded. The king sent two original demons to escort him, and upon reaching his own door, the demons said, "The previous words cannot be eaten!" Clear replied, "Yes." Upon entering, he revived, having been dead for two days. He then got up and immediately ordered his family to buy two pieces of gold and silver paper money, quickly preparing offerings for the afterlife, and personally burned them outside the main gate.

Initially, Qing saw a group of village women surrounding a young woman, trying to persuade her. He asked them about it. One woman said, "We just saw this woman come here and sit down. We heard the crying of the child she was holding, which was very urgent, but suddenly it stopped. The other women were suspicious, so they quickly looked and saw that this woman was about to strangle her child with a rope around its neck. She said that her husband had been trading in Dezhou for two years and hadn't returned. She had come to look for him with her child, but she hadn't seen him. Her funds were exhausted, and she was ashamed to beg. She was going to strangle her child and then take her own life." At that time, many women were still trying to persuade the young woman. Another woman said, "There's no need to keep persuading her. Even if we persuade her here, if she goes somewhere else, she will still strangle her child. Who will always be there to persuade her?" Qing heard this and said, "That's true." He then asked about her background. The woman said, "My husband is Li Zhi, from Jiyang." Qing said, "You can stay temporarily in my home. I will send you back when the time is right." The young woman, upon hearing this, bowed with tears. The woman stayed in Qing's home for two months. At that time, Li Zhi returned and looked into his room but did not see his wife. He then traced her to Dezhou. When the couple met, Qing gave her some money to start her journey and sent her back. Qing's act of saving the lives of the mother and child was indeed this event.

After the Qing dynasty, one strictly prohibited family members from eating beef and sold all the cattle raised to farmers for use. If a Muslim sold meat in the market, they were pursued and ordered to sell it. Seeing that the local official banned the slaughter of oxen to emphasize agricultural importance and clear up theft, the notice was very good, so it was recorded to warn descendants. The notice read: "It is known that agriculture cannot precede animal husbandry, and slaughter indeed harbors the potential for theft. Therefore, those involved in accumulating stolen goods, from one ox to ten, are subject to severe penalties; the laws clearly state that the punishment ranges from corporal punishment to exile. Even if the ox belongs to oneself and is slaughtered privately, it is still subject to official punishment and serves as a warning. The regulations are extremely strict, and the orders are very stringent. Recently, the风气 has become increasingly corrupt, focusing only on profit and market gains while neglecting the fundamental principles. Those who sell swords and buy them again are aiding in the plowing of rain and clouds; those who wield knives and cut are opening the way for theft and burglary. There are bold Hui people and rowdy local ruffians who use the excuse of boiling meat to gather followers and accumulate accomplices. How many cattle are there, and how many hungry eyes are there; how many laws are there, and how many hardened hearts are there. They do not care about the disaster in the county, relying only on the butcher's skill; they act like thieves, how can they distinguish between oxen and horses. They wait for the right time to change the fields, then take advantage of the opportunity to steal. They sell at low prices, regardless of whether the source is clear; they hide the remaining skin, intending to erase the evidence of theft.

There are now people with cruel hearts who harm reason, and they set up offerings on tables. Observing their trembling, not only is their breathing frightening, but seizing sacrificial animals is not just a breeze worth worrying about. The transgression is the most severe, and the prohibition should be strict. Therefore, this edict is intended to inform the Han and Hui people and others. The Great Wu has one eye, and the Great Sacrifice is not a regular offering. Even if precious delicacies are specially roasted, without a reason, how can it be cut into large pieces? To prevent the wanton killing of living beings, test the longevity of different groups; remove hidden traps and lures, and rest in peace and warmth. Promote broad love and kindness, and dispel the illusions of prayer and incantation. From now on, everyone must change their old ways, cleanse past sins, destroy their hearths and stoves, and quiet their chopping blocks and axes. Yellow calves will not be stolen, and black oxen can rest in peace on farms. May all come and ascend, to assist ten thousand couples; even if rewards are given, they will not be stolen, how can there be worry about groups of three or five. At the head of the red apricot village, deeply sow a plow of sweet rain; by the green willow embankment, slant and rush with two horns of clear smoke. Present to my people the blessings of village education, and depict the scene of peace. Those who see it, how excellent it is! If anyone dares to be rebellious and stubborn, not listening to me, once investigated and apprehended, they will definitely be severely punished. Like gnawing at one's navel and being unable to reach, why not sharpen the knife and hide it deeply. Strictly follow and do not violate, sincerely and particularly shown. From the Qing dynasty to the present, it has been three generations, and every generation has enjoyed prosperity. It is said that some have already achieved fame and success.

The Virtual White Daoist said: "Rewards are not excessive and punishments are not excessive; the divine path is called the utmost fairness. When the clear sees evil actions, they immediately impose the punishment of the knife mountain; when the clear hears of good deeds, they immediately show the way back to life: is this not the utmost intention to encourage rewards and deter punishments? When the clear suddenly reforms their previous evil, the gods bless their descendants; the gods especially commend people who repent and reform themselves."

Reading this would be enough to make those who slaughter and eat oxen shiver with fear, and the accompanying notice is especially clear and forceful. Zhang Zicheng "牢" comes from "牛," and "狱" comes from "犬." By not eating oxen and dogs, one can avoid prison. This is because oxen are the divine essence of the Black Warrior (玄武) from the heavens, and the earthly, demonic energy of dogs and prisons. Oxen should not be used except in the context of sacrificial rituals, and only heavenly deities should enjoy the sacrifice. Therefore, oxen are not suitable for consumption by the common people. Moreover, oxen plow vast fields, contributing to the world; they eat the grass of spring without harming anyone. How can one bear to both benefit from their labor and then eat their flesh? They toil under the sky, their blood and sweat on the fields not yet dry; their bones are cut open, and their limbs are dismembered in the kitchen. How pitiful! If local officials strictly prohibit and apprehend, making butchers aware of the warning, it can certainly bring infinite blessings. If scholars and common people warn each other not to eat, they can permanently avoid prison. Is this not a good thing? This writing is clear, forceful, and enjoyable, truly beneficial to the world. It should be urgently printed to encourage the world. Hou Baili After the explanation of "Wanghui" in the *Zhou Book*, there is an appendix of edicts issued by Yi Yin for the four directions, and such examples are especially numerous in the *Zuo Zhuan* and the *Records of the Grand Historian*. The main text follows with a notice prohibiting the slaughter of oxen, taking the oldest judgment. The narrative of saving two lives is supplemented, also following the method of separation and combination. Ma Zhugu

There was once a Manchu censor who requested the lifting of the ban on slaughtering cattle for Muslims. The emperor severely criticized him. This text enlightens the world and awakens the people, sufficiently supplementing the law where it falls short, thus being beneficial to the world. Shangyuan Li Yu carefully annotated it. --- Jin Rui --- During the Ming dynasty, there was a man from Guiyang named Jin Fengxiang, who was a juren (a degree obtained through the imperial examination) and was assigned to serve as a county magistrate in Sichuan, on reserve, but did not take up the post due to an incident. He had two sons, named Rui and Zhang, who all studied under a private tutor. There was a man named Yue, also from a prominent family, who went to Rui's school when the teacher was not present and played chess with him. They quarreled and became angry, and Rui and Zhang beat Yue together, causing him to die immediately. Rui and Zhang hurriedly returned home and told their father. Jin Fengxiang was greatly surprised and said, "To kill someone and have them die instantly, by law, it is punishable by death. Which of you killed Yue?" Rui said, "I quarreled and beat him in anger, so I killed him." The father said, "If that is the case, I will take you to the authorities to confess and seek punishment." As they were about to go, Zhang said, "It wasn't my brother who killed Yue. I saw him beating my brother, in a moment of extreme emotion, I beat him and killed him, it has nothing to do with my brother." The father said to Zhang, "If you killed Yue, you will pay for his life, do not regret it!" Zhang said, "Killing someone and paying with your life is just, what is there to regret? Besides, if I involve my dear brother in my own fault, I cannot bear it in my heart." Rui said, "It was really me who killed him." Zhang said, "It wasn't my brother who killed him." The father said, "The elder does not blame the younger, and the younger does not blame the elder, they can be called virtuous!" After saying this, the father also had no idea, he bowed his head and remained silent.

Rui and Zhang were not born of the same mother; Zhang was the son of Hui, the stepmother. When Hui was present, she secretly rejoiced at Rui's words but felt hidden worry at Zhang's. Upon hearing that Rui and Zhang were arguing over who killed their father, she could not help herself and said to the father, "Since Rui admits to killing the father, Rui should be made to pay for it." The father angrily replied, "Zhang also admits to killing the father, so why should Rui pay for it?" Consequently, when Rui admitted to killing the father, the father struck Rui in the face; when Zhang wanted to take responsibility, Hui cursed Zhang. Rui said, "One of us brothers must take responsibility for the father's death. If it is my brother who takes responsibility, it will cause my mother lifelong worry, and I truly cannot bear it." Hui said, "If you say so, then a child without a mother will have no one to mourn for? I will definitely make your brother take responsibility!" The father said, "If that is the case, I must first send Rui away to avoid conflict." Thus, he first sent Rui away and then took Zhang with him to see the magistrate. The magistrate, being a close relative of the father, pretended to say to the father, "If someone kills themselves, their crime is reduced by one degree." After the father returned home, the magistrate ordered Zhang to accuse Rui of joint assault, intending to arrest both to vent his anger, but Zhang refused to accuse Rui. The magistrate used extreme punishment on Zhang, causing him to die and revive twice, but Zhang did not change his statement. The magistrate then stopped and punished Zhang, sending the case to the provincial office. Jin, upon hearing about the incident, became furious and died of illness.

The magistrate's constable escorted Zang to the county seat. Just as they left the city, the constable demanded money from Zang, but Zang refused to give it. The constable struck him with a stick, causing Zang to fall unconscious. Zang then felt someone put a pill in his mouth and immediately covered his mouth, nose, and eyes, saying, "Pretend to be dead, but do not actually die." After swallowing the pill, Zang felt unable to breathe but without discomfort. He then lay on his back on the road, not moving much. Hearing the constable say, "The criminal is already dead; you should quickly report to the official," and later hearing that the official had come to examine the body and ordered the body to be left in a ditch, the constable left after the official departed. After a long time, Zang felt someone shaking him. Fearing that the constable might return to look, he did not dare to move. The person said, "I am not someone else; I am the one who instructed you to pretend to be dead." Zang opened his eyes and looked at the sky, which was full of stars. He quickly stood up and saw a woman standing in front of him, unable to distinguish her beauty. The woman said, "You can quickly run! If you delay, you won't be able to." They then walked together by the hand, moving as fast as flying. They arrived at a cave with lights. Zang looked at the woman, who was a beautiful woman of marriageable age. He bowed and thanked her, and the woman accepted it directly without hesitation, saying, "It is indeed appropriate to thank me; without me, you would have surely died at the hands of the constable."

The woman set out meat dishes and wine for璋 to drink together.璋 said, "You, what kind of immortal are you? I beg you to enlighten me." The woman replied, "Since you know I am an immortal, there is no need to delve deeper." When asked about her name, she answered that she was a descending immortal.璋 looked around the cave and saw only one bed, and said, "Where shall I sleep?" The woman pointed to the bed and said, "Here." He asked, "Are you sleeping on the bed?" The woman smiled but did not speak. When they lay down,璋 took the woman and they slept together. The woman said, "The wound has not yet healed, so it is best to rest quietly."璋 said, "Since there is no pain, the scar can be ignored." So they slept together. After a month, the scar had healed. The woman said, "Staying here for too long is not beneficial. I will find a suitable place for you to live." They then set out, and the host at the place of lodging exerted every effort to serve, as if there were some kinship with the woman. When asked about the woman, she did not give a truthful answer. One day, they stayed at a household, but due to heavy rain, they could not continue their journey. The host entertained them in the courtyard. Suddenly, a small fox rushed in, and the host exclaimed, "There are guests here, and this will cause them to laugh!" The fox suddenly transformed into a young girl of ten or so years old. The descending immortal took the girl in her arms and said, "My little sister has revealed our secret." Zhang realized that the woman who descended was a fox spirit.

One day, Zhang and his sister took a brief rest in a tea house. A son from a high-ranking official's family saw her, stood and looked at her for a long time before leaving. Soon, the owner of the tea house pointed to her and asked Zhang, who answered that she was his sister. The owner said, "Do you want to marry someone?" Zhang replied, "No." The owner said, "The one who loves your sister is the son of that official. You arrived alone here, fearing that matters will not be under your control; it might be better to marry your sister to him, asking for a large dowry to enrich your resources." Zhang secretly discussed with his sister, who said, "Yes. You take the money and go north, at dusk, go to the place where there are five willow trees to stay the night, no later than two hours past midnight, I will come myself." Zhang saw the owner and told him what he wanted, and the owner said, "Yes." So, he gave Zhang fifty gold pieces, and Zhang left. The son of the official sent a maid to dress the sister in fine clothes and took her away. At night, he slept with her, indulging in intimacy. The next morning, he saw that she was his own sister. The sister returned and told Zhang, "The son of that official was the one who inflicted severe punishment on your son." She then told Zhang about the details of the insult, and Zhang greatly delighted and said, "You have avenged my long-held resentment on my behalf!"

After traveling to Tongzhou, the fairy said, "There is a happy land here, but I wonder if your fortune and destiny can bear it?" It happened that there was a wealthy family named Dong in the state, who had a daughter but no son. The fairy and Zhang descended to offer their services, willing to work as hired laborers. Dong welcomed them warmly, and because Zhang was cultured and weak, he was assigned to handle light work in the front courtyard, while the fairy managed needlework with the other women in the back courtyard. In reality, Zhang and the fairy spent every night together. After some time, the fairy asked, "Have you seen the master's daughter yet?" Zhang replied, "Not yet." The fairy said, "She is indeed a beautiful person." Zhang asked, "How does she compare to you?" The fairy replied, "I am not as graceful and beautiful as she." Zhang said, "Can you let me see her face?" The fairy responded, "Not only will you see her, but I will also arrange for you to marry her." Zhang eagerly asked when this would happen, and the fairy said, "Why are you so eager? It won't be long." By the second drumbeat, the fairy said, "I will check if she is still awake," and then left.

Afterward, he returned and said, "The woman is asleep! If she asks your name, you may tell her the truth, but do not speak of anything else lightly." The woman escorted Jin to the tower of Dong's daughter and then returned. The lamps were still lit, and it was the height of summer. He saw the woman lying naked in her bed, and he secretly approached her. The woman woke up, and after Jin had finished, she asked, "Are you Jin?" Jin replied, "Yes." "How did you get here?" Jin did not answer, got up, and disappeared, leaving the woman greatly puzzled. The next night, the same thing happened again. Jin told the woman what Dong's daughter had asked, and the woman said, "If she asks again, tell her that a fairy helped you, and do not mention me." On the third night, the woman set out a feast in anticipation, but after the second watch, Jin was not seen. She then spoke to herself, "Jin, if you are coming, come then, why wait for me to sleep?" Jin responded and entered, saying, "I have come!" The woman poured him a drink and offered it to him, and again she asked why he had come. Jin answered with the words of the fox woman, and the woman believed him. The tower was connected by a movable ladder, and the woman's parents set up the ladder during the day and removed it at night, so it was certainly beyond the reach of ordinary people.

During the drinking, they asked Zhan about his life history. Zhan still considered the fox girl as his sister, avoiding mentioning that he was a fox, and recounted the rest in detail. The girl, upon hearing this, felt sorrow like that of a married couple. After a month, the girl became pregnant. Her mother, Liang, saw this and became suspicious, telling her husband, Dong. Dong said, "At night, with no ladder, who could come up?" Liang replied, "Indeed, but the girl's situation is truly suspicious." At night, Dong and his wife secretly listened, and indeed, they heard soft voices of a man and a woman. Liang secretly set up a ladder, climbed up, and peeped through a hole in the window, seeing a man speaking with the girl. They knocked on the door and entered, finding only the girl inside. They asked her, "Just now, we saw a man here. Where did he go?" The girl replied, "There was indeed such a person, and his comings and goings were all aided by divine power." They asked, "Who was that person?" The girl answered, "Jin Zhan." Liang said, "Alas, he is just a hired worker!" The girl said, "Now he is a hired worker, but he is actually the son of an official family." She then recounted Zhan's family background and his experiences.

"Liang told Dong, saying, 'The two of us have no children, and for a long time we have wanted to take in a son-in-law to rely on for support. Jin has a family but cannot return, and taking him in would be greatly beneficial. Moreover, he has had a private relationship with your daughter for years, so we cannot help but take him in as a son-in-law.' Dong agreed, and they chose a favorable day to perform the wedding ceremony. Zhang drank with the daughter, when suddenly a fox girl appeared. Dong's daughter stood up and said, 'Why did you come so late, sister?' The fox girl replied, 'I am not your sister; I am the legitimate wife of your husband.' She told Zhang, 'You have a good place, and you do not need me. Please part ways forever. I took the money from selling me and left with it!' She then disappeared. Dong's daughter was surprised and quickly asked Zhang. Zhang said, 'She is a fox. She took your daughter as her wife, and it was all her effort. She also saved my life.' He then explained the whole story. The daughter said, 'If she has done you a favor, why did you sell her?' Zhang again explained why he sold her, and the daughter laughed and said, 'She got the price of a person, and then replaced it with her own sister. The fox sister can truly be said to be clever in taking revenge!' Two years later, Dong's father died, and Zhang changed his surname from Jin to Dong, inheriting all the property."

After Jin Gong's death, his second wife, Hui Shi, took full responsibility for the funeral arrangements. Upon hearing that Jin Zhang had died, she was overwhelmed with grief and fell into a state of delirium, discarding her wealth and destroying her possessions. Her enemy, Yue, then plotted against her. After her recovery, she found herself penniless. Desperate, she took up work as a servant, but Yue's family would not allow the master to keep her, and no one would give her food. Hui Shi then moved far away from her hometown, begging in villages during the day and sleeping in earthen kilns at night, enduring all kinds of suffering. Meanwhile, Jin Rui, after escaping, did not know where to go and wandered aimlessly, traveling for several months without finding a place to settle. He eventually arrived in Huizhou, where his funds were nearly exhausted. Forced to take up work, he became a servant in a household. The master, also surnamed Jin, employed a tutor named Jia, who was a scholar of the imperial examination system and had been a contemporary of Jin Gong. The tutor had two students, a son and a nephew, both sixteen years old, named Zhen and Lin. One day, the tutor had to attend to official business and left them with a topic to write on. By midday, neither Zhen nor Lin had written a single word. Jin Rui said, "Do not worry, I will write it for you." He quickly drafted the piece, instructing Zhen and Lin to copy it. By evening, both essays were completed. The tutor was shocked and said, "This writing is beyond what you two could produce." Zhen and Lin told him the truth, and the tutor informed Jin Gong.

The official asked Rui, "With such talent and learning, why did you flee here?" Rui spoke frankly. The official Gold was pleased to say, "Your father and I and my teacher were of the same year in the imperial examination; we are great contemporaries. To make the younger nephew a servant greatly violates the friendship!" He instructed Rui to study under the merchant teacher and adopted him as a nephew. Rui passed the children's examination and achieved consecutive success, being appointed by imperial decree as the head of the office and assigned to the Ministry of War. When he met a fellow villager, he asked about the family situation and learned that his father and brother had both died, and the whereabouts of his mother were unknown. He then wept bitterly. The fellow villager advised him, "Your mother has no support; you should urgently find her and take care of her. Crying does no good." Rui then resigned his position to search for his mother. Upon reaching the village, he stayed with an old neighbor, carefully inquired about the mother's whereabouts, and learned that she had worked for someone in a certain village. He visited the person to ask, and the person said she had worked there for a few days and then left. Rui searched the surrounding villages carefully, but after more than a month, there was no news. His bag contained little, and he dared not eat to his fill, because he feared that expenses would not continue. One day, he visited a village, and the villagers said, "A few months ago, an elderly woman died here. We do not know her surname, and the villagers buried her in the temple grounds at the head of the village." Rui dared not say it was not his mother, nor could he say it was his mother, so he wept at her grave. Suddenly, a young woman appeared and addressed Rui as her elder brother, saying, "The deceased was not your mother. If you wish to see your mother, you must urgently search for her at the foot of a certain mountain." After speaking, she disappeared. Rui was greatly delighted, thinking it was a divine indication, and hurried to the mountain to search. For several days, there was still no news. One day, encountering rain, he sought shelter in a stone temple at the foot of the mountain.

A moment later, the rain stopped, and he saw an elderly woman binding firewood with a rope and dragging it down the mountain. After the rain, the mud was slippery, and she lost her footing and fell, crying, "I saw my son Jīn Ruì, I don't know how heartbroken I am!" Jīn Ruì heard her but did not believe it, so he looked at her urgently. She was wearing ragged clothes, her face was dark and thin, and she looked nothing like his mother. Soon after, the woman fell again, speaking as before. Jīn Ruì hurriedly went to her and said, "Jīn Ruì is here." The woman wiped her eyes and looked at him, saying, "Jīn Ruì, you have finally come!" Jīn Ruì recognized her voice and knew it was his mother, so he exclaimed, "My mother..." and fainted on the muddy road, unconscious. After a long while, he regained consciousness and saw his mother sitting in the mud and crying. Fearing that his sorrow would cause his mother to grieve deeply, he forced a smile and said, "My mother and I are fortunate to meet, it is a great blessing." He quickly got up and helped his mother up. The mother ordered him to drag the firewood. Jīn Ruì wanted to abandon it, but thinking to himself that his funds were nearly exhausted, he could not help it. With one hand supporting his mother and the other dragging the firewood, he walked. Jīn Ruì asked, "Where does your mother live?" She replied, "Not far." There is a tile kiln several dozen steps down the mountain. Walking to that place, she said, "I live here." Jīn Ruì saw it, and tears streamed down, fearing that his mother would see, he turned back and wiped his tears.

The narrator carried his mother into the kiln, her head bowed in humility. A clay pot and a begging basket were placed nearby. The narrator could not bear the bitterness of the situation. The mother asked about the narrator's circumstances after leaving home, and he told her about his successive success in the imperial examinations, which made her extremely happy. The narrator hurriedly went to a nearby village to buy rice and cook, seeing his mother eat sweetly like honey, expressing mixed emotions of joy and sorrow. The next day, there were no resources, and the mother said, "You are already an official and are not used to begging. You stay here, and I will do it on your behalf." The narrator wept and said, "Taking care of my mother is a duty within my proper role, even if it means enduring great hardship, let alone begging?" After speaking, both mother and son wept. Suddenly, a young woman came, prostrated and bowed to the mother, and then showed respect to the narrator. The narrator looked at her and recognized her as the person who was sent to find the mother in the mountains. The mother asked, "Who are you to him?" She answered, "I am the wife of the second son."

The mother was startled and said, "My son had not married before he died; why are you speaking such nonsense?" The woman replied, "It is not nonsense. Your second son is still alive; he currently resides in a certain place in Tongzhou. He is wealthy, has changed his name to Dong Zhang, and has already passed the local recommendation examination this year. His wife has saved several taels of silver, which can be used as travel expenses to go and join him." After speaking, she placed the silver on the ground and disappeared. Rui and his mother were overwhelmed with joy and surprise, and they first exchanged some silver for travel expenses. When they were exchanging the silver, it was seen by a thief, who stole the silver at night, leaving only a few coins remaining. They then carried the mother on their back and walked. After several miles, the mother could no longer walk, so Rui carried her on his back. After about a mile, they were exhausted and rested briefly before continuing. With all their strength in a day, they could only manage to walk forty to fifty miles. Two days later, their feet were blistered and their legs were sore, and Rui could no longer proceed. Fortunately, there was a truck from Tongzhou returning empty, and Rui offered a small amount of money for a favor. The truck driver felt pity and agreed to take them. After arriving at the truck driver's home, they were only sixty to seventy miles away from where Dong Zhang lived, and Rui secretly rejoiced. The next morning, they carried the mother on their back again, and by midday, they rested briefly on the road.

A small carriage suddenly appeared, with an elderly woman and a young woman seated inside, followed by two empty carriages. The elderly woman descended and asked Rui, "Do you have the surname Jin?" Rui replied, "Yes." The elderly woman then pointed to Rui's mother and asked Rui, "Is this your mother?" Rui confirmed. The elderly woman turned to the young woman and said something to her. The young woman hurriedly got off the carriage and bowed on the road, saying, "The second son's wife, Dong, greets you respectfully, Mother." She stood up and then asked Rui about his brother's well-being. Rui's mother was surprised and said, "Are you the second son's wife again? If so, you must not run away!" Dong replied, "I was late in greeting you, and I have committed a serious offense. How could I run away? I truly cannot run away." Rui's mother asked, "Why isn't your husband here?" Dong answered, "He is away taking the imperial examination." They then helped Rui's mother get into the carriage and returned home. Upon arriving, Rui's mother said, "There was an event yesterday that still surprises me."

Dong asked him, and the mother spoke through a young woman as if she were the daughter-in-law, presenting travel funds formally, and then disappeared without speaking to him. Dong said, "She is actually the daughter-in-law, but is actually a fox. The daughter-in-law came to welcome, and it was also the fox sister who said the mother was coming." Rui quickly prepared his luggage and went to the capital to find his brother, then submitted a memorial to himself, stating that he had abandoned his official position to find his mother and beat to death his father-in-law, that the sin was self-inflicted, and had nothing to do with his brother Zhang, and was willing to take the blame and other matters. The emperor praised Rui's filial piety and Zhang's brotherly love, and all were pardoned; Zhang was ordered to resume his original surname and take the imperial examination, and also took the imperial examination in the next session. On the occasion of his mother's birthday, a feast was held to celebrate, and the fox girl suddenly arrived, and the mother was extremely pleased, forgetting that she was a fox. By evening, Zhang asked the fox girl, "Your coming, is it also because of the affection between husband and wife that cannot be ignored?" She replied, "No. One is to wish the mother a long life, and one is because I have a great joy, just to inform you." Zhang asked her, and the fox girl said, "I was able to avoid the calamity because I helped you and your brothers." After speaking, she immediately disappeared.

The Daoist Master Xubai said: If Jin Zhang sacrifices himself to save his brother and ultimately dies, and Jin Rui resigns his position to seek his mother and ultimately loses his position, people will think that the Creator is foolish or indifferent, and will lament and be moved without end. The fox rescues and guides them, making Rui and Zhang's filial and fraternal deeds famous in their time and passed down to future generations. The fox's merit can be considered great! Yet the fox, by this act, escapes the cycle of calamity, and what the fox does is no less than what the Creator does. Reading this will inspire filial and fraternal feelings, open up broad and open-minded thoughts, not just because of its fine writing. Wang Zisant. The subtlety of the response, as if one sentence throughout, this is truly a work of great literature. Ma Zhuwu.

Criticizing and correcting societal ills has done significant service to the fundamental social norms and moral order, and should not be dismissed as flattery. Annotated by Li Yu of Shangyuan. **Volume Three of the Yizhi Lu** **Su Yuzhen** Xiao Peizhi of Wu County, a member of a prominent family, entered school at the age of fifteen and was already in his twenties. He had not yet married and was studying at a separate residence near his home. One day, while reading until around midnight, he suddenly heard someone outside the window say, "Mr. Xiao, you are diligent in your studies!" The voice was gentle and soft, like that of a woman. She then pulled aside the curtain and entered, and upon seeing her, he realized she was indeed a quiet and beautiful woman. Xiao knew she was not human and asked her why she had come. She replied, "Can you leave a name on a memorial arch for filial piety and chastity? You gain a beautiful woman, and I love a talented man. We met by chance, and together with you, we shall be virtuous, which is fortunate indeed. Why ask further?" They then became intimate. Later, Xiao asked her again, and she revealed that she was a fox. From then on, she visited him every night. After nearly a month, the fox suddenly asked, "Why have you not married by now?" Xiao replied, "My mother has strict demands: the family background should be appropriate, and she seeks a virtuous woman; she demands beauty and a good family background, so it has been delayed until now."
