The fox said, "Why look far for someone? The daughter of the scholar-official Su to the east, nicknamed Yuzhen, is unparalleled in both talent and beauty. Isn't she a perfect match?" Xiao said, "The family backgrounds are similar, but the living conditions are very different. How could she agree to marry down?" The fox asked, "Have you proposed through a matchmaker?" Xiao replied, "Not yet." The fox said, "Let's try matchmaking. If it doesn't work out, I will take care of it."

Criticizing and correcting societal ills has done significant service to the fundamental social norms and moral order, and should not be dismissed as flattery. Annotated by Li Yu of Shangyuan. **Volume Three of the Yizhi Lu** **Su Yuzhen** Xiao Peizhi of Wu County, a member of a prominent family, entered school at the age of fifteen and was already in his twenties. He had not yet married and was studying at a separate residence near his home. One day, while reading until around midnight, he suddenly heard someone outside the window say, "Mr. Xiao, you are diligent in your studies!" The voice was gentle and soft, like that of a woman. She then pulled aside the curtain and entered, and upon seeing her, he realized she was indeed a quiet and beautiful woman. Xiao knew she was not human and asked her why she had come. She replied, "Can you leave a name on a memorial arch for filial piety and chastity? You gain a beautiful woman, and I love a talented man. We met by chance, and together with you, we shall be virtuous, which is fortunate indeed. Why ask further?" They then became intimate. Later, Xiao asked her again, and she revealed that she was a fox. From then on, she visited him every night. After nearly a month, the fox suddenly asked, "Why have you not married by now?" Xiao replied, "My mother has strict demands: the family background should be appropriate, and she seeks a virtuous woman; she demands beauty and a good family background, so it has been delayed until now." The fox said, "Why look far for someone? The daughter of the scholar-official Su to the east, nicknamed Yuzhen, is unparalleled in both talent and beauty. Isn't she a perfect match?" Xiao said, "The family backgrounds are similar, but the living conditions are very different. How could she agree to marry down?" The fox asked, "Have you proposed through a matchmaker?" Xiao replied, "Not yet." The fox said, "Let's try matchmaking. If it doesn't work out, I will take care of it."

萧 immediately informed his mother, troubling a close friend to act as a matchmaker, but it indeed did not succeed. The fox, angered, said, "Why does he place himself so high! If you truly wish to marry her, I can devise a hundred ways to accomplish it." He replied, "I vow to make her my wife." The fox said, "This heart cannot be swayed by circumstances." 萧 said, "Agreed." Yùzhēn, who was young, learned from her father and excelled in poetry. Whenever there was a poem or verse, she would have the young maid present it to her father. Accidentally, she composed a short poem. Her father read it and commented: "The rhyme is steady, and the conception is novel." The poem read: "After finishing the embroidery, she often called her sisters to admire it; warm breezes and sunny days filled the balustrades. In the flowers, the double butterflies were scattered; they flew over the eastern wall and formed a cluster again." One day, deep into the night, Yùzhēn still repeatedly pondered over the poem. Suddenly, a young man entered from outside. Looking at him, it was Xiāo Péizhi from the western neighbor. The girl was surprised and asked, "Why are you here so late at night?" He said, "I have come only to ask for your advice." Seeing the girl sitting alone on the long couch, 萧 also sat beside her and said, "Yesterday, I troubled the matchmaker, why did you refuse?" The girl said, "This matter is not something the two of us can decide." 萧 said, "This matter is not something the two of us can decide, but it is something the two of us can do." Hearing this, the girl's face turned red beyond her ears, and she felt ashamed and could not bear it. She wanted to leave, but he held her back. The girl said, "Please tell me if my poem is good?" She then handed the seven-character quatrain she had composed to 萧, intending to use it as an excuse to escape. 萧 said, "I have no time to read this." After he spoke, suddenly, as if waking from a dream, he still sat alone in the study, and the poem was still in his hand.

After reading it, he admired it to the utmost and took up his pen to write after the poem: "If I could have this woman as my wife in this life, I would build a golden house in advance to wait for her." He wished the fox to come and tell him, but the fox did not come. The next day, without heart for reading, he composed a seven-character quatrain: "I once visited Yuzhen at Tian Tai, with a bright moon like silver overhead. The deep and secluded courtyard is locked by heavy gates, who would think of Xiao Lang as a stranger?" He then wrote "Neighbor Xiao Peizhi respectfully presents" at the end. By evening, recalling Yuzhen's beautiful appearance, he wished to go again, but regretted that his dreams were no longer vivid. Soon after, a woman pulled the curtain and entered; looking at her, it was indeed Su Yuzhen, the girl from the east neighbor, and he was greatly pleased. She also said, "What are you doing here at night?" The girl seemed dazed and confused, not knowing what to do. Xiao then embraced her, her cheeks and eyes gazing, her emotions and demeanor difficult to describe.

Desiring to be intimate with her, the woman refused, saying: "I do not disdain ugliness and beauty; I only wish for a harmonious and lasting relationship. If we were to secretly unite, I would absolutely not agree. If I were to lose my virtue, you would surely reject me. At that time, I could not have two husbands, and the circumstances would make it difficult to return to you. I would be left alone for life, how terrible it would be!" Xiao vowed to definitely marry her, and presented his family's jade如意 as a token of his commitment. The woman also gave him a small gold如意 used for combing hair as a gift. At that time, she would only follow Xiao's lead. However, the woman suddenly disappeared, and Xiao deeply regretted it. The next evening, a fox appeared. Xiao told the fox about the events of the previous night, and the fox merely smiled. Xiao said, "Is this all your doing?" His face showed a hint of anger. The fox replied, "You wished to be husband and wife with Yuzhen, guiding you to her and me to you, all to strengthen your marital bond. Yet you resent me for not allowing a fleeting union. Is a single night's pleasure enough to fulfill a lifetime of happiness?" Xiao looked thoughtful and apologized.

Scholar Su Xianlin, though he had initially refused Xiao's proposal of marriage, lived next door and often visited each other. One day, he happened to visit Xiao's study, only to find Xiao absent. On the desk, he saw a poem, which he read and found it to be a seven-character quatrain written by Xiao's daughter, with comments that were very disrespectful. He then took the poem back. Later, he also picked up Xiao's poem given to the girl outside her room, greatly suspicious, and told his wife. His wife said, "If you say that the daughter had an affair, our family's strict rules would certainly not allow it; if you say it is not true, why is the daughter's poem there and Xiao's poem here? There must be some other reason. It would be better to hint to Xiao, to make him go through the formal process of matchmaking. Once the marriage is arranged, all doubts will be resolved." Scholar Su agreed. While talking, the chief maid Chunfang of Yu Zhen secretly listened and then told Yu Zhen, who secretly delighted. Scholar Su was just about to hint to Xiao, but Xiao passed the provincial examination; again, he wanted to hint, but Xiao was about to become a palace scholar and became a compiler in the Hanlin Academy. Before, Xiao tried to arrange a match with Su but Su did not want to; now, Su wants to hint to Xiao but Xiao has suddenly become prestigious. Scholar Su feared there might be rumors of seeking favors, and the matter was thus abandoned.

Initially, Yu Zhen heard what the senior maid Chun Fang had to say and thought her wishes would be easily fulfilled. Later, upon hearing about Xiao Gui, and since there was no definite arrangement for the marriage, she became deeply troubled, losing interest in her needlework and gradually reducing her meals, eventually appearing listless and ill. Chun Fang observed her inner turmoil and said, "Miss, you consider me as your confidante. If you have any heart matters, the only one in the room who can speak with you is me." Yu Zhen did not respond. After a while, Chun Fang repeated, "Is it not about the matter of Xiao Lang?" Yu Zhen was startled to have her inner thoughts guessed and admitted, "It is indeed about this." She then recounted her dreams and experiences in detail. She added, "I have a token of sincerity here, and I want to send someone to deliver it to test his feelings, but I lack a suitable person." Chun Fang suggested, "I can dress as a man and accompany an old gatekeeper to go there. Would that be acceptable?" Yu Zhen replied, "It would be fine, but it would be difficult to explain to my parents." Chun Fang volunteered, "I will speak to them on your behalf." Yu Zhen had a step-brother of the same age, and Chun Fang decided to go under his name.

After they had met, Xiao said, "You are not Brother Su." Chunfang replied, "Indeed, I am Brother Su’s cousin, Wang." Xiao admired his refined appearance and scholarly demeanor, and he felt a deep affection for him. He asked, "What advice do you have for me?" Chunfang said, "My cousin has an object, which he says belongs to you, and he has sent me to return it to you." He then handed an object to Xiao. Upon seeing it, Xiao realized it was the jade green jade lucky charm that he had given Chunfang’s cousin. He was greatly startled and asked, "Has your cousin been betrothed?" Chunfang replied, "No, she has not." "Is she about to be betrothed?" "No, she is not either." Chunfang asked mockingly, "Why do you, upon seeing this object, immediately ask whether my cousin has been betrothed?" Xiao said frankly, "I will tell you frankly. I have dreamt of meeting your cousin twice, and we made solemn vows. I once gave this object as a gift. I am testing my heart. Please return the original object. My heart remains as it was at first, and it has not changed since then." He then returned the jade lucky charm to Chunfang.

Afterward, the cook served the meal, and Pēi Zhī took a cup to urge Chūnfāng to drink, but she refused, saying she never drinks. At that moment, Hú, who was with Pēi Zhī in the capital, whispered, "The guest is not Su's cousin, but actually Chūnfāng, a servant of Yùzhēn." Xiāo, upon hearing this, was greatly pleased and insisted on offering her a drink. Chūnfāng, unable to refuse, reluctantly drank a cup, and a flush rose to her cheeks. Under the lamp, she appeared especially beautiful. Pēi Zhī asked, "May I inquire about your age?" She replied, seventeen. He then asked, "Is your wife still young?" She answered, "My younger brother is two years old, and the marriage has not yet taken place." Pēi Zhī laughed and said, "The plum tree is delayed in blooming; perhaps you can endure it, but at your age, you should be bearing children and have not yet experienced intimacy. You could be called a woman who laments her unmarried status!" Chūnfāng, upon hearing this, blushed deeply and was ashamed to face others. Shortly after, the banquet ended, and the dishes were cleared. The attendants all left. Pēi Zhī then said, "What about sharing a bed tonight?" Chūnfāng refused, saying she was not accustomed to it.

Péi said, "After the flower candles, will you sleep alone in the bridal chamber?" Chunfang replied, "She is a woman, and naturally happy." Péi said, "You are a man, and I am not willing to share a bed with you." Chunfang heard this and blushed, remaining silent. Péi said, "I have known you for a long time, and you are a good friend of Yuzhen. Now that you have voluntarily entered a dangerous situation, can you still escape? Even a well-set trap cannot catch a wise person, how clever am I?" He then embraced the woman, desiring to engage in intimacy. The woman said, "I have come here, and it is inevitable. But I came here on orders, and if I precede the master, not only will I fall into impropriety, but you will also be unfeeling. If you do not嫌弃 my lowly status, I am willing to be a minor star, but I dare not obey now." Péi persisted in his requests, and the woman said, "If there is no other way, please do not remove my middle clothes, and allow me to submit to your will, but I will die rather than comply with anything else." Soon after, the fox entered. Péi told the fox about the situation. The fox said, "She is truly a virtuous woman, and you should allow her to maintain her integrity." Péi returned home and told his mother about the matter. His mother ordered him to marry Su as his wife and take Chunfang as a concubine, and the fox finally ceased its involvement. Xubai Daoist said: To unite marriages between two families, each according to their own wishes, without forcing anyone; to bring men and women together in one place, fostering deep affection without allowing hasty union, all are expressions of proper emotion. And the fox's arrangement was so, showing that the fox also understands human feelings. Our emotions, when used improperly, lead to imbalance, but when used properly, they lead to harmony. How can those who use emotions be careless?

Su Xiaolian, a scholar-official, stopped the marriage proposal due to Xie's nobility, which is indeed extraordinary. Ma Zhugu The fox arranged the marriage through two poems, which can be called a poetic matchmaker. Shangyuan Li Yu respectfully annotates. The Righteous Wolf The southeast of the provincial capital has many mountains, and wolves often gather in groups. People in mountain villages are accustomed to seeing them and are not greatly afraid. There was a carpenter named Cai, who frequently traveled through the mountain paths. One day, he saw a small wolf crying pitifully at the entrance of a wolf den. It seemed that the mother wolf had been captured by hunters, so the small wolf was hungry. Cai took it back, cut off its tail, and named it "As You Wish," raising it like a dog. After two years, the wolf grew larger than a dog. The family scolded it, but it still had a sense of fear. People in the village did not dare to treat it maliciously. The wolf then looked at the children in the neighborhood with a fierce gaze, as if it had the intention to devour them. Cai was afraid it would hurt someone and said to it, "If your mother had not died, I would not have taken you in, and you would have starved to death in the mountains. Now that you have grown up, you should not stay in the village. Today I am sending you back to the mountains." He then fed it a full meal and sent it to the deep mountains. Cai returned, and the wolf followed him. Cai said, "Are you sending me? You don't need to send me." The wolf stopped. After walking about a mile, he looked back, and the wolf was still sitting on a rock, looking far away.

Tài, the original woodworker, consistently returned home after dusk. One day, he was slightly late and, on his way, encountered three wolves blocking his path. Although he held a long pole, it was difficult to rely on it to feel fearless. Fortunately, three steps behind him was a sheer cliff, so he retreated and leaned against it to protect his back. Soon after, two more wolves came, and not long after, there were over ten, surrounding him in front. He was extremely distressed and said loudly, "Now I am destined to die here!" After he spoke, one wolf heard his voice and suddenly stood up, head facing outward, tail pointing towards Tài, retreating to in front of him, as if wanting to make him see its tail. At that time, the moonlight was faint, and Tài saw that the wolf had no tail, knowing it was Rúyì. He called out, "Rúyì, save me!" Rúyì forcefully drove away the pack of wolves. He was very happy and said, "Rúyì, send me!" Upon arriving home, he said, "If I hadn't met Rúyì today, I would have been buried in the wolves' bellies!" He fed it with food to make it leave. From then on, Rúyì often waited on the road, and when encountered, it would send him. When villagers encountered a pack of wolves, among them was one with a short tail, and calling it Rúyì, it would lead the pack away.

One night, the talent heard the urgent knocking on the door. He got up to ask, but there was no response. When he opened the door and looked out, a wolf suddenly rushed in and lay motionless in front of the courtyard. Suspecting it to be "Like Intention," he lit a candle to see, and indeed it was. He saw the wolf sweating profusely, with a cloth bag beside it containing two hundred taels of white gold. It is said that the pack of wolves had injured a traveler, left this on the road, and brought it to repay the talent's kindness. Later, they would come at night bringing objects, and the talent lived in relative comfort. After some time, the wolf would also come during the day, sometimes lying in the talent's house and sometimes resting in the streets. Those who were fond of it often fed it with food. When the talent died, he was buried outside the village. People often saw "Like Intention" lying by the grave, and after more than a year, it was no longer seen.

The Daoist Master Xubai said: An ancient saying goes, "A wolf cub has a wolf's heart." Of all creatures, only this one possesses the most cruel and vicious nature. Yet, it never forgets the kindness of nurture and always finds a way to repay it. Therefore, those who betray kindness and act unrighteously should be ashamed before this wolf. Those with human faces but wolf hearts, I have heard of them; those with wolf faces but human hearts, I have not seen such a person. Distinctions between giving and receiving are clear. Can humans be inferior to wolves in this regard? It is said that The character for jackal is composed of "talent," and the character for wolf is composed of "goodness." These are animals that possess some qualities of talent and goodness. Considering this, even the legendary white wolf child associated with the Song Dynasty ruler Wang Yi is not worth mentioning! This is respectfully annotated by Shangyuan Li Yu. Gao Wuguan Yao Fawu was a farmer. His family's wealth was not abundant, just enough for the current year's needs. His wife, Lady Hui, gave birth to a daughter, whom they called Wuguan. At the age of ten, her mother passed away. Yao then married the sister of Yang Yuli as his second wife, and she gave birth to a son who was just two years old when Yao died. Yang said to her sister, "You are still in the prime of your youth, and no one is taking care of you. If you sell all the family's property to others, and I choose fertile fields near the market for you, and I will manage them for you, wouldn't that be mutually convenient?" The sister thought it was a good idea and agreed. Yang then secretly changed all of Yao's property records to her own name, while her sister was unaware of this.

A Wealthy Man Zhang There was a wealthy man named Zhang who fell in love with a woman named Wuguan because of her beauty. He wished to buy her as a concubine and knew that Yang was her maternal uncle. So he went to Yang's place, directly stated his intention, and offered two hundred taels of silver. Yang was very happy and tricked Wuguan to his home, then forcibly handed her over to Zhang's people who carried her away. The silver was mostly taken by Yang, and Wuguan did not receive even a fraction of it. Later, Yang forced Wuguan to divorce her husband. Wuguan, whose husband had recently died and who had a three-year-old son, could not bear to abandon them, and vowed not to remarry. Yang spoke to her repeatedly, but Wuguan still refused. Yang's neighbor, a ruffian named Li, heard that Yang's sister was beautiful and widowed, and asked Yang to act as a matchmaker, offering a large reward. Yang did not consult his sister and ordered Li to forcibly take her away. Wuguan went to Li's house, vowed not to sleep with him, and despite many attempts to persuade her, she refused to comply. Li became angry, stripped her naked and beat her, leaving her almost dead. After she recovered, Li again wanted to violate her, but Wuguan still refused. Li angrily said, "I spent more than ten taels of silver for you, and now both man and money are wasted, how can I be satisfied!" Wuguan said, "If so, may I redeem myself with the original money?" Li said, "Yes." Wuguan still had the money she received from Zhang, so she gave it to Li, and Li allowed her to leave. Wuguan wanted to stay with her brother, but feared he would again force her to change her status, so she stayed in a poor house behind a temple, worked for a wealthy household doing needlework, and lived far from home. Yang's son, whom Yao named Zeng when he was alive, was treated by Yang like a dog or pig. At five years old, Yang forced him to beg in the village, and those who saw him all hated Yang for his wickedness.

Five Officials served as a concubine in Zhang's household. The principal wife was jealous, and she endured unbearable suffering all day long. Fortunately, she was favored by Master Zhang, which slightly comforted her. Hearing that her brother Zeng was weak and begging for food, she felt deeply distressed. Out of necessity, she pleaded with Zhang, asking for his compassion and mercy. Zhang knew that his wife could not tolerate others, so he entrusted Zeng to his close relative Meng Gong. Meng Gong was kind and good-hearted. Seeing that Zeng was young and weak, he entrusted a hired woman to take care of him, providing him with small amounts of money to buy clothes and shoes. When Zeng grew up, his status was the same as that of a hired worker, and Meng Gong managed his earnings each year, accumulating a certain amount by the time he reached the age of twenty. Five Officials had no children in Zhang's household. After Zhang's death, the principal wife could not tolerate her, so she sent Five Officials back to Yang's place. Yang saw that she was still in good health and wanted to take her as a concubine. Five Officials refused, and Yang made his wife get her drunk with wine and defiled her. Whenever Five Officials saw Yang, she would curse him, and she often sought to commit suicide. Yang feared her, wanting to sell her, but found no buyer. Suddenly remembering her nephew in the Meng family, who had gradually established a family business, she entrusted a woman from another family to marry, advising Zeng to marry her. Zeng followed her advice. When he passed through the door, his sister and brother did not originally know each other. Five Officials saw that the house belonged to her mother's family and said to Zeng, "Are you not Yao Zeng?" Zeng replied, "Yes." She said, "I am your biological sister. For a sister and brother to marry, it is the same as the behavior of beasts!" She then ordered Zeng to sleep separately. The next day, Five Officials ordered Zeng to bring Yang's unmarried daughter to deceive her into coming, forcing her to marry Zeng.

The Five Senses feared Yang's violent and oppressive behavior and pitied the neighbors who protected and sheltered them. After several days, upon hearing this, Yang indeed came to forcibly take the daughter, intending to use force. The neighbors and protectors all became angry, and Yang, fearing retribution, finally left on his own. The next day, Yang sued in court, and the Five Senses also reported the case to the authorities. The judge asked the Five Senses, "Did you entice Yang's daughter and force her to be the wife of your younger brother?" They replied, "Indeed, it is true, but there is a lower-level complaint to appeal." They then clearly explained the circumstances of seizing property and other matters, and said, "He intended to marry her to Zeng, with his own sibling as a married couple, destroying the natural order of family relationships. Fortunately, she recognized and exposed this, preventing incest. In her desperation, she deceived her to come and married her to her younger brother." After speaking, they cried loudly. The judge, upon hearing this, became very angry and severely punished Yang in court, and judged Yang's daughter to be the wife of Zeng. The judge asked the Five Senses, "Can your anger be calmed?" The Five Senses said, "Yao's property, he should not have seized it; the funds from the mother-in-law who sold her daughter, he should not have swallowed them; we pray to recover them one by one." The judge asked about Yang's property, and Yang truthfully stated it. Since Yang seized the property, he later acquired several fields. The judge said, "Half is sufficient for your enjoyment." He then decided to give half of the property to Yao and Zeng, and the case was concluded. Zeng then returned to his mother. The Five Senses had private savings, and his mother had surplus, and thus Zeng became a wealthy family. Shortly after, Yang and his wife died in succession. Yang had no children, and no other relatives, so all of his property belonged to Zeng.

The Daoist Master Xubai said: "The evil of Yang can be said to be extreme! However, when the son of a powerful man is born, all the wealth of the father belongs to the son; when a woman is bought from Yao, the woman of the buyer eventually belongs to Yao. Those who deceive others always deceive themselves. It is the constant law of heaven's way."

If one seduces another's wife or daughter, the retribution will come upon one's own wife or daughter. The heavenly net is vast but does not miss anything. Truly, as a warning. The retribution is swift and satisfying; it is appropriate to toast to it. Comment by Li Yu from the Shangyuan period. Ruhong Yin Shangde of Pingyang, Shanxi, a scholar from the county. Having been a wealthy family for ten generations, the old family's mansion is the finest in the village. The garden beside the house connects to the inner chambers. Within the garden, a hall and pavilion were built as a place to entertain guests and study. The flowers and trees form paths, and the pavilions are connected. There is a square pond about half an acre in size, connected to a small island. It is covered with a dense iron grille, and inside, it houses hundreds of gold and silver fish with horizontal tails. By the time of Yin Shangde, the fish grew to be about a foot long and as thick as a bowl's mouth, too numerous to count. When they swam together, the pond water turned completely red. Indeed, a great sight in the region. Initially, Yin Shangde's uncle had no son, so Yin Shangde was adopted by his uncle. Then his biological father had no son, and his uncle and father agreed to each marry a wife. When they had grandsons, each would inherit the ancestral temple. Just then, there were twin daughters of a certain Shao, who were about the same age as Yin Shangde. The uncle married the elder, and the father married the younger, and they were married on the same day. Their beauty was unparalleled. The following year, each had a son. Yin Shangde was extremely happy. However, with the double burden, his body gradually became weak. Moreover, the relatives he had inherited from his adoptive father and uncle passed away one after another. He fell ill from overwork, and the doctors could not cure him. He then moved to live in the garden, staying alone in the study to convalesce. He hired a servant to attend to him.

One day, I chanced to see two beautiful women wandering by the pond through the window. One was dressed in purple, and the other in a light, soft white fabric. I thought they were neighbors' daughters. Upon realizing that there were no such beautiful women nearby, I quickly went out to see them, but the two women had already disappeared. Later, I often saw them. By the pond stood a hexagonal pavilion, so despite being ill, I went to the pavilion and hid myself to wait. Soon, I saw the two women coming from the door. They had beautiful appearances and extraordinary beauty. As soon as they approached the pavilion, they turned around to leave. I quickly came out of the pavilion to see them, but the women had disappeared. After lighting the lamp, I recalled their beauty and wrote a poem in a hurry: "Like flowers, they are still more beautiful than jade; unparalleled and captivating. Sickness would dissipate their intention, but the view still ties my feelings." It was already deep into the night, and I suddenly felt thirsty. I called for the servant, but got no response. Suddenly, someone outside the window said, "The servant is asleep, I can do it instead." After entering, looking at her, it was the purple-clad woman seen earlier. I was extremely happy, despite illness, and got up to thank her. She said, "This does not deserve thanks, are you not thirsty?" Then she lit charcoal to boil water, after it boiled, brewed tea to serve.

Yin Fu greeted her with a kiss, and found the flavor different from others. His spirit was refreshed, and he said, "Is this not tea?" The woman replied, "Yes, but the brewing method is different." They then used tea as wine, drinking it slowly. Yin Fu said, "Are you not a fairy?" She answered, "No." "Are you a ghost or fox spirit?" He asked. She replied, "No, it is not that either. You will know in time, there is no need to press further." Yin Fu said, "I wish to hear your beautiful name." The woman said, "My small name is Ruī Hóng. The one wearing light white soft silk is my younger sister Ruī Bá." Yin Fu said, "Why doesn't she come along?" Ruī Hóng said, "People have their own intentions, and cannot be forced." Yin Fu said, "I am sorry to have the worry of gathering firewood, and I feel ashamed that I cannot repay you. I greatly disappointed you for coming." Ruī Hóng said, "I came specifically because of your illness, not truly to work as a servant. My sisters and I owe your family ten generations of kindness. If we were to sit by and do nothing about your illness, that would be ungrateful. I have been refining the golden elixir since the day you fell ill, and it has now been completed. But since we just met, I fear you may not believe me, so I dare not give it to you immediately." Yin Fu said, "If there is a fairy elixir, please use it to treat me. Even if it does not cure me, I will not blame you." Ruī Hóng said, "My medicine cannot cure you immediately, but if you take one pill per day, after three pills, your spirit will be restored to its original state. But you must strictly abstain from sexual activity. Since you do not have a family, there is no need to worry about this."

Lord Yi was given a pill to hold in his mouth, which was immediately followed by the tea he had been drinking. He was told, "You may now sleep!" He took off his shoes and clothes, and his wife was not as attentive as usual. Lord Yi usually did not sleep at night, but after taking the medicine, he slept soundly. When he woke up, it was already noon, and he felt that his long-term illness had been reduced by half. He was greatly pleased. Knowing that Hong would definitely come in the evening, he looked forward to it eagerly and even resented the slowness of the evening. When evening came, Hong arrived and gave him another pill. After three pills, his spirits were revived, and he seemed like a young man again. Lord Yi wanted to engage in intimate relations, but Hong said, "Having just found a way to live, do you want to seek death again?" Lord Yi replied, "I have now found a way to die." Hong advised, "He still needs to abstain from sexual activity for over a month. After thirty days, he can engage in it daily without fear." Lord Yi then stopped. The two Shao brothers saw Lord Yi's strong physique and asked him about it. Lord Yi told them the truth. Shao said, "Hong is in the room, how can you sleep alone?" Although she is there, she actually does not sleep all night. At night, the two Shao brothers peeked through the window and saw that Hong was indeed sitting cross-legged at the head of the bed. While secretly peeking, the two Shao brothers felt a sense of fear and hurriedly returned. The next evening, the two Shao brothers went to the study together and strongly urged Lord Yi to sleep in the inner room.

Red said, "Although the husband has recovered, he still needs to rest for another month." The two Shao brothers did not listen and insisted on going. Red became angry and said, "Is it hard to control lustful thoughts? Even if it were so, it would not be by you!" Shao, upon hearing Red's words, if he feared the imperial decree, went away obediently. When the period of abstinence was about to end, Yin wanted to break the vow. Red said, "Wait another two or three days, and you can enjoy a hundred years of glory; if you break the vow now, you will lose ten years of your life." Yin said, "Dying at ninety is not a young death." They then engaged in intimate relations. Yin asked, "Yesterday you said there was a ten-generation bond between our families, what do you mean by that?" Red said, "You will know in the future." Yin asked again, "Why hasn't your sister come once?" Red said, "My sister is not as emotionally attached as I am. Gradually persuade her, she will eventually come." In the morning, Red wrote a five-character poem: "Her beauty like jade and flowers, smiling and charming Yangcheng. Shyly presenting her ugly self, just to repay the affection." Yin said, "You are indeed a woman of both talent and beauty." At night, Yin said, "Tonight I want to move into the inner courtyard, is that okay?" Red said, "Do not discuss this with me, the distant should not interfere with the close, the new should not interfere with the old. How can a fleeting relationship dominate a lifelong marriage? I am here, like your summer house, coming and going is up to you, I dare not stop you."

Red had not arrived for three days, and Yin began to doubt him. After the second watch of the night, Rui Bai suddenly appeared, and Yin was greatly delighted, saying, "Are you also pitying me, sir? Your elder sister frequently conveyed my deep feelings, yet you did not come even once. I thought I had no good fortune to enjoy the beauty of two women. Now that you have graced me with your presence, how fortunate I am!" Upon examining him, Bai had a worried expression on his face, and his eyes were filled with tears. Yin was startled and asked, "What is the matter?" Bai replied, "My elder sister has suddenly contracted a strange illness and is on the verge of death. Only my scholar's Nine-Transformation Life-Saving Pill can cure her. Do you not mind helping me obtain it?" Yin said, "Rui Hong, my beloved wife, how could I sit by and do nothing!" He then wanted to go immediately. Bai could not help but laugh and said, "How could it be proper to disturb someone at such a late hour? You may go in the morning." Yin asked, "Once I get the medicine, will you prepare it and take it?" Bai said, "I truly cannot. I still beg you to prepare it yourself. The matter has come to this, and I cannot hide it. Tomorrow night, there will be a sick goldfish floating on the surface of the pond. The fish is my elder sister. You must quickly use a large basin to scoop up the pond water and place the fish in it. Then move it to a secret room, so that it does not see the light of day again. Sprinkle the pill powder finely into the basin, and after a night, it will revive itself." After saying this, he took his leave. Yin wanted to stay and share the night with him, but Bai said, "How unfeeling you are! My elder sister is gravely ill. How could I have the mood to enjoy intimacy with you?" So he left. Yin then realized that Rui Hong and her sister were both fish spirits.

The next day, Yin purchased elixirs to wait. In the evening, goldfish indeed floated out, and Yin handled them exactly as White had said. In the morning, he checked the fish bowl, and the fish were gone. By evening, White arrived and said, "Do not worry, your beloved concubine has found a way to live. But a severely ill body cannot recover in less than a hundred days, so the meeting time is somewhat distant." After saying this, she took her leave. Yin said, "Your sister suddenly left, causing me to lose my appetite and sleep. If you would stay a little longer, it would comfort my thoughts. Why are you so resolute?" White then stayed. Yin hugged her, and White said, "Do not do that, the night is long like a year, how far does the emotion go? I think that everything should leave some room, after being satisfied, it becomes tasteless, it is better not to be as long-lasting as admiration." White saw Yin's poem about his affair with Ruihong and said, "The good work is overpraised, my sister's poem is too modest, none of them satisfy my heart." She then picked up a pen and wrote, "The celestial maiden is called the national beauty, one glance can topple a city. There is no intention to praise beauty, only because of old feelings." Yin said, "You are beautiful on the outside and wise within, truly makes one love and forget death." White said, "Do not die, do not let my sister blame me forever!" After that, they went to bed, in the bed, it was light but flavorful, compared to intense passion, there is another kind of pleasure.

"White said, 'I was first experienced the way of humans, and I feel that the more distant the relationship, the deeper the affection.' Yin replied, 'Indeed, you can be said to be skilled in examining things.' White then left, and after several nights, she would come once, and when she came, they would engage in intellectual discussions, always sleeping at midnight. Suddenly, after half a month, she did not come at all, and his thoughts were very intense. One night, a beautiful woman came from outside, and he thought it was White who had come, but upon closer inspection, it was not White but Red. He was greatly delighted and said, 'Is your body still as usual?' Red replied, 'Not only is it as usual, but it is even better. You ask me about this when you see me, it seems there are still the words "Ruihong" in your heart.' Yin said, 'One day without seeing you feels like three months, how much more so after three months, how intensely I miss you!' Red said, 'How beautiful it is in its freshness, seeing me as a mere hat or hairpin, still pretending to say such words to deceive people?' Yin was about to express his feelings, and suddenly White arrived. Seeing Red, she was startled and said, 'Why didn't you let your sister know when you came, thinking that if I knew, I wouldn't let my sister come? I have been a substitute for your sister as a wife, the hardships are hard to describe, yet you still doubt me with jealousy, I truly have an unspoken grievance. Please let us part ways forever, don't be like the sisters from the Shao family competing for the same man.' She left in a huff. Red said, 'Sister will not come again!' Yin, having reunited with Red, completely disregarded White."

Yin's wife, Da Shao, passed away. Da Shao had a son named Ru. Yin entrusted Ru to Xiao Shao, Da Shao's sister, because although Ru's mother had died, Xiao Shao and Ru's mother were sisters and would surely treat Ru as if he were her own child. However, within less than three months, Ru became extremely weak and pale, like a ghost. Yin thought Ru was ill, but he did not suspect that Xiao Shao had been harming him. When Yin saw a rope mark on Ru's wrist, he asked him repeatedly, but Ru remained silent. It turned out that Ru had previously spoken about his suffering under his father, and Xiao Shao knew this. At night, she would use a needle to pierce Ru's lower body, doing so a hundred times before stopping, which is why Ru did not dare to speak about it. Yin told Hong about this, and Hong said, "Let me go and observe him." The next day, Hong said, "The young master's suffering is unbearable! He is tied up with ropes and forced to sleep on the ground at night." Yin became angry and wanted to scold Xiao Shao. Hong said, "Sir, please calm down and do not be rash. Try asking her what she has to say." Yin asked Xiao Shao, and she said, "Ru wets the bed every night, so I use this as a warning." Yin angrily said, "Even if Ru wets the bed, he should not be treated like a dog or a pig!" Xiao Shao smiled and said, "Sir, please do not be angry. I regret having been too harsh in my discipline." Yin left in a huff. The next day, when he checked Ru's wrist again, there were no rope marks, and he changed from anger to joy. However, within three days, Ru became extremely weak and ill. He did not eat and slept continuously, unable to be woken. When he woke up briefly, he fell asleep again. Yin became very suspicious. By evening, Hong arrived, and Yin told her about Ru's condition. Hong said, "Let me go and see him."

He replied, "The prince's suffering is even more severe! He is tied up by his hair to the beam, forced to stand while sleeping." Yu was half-believing and half-doubting, remaining silent. Hong said, "Why don't you go and see for yourself?" Yu said, "The doors are heavily locked, how can I get in?" Hong said, "Try pushing the door with your hand, or open it yourself." Yu went, and everything happened as Hong had said. He became furious, quickly released the prince, and whipped Xiao Shao multiple times before leaving. Hong said, "You have made a deep enemy for the prince, be cautious." One day, Yu was napping, and Hong urgently woke him up, saying, "The prince is in danger, hurry to save him! Xiao harbors a long-standing grudge and plans to cut the prince into pieces to vent his anger, he doesn't fear the consequences." Yu heard this, became very afraid, and hurriedly got up to go. Hong said, "Wait a moment, the situation is already clear. If you suddenly appear, he will surely kill the prince. You cannot reach in time to save him." Yu said, "Then what should we do?" Hong then pointed to the air and drew a symbol, ordering Yu to shout loudly. Yu shouted, "How could Xiao do such a cruel thing!" After shouting, Hong said, "You can go now, but bring the prince with you, and do not hold a deep grudge against Xiao." Yu agreed and left. When he arrived, he saw Xiao with wide eyes and tongue out, holding a knife in his hand, standing facing the sky, as if afraid of being struck by a divine wrath. The prince was tied up on the ground, looking like a corpse.

Yin then took Ru aside and said to Xiao Shao, "With a heart like that of a wolf, what is there to punish?" He then led Ru away. Turning to Hong, he said, "This danger is indeed hard to guard against. I wish for Ru to stay with you, but I wonder if you would agree?" Hong replied, "It is indeed what I desire. But I fear my ability is insufficient to bear such a heavy responsibility." After a moment, she added, "I will entrust this matter to my younger sister." Yin said, "She will not deign to come, what shall we do?" Hong said, "To have her come is quite easy. Take a sheet of plain paper, write the words 'Ru Bai' in red ink, and draw a fish with a horizontal tail over the words with a black brush. Then, write the character 'lei' on the body of the fish, and on its back, belly, and tail. Recite the incantation three times, and burn it with fire. She will surely arrive immediately. The 'lei' character on the head must not be written, as this is the Five Thunder Talisman." She then orally taught the incantation to Yin. Yin said, "You lack self-control; this talisman can control your younger sister, and it can surely control you. Your sisters' lives are now in my hands." Upon hearing this, Hong turned pale with anger. Yin said, "Do not be afraid. My bond with your sisters is deep like the sea; how could I harm them? If this falls into the wrong hands, you will surely regret it deeply." Hong bowed repeatedly and said, "I will leave temporarily. You handle the matter yourself. When you see my younger sister, do not say that this talisman was taught by me."

By evening, the magistrate followed Red's advice and wrote and drew thunder talismans, which he then burned with fire. Not long after, White arrived, looking very different and moving awkwardly. She spoke cautiously, saying, "You have been served by my sister, which has comforted your heart. Why do you need me?" The magistrate replied, "The reason I hoped you would move your jade (a metaphor for yourself) is not for this." He then told her about Confucius's misfortune and his own trust in her, speaking earnestly. White said, "Your family has suffered this misfortune. It is only right that you come as soon as you hear about it. Waiting for me to be brought to you before coming is not only making you seem unkind, but it also makes it hard for me to explain my warm-hearted nature. My sister did not tell me about this." Upon hearing this, the magistrate was extremely grateful. Later, White asked, "Do you have a plan for this matter?" The magistrate said, "I want you and Shao to each live in a separate room to protect Confucius day and night." White replied, "Living together would make it hard to avoid danger." The magistrate suggested, "Can we live with Confucius in the garden?" White said, "It is not convenient to stay in a guesthouse for a long time. I have a method that is better than this. When my ancestors were alive, the house was originally two courtyards, later merged into one. Now we can still divide it into two. I will live with Confucius in one courtyard, and inherit my elder brother's property. You can come and go between the two, managing everything, isn't that good?" The magistrate agreed. White hired a teacher to teach Confucius, with the teacher instructing during the day and White teaching at night. Confucius entered the school at sixteen. Later, she married Confucius to Liu, who considered White her mother-in-law but did not know there was another mother.

Xiao Shao's son died at the age of twelve due to illness. Xiao Shao no longer had any children. He saw the Confucian scholar enter the school and marry, and he extremely wanted the scholar as his son-in-law. However, the scholar did not go to visit. Xiao Shao then told his own intentions to the magistrate, and said he would repent. The magistrate agreed, and Xiao Shao told it to Bai. Bai said, "It can be done." So they sent the scholar to visit him. The scholar was afraid, and Bai said, "Go, I will follow you secretly." The scholar saw Xiao Shao, bowed, and finished the ceremony. Xiao Shao ordered the scholar to sit, and the scholar's face turned pale, and his teeth chattered with sound. Xiao Shao then hit his own face with his hand, saying, "If my child sees me in such fear, I feel even more that my past actions were heartless." After speaking, tears streamed down. Bai said, "Indeed, there is a heart of repentance." So he ordered the scholar and his wife to visit Xiao Shao twice a day. Xiao Shao showed extra care, more than for his own children. Within ten days, the scholar had no fear, so he ordered the scholar and his wife to live in the same courtyard as Xiao Shao.

For several months, one day, White sent someone to invite Mr. Yin and Mrs. Ru to come. Mr. Yin said, "In the past, I came here for household matters, and after settling them, I was always urged to leave. Now, suddenly, someone is sent to invite me. How different the sentiments before and now!" White said sternly, "There is an important matter to inform you about. Is it that I stayed awake all night, hoping to spend a good evening with you?" Mr. Yin said, "Why are you so strict?" White said, "I dare not impose on you. I have trained your son and taught your daughter-in-law. I feel I have done some good. Now the whole family is harmonious and peaceful. What you entrusted me with is now complete. Please allow me to part from you forever." Mrs. Ru heard this and wept, saying, "Without my mother, we would not have reached this point. The kindness is like the sky, hard to repay in a lifetime. Mother suddenly wants to leave forever. It prevents me from repaying even a fraction of your kindness." She wept uncontrollably. White said, "Do not cry. Let me stay with you for a few more months." That night, Mr. Yin stayed in White's room. In the morning, he woke up and White was gone. He quickly got up, went to the garden, and Red was also not there. On the desk, there was a note, which was a farewell poem by Red. The poem read: "To repay the ten generations of kindness from the pond, we will marry as sisters. The sentiment of giving away jade pendants at Hanshan is equally deep. The friendship in dreams is even more sincere. Always working together to support the wings of the swallow. From now on, we will pass through the dragon gate. If my name is tied to your thoughts, year after year, peach blossoms will bloom in the garden."

Xubai Daoist said: Women are those who serve others with their bodies. To repay kindness with one's body, that is already the utmost! Yet Red and White do not stop there; they refine elixirs to cure the chronic ailments of virtue and serve like dogs and horses to protect the virtue of their descendants. Compared to those who bend their will to please others and intend to avoid disobedience, the difference is vast indeed. Those who repay kindness like Red and White are commendable.

To repay kindness with kindness, even fish know it, showing how deeply kindness can move people. Huang Qinxuan The legend of a fish in a pond swallowing a ring is an ancient tale. This story, when told, naturally arouses good thoughts in people, not just bright red and white, but forming a pattern or design with five colors. Ma Zhuwu The story of Gao Tang in the Three Gorges is particularly strange and beautiful. Shangyuan Li Yu, with careful notes Celebrating the Newly Confucian Scholar A person from the eastern region, whose name is not specified, is known for being rich and stingy, often wanting to be a guest but unwilling to be the host. During the Qianlong era, this person, who had passed the local examination, participated in the provincial examination. After the three rounds of the examination, there were still six or seven days until the results were announced. Fellow students from the same district wanted to have him drink but did not know how to do so. One person said, "I remember the first half of his first essay. You can pretend to be sick and leave to copy the examination papers. Write down the essay of the sixteenth-place winner, which is very good, and if you can remember the first half, it is indeed excellent, and he will not have passed by chance. If he asks about the essay, you can recite his essay to him, and he will be happy that he passed and will surely provide a small banquet." Everyone agreed, and they went to him one by one. The first to arrive planned everything and told the person, "Your essay is indeed your first essay."

The person pretended to be happy and said, "If it is so, you have ranked 16th in the examination, congratulations." Afterwards, six more people followed, and their words were all the same. Jia then believed it to be true. The person who had planned this came later, and the six people told him the same thing. The later-comer said, "It is so, my brother's writing, I have seen it in the examination hall." So they ordered Jia to prepare tea and snacks, hired a performance with clear and pleasant music, and rented a boat to tour Daming Lake in advance to celebrate. Those who saw and heard knew it was to congratulate the 16th-ranked scholar, and the news spread, eventually reaching the ears of the exam supervisors. The exam supervisors were greatly suspicious and said, "Not yet announced, you are already celebrating in advance, clearly there was collusion and leaking of information." When the list was posted, the exam supervisors told the chief examiner to remove the 16th-ranked scholar's examination paper and replace it with another paper. When the results were announced, Jia indeed ranked 16th.

The Vain White Man said: If not replaced, then A would not pass; if there were no advance celebration, then replacement could not be made. Some say that A's passing can be considered fortunate, but I believe A must have inherited merit and virtue from his ancestors. If the person being replaced had no hidden evil or wicked deeds, they would not have been replaced. Even those who are rich but stingy can achieve high success in the examinations. It is called stingy because of their close friends who enjoy drinking and feasting. Otherwise, how could the world's misers not all succeed in the examinations! Note by Li Yu from Shangyuan. NIE WENHUAN NIE Wenhuan was from Yongping Prefecture in Zhili Province. I have forgotten the specific town or county of his origin. As a young man, he entered school but was stuck in the examination halls, surpassing the age of 50, he also became disheartened. Moreover, he was poor, with no funds for travel, so the major examinations were approaching, and he had not yet decided on his departure date. Wealthy neighbors and relatives helped him with his travel expenses and urged him to hurry. After calculating the distance and route, he set off immediately, still not missing the examination deadline, but if he delayed, it would be too late. Thus, he quickly packed his belongings and hurriedly set out. After traveling for only three days, he suddenly saw an old tomb on the side of the road, with two young men and women kneeling and crying beside it, their mourning being unusually intense.

Nie then asked, "Why are you crying so bitterly?" The man replied, "You cannot share the worries of the poor. What good would it do to ask?" Nie said, "If I could share the worries, I would not know how to do so without understanding your troubles. Are you brother and sister, or husband and wife?" The man said, "I am Lei Fasheng, and this is my wife, Tang Shi. Due to a poor harvest and famine, it was impossible for us to live together, so I sold her to find a way to survive. The pain of living apart is unbearable, and I cannot help but be overly sad." Nie asked, "How much did you get for her?" Lei said, "Fifteen taels of silver." Nie said, "If you wish to reunite, why don't you just return the original money?" Lei said, "How can you say it's so easy? Money in hand is like food in mouth. The hunger in the belly is unbearable, and I had to buy food and fuel. I have already spent some of it. The original money is not enough, how can I return it?" Nie said, "I have thirty taels of silver in my bag. Besides the original money, there is still some left. Can you couple use it to survive?" When Lei and Tang heard this, they bowed and thanked him with tears. Lei said, "Where are you going with the silver, Master?" Nie replied that he was going to take an exam. Lei said, "If you give me the silver, it will hinder your exam schedule, and I would feel uneasy." Nie said, "Even if I go, I may not necessarily pass." He then gave the silver to Lei and left.

Upon arriving home, those who had helped with funds were surprised and asked him, and Nèi explained that the travel expenses had been lost. By the next examination session, wealthy households and various relatives and friends still gave him gold to encourage him, and Nèi prepared again and went. Having entered the examination hall, after seeing the questions he suddenly felt sleepy and fell asleep in the hall. In his dream, he saw a person wearing Ming dynasty attire who addressed Nèi, saying, "Are you tired, sir? The questions are not very suitable for you, but I have the entire examination's essays and poems, which you can copy." Upon waking, the essays and poems were still in his hand, so he copied them and was recommended for the provincial examination. He then set up a school in the capital in preparation for the metropolitan examination. When the time came, after entering the examination hall again, he dreamed of the previous person who gave him the essays and poems, which he copied and passed the metropolitan examination. With limited funds, he walked back home. At the halfway point, a dozen steps away, he saw a farmer placing his tools and removing his hat, rushing to the front and kneeling in the middle of the road, saying, "What brings you here, sir?" Nèi asked, "Who are you?" The person replied, "I am the sound of thunder. Previously, I relied on your great kindness, and fortunately, we have not been separated. I used the remaining money for livelihood. Relying on your great fortune, initially small but gradually growing, now the property I bought is enough for sustenance." Nèi heard this and was greatly pleased.

Lei invited Nie to his home and told his wife, “Our benefactor has arrived; please prepare tea quickly!” She asked, “Who is our benefactor?” Lei replied, “Without him, you would have had a child with someone else.” Tang heard this and paid respects to Nie face-to-face, as a wife would to her father-in-law. At that time, Lei went to the market to buy wine, while Tang cooked chicken and boiled eggs, tending to the kitchen, and both showed great hospitality. Nie sat alone in the room, seeing a scroll of paintings on the wall, tied with a rope and hung on a nail. Suddenly, the rope broke and the painting unfolded, revealing it to be a portrait of Lei’s ancestors, and indeed, the very person who had given him poetry in his dreams twice. He was greatly startled. Soon after, Lei returned, and Nie asked, “Whose portrait is this?” Lei answered that it was his grandfather, who had once held the position of compiler in the Hanlin Academy during the Ming dynasty. Nie said, “My dreams were all due to your ancestor’s virtue and grace.” Lei was surprised and asked why. Nie recounted the dreams in detail. Lei said, “This is how my ancestor repays the kindness of your family!” Lei stayed overnight and returned home only after seeing Nie off. From then on, the two families visited each other frequently as if they were close relatives. Later, Nie rose to the position of governor, bringing Lei along to his post, entrusting him with important tasks, and Lei also became quite wealthy. The Vagabond Daoist said: It is said, “There is a record of good deeds in the afterlife.” Observing this story, one can see that it is true. Nie gave Lei silver, preventing him and his wife from separating, which was a small favor; but by ensuring Lei’s ancestors had descendants, it was a greater deed. Lei’s ancestors, being spirits, could not help but constantly remember this! To repay them with writing is certainly not excessive.

Xiu Junfu passed the imperial examination in the year 1784 during the reign of Emperor Qianlong, and this is the same as the previous event. Ma Zhugu The ancestors of Lei were indeed worthy of being called the "Tying Grass Elder." This is due to Fangru Mr. Hu Yuanfeng's "Only Dust Talks" contains a section titled "Field-Outside Juren," which, when considered together with this, shows that those who are complete in body and spirit have the most outstanding virtue and the most extraordinary effects. Annotated carefully by Li Yu from Shangyuan Fúdé Hall The Fúdé Hall in Jinan is a financial center. Its buildings were rebuilt by the Tongcheng Silver House, hence the halls, rooms, and houses are the best in the market. High-ranking officials and wealthy families often use it to entertain guests. There is a wild scholar in the town, who is honest and straightforward and not bound by conventional boundaries. One day, while drunk, he passed by the gate and heard a play being performed inside the hall. He asked a merchant sitting at the gate, who told him it was a birthday banquet for Zhang. The scholar then bought four kinds of birthday gifts, wrote his own name on them, and sent them in. The merchant asked, "Whose birthday banquet is this?" The scholar answered that he did not know. The merchant said, "If you don't know him, why are you congratulating him?" The scholar realized his mistake, but the gift had already been sent, and he regretted it. Shortly after, a young man came out to greet him, dressed in fine clothes. The scholar looked at him and found him as beautiful as a jade ornament. Although they did not know each other, it was also inappropriate to ask about his name and surname. After entering, he saw the banquet arranged with officials and scholars, with men and women seated separately. In the central seat, an elderly man with white hair was sitting alone, whom the scholar recognized as the celebrant of the birthday. He approached to pay respects. The elderly man stood up and bowed, and the young man stood beside him and bowed along. Afterward, the young man invited the scholar to sit alone at the end of the table. The scholar looked at the other guests and did not recognize any of them.

Looking at the women on the right side, each was extraordinarily beautiful, surpassing the ordinary. Among them, a select few young ladies had faces as fair as fairies, standing out among the other women like a crane among chickens. The narrator frequently stared at them, and the young lady would sometimes turn to look back. Soon, the exchange of gifts and courtesies was very frequent, and the guests had not yet dispersed, but the narrator was already drunk to the point of stupor. He felt someone help him lie down on a couch and leave. After some time, his drunken eyes slightly opened, and light shone on his face. He wanted to get up and go home, but he stumbled and could not get up. The alcohol in his stomach suddenly rose, and he vomited messily, becoming even more unconscious. He felt someone wiping his face with a handkerchief and giving him fragrant tea to drink, saying, "The smell of alcohol is offensive, it is really unbearable." The narrator thought the host had sent someone to take care of him. Vaguely seeing it, it was the young lady he saw during the day who looked like a fairy. He was very happy in his heart, deeply regretting his drunken state, and could not hold hands to express gratitude, slightly expressing affection. He saw the young lady pouring tea into a large bowl, and if it was too hot, she would pour it into a smaller bowl to cool it, saying, "If the guest is so drunk and no one takes care of him, it is very unkind." She poured it many times, finally bringing the tea to the guest's face, and left without saying goodbye. The narrator drank it all and fell asleep. When he woke up, it was already early afternoon. He quickly got up and asked the innkeeper, who only knew the host's surname was Bai, and knew nothing else.

The son returned home, and his mother scolded him, saying, "You always go out to drink and feast, leaving the household nearly out of firewood and rice. You ignore it as if you hadn't heard, and are you not worried about starving to death?" Upon hearing this, the son could not bear the worry and anxiety. He then recalled the beautiful appearance of the maiden and her compassionate nature, and once again set aside his concerns about food, pushing them far away. At first, he engaged in deep contemplation, and soon he forgot to eat. Day after day, he eventually fell seriously ill. When the mother asked him about it, he told her the truth. She said, "If she is indeed a fairy, praying to her will surely bring results." So, during the quiet of the night, she burned incense and silently prayed for several consecutive nights. One night, the son felt someone shaking him, and when he opened his eyes, it was his beloved maiden. She said, "Are you also pitying me?" The woman replied, "How foolish, Master! With no boundaries in your heart, how could you become ill because of me?" The mother, hearing two voices in the sickroom, hurried in and saw the maiden's cheeks reddening, her head lowered, and she remained silent. Examining her, the mother said, "I still love you, do not blame my son for becoming ill because of you. You must find various ways to help him. Otherwise, not only will you be failing my son, but I will also be left alone and helpless." Speaking these words, she wept with tears streaming down her face. The woman, blushing, said, "Mother, do not be sad, the illness is serious but still curable." Upon hearing this, the mother turned her sadness into joy and asked, "What kind of medicine is needed?" The woman replied, "I have my own remedy, but you just need a cup of fragrant tea."

The mother hurriedly brewed a pot of tea and gave it to the daughter before leaving. The daughter wanted to give the elixir, but the tea was still hot, so she sat quietly waiting. The man asked, "How many doses of this medicine can be taken?" The daughter replied, "One dose will cure." The man said, "Such a serious illness, cured by just one dose, must be an elixir of the immortals; are you not an immortal?" The daughter said, "I dare not call myself an immortal, but I feel it is very easy to become one." As they spoke, the tea had cooled, and the daughter told the man to put the elixir in his mouth and take it with tea. After swallowing it, the man held the daughter's wrist and said, "I am deeply grateful for the medicine and treatment. But the wonderful medicine is in you; my illness cannot be cured by just the elixir." The daughter smiled and said, "I came here on strict orders and will not leave; I will also use my body to treat your illness." Upon hearing this, the man felt a refreshment of spirit and realized his illness had gone away half. Soon after, he fell asleep. When he woke up, his illness seemed to have disappeared, and the eastern sun had already risen, but he did not see the daughter. He hurriedly got up and saw the daughter helping her mother in the kitchen, wearing simple clothes and peasant attire, with a different style compared to her elaborate makeup. She served food and handed him chopsticks, being extremely attentive. By evening, their relationship had deepened. He asked her name, and she replied, "I am Lady Bai, the daughter of the host who wished you well earlier. I sincerely wished you well, and at that time, I felt a connection, and I didn't expect things to go this far." He then asked about her family background, and the daughter did not hide that she was a fox spirit.

The woman stayed at her husband's home for two months, when she suddenly wished to return to her natal home to visit her parents and requested to return in three days. The husband agreed to her request. After more than a month passed without any news, the husband longingly thought of her without limit, and his old illness recurred. The mother greatly feared for her son's health and renewed her sincere prayers. The wife returned, used medicine to heal him, and he was cured at once. The wife said, "I have been tormented to death by you and your mother! I indeed cannot serve you for life. Pray find a suitable partner early." The husband said, "I am as poor as if washed, who would lower themselves to marry me?" The wife replied, "Do you have any long-held wish?" He answered, "Yes. There is an adopted daughter of the Yang family on a certain street who is as beautiful as you, but her reputation and price are too high, beyond my means." The wife said, "How much silver is needed?" He replied, "Fifty taels." The wife said, "Fifty taels is already considered expensive?" She laughed and said, "As you say, I am also worth only fifty taels! Hurry and find someone to matchmake, and there is no need to worry about the dowry." After the match was made, the wife hurried to complete the wedding within ten days.

The wedding day was approaching. The woman gave five hundred taels of silver to help with the preparations and said, "We are about to part forever, and my true feelings cannot be hidden. My career, for your sake, has been diminished by seven parts out of ten. Now, my abdomen is moving, and I do not yet know if I will bear a boy or a girl. Please give the child a name now, so we can recognize each other in the future." The man replied, "After you give birth, why not entrust the child to my second wife, Yang, for her to raise?" The woman said, "That is not appropriate. A stepmother raising a legitimate son has its drawbacks: if she is too lenient, she will indulge him in everything, and he will often be unworthy; if she is too strict, the mother and child will be at odds, and the situation will truly be ominous. Only a kind and wise person can fully consider both emotion and reason in such matters." The man, hearing this, deeply agreed and said, "If you give birth to a boy, you may name him Fu; if it is a girl, you may name her yourself." After he finished speaking, the woman had already disappeared, and the man could not help but be surprised. With the silver in hand, it was not difficult to manage the joyful affairs. When the new wife came to the door, she was beautiful and pleasing to the heart. He then recounted in detail to Yang the previous encounter with the fox woman.

Seven years later, an elderly servant suddenly appeared to request an audience. The teacher asked about his purpose, and the servant replied, "I wish to invite you to teach at the master's house." The teacher inquired, "Who is the master?" The servant answered, "There is no male elder in the family, only a young master named Mr. Fu, who is willing to become your student." Upon hearing this, the teacher was startled and thought to himself, "Could Bai have given birth to a child?" Reflecting on the fact that there were many people with the same name, and considering the generous teaching fee, the teacher agreed to the request. After entering the school, Mr. Fu, being young and wise, could understand things after a single reading, and at the age of fourteen, he entered the school. The teacher, upon seeing Mr. Fu's appearance during an examination, realized that Mr. Fu was indeed Bai's son, but after eight years of separation, he had never met Mr. Fu's mother, and thus hesitated to acknowledge Mr. Fu as his son. One day, Mr. Fu's mother prepared an invitation for Yang, and upon her arrival, Mr. Fu invited the teacher to enter. The teacher saw that Mr. Fu's mother was indeed Bai, and the feelings of long separation were even more intense than those towards Yang. Bai said to Mr. Fu, "Your teacher is also your father; do not treat him merely as a teacher." Thus, the teacher, Mr. Fu, and his mother were reunited, and the joy was indescribable! Bai said, "This house was bought for two thousand gold pieces, and there are two other vacant houses nearby. Living frugally, with food and clothing provided." One evening, the teacher wanted to sleep with Bai, and she agreed. Upon waking up, the teacher found himself still on Yang's bed, and Bai had disappeared.

The White Void Hermit said: Those who respect others are always respected in return; indeed, this saying is true! When I wished longevity upon the White family, though it was said in a drunken state and was a lie, the person receiving the blessing considered it a gracious favor and felt obliged to return it, so the beautiful wife and son were all obtained through this single blessing. Thus, it is known that showing humility is not a futile act. Those who are arrogant, how can they be mentioned in the same breath? In the Fortune and Virtue Hall, there was a fox who held a grand birthday banquet, which is also a truly strange and astonishing matter. Ma Zhugu Yang Caoyun Yang Caoyun was from Caozhou. Holding a recommendation letter from the local governor, he went to the capital to serve a certain minister. His nature was straightforward and he enjoyed drinking, was not attached to wealth, and valued righteousness. Once, while walking in Maotou Hutong near the Zhenwu Temple, he found a piece of paper with a money note for ten thousand cash written on it. As he walked to Hei Zhima Hutong, he saw one person grabbing another and beating them, while everyone stood by indifferently. Looking at the person being beaten, he was about twenty years old, with a tall and robust figure, a dignified appearance, and he bowed his head to endure the beating without retaliating. Yang questioned them, and they all said, "He is a hired worker, and because he lost the money note, his master beat him." Yang asked about the date and amount of the money note, and generously gave it to him. The beaten man hurried to Yang and said, "I would like to hear your name." Yang told him honestly. The man did not express gratitude, but left with a haughty demeanor. Yang also did not inquire about his surname. ---

Yang Caiyun had lived in the capital for three years when he accidentally offended the officials and was expelled. Fortunately, he had some savings and bought a horse to return home. One day, while traveling, he missed the post station and the sun set in the west, leaving him still more than twenty miles from the inn, deeply fearing for his safety. Soon, seven large bandits lined up across the road, each holding weapons. Yang knew he could not escape and quickly dismounted, handing over the horse to them and saying, "All my belongings are on the horse, you can take them." One bandit still wanted to strip Yang of his clothes and harm him. Yang replied, "I, Yang Caiyun, have always been indifferent to wealth. Even if all my money is lost, I won't mind. I have no grudge against you, why should you harm my life?" One of the bandits asked, "Are you Yang Caiyun?" Yang answered, "Yes." The bandit turned to his comrades and said, "My elder brother once mentioned a close friend named Yang Caiyun. Why don't you take this person to see him? If it is indeed him, it will please my elder brother; if not, we can temporarily use this person as a sacrifice, cook him to drink wine, it is also delicious." The others thought it was acceptable. They then tied Yang to the horse and whipped it around. Around midnight, they arrived at an old temple in a mountain area, where six or seven people were drinking together. The person sitting above saw Yang and invited him to sit at the head of the table, showing great hospitality. Yang looked at the person and seemed to recognize him, but forgot why.

The man said, "Have you forgotten the person who lost a money ticket?" Yang then realized and asked, "May I know your surname?" The man replied, "During our previous meeting, you did not inquire about my name, and it is also inconvenient to reveal it now. You may simply call me Old Li." Yang then told him about his plans to return to the east. Li said, "The journey ahead is difficult, and there might be unforeseen events. I will accompany you, and you can be assured of safety." Yang was good at drinking and spent a long time drinking with others. Li said, "You may go." He then gave what he had saved to Yang and told everyone, "I will accompany the guest on a long journey and cannot predict when I will return. Each of you should attend to your own matters and do not wait for me." After saying this, he left. They arrived at the border of Shandong, and Li bid farewell, but Yang did not agree and firmly invited him to Cao, where he was warmly hosted for several days. During a drinking session, Yang said, "I have a piece of advice to offer; please listen respectfully. With your talent and appearance, wherever you go, you will achieve success. Why engage in such illegal actions? If they were to be exposed, it would be too late to remedy the situation." Li replied, "Your words are as solid and enduring as gold and stone, deeply memorable. I will depart to another place." Yang was very pleased and immediately gave the money received from Li to someone else. Li refused, and Yang said, "If you give something to me and I accept it, but I give something to you and you refuse it, it would be an act of pretense!" Li then accepted the money and left.

When Yang was young, he betrothed himself to a family named Jia in the same county. Later, the Jia family moved to Fan County. Yang chose a favorable day for marriage and sent someone to Fan to make arrangements, intending to have the Jia family send their daughter to his home. However, the Yellow River suddenly opened up, turning Fan into a watery land, and the Jia family had no news. Yang wanted to marry into a different family but neither accepted nor rejected any proposals. He served as a county magistrate in a certain county in Hubei for several years and was entrusted with many responsibilities. His purse was somewhat full, and he had no worries about descendants, but he often felt melancholy. Suddenly, lake bandits rose up, and the magistrate died a heroic death. His family was all killed. The bandit leader saw that Yang was cultured and wanted to marry his daughter to him. Yang feared that it would be a burden and a danger, so he hesitated and could not decide. The bandit leader became angry and wanted to kill him. Yang, greatly afraid, then said, "The reason why I hesitated is that I fear it would be a burden to the leader, not that I dare to exclude myself." The bandit leader then became pleased.

On the evening of their wedding, the groom saw his new wife's appearance surpassing the ordinary. She seemed older than her actual age, and he asked her about it. The woman said, "I am twenty-seven years old, still a maiden." As she spoke, tears dripped onto her robe. The groom was surprised and asked her, "Are you the daughter of the merchant?" She replied, "Yes." He said, "Do not be sad, I am your husband, Yang Caiyun." The woman did not believe him. Yang recounted the details of their engagement, including the dates and events leading up to it, explaining everything to her. She then changed from sadness to joy. Yang said, "I have long heard that your father is honest and trustworthy. Why would you do such a thing that would destroy the family?" The woman replied, "The person doing this is my adoptive father. At first, when the area of Fan suffered from a flood, I persuaded my father to go to Cao, but we were blocked by the Yellow River. We had no choice but to flee with my father to Lake Hu."

Seven years have passed. My father once wanted to marry me to a man from Lake, but I did not want to. My father suddenly died last year, and we were poor and unable to bury him, so we wept in the fields. My adoptive father saw me and pitied me, and he took me in as his adopted daughter, burying his father on his behalf. Until now, it has only been a month. The girl spoke, alternating between sadness and joy, as if they had been separated for a long time. I said, "In our current situation, if the heavenly troops come to subdue, it would inevitably lead to the destruction of both the good and the bad." The girl said, "I also have this concern. I am about to persuade my father to send us away first, then persuade my father to hide his name and identity, and escape calmly and quietly, so as to avoid disaster. If my father does not agree, I will think of another plan." When we slept, bloodstains appeared on the bedding, and I then believed that the girl had no other intentions. After delaying for more than a year, the army arrived, and the bandits were defeated, and the false leaders were all captured. I was also among them, and I realized that I had no hope of survival.

A group of military officials sat together to interrogate suspects. After a few words of questioning, they ordered the execution. When Yang's name was mentioned, one of the military officials was surprised and closely examined Yang's face. He then turned to the others sitting with him and said, "This is my closest friend, definitely not a bandit."
