Yang secretly observed him and realized that he was the same person who had earlier commanded him to be called "Old Li." Yang secretly rejoiced and then claimed to have brought his family to trade in the lake area, saying that he had been caught by bandits and had no way to escape. Li asked, "Where is your family?" Yang replied, "I suppose they are also among the female prisoners." Li ordered him to go and recognize them. Yang found Jia and went together to Li's camp to thank him. He also claimed that all his trading capital was buried in the city and begged Li to retrieve it for him. Li agreed and gave him a token of authority, provided him with horses, and sent five soldiers to escort him. Yang then entered the city, went to the old residence of the false commander, and took all the silver and gold he had hidden there. After leaving the borders of Hubei, he generously rewarded the soldiers and returned home. Master Xubai said: To pick up lost property and return it is indeed a minor virtue, but nowadays it is not commonly seen! Yang, through this act, found life in a desperate situation twice, and a good match also happened to come together. Is it not the will of heaven? Thus, we know that returning lost property is indeed a virtuous act.

Yang's encounter was extraordinary! Throughout, he met Old Li, which was even more extraordinary! Huang Qinxuan Do not say that good deeds are too small to perform; the Way of Heaven rewards goodness, indeed! Gai Fangru Zhao Gelaoren In Baiyu Village, Taian, the old residence of Zhao Gelaoren is located. It is said that after entering the school, he once studied at the lecture hall on Mount Aolai in the west of Mount Tai, serving as a disciple to the filial scholar Xiao in the county. Every time the examination period came, after breakfast, he went to Xiao's school to receive the questions. One day, when Xiao was out, he returned just before noon. After receiving the questions, he quickly returned. It was summer, and the weather was as hot as a furnace. The intense heat was unbearable. He wanted to hurry to the lecture hall to rest. Upon arriving, he saw a white fox sleeping on his bed. Yang was bold and did not mind it. He sat quietly to observe what would happen. After a while, the fox stretched a little. Yang thought it was a male fox and said with a smile, "What a beautiful sleep! However, sleeping during the day is like a piece of rotten wood that cannot be carved!" In an instant, it transformed into a beautiful woman, her eyes half-closed, adding to her charm. Yang was very pleased and hugged her closely. The fox said, "Sleeping so deeply that you didn't wake up, revealing your presence, I hope you don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "嫌弃异类" is a bit ambiguous and could mean "don't嫌弃异类" or "don't嫌弃异类" (Note: "

The fox said, "Today's assignment, hurry up and write, do not let personal matters harm public duties." The official replied, "It is not too late to do it tomorrow." After speaking, the fox suddenly vanished. The official hurriedly said, "Do not flee! I will write, I will write." He looked around, and the fox was still in front of him. The official then carefully considered the topic and began to draft. Just as he started a short lecture, he suddenly thought of the fox's thin, small, and cute legs. The fox said, "Is your heart focused on me? Using your mind in two ways, how can there be good writing?" The official said, "No." The fox said, "You love my thin legs, and you still say there is no distraction?" The official was overwhelmed with surprise and thus concentrated and thought deeply while seated at the desk. Every time a thought arose, the fox knew about it, and the official dared not have any idle thoughts. Before the sun set, he had finished the draft and quickly copied it clearly to satisfy the fox's demand. The fox said, "Do you read every night?" The official said, "I read until two o'clock, sometimes even until three o'clock." The fox said, "From now on, use three o'clock as the standard, do not expect to sleep until two o'clock and four minutes. And while you read, I will accompany you, the feeling of loneliness will disappear, will not your reading enthusiasm increase?" When it came time to sleep, the fox acted with great care and attention, more than with his wife.

When the gentleman was studying in the lecture hall, there was originally a hired servant attending to him. The fox said, "I am here doing nothing, just to see me drawing pictures, it's utterly uninteresting. Please send the servant home to serve, I will take over all his duties, or perhaps I can even better understand your wishes." The gentleman agreed. Indeed, the fox became very attentive and diligent, and the gentleman greatly appreciated her. One morning when he woke up, the fox suddenly said to the gentleman, "This afternoon, your esteemed teacher and friends will visit here. The cooking duties, I can handle, but there is no one to serve the dishes, what shall we do?" The gentleman replied, "If you want to send the servant away, there will be no one to carry out orders, who will be responsible for that? If not, I will urgently call him from home, but I'm afraid he might be sent out and not come when called." The fox said, "No need. Do you have any items for play or amusement?" The gentleman said, "There is a jade如意 here." He then opened the chest and handed it to the fox. The fox received it and threw it outside the door.

The host was anxious about the possibility of something being destroyed. Shortly afterward, a young servant entered from outside. The servant had a graceful and beautiful appearance, standing with hands hanging down by the host's side. The fox said, "Can such a one serve the guests? But do not speak to him." The host was delighted. Around noon, Xiao arrived with his guests. The servant served tea, and the hired laborers were not as efficient. Xiao was knowledgeable in the arts and techniques. During the meal, he looked at the servant and laughed. The host asked, "Why is the learned man laughing at the servant?" Xiao replied, "Ask the kitchen staff; they know." The host hurried to the kitchen, intending to ask the fox. The fox said, "What the lady has done, the learned man Xiao already knows. Do not ask about it again. Tell the guest facing west: 'The food you desire will arrive immediately.'" The host relayed this to the guest, who said, "I want a fine-scaled fish from the rivers and lakes." Before the host had finished speaking, the servant already brought the fish. The guest was surprised and asked, "How did you know what I wanted?" Xiao smiled and said, "Eat it, and do not inquire further." After the meal, the guests left, and the host saw the servant disappear.

Two months later, Xiao told the gentleman, "Your poetry and prose have greatly improved, but your body has gradually become weak. One cannot ignore this concern." The gentleman asked Xiao to treat him. Xiao said, "I truly cannot. You return and seek treatment from the person who caused the illness; there must be a miraculous method." The gentleman told the fox, and the fox said, "This is an easy matter." The fox then instructed the gentleman to lie face up on the bed, unbutton his robe, spit out a red pill, and press it on the gentleman's chest while rotating it. Initially, the gentleman felt extremely hot and could hardly bear it. Then the whole body felt cool, and the spirit suddenly became refreshed. After a while, the fox put the pill back in its mouth and swallowed it. This process was repeated for three days, and the blood and qi were revitalized, not less than usual. Xiao saw this and said, "If not a divine pill, how could it heal so quickly?" He then asked about the details of the treatment, and the gentleman told him the truth.

Xiao said, "The red pill that comes from the mouth is the elixir refined by qi; if one were to swallow it, their lifespan would rival the mountains!" The man returned home, and the fox said, "Do you wish to swallow my red pill?" The man replied, "The master said so, but I did not deeply believe it. Besides, the red pill is in your belly, how could I possibly eat it?" The fox said, "It is likely that you sought it but did not necessarily obtain it." The man and the fox lived together for more than a year. One day, the man drank with the fox, and he strongly urged her to drink wine. The fox became very drunk. The man helped her lie down on the bed, and then he saw the fox's mouth expel the red pill, which floated in and out with her breath, gradually rising higher and higher until it was three feet or more above her mouth. Suddenly remembering Xiao's words, the man quickly scooped up the red pill with both hands and swallowed it. The fox suddenly woke up and said, "Even if the Daoist practice is lost, it is not difficult to forcibly seek it. However, since you are a noble person, I dare not offend you, but I will return it in three years." The man feigned agreement. The fox then said, "If I lose this, I will surely die. I beg you to show compassion for our deep affection of a month, and cover my body slightly, so that dogs do not feast on it. Then I will be infinitely grateful." The man asked, "Where can I find your body?" The fox replied, "In the stone cave above Black Dragon Bay." After speaking, she vanished. The next day, the man searched for her, and indeed found the fox dead in the cave. He covered her body with broken stones.

This year, the official was recommended to the local examination, and the following year, he succeeded in the imperial examination. Returning home to perform ancestral worship, he took refuge in the Pu Zhao Temple at the foot of Mount Tai to escape the heat. After drinking, he suddenly remembered the fox's feelings and wished to see its body. Upon arriving, the stone seal was still intact. Upon opening it, he saw that the hair had fallen out, and the smell was like that of a mole. He vomited, and a red pill came out, just as it touched the fox's body, the fox quickly got up and hopped away. Daoist Master Xu Bai said, "The fox can kill people, how fortunate it is that you did not suffer harm? Some say that your good fortune and destiny are great, and the fox is not of the same ilk as those who seek to enhance their vitality. I think otherwise. When others saw the fox lying down during the day, they would have killed it, but you sat alone waiting for it to wake up, thus showing your kindness to the fox. If the fox were to repay you with resentment, even though it is a different kind of creature, it would not act so wickedly. It can be known that if one has no intention to harm others, others will not have the intention to harm you. The virtue of cherishing life is not a trivial matter! Writing casually can be entertaining, and it can make one's thoughts dissipate. — Ma Zhugui This fox was indeed full of emotion. The Grand Secretary was greedy for extending his own life, causing the fox to die, thus betraying the fox! — Ye Yunsheng The narrative is concise, but the commentary does not match the thoroughness of Ye Yunsheng's. — Fishing and Woodcutting Scattered Man The Grand Secretary, a Confucian scholar, has such elegant and graceful words, showing that writing the *Plum Blossom Ode* by Song Guangping does not hinder his iron will. — Shangyuan Li Yu, carefully annotated

琼华岛 Xiang Qingyun, a man from Qiongzhou, was exceptionally intelligent and skilled in writing and calligraphy. He was famous far and wide, yet he failed to pass the imperial examination. His father made a living by sailing the sea, and the family was poor. Xiang continued his father's business. Among the seven companions he was with, all considered him weak and appointed him to handle accounting, not allowing him to take on heavy tasks. Thinking that years of hard work would be lost in an instant, Xiang became frustrated and decided to take all his poems and writings on the boat, placing them in a separate cabin. He read them when the boat stopped, to relieve his boredom. One day, as the boat was set to sail well, suddenly a strong northwest wind arose. He quickly dropped anchor, but the anchor broke. He held the rudder tightly, but the rudder broke, and the boat suddenly swayed back and forth, only allowing it to drift with the wind. The wind did not stop for three days and nights. When the wind finally ceased and the boat came to a halt, the six others were all drowned, leaving only Xiang alive. He looked and saw the water in the cabin had risen halfway. Fortunately, the boat came to a stop at the foot of a mountain, where he could jump ashore. He quickly moved the books, grains, and other supplies. Just as he finished, the boat sank. Looking up, he saw a stone cave about ten or more steps away, though not deep or wide, it could serve as a temporary shelter from wind and rain. Xiang had not eaten for three days and was eager to cook food. But the fresh water on the boat had been washed away by the sea, so he was forced to cook rice with seawater, the saltiness of which was hard to swallow. After just two days, he was suffering from asthma and could barely stand. Suddenly, he noticed a damp mark beside the cave. Examining it, he saw a thin stream trickling, almost like a leak. He tasted it and found it very sweet. Using a bowl to collect it, in half a day it only filled two bowls, but as drinking water, it was enough for one person, and his mind was slightly eased.

琼华岛 Xiang Qingyun, a man from Qiongzhou, was exceptionally intelligent and skilled in writing and calligraphy. He was famous far and wide, yet he failed to pass the imperial examination. His father made a living by sailing the sea, and the family was poor. Xiang continued his father's business. Among the seven companions he was with, all considered him weak and appointed him to handle accounting, not allowing him to take on heavy tasks. Thinking that years of hard work would be lost in an instant, Xiang became frustrated and decided to take all his poems and writings on the boat, placing them in a separate cabin. He read them when the boat stopped, to relieve his boredom. One day, as the boat was set to sail well, suddenly a strong northwest wind arose. He quickly dropped anchor, but the anchor broke. He held the rudder tightly, but the rudder broke, and the boat suddenly swayed back and forth, only allowing it to drift with the wind. The wind did not stop for three days and nights. When the wind finally ceased and the boat came to a halt, the six others were all drowned, leaving only Xiang alive. He looked and saw the water in the cabin had risen halfway. Fortunately, the boat came to a stop at the foot of a mountain, where he could jump ashore. He quickly moved the books, grains, and other supplies. Just as he finished, the boat sank. Looking up, he saw a stone cave about ten or more steps away, though not deep or wide, it could serve as a temporary shelter from wind and rain. Xiang had not eaten for three days and was eager to cook food. But the fresh water on the boat had been washed away by the sea, so he was forced to cook rice with seawater, the saltiness of which was hard to swallow. After just two days, he was suffering from asthma and could barely stand. Suddenly, he noticed a damp mark beside the cave. Examining it, he saw a thin stream trickling, almost like a leak. He tasted it and found it very sweet. Using a bowl to collect it, in half a day it only filled two bowls, but as drinking water, it was enough for one person, and his mind was slightly eased.

After the meal, Xiang roamed and gazed around the cave. Half a li away, he saw a winding, narrow path that seemed to have been traveled by people. He then dressed in formal attire and climbed up by clinging to vines and climbing plants. Suddenly, he saw chickens, dogs, and mulberry trees, no different from what one might find in China. A short while later, several youths came out from the village, all dressed in clothing of the Ming era. When they saw Xiang, they were all surprised and stepped back. Soon after, an old man came over and said, "This is a person from the Chinese mainland." He invited Xiang to his home, prepared a feast, and asked Xiang's name. Xiang told him the truth. The old man asked, "Is your attire in accordance with the emperor's regulations of the time?" Xiang replied, "Yes." The old man said, "This place is called Qionghua Island. The island spans four hundred miles in both length and width. The island's inhabitants are only the Zhang and Zhao surnames, who have intermarried for generations. It is said they are descendants of famous generals of Shu Han. When Anle Gong (the last ruler of Shu Han) was exiled eastward, some of Zhang and Zhao's descendants hid by the seaside. Later, they declined and lived on boats, being blown here by the wind."

At that time, each left behind only one young son. Heaven sent two fairy maidens to marry them, and their offspring multiplied daily. By now, there are over twenty thousand strong men and women, descendants of my Master Zhang. Xiang asked, "Do you have a ruler or teacher?" The old man replied, "Yes. From among several people chosen for their even temper, they draw lots to appoint one as their leader, and follow his commands. If he dies, they will choose another in the same way, and it will not pass down through generations." Xiang then asked about the lightness or heaviness of the taxes. The old man said, "There is no such thing as taxes here. There are thirty hectares of fertile land, which is distributed among the farmers to cultivate, and the grain they harvest is given to the ruler. No other land is taxed." Xiang was about to ask more about the customs and people, when the old man said, "There is a strange event: I have a daughter who has come of age but is not yet married, and she has had a dream for several nights of an immortal who said, 'She should marry a man from Central China.' I thought that since Central Chinese men have never been seen before, the dream must be false. Now that you have arrived, it is a match made by heaven, and tonight she will be given to you." Xiang nodded in agreement.

The old man left. Xiang, a traveler who often suffered from having no place to rely on, now found himself married, secretly rejoicing that food and clothing were dependent on this union. However, when the lamp was lit, there was no sign of the expected ceremony. Xiang's heart doubted and said, "Did it sleep in the matter? Or did they choose another auspicious day?" Not long after, a beautiful woman in red makeup appeared and said, "Please, the official, go to sleep." Xiang did not know why, and stayed firmly seated without moving. The woman repeated, "The bed is in the inner chamber; please, the official, follow me inside." Xiang then followed the woman into the room, where he saw two bright candles and a fresh, red canopy. Yet, there was no one inside the canopy, and he was very puzzled. Soon after, the woman closed the door, and Xiang realized that the woman who had closed the door was the bride. He said, "Is the ceremony of the joined cups only like this?" The woman replied, "This is the highest level of ritual. Next time, the room will have no lamps or candles, and there will be rejoicing and meeting all night, but it is uncertain whether it will be beautiful or ugly."

Xiang was so amused that he spit out his food, saying, "What formalities will take place tomorrow morning?" The woman replied, "None, just follow my designation." When they went to bed, she appeared pure and innocent, without any shame. The next day, Xiang said, "There are still some items by the sea, please send someone to fetch them." The woman agreed. When the woman saw the book, she asked, "What is this?" Xiang showed her the book. She asked, "What is its use?" He replied, "Reading it can lead to success or fame." She said, "You should try reading it to me." Xiang then read the current literary works, following the rhythm and cadence, with a measured and varied tone. She said, "It is well-balanced and rhythmic, there is no song in the island that surpasses this." Xiang then read five-character and seven-character regulated verse to her. She said, "The words are different but the rhythm or rhyme is the same, it sounds even more pleasant to the ear." Xiang read in the room, and the woman listened to him all night without getting tired.

One day, standing outside, I saw a young man twisting the back of an elder, and the onlookers laughed and said, "So strong, yet still cannot defeat a child of ten or so." When I asked the young man, he revealed that they were cousins. I then saw a young woman walking with a sense of grievance, and someone tried to detain her. The onlookers said, "If she wants to return home, she should be allowed to do so; why detain her?" When I asked, it turned out they were husband and wife. I remarked, "Why are there no proper relationships like this?" I was disgusted by the empty room and the bare walls, so I wrote inscriptions on plaques and couplets to embellish them. Zhang happened to see them and said, "These are unique skills of China; my own collection falls far short of this." He turned and saw the inscriptions and asked, "Are these Chinese books?" I replied, "Yes." Zhang asked, "What do they discuss?" I explained, "They mostly talk about the five relationships and the five virtues, specifically filial piety, fraternal respect, loyalty, and trustworthiness." Zhang asked what the five relationships were, and I gave a brief explanation. Zhang said, "I have never heard of these before. Such a learned person should be reported to a higher authority." He then left. After some time, a middle-aged man arrived, with an extraordinary appearance and attire similar to Zhang's. I greeted him briefly. Zhang said, "Could you please explain in detail the five relationships you mentioned earlier?"

Xiang then carefully and thoroughly expounded, explaining the ten virtues within the five relationships, the substance and function within those virtues, the efficacy within the substance and function, and the sequence within the efficacy—covering everything that could be conveyed through words, one by one. The listener heard this and was greatly pleased, saying, "This is the way to govern the state and pacify the world. We should immediately build a lecture hall, gather the islanders, and instruct them. Although our island is beyond the civilized world, it will not be difficult to gradually attain a higher level of governance." Xiang replied, "Only by holding the reins of the state can one begin to govern; you only have the wish, that's all!" Zhang said, "This is the king or prince of the island." Xiang suddenly showed respect and performed a formal bow. The island king/prince said, "What is this ritual?" Xiang said, "In Huaxia, people treat each other equally in this way." He then explained the rituals of court audience and the ceremonial code. The island king/prince said, "Is the ruler of Huaxia so exalted?" He then elevated Xiang to the status of a distinguished guest. When the lecture hall was completed, he gathered the islanders in it and ordered Xiang to lecture and instruct them. Xiang first explained the moral norms and social ethics, and the listeners had some who danced with their feet and hands, and others who bowed their heads and wept—both because they had just heard about ethics and their innate goodness was stirred. Xiang selected those who were young and wise, taught them to read, and after ten years, held examinations to select officials. The customs became unified and harmonious, and the island also took on the atmosphere of the Central Plains.

During his spare time from official duties, Xiang often roamed around the island. He came upon a mountain that, though not very high, had a valley near a stream where there were many ginseng plants. He then sent people to collect them and process them according to regulations. His wife asked what use these were, and Xiang replied, "These are treasures of the Heavenly Dynasty." He also found a turtle shell on the beach, knowing it was unusual, he kept it as a treasure, believing that with these two items, he could become extremely wealthy in China. Eight hundred miles west of the island lies the Island of Show Clouds, whose population is equal to that of Qionghua, and whose people are strong and fierce. Suddenly, someone came and demanded that Qionghua provide a certain amount of silver and rice annually as regular tribute. If they did not agree, they would launch a punitive expedition. The island's ruler was greatly frightened and entrusted the matter to Xiang. Xiang replied to the envoy, "A certain amount of silver and rice cannot be prepared immediately; please give us five months." The envoy then left. Xiang then ordered the islanders to make cannons, bows, arrows, and weapons, and to practice every day. When the time came, the Western Island again sent someone to urge them. Xiang was angry and cut off the envoy's ears, then let him go. The islanders were greatly frightened. Xiang said, "If we do not resist and allow them to demand everything, all that we produce will be taken by them, and we will starve. Rather than die of starvation, it is better to die in battle. Moreover, a single victory can benefit future generations."

The islanders all realized and then honored Xiang as their military advisor, saying, "We are willing to follow your orders, even if it means death, without regret!" Xiang said, "To the west of the island lies a long snake-like valley, the only route enemies must take to enter the island. We can use a plan to capture them there." He divided the islanders into three teams, each with a command. On the fifth day, the people from the western island indeed arrived by boat, as brave as tigers and wild boars, their momentum was unstoppable. After landing, the first team of Qiong arrived first, engaged in a brief battle, feigned defeat, and retreated. The enemy thought they were cowardly and pursued them into the long snake-like valley with all their might. Once inside, they did not see any islanders; just as they wanted to leave the valley, rolling logs and stones blocked their path. Soon after, arrows rained down like rain and cannons roared like thunder. The enemy was greatly frightened and wanted to retreat, but the entrance to the valley had already been blocked by the second team of Qiong. The people from the western island were completely trapped inside the valley. After three days, their leader requested to speak with the main general of Qiong, saying he had something to say. Xiang arrived and questioned them.

He said, "If you are willing to lift the siege, I would persuade my lord to submit and promise not to invade again." Xiang agreed to this, and they agreed to leave behind their military equipment, departing one after another in an orderly manner. The enemy adhered to the agreement, leaving unarmed and by boat. Xiang then said, "Though the enemy's forces have departed, their leader will surely come in person. We should devise another plan to defeat them." He secretly gave orders, arranging daily feasts with the island lord to wait for them. Soon, the Western island leader indeed led all his men to come. Upon arriving, they saw that the border was unprepared and ordered his men to advance. Once they entered the valley, the islanders surrounded them from behind in the valley, attacking with guns, cannons, bows, and arrows. The Western island men rushed out of the valley in haste. Once on the island, the islanders formed up three miles away, and the Western island leader ordered his men, "There is no way to retreat; we should attack!" His men then rushed forward in a swarm, and dust suddenly arose, with half of them falling into pits and ditches.

The islanders then used arrows and cannons to attack from all sides, filling the pits and depressions with their efforts, and proceeded to bind the enemy with their own belts. They brought the chief of the western island to the military tent, where the general personally untied him and treated him with the courtesy of a guest, saying, "Since we are neighboring islands, we should be friendly and harmonious. Why did you trespass on our territory?" The western island chief bowed his head and remained silent. The general then said, "My lord wishes to send you back to your island, to establish everlasting friendship. Would you be willing to accept our lord's favor and obey his commands?" The western island chief replied, "If we receive your great favor, we will be willing to obey your commands and pay regular tribute." The general said, "Tribute does not have to be regular; it is sufficient to offer gifts as a sign of respect." They then released all the captured enemies, allowing them to return to their ships, waiting for their leader. A grand feast was held to entertain the western island chief, but before the feast was over, he stood up to take his leave. The general personally saw him off to the shore and watched as he set sail. All the surrounding islands that heard of this event paid tribute, established friendly relations, and respected his rites and teachings.

He had stayed on the island for thirty years, and both his sons had grown up. He often thought of his elderly mother, but the island had no boats, so he could not return to visit her. One day, he told his wife, Zhang, "If there is an opportunity, could you accompany me back?" Zhang replied, "I truly cannot. In the past, if a man in the island did not marry, his wife would remarry. Now that I have heard your wise teachings, I understand the importance of loyalty and righteousness. Until death, I will not change my loyalty, which is enough to repay your deep affection. But I have no children, so I will be alone forever; you have no children, and not having descendants is a great concern. You should take the younger son with you, leave the elder son here to manage the island affairs on your behalf. When my mother passes away, I hope you will come back." He agreed, and he gradually entrusted the affairs of the general and strategist to the elder son. One day, someone from the island reported, "There are two merchant ships currently, blown by the wind and anchored at the island."

Xiang was greatly pleased and then took thirty or more servants to the island. The boatman saw them and was greatly frightened, acting in a panicked and disorganized manner. Xiang then had his followers stop at a hundred steps away and went alone onto the boat. He asked about their origins, and they said they were carrying passengers and goods to Guangdong. Xiang also explained his intention to return to China. The boatman said, "I have heard that there is a Xiu Cloud Island ahead, and its people are fierce and evil, and we might be harmed." Xiang replied, "With my servants, there is no danger," and he gave two pounds of ginseng as a gift. The boatman happily said, "The wind will calm down a bit, and we can set sail tomorrow morning, please hurry and prepare." The next day, Xiang and his second son carried their accumulated wealth onto the boat. Zhang and his eldest son saw them off to the beach. Xiang raised a large flag on the boat, inscribed with the five characters "Qionghua Island Military Advisor," and he bid farewell to his wife and children with tears. They arrived at Xiu Cloud Island, and the islanders saw the large flag and each had a sense of fear, so they let them pass.

Xiang returned home and was overjoyed to find his old mother in good health. He used the gold and silk gifts from the island lord to celebrate his mother's birthday. Ginseng, turtle shell, and other valuable items were gradually sold in the market, and he became very wealthy. His second son married into the gentry, and all household affairs were entrusted to him. After a year, Xiang's mother died, and the burial arrangements were completed. Whenever he wanted to sell the ship and return to the island, the boatmen did not know where it was, and none dared to go. Suddenly, someone brought a letter, which upon opening revealed it was from the eldest son. The letter said that according to the mother's command, a boat was prepared to welcome him, and it also mentioned that after the father left, Xiu Yun was again being harassed, in danger of life and death, and hoped the father would return quickly to save the people of the island. He then told the second son, "I am going to visit your mother and elder brother; I will return in three years." He then left and never returned. The Taoist monk named Xubai said: Wandering at sea and encountering a storm, surviving alone on the island, this was indeed Xiang's good fortune. But in fact, it was not just that; the island is so large, and the islanders are so numerous, that when Xiang arrived, the customs changed dramatically, and everyone came to know the principles of propriety and morality, which were truly the work of heaven. If one knows that the matter originated from heaven, then one realizes that what seemed to be the good fortune of one person, in reality, was the good fortune of the twenty million people on Qionghua Island. When Xiang had not yet entered the island, fresh water was completely gone, and he had to cook with seawater, leading to severe asthma and difficulty breathing, making it as if there was no fresh water, just like there was no food. Inspired by the saying that the sea is salty and rivers are fresh, I add this commentary here.

Why is the sea salty, and why are rivers fresh? No one today has spoken of this, and even the ancients have not discussed it. I have often used my limited knowledge to discuss it. The water of rivers comes from springs, and the water of springs originates from rain. How can this be explained? When rain falls, half of it seeps into the ground; the water that seeps into the ground, half of it emerges as springs. Therefore, the water on high plains is scarce, while springs are abundant in low, marshy areas. This shows that the springs in marshy areas are nourished by the rainwater from highlands. Even for famous springs and great sources, which flow day and night, it seems unlikely that such vast amounts of water could come from mere tiny seepages. However, it is simply that their sources are far away, and they receive water from many places, but ultimately, they are not water that naturally exists underground and emerges as springs. Otherwise, how can it be that springs dry up during droughts and become abundant during floods? If rain is fresh, then rivers are fresh, and this reason is not clearly visible, needing no deep thought to understand it? As for the sea, it is quite different from this. It is like a heavenly pool, a vast ravine, with all rivers flowing into it without ever filling it, and the drain at its bottom never empties it. During the reign of Yu the Great, there were nine years of flooding in ten, yet the sea did not overflow; during the reign of Tang the Great, there were seven years of drought in eight, yet the sea's shores did not diminish. It accepts the tributaries of the Yangtze and Han rivers and the boundless surges of the sea. To attempt to create a theory to explain it is no different from trying to measure the vast ocean with a cup. Yet the theory of the sea's saltiness seems to have subtle principles that can be explored. The sea is the destination of all waters.

All waters tend to be fresh, yet the sea alone is salty. Is it not because the sea is so deep and vast? Even though the sea is deep and vast, countless rivers flow into it ceaselessly throughout history. How is it that the volume of water in the sea does not increase, and its salinity does not decrease? Let us examine ancient theories and the geographical situation to understand this. I have heard that the earth is suspended in water, and the sea might be the result of water seeping from the earth. I have also heard that the heavens and the earth are like a chicken egg, with the earth being the yolk and the sea being the egg white. Moreover, the distance from the South Pole to the North Pole is eighty million li, while the distance from the South Sea to the North Sea is only forty-nine million li. Is the area beyond forty-nine million li not entirely the sea? The distance from the East Pole to the West Pole is ninety-one million li, while the distance from the East Sea to the West Sea is only thirty-five million li. Is the area beyond thirty-five million li not entirely water? It seems that salt belongs to the color black, and black belongs to the concept of "子" (son or offspring).

The water of Zi, which is the water born by Tianyi. Being the water born by Tianyi, it is the water inherently present in the universe. Therefore, wherever the earth reaches, water carries it; where the earth does not reach, it must all belong to water. Water carries within the earth and connects outside the earth. Thus, the sky does not obstruct its left rotation, and the sun does not hinder its right movement. Moreover, it is said in the *Zhuangzi* that "China in the sea is like a grain of millet in a large granary." From this, we can understand the nature of China's water, and the nature of the sea can also be understood. Is the sea's saltiness still seen as increasing through pouring in? Is it still seen as changing its original nature through mixed throwing in? This is where the saltiness of seawater begins to be seen. The immeasurability of the sea, to create theories and establish arguments with limited knowledge is indeed rash. But I have my own views, and with special thoughts, I am just a scholar.

The discourse encompasses simple and clear principles, as well as the profound and subtle aspects that are hard to measure, all revealed in full. Truly, this is an unprecedented marvel in history. Scholar-physician Du Zhong, a native of the Jin state. His father and grandfather both practiced medicine, and by the time Du Zhong came along, he had further refined their art. He did not demand gifts from the poor nor impose excessive demands on the wealthy, truly aspiring to benefit the world through his work. One day, a handsome young man brought a heavy gift and respectfully invited Du Zhong to treat his father, who had been ill for over a month without recovery. When asked about his name, the young man replied that his name was Shen Shi. When asked about his residence, he said it was not far away. There was a small carriage outside, and he requested that Du Zhong accompany him there. Du Zhong agreed. The journey was unfamiliar, and they arrived after more than two hours. Upon arrival, they saw a man waiting at the door. After getting out of the carriage, Shen Shi said, "This is my cousin, Jiang." Du Zhong bowed to him, and they entered the guesthouse together. After tea, Shen Shi asked Du Zhong to examine his father. Upon entering, they saw a young woman of marriageable age standing by the sickbed. Du Zhong gave her a brief glance and found her beauty unparalleled. She saw Du Zhong enter and slowly left the room. After the examination, Du Zhong stepped out. Jiang asked, "How is my uncle's condition?" Du Zhong replied, "It can be treated, but his spleen is too weak. It will take some time, and it will not fully recover in less than two months." After prescribing a formula, Du Zhong prepared to leave. Shen Shi insisted that he stay, and Du Zhong agreed. Shen Shi said, "I will come to fetch you tomorrow morning, please arrive early." Du Zhong agreed. While drinking, Jiang suddenly clutched his chest and cried out in pain. When asked what was wrong, he said, "I have a root cause of stomach pain that occurs every few days. When it happens, it feels like a knife twisting in my heart, unbearable. Please save me, Master." Du Zhong said, "This is easy to treat." He performed acupuncture, and the pain immediately subsided. He then prescribed another formula, saying, "Take three doses in succession, and you will be free from this condition for life." Jiang was deeply grateful.

仲每视病，辄见女郎，后直出入不避。仲借久候脉息以偷视之。沈家每奉饮馔，江恒陪坐。一日，见仲俯首蹙额，遂问曰：“夫子若有不豫色然？”仲曰：“然。家务累心，不能忘怀。”江曰：“有何愁苦，如是耿耿？”仲曰：“同酌有日，不妨直言。弟幼婚邻庄邵氏，定期过门，暴病身亡，是以不能漠然。”江曰：“如君才貌，无难再觅良匹。”仲曰：“苦无佳者。”江曰：“君平生未见丽人耶？”曰：“见之。势分不同，见犹未见。”江曰：“君试言所见丽人里居。”仲曰：“远在千里，近则咫尺耳。”江会其意而笑曰：“君注意家母舅前之表妹乎？”仲不答。江曰：“舍表妹小字芳卿，年十七，德容兼备。” Translation: Whenever Zhong attended to patients, he would often see a young lady, who later entered and exited without hesitation. Zhong took the opportunity to observe her pulse and breath while pretending to check her health. The Shen family would always serve him food and drink, with Jiang sitting beside him. One day, Jiang noticed Zhong with a worried expression and asked, "Is there something troubling you, Master?" Zhong replied, "Indeed. Household matters weigh heavily on my mind, and I cannot forget them." Jiang inquired, "What is causing you such distress and unease?" Zhong responded, "There is a time for sharing wine; I may as well speak frankly. In my youth, I was betrothed to a family in a neighboring village, the Shao family, and we were to marry. However, she suddenly fell ill and passed away, which is why I cannot remain indifferent." Jiang said, "Given your talents and appearance, it should not be difficult to find another suitable match." Zhong replied, "Alas, there are no good prospects." Jiang asked, "Have you never seen a beautiful woman in your life?" Zhong answered, "I have. But our circumstances are different, so even though I have seen her, it is as if I have not." Jiang then said, "May I ask where the beautiful woman you have seen resides?" Zhong replied, "She is far away in thousands of miles, but near, she is just a step away." Jiang understood his meaning and smiled, "Are you thinking of your maternal uncle's previous cousin?" Zhong did not respond. Jiang continued, "My cousin, whose pet name is Fangqing, is seventeen years old and possesses both virtue and beauty."

The gentleman, please pay attention to this, as it truly shows your discerning eye. However, my maternal uncle is very strict in choosing a match, and surely the matchmaker will not succeed. Now, we can devise a plan to deal with it. "Zhong asked, 'What is the plan?' Jiang replied, 'At this moment, it is the right time to use you. Tomorrow morning, I will go to meet him, and you can use an excuse to not come. I will use this opportunity to make a proposal, and perhaps he will agree.' Zhong was very pleased. The next day, as expected, Zhong did not come. Shen was afraid and told Jiang. Jiang said, 'Mr. Du saw my cousin yesterday and asked me to act as a matchmaker. I did not have time to tell my uncle. His absence today, perhaps it is because of this?' Shen remained silent. Jiang continued, 'Zhong has an unremarkable appearance and a very clear lineage, so it is not impossible for us to marry. After a long while, Shen finally responded. Jiang said, 'If so, I must go myself, to report the good news and also invite him to come for a consultation.' Shen said, 'Yes.' Jiang added, 'I am seriously ill, so it is inconvenient to perform the ceremony. After recovery, I will send my daughter back home, and there is no need for a formal wedding.' Jiang agreed. The next day, Jiang went to Zhong to inform him of the agreement, and they returned together. When Zhong was attending to the patient, he did not see Fangqing, and his heart was very troubled, still not as happy as when he had not yet been engaged and could see her beauty and forget his hunger.

仲理患重病已四十多天，沈已能拄着拐杖行走。一天，仲来得很晚，诊视之后，天色已晚，点燃了灯，住在沈的房间里。刚过二更，仲准备就寝时，芳卿忽然到来，说：“我父亲突然起了歹心，要加害于您，赶快回去吧！”仲非常害怕，说：“深夜没有蜡烛，不熟悉路途，怎么能回去？”芳急忙递给他一把纸扇，说：“摇动它就能回去，不会迷失方向；而且拿着它走，速度异常快。”仲展开一看，只见扇上画着一轮圆月。接过扇子后仍紧握着芳的手腕，不忍心立即离开。芳说：“利刃在颈，还如此依依不舍，可以说是胆大包天！稍有迟疑就无法离开，比生离死别还要悲惨。”仲说：“从此再无相见之期吗？”芳说：“我非你不嫁，请耐心等待！”说完，催促仲回去，仲于是松开芳的手离开了。果然，他所走的路如同白昼，旁人看来却是漆黑一片，而且感觉像鸟儿飞翔一样，不久便到了家。回忆起芳的情意，内心铭感不忘。等到沈派人加害仲时，仲已经离开很久了。沈问家人：“是谁让仲离开的？”芳卿说：“是我让他走的。”沈大怒。芳说：“请父亲不要生气。”

The father had already betrothed the daughter to Zhong, making Zhong her son-in-law. Is there anyone who would sit by and watch their husband die without helping? Se said, "I betrothed you to him, but I never married you. How can you consider Zhong as your husband?" Fang replied, "Father betrothed the daughter to Zhong, but the daughter looks at it from the outside. This is deliberately disobeying the father's command, which is unfilial. Since there is the name of husband and wife, there must be the duty of husband and wife. Knowing the duty and not fulfilling it, this is unjust. Although you are a woman, you will not do an unfilial or unjust act." Se said, "What do you intend to do?" Fang replied, "Marriage him." Se said, "It will not be decided by you. You will also definitely follow the father's command for governance, not the father's command for marriage." Se became very angry and instructed the family not to let Fang leave the house, afraid that she might escape back to Du Zhong. Zhong's mother wanted to arrange another marriage for Zhong, but Zhong did not want it. After more than a year, there was no news, and Zhong also began to doubt. It was during the Qingming Festival, and Zhong returned after the ancestral worship. He saw two dogs biting one dog and felt very pity for it. He then picked up a stone and chased the two dogs away.

Looking at it, it was not a dog, but a small fox. Seeing its body covered in dust, he was about to abandon it and leave. The fox roared loudly, as if seeking help. The two dogs, seeing Zhong depart, were about to bite it again. Zhong, having no choice, wrapped it in a cloth bundle and carried it home, placing it on the floor of his bedroom. In an instant, it transformed into a beautiful woman. Looking at her, it was none other than Fang Qing. Greatly pleased, he helped her lie down on the bed and fed her rice porridge. By evening, he helped her take off her clothes and held her as she slept. In the middle of the night, the woman could finally speak, saying, "If not for your rescue, I would have died in the dogs' stomachs." Zhong asked, "Where do you wish to go? You have suffered such a great misfortune." The woman replied, "I have come specifically to serve you. My father is stubborn and does not follow my wishes. I wanted to commit suicide to repay you, but my mother pitied me and sent my eldest brother to escort me, leading to this difficulty." Zhong said, "You are truly a woman of integrity and righteousness!" After speaking, he wanted to engage in intimacy with her. The woman said, "My heart is anxious, and my whole body feels paralyzed. How could a person who loves me do such a thing?" Zhong then desisted. In the morning, she rose to greet her mother and performed household chores like a village woman.

Two months later, Shen Shi suddenly arrived. Zhong asked him about his intentions, and Shen replied, "My father's old illness has relapsed, and I respectfully request treatment." Zhong said, "To sever the marriage and plot harm, regarding you as an enemy, I absolutely cannot go!" Shen said, "If that is the case, my father's life will surely be in danger." Zhong said, "My husband is unworthy, even after his death." Shen said, "I pray for my younger sister, if you could come once." Zhong said, "That is true. Your affection, my sister, is always on my mind; if he personally comes, I will immediately arrive." Shen wept and said, "Two months ago, when I sent my sister back home, we encountered hunting dogs chasing her, and there has been no news since then. I suppose she has died in the netherworld." Zhong said, "If it is like this, even if Qin Yi were to be reborn, he could not convince me to go!" He turned away and entered. Shen left with tears in his eyes.

Zhong informed Miss Fangqing. Miss Fangqing said, "Are you a doctor?" Zhong replied, "With you here, how could I sit by and watch? If you indeed die, I want to stand and watch your death, to resolve the old grudge, can I still serve as a dog or horse?" The woman said, "How pitiful, sir!" Zhong said, "I want to go and see, but the path is unfamiliar, what can I do?" The woman said, "I will go with you." She then drew two talismans on the ground and each of them followed one. They felt it was very slow, but they heard the wind in their ears, and in no time they arrived. The woman went in first. The whole family was just in tears and sobs, but when they saw her, they all turned sadness into joy, and young and old gathered to ask. The woman said, "This is not the time for long explanations, the esteemed guest has come, you should quickly welcome him." Shen heard and hurried out, and indeed saw Zhong standing outside the door, agreeing to enter together. As soon as they sat down, Zhong said, "What I said earlier at your humble abode was just a joke, hope you do not take offense!" Shen said, "Since you have graced us with your presence, how fortunate! My father's illness, I still hope you will diagnose and treat with all your heart." The woman saw Shen, and Shen said, "I had suspected that you were not dead." He then asked about the details of escaping death, and she told him in detail. Zhong performed the father-in-law and son-in-law ceremony and then went to the sickbed to carefully diagnose the six pulses, saying, "This is because the root of the disease has not been removed, so it has recurred. If you had taken more than ten doses before this, there would be no today!" Shen heard this and was ashamed and grateful mixed together. Zhong stayed at Shen's house, treating him daily, and after a month, he was robust as before. Shen's daughter gave birth to two sons who were all noble, and she lived together with Zhong until old age, with no other strange occurrences.

The Taoist hermit Xubai said: "Unable to benefit the entire world, one might still actually benefit one place or region, which is a common saying among doctors. Broadly observing this world, who truly has a heart to benefit others? When faced with a modest sum of money, they decline due to an excuse, yet those who claim to help others, are they really like this? But Zhong, poor and not seeking a gift, wealthy but not being overly demanding, gained a mysterious wife and gave birth to a noble child, knowing that it was also due to divine retribution."

He drank with friends, got drunk, and staggered home. On the way through Huaxia, there was a very large solitary pine tree by the road. Suddenly, he vomited, fell down under the pine tree, and could not get up, falling asleep after some time. He woke up at midnight, seeing himself lying in a spacious summer house with green curtains, and a woman with a hairpin sitting opposite him on the bed, her eyes drooping as if she were sleeping. Looking at her, she was as beautiful as jade, and he thought about getting up to seduce her. When he moved, everything disappeared, and he found himself lying under the solitary pine tree. He was greatly surprised and hurriedly got up to return home. Every time he remembered the girl, he neglected his sleep and meals. He specially went to see her, touched the pine tree, and there was no response. He then thought about the girl he met after drinking, pretending to be drunk at dusk, so he passed by that place, lying on his back as before. When the stars were fully out, he was still lying in a spacious house. The woman with a hairpin said to Wei, "Please get up quickly, do not think that pretending to be drunk can deceive people." Wei was delighted, quickly got up, and wanted to take the girl. The girl refused and said, "Please be patient."

Wai took his seat, and the woman poured him a cup of wine, presenting it to him and saying, "You are a virtuous man, but your fortune and destiny are too thin. Therefore, I have recommended myself to assist you. Please drink this wine for yourself, and I will return shortly." After a short while, she entered holding a book, opened it, and showed it to Wai. Wai looked at it, and his name was listed on it, with a note saying, "Dies of illness at the age of twenty-three." Wai was greatly shocked. The woman said, "Do not be alarmed. If you add the character '百' (meaning 'hundred') above the character '二' (meaning 'two'), you can live to be one hundred and twenty-three years old!" Wai added the character as instructed. The woman held the book as if to hand it over to someone, and the book immediately disappeared. The woman said to Wai, "You have received the first of the Five Blessings, longevity, which is worthy of celebration and joy!" Wai asked about the woman's background, and she replied, "I am from the Fan family. There is no need to ask about anything else." They extinguished the light and went to bed together. Fan said to Wai, "From now on, please return early in the evening and stay overnight." One day, Wai returned at dusk. Upon arriving, he saw only cold waves in the air and nothing else, which made him suspicious. After a while, he found himself standing in a large courtyard. Fan came out of the hall to greet him and said, "Why did you come so early? It is still early in the evening, and the yang energy is still strong.
