Previously, Wei, a man from a neighboring village, saw Hao's daughter, who was extremely beautiful, and fell in love with her. He then asked a matchmaker to propose marriage, and the marriage was agreed upon but not yet formally confirmed. A local power figure, Wan, heard about this and wanted to marry Hao's daughter to his son. He forced Hao to agree to the marriage and set a date for the wedding. One day, Wei went to see Fan. As soon as he arrived, Fan said, "Wan's son is returning home drunk right now. On the way, he encountered an enemy and was angrily beaten. You should wait until the beating is over. If he is not dead, beat him to death, and I can make Hao's daughter return to you." Wei did not want to do this. Fan gave him a stick and encouraged him, and Wei finally left. It was dimly lit by the moon, and he indeed saw two people fighting. Soon, one person fell. Wei knew the fallen person was Wan and pitied him. He loudly said, "Do not harm others!" He held the stick and went straight to them, and the winner left. When Wei arrived, the fallen person also got up, and he saw it was indeed Wan. Wei sent Wan home and returned. Fan said, "Why did you not only not kill Wan but also save him?" Wei replied, "Not rescuing someone when they are dying is not the act of a benevolent person. Moreover, killing someone and then marrying their wife, I would become a widower for life, I won't do that!" Fan happily said, "You truly do not shame yourself as an upright person, doing things like this, you will eventually find a beautiful partner."

One day, Wei showed his worry on his face. Fan asked him, and Wei said, "My daily expenses cannot be sustained, so I have hidden worries." Fan also frowned and said, "What can be done about it?" After a long time, he said, "There is something that can make us wealthy, but it will harm my wife. When my wife is in dire straits, I ask you to pity and rescue her." Wei said, "The harm comes from me, and it is reasonable to save and rescue. Even if it is inappropriate to sit idly by and watch you drown without offering a hand." Fan then said, "There is a cave in Shanyin, as large as a dog's den. Inside is a female fox spirit who is good at sleeping and often sleeps for several months before waking. Choose a day of the Jiazi cycle, crawl in like a snake. Twenty steps in, it becomes spacious and bright. A copper mirror hangs on the stone wall, as large as a bowl mouth. Take it out with you. There is a beautiful woman in the mirror, which is the true form of the fox spirit. There are gold and silver treasures underground. Use the mirror to reflect, and even if it is in the underworld, nothing is unseen. Dig and take, and you can lead to great wealth." Wei entered the cave and took out the mirror. The mirror's magical properties were exactly as Fan had described. Fan helped Wei dig and take the treasures, and within a few months, their family's wealth was immense. Fan advised Wei to stop, and Wei agreed. One day, Fan said in fear, "The fox maid is coming. If she sees me, she will surely be very angry with me. If she uses force, I will find it hard to resist. I ask you to watch for any signs of my defeat, then hold your breath and blow hard at the back of the treasure mirror. She will fall."

The mirror reflects the image of the fox." Wei hurriedly said, "You must not take advantage of its vulnerability and harm it." Fan nodded in agreement. And he said, "You have a predestined relationship with the fox. You should keep the precious mirror close to you during the day and at night, under your pillow. When the fox sees you, it will surely be pleased to share your bed." He finished speaking and disappeared. On the third evening, Wei poured himself a drink and drank alone when the fox girl suddenly appeared, angrily asking, "Who made you steal the precious mirror?" Wei pulled the girl to sit down and poured her a drink to encourage her to drink. The girl drank several cups in a row. Wei said, "Please don't be angry, this evening is not the time to be angry." The girl laughed and said, "Is anger always on a specific day?" Wei replied, "No. You and I are drinking together, it's not just a wedding ceremony, how can there be anger on the night of a wedding?" The girl remained silent. During the drinking, Wei stared at the girl intently. The girl said, "Your ordinary appearance is in the mirror, you have been admiring it for months, still not enough?" Wei said, "The beautiful appearance in the mirror is like a flower in the mirror, wanting to play with it but can't, now I can personally pick it!" He pulled the girl to sleep together, and the girl did not resist. The next day, the girl still asked about the reason for taking the mirror. Wei explained it in detail, saying, "I don't know who she is." The girl said, "She is a ghostly immortal. Her original body is under the pine tree, so she often relies on the pine tree. You should go with a stick to the north side of the tree and hit it twenty-five times in a row, she will definitely come in the evening, I will personally kill her!" Wei was very worried for Fan.

Wei frequently struck pine and returned, and by evening, only then conversed with the fox. Fan suddenly arrived. The fox girl saw him and was greatly angered, saying, "The mirror that the good man took, a treasure of the noble house, the mountain god ordered me to guard, and does not allow lending to others. Because lending to the wrong person, they relied on it to become suddenly wealthy, no different from helping someone to do evil. Now I cannot avoid it, I must vent my anger on him to be satisfied." After speaking, she grasped a stick to attack, and Fan was afraid and fled. The fox girl chased him, and they fought in front of the courtyard. Wei was afraid that in the dark, the two would be hurt, and ordered the family to light many candles to illuminate. After some time, Fan could not withstand it. Wei did as he did, took a big breath and blew on the back of the mirror, and the fox girl indeed fell. Fan stood and waited without moving forward, and the fox girl got up and fought again. Wei saw that Fan was about to lose, blew again, and the fox girl fell again. She fell several times, exhausted her strength, and Wei persuaded him to enter the room. Fan also entered with him, saying, "The matter has reached this point, cannot be reversed, I wish to advise the young master to distribute wealth to the poor to atone for his sister's sin." The fox girl said, "There is only this one method to deal with it." Wei agreed again. The fox girl changed her anger to joy. Wei prepared a banquet and drank together to resolve long-standing grievances. The fox girl said, "I cannot serve as a wife for a long time." Fan also said that he would leave after a month. Wei worried and said, "I have two wives, soon to be a widower, not only is it difficult for me emotionally, you also are too cruel in your intentions!" The fox girl said, "If your fate is good, you will naturally have a beautiful match."

”Wei asked, 'Where is the beautiful couple? With this household, it is not difficult to remarry, but the one I marry may not have both virtue and beauty; if it is not good, it will be a regret for life.' The fox girl said, 'Choose one.' Wei replied, 'A woman of marriageable age does not leave the inner chambers, how can one choose?' The fox girl said, 'There is a precious mirror that can be relied upon.' There was a beautiful woman, and when you look into the mirror, the image of the beautiful woman remains in the mirror; if you look at another person, the previous image disappears. She then stood up and told Fan, 'I dare to trouble the fairy sister to do it, please make sure to bring a beautiful woman tomorrow, and we three will view it together.' Fan agreed, and the fox girl then gave the mirror to him. Wei wondered, 'The mirror was in my possession, how could she take it away?' He felt it, and it was no longer there. The next day, Fan reported back. The fox girl looked into the mirror and found the person not satisfactory, saying, 'Even I find it displeasing, how much more so the gentleman?' Wei looked at it and indeed nodded his head and remained silent. After looking at several people, Wei did not think any of them were suitable. Later, when a beautiful woman appeared in the mirror, the fox girl saw her and said, 'I have found her! The gentleman will surely be overwhelmed and lose his senses!' Wei saw her and said, 'With this, I can no longer seek pity from the two of you. Whose daughter is this?' Fan replied, 'A gentleman named Zhong, who lives in the same prefecture, more than one hundred sixty miles away.' Wei quickly troubled the matchmaker to arrange the marriage. When the wedding was about to take place, Fan and the fox girl both left.

At the age of sixty, Wei fell seriously ill, and medical treatment proved ineffective. Realizing that his allotted lifespan was long, he wondered why he would not recover and concluded that there must be another reason. He then told his wife Zhong, "You should call my legitimate wife Fan here!" Zhong asked, "Where should I call her from?" Wei instructed her to strike a pine tree at a certain place and tell it the number of times to strike it. Zhong hurriedly arranged for a carriage and went to fetch Fan, thinking, "Striking the pine tree will bring Fan here, and Fan must be afraid of the strike." She doubled the number of strikes. Upon returning, she told Wei, "Twenty-five strikes, half of the large derivation, she has already been shocked beyond endurance. Now, striking her with the number of the large derivation, she will surely resent you." Zhong replied, "I am not afraid!" Soon after, Fan arrived and angrily asked Wei, "Who did you send to strike the pine tree?" Before Wei could answer, Zhong responded, "I did!" Fan became furious. Zhong pulled her to sit down and said, "Let me tell you: a wife, when alive, is the lifelong hope of her husband; when dead, she leaves behind half a life's regret. Wearing mourning clothes for her is equal to mourning for one's in-laws and parents; being a widow is even more pitiable than being a widower or an orphan. Since you are among the ghosts and immortals, you must know about my husband's illness. Knowing it and yet ignoring it, you lack both will and virtue, and your actions are neither proper nor just. Even if you attack with drums and bells, is it not a fault? And still, you blame others?" Fan, upon hearing this, stood up and spoke respectfully, "It is my fault!" She said to Wei, "Your illness cannot be avoided, but only by being generous and charitable can it be cured." Wei replied, "I have been donating money to help others for over thirty years."

Fan said, "Do not dwell on the past; it is necessary to expend great effort and donate tens of thousands of taels of silver." Wei replied, "If one wishes to donate, one must first recover from illness." Fan said, "If you hold this heart, you can be healed immediately." Wei said, "Certainly as you say." Indeed, the illness was cured within ten days. The silver tax (地丁银) of Huayin amounted to more than thirty-six thousand taels. At that time, there was a famine, and the people suffered from taxes and corvée labor. Wei met with the magistrate of the county and said he was willing to pay the entire county's taxes on behalf of the people, and would additionally give the magistrate some silver. The magistrate was pleased and agreed. After completing the payment, Fan was delighted and said, "This act not only ensures that the noble descendants' blessings and fortune cannot be measured, but also allows the fox sister to rely on it to be forgiven for her past sins!" Shortly after, the fox girl indeed arrived and thanked Fan, saying, "My sister's escape from heavenly punishment is certainly because my husband spends money like dirt, but it is all due to my sister's encouragement and urging."

Wei said, "I have something that is constantly on my mind, I wonder if you can help me with it?" The fox woman asked, "What is it?" Wei replied, "It is an act of extreme filial impiety." The fox woman said, "It is easy to do." She examined the maidservants and found one who was extremely ugly. The fox woman said, "This maid is suitable for bearing a son, if you take her as a wife, you will surely have a good heir." Wei found her ugly. The fox woman said, "If you take her as a wife, it will also somewhat reduce the enjoyment of having beautiful wives and concubines." She told Fan, "We can act now!" After speaking, they all vanished, and did not return. Wei, nearing seventy years old, still had no son, and had no choice but to take the ugly woman as a wife. Indeed, he had a son. His descendants did not change their ways, and enjoyed their blessings throughout generations. In modern times, people especially value scholars. If poor scholars or those who have lost their teaching positions or are traveling for study happen to visit his home, they are treated with the courtesy due to guests, provided with food and clothing, and the politeness does not diminish.

The Virtual White Man said: In the world, those who acquire wealth from external sources but cannot enjoy it for long are not necessarily people with thin fortune or fate; it may be due to their extreme attachment to wealth. Wealth that is not rightfully earned should be shared with others, not kept for oneself. If one keeps it, one violates what the Creator abhors, and how could one enjoy it for long? If Wei did this, it would be a way to properly handle wealth! His descendants highly value scholars, which is particularly commendable. The words of the Virtual White Man are indeed true! People who pass the imperial examinations but do not hold an official position, or those who accumulate a lot of money, are not unremarkable for a time. But sometimes their wealth fades away by the time it reaches their descendants, or it runs out during their own lifetime; there is no other reason; it is because of improper conduct. Wei's fortune was gained through proper means. The magistrate of Huayin accepting bribes will certainly not be able to enjoy his wealth for long! —— Carefully annotated by Li Yu of Shangyuan How refreshing and thorough it is! The story is better than *Lotus Fragrance Transmission*, and the writing is as smooth and well-coordinated as a team of three horses pulling a chariot. —— Recorded by Fisherman and Woodcutter Wanderer *Wisdom Record* (Volume 2) by Xie Jian of the Qing Dynasty

The Scattering Flower Celestial Maiden (Draft Version: "Scattering Flower Celestial Lady") Filsial son Wu Qingtai, a scholar from Taiping in Anhui, was born into a family with a long tradition of integrity. His grandfather, while in office, was upright and left little wealth behind him, which was exhausted upon his death. His father, raised in an official family, was not accustomed to diligence and frugality, and the family's fortunes gradually declined. By the time Wu Qingtai became a scholar, the family was so poor that they barely had enough for two meals a day. With no other choice, he sold household items to start a small business, earning only a meager profit that was insufficient to support his parents. He heard that there was a job copying documents, so he applied for it. During the day, he managed his small trade, and at night, he worked as a copyist, using the money he earned to buy food. The family rationed their food, with Wu Qingtai eating coarse grains while his parents received the better food. Occasionally, his parents would give him the leftover food, which he pretended to eat but saved for his parents. He often lamented, "My parents have endured countless hardships, and it is difficult for them to have two meals a day." The family's daily expenses were insufficient, and his parents would often lament throughout the night. Wu Qingtai, upon hearing this, felt as if needles were piercing his heart, and he deeply regretted not living up to his parents' expectations. At that time, there was a famine, and the provincial governor issued an order to provide relief. A classmate suggested, "Why don't you go and get it?" Wu Qingtai replied, "To travel over twenty miles for a single bowl of rice is truly unbearable. Moreover, going to collect relief would interfere with other tasks. Even if I am not hungry, what about my parents who are starving?" The classmate said, "I have heard that the provincial governor is very considerate of scholars. He does not allow them to go to the relief center. Instead, he has them register at their school, and relief is distributed according to a list." Wu Qingtai was half-doubtful. The classmate said, "There is a notice posted by the provincial governor. You can come to my study to see it." Wu Qingtai went, and the notice read: "To distinguish and assist those in urgent need to demonstrate preferential treatment."

Official Notice to Scholars Respected scholars, From ancient times, officials have always treated scholars with courtesy and respect. It is essential that each of you cherishes your own reputation and conduct yourselves with dignity. Scholars are like precious treasures, valued as highly as treasures worth cities. Even if your situation is as poor as a house as empty as a gong, you should not lightly regard the begging bowl. Chickens and cranes should not be equated, nor should slow horses and swift horses be treated equally. Now, as we distribute relief, it is important to differentiate and provide appropriate assistance. Recently, we have heard of poor scholars with eight mouths crying for food, causing their households to suffer criticism. Unable to have two meals in a row, they want to borrow but have no way. Wanting to receive even a small amount of help, their faces are quite that of heroes. Carrying a bag on their back, they feel like beggars. Like hungry swans in a marsh, getting a spoonful of water to save a fish, although it is said to be a gift from heaven, it feels that the dignity of scholars is being tarnished. Officials and scholars, the department sincerely feels heartbroken. The act of summoning students, you scholars should feel ashamed. It is appropriate to issue a notice to inform you: not born in good times, the appearance of covering one's face is pitiable. Poverty is not a disease, the demeanor of scholars still exists. When cooking is interrupted and the bag is empty, help can be received. Matters like cold food and cold wine, how can one bear the shame? After this notice, students do not need to go to the relief factory again. Each should register at their school to be issued relief according to the register. So that the distribution of relief is differentiated, and the reputation of scholars is not tarnished. Real benefits are still received, and people do not look down on scholars. The example of grace in the granary can support studying by the light of fireflies. Expecting generous treatment in the music of flutes and reeds, awaiting the banquet of the deer calling. The reason why the department distinguishes and helps those in urgent need to show preferential treatment is for this reason. Especially ordered. The student read it and was very happy, then reported to his parents and went to the province.

When he collected the money, the sun was already slanting, so he urgently bought rice and returned home. Joyfully, he said, "To eat to my fill with my parents for several days is also a rare blessing in recent years." Since morning without eating, he was happy and forgot his hunger. Walking midway, he suddenly felt intestinal rumbling, painful and unable to walk. By the roadside under the willow shade, there was a stone bench, and he temporarily rested a little. A young girl arrived, also resting under the tree, with beautiful eyebrows and feet, unparalleled in grace and beauty throughout the ages. He also had no intention to look at her. The girl then looked at him with her eyes and smiled, taking out gold from her sleeve and placing it on the stone, saying, "Can carry this and return quickly. Still wait for you here after five days." She disappeared in an instant. He found it strange. Five days later, he went to the tree, and the girl indeed waited there. He followed the girl to walk together, and after a few steps, felt his feet not touching the ground, swift and urgent like flying. Soon, he arrived at a mansion, spacious like a hall, with bed, table, blanket, and curtain all prepared.

The woman then took wine and shared it with the man, holding the cup and urging him to drink. Their emotions were joyful and harmonious, and they did not realize they were drunk. She helped him lie down on the bed, and they lay down together on the same pillow. In the midst of their intimacy, it was as if they were in a dream of yellow millet. By the next morning, the man asked about the woman’s background. She replied, “I am the Heavenly Flower Princess. I admired your filial piety, so I offered myself to share your burdens.” From then on, all their food and clothing came from her. After three years, their parents heard about them and worried that the woman would not bear children. The man turned to the woman, pleading, “You are truly asking me to do something difficult. This matter cannot be forced. Moreover, the power does not lie with me. Why should I ask others to do something that I should do myself?” Soon after, the woman became pregnant and gave birth to a son, whom they named Tao. She said, “This son will later cause trouble involving life and death.” At the age of fifteen, they arranged his marriage. Two years later, the man’s parents passed away one after another. After the funeral, the woman gave Tao a paper packet and said, “If there is a great calamity, you may open it and look inside.” Tao cherished it. In the morning, when they paid their respects to their parents, the two of them were nowhere to be found, leaving only anxiety and grief.

Twenty years later, Tao accidentally caused someone's death and intended to flee. Suddenly remembering a relative's instruction, he quickly opened the sealed envelope to read it. It said, "The magistrate of this county, Wu Shu, is actually your younger brother." Tao gladly surrendered himself to the authorities to confess his crime. The magistrate considered it an accidental injury and allowed him to pay a fine to be released. After returning home, the magistrate came to visit and said that he was from Daxing and became a Jinshi (high-ranking scholar) at the age of 17, and was selected by the Ministry of Personnel to serve here. Your father said that your younger brother and you are full siblings, so he came to recognize you. Tao quickly went with Wu Shu to the magistrate's office to see your parents, but they had already passed away. The brothers then realized that their parents were immortals. Wu Shu, because his ancestral tomb was in Tai, moved there and lived with Tao.

The Censor-in-Chief demonstrated relief and favor to the scholars, an act so outstanding that it naturally deserves to be passed on. — Ma Zhugu Dog's Grave Slope (the manuscript version is "Loyal Dog") In the north of Mount Emei in Changqing, there is a Dog's Grave Slope, on which stands a grave of a loyal dog. There was a certain Zhao who lived in the outskirts of Changqing and traded in the city. One day, he planned to increase his capital, arrived home, sold several acres of land, and got two hundred gold coins. At dawn, he carried the silver and rode a donkey to the city, unaware that his family's dog was following him. When he reached the slope, he suddenly needed to relieve himself, tied the donkey to a tree, and did so. Afterward, he got back on the donkey and continued, only to realize upon arrival that the silver was lost. He hurried to return to look for it, but it was already dusk. He thought that losing something in a busy street, how could it possibly be found again? The next day, he sent someone to look for it, but found nothing, so he consoled himself with the idea of breaking the loss to avoid disaster. After more than a month, Zhao returned and saw the dog dead on the slope, its fur and skin already deteriorated. He kicked it with his foot, and the bag came out. Upon opening it, the white gold was still there, and he realized that the dog had been guarding the lost gold and died of hunger. Zhao then buried the dog on the slope, and the slope also became known by the dog's name.

Chaste and Loyal Woman Shou Rushan was a person from the state of Jin. He was wealthy with land and property, having one son and one grandson. Not long after his wife died, his son and daughter-in-law also passed away in quick succession, leaving only his grandson named Deren, who had not yet married. After completing the mourning rites, they were planning to choose an auspicious date for the wedding, but Deren died again. Upon hearing of the divination, Bian Shi, who was engaged to Deren, wished to rush to the funeral, but her mother did not want her to go, saying, "There is no one left in your family to rely on. If you mourn for him, it will be difficult to find a suitable match." She replied, "‘Mother, heaven, do not understand me!’ Until death, I will remain faithful, as this has always been my intention." She wept uncontrollably. Her mother said, "Wait until the funeral date and I will accompany you." She said, "When I hear of the death, I should go. Why wait!" She went to Shou's house and, in front of the spirit tablet, she wept bitterly, her grief being extraordinary. At first, people thought her reaction was excessive, but soon they all felt her plight was pitiful. By evening, her mother tried to persuade her to return. She said, "This is my family, where shall I go?" Despite repeated attempts to persuade her, she refused to listen, and her mother returned home. The funeral was still more than a month away. During the day, she managed the funeral arrangements, and at night, she slept beside the coffin. In her spare time, she called out to heaven and wept, until her tears ran out and were replaced by blood. The neighbors heard of her grief and were all moved to tears. When it came time for the burial, as she supported the coffin to the grave, her face showed deep sorrow and her crying was heart-wrenching. All who saw her could not help but feel sorrowful.

The *Shou* family had been single-line for many generations, with no one to adopt. The mother was deeply anxious about not having descendants. Suddenly, she thought of her elderly grandfather, who was still healthy, and decided to buy a beautiful concubine to serve him. After about two years, a son was born, and he was named *Jing*. When *Jing* was still in swaddling clothes, the concubine suddenly died. The mother then hired a wet nurse to breastfeed him and personally took care of him, not lightly entrusting him to others. She raised and nurtured him with the same diligence as a mother would. When *Jing* grew older, she hired a teacher to educate him in reading. *Jing* was intelligent, able to remember what he saw, and at sixteen, he won the championship in the imperial examination, thus marrying the daughter of a scholar-official. After the grandfather passed away, *Jing* became heartless and unrestrained, loving only to wander aimlessly. The mother tried to advise him, but *Jing* considered himself a younger uncle and ignored her advice. Within a year, almost all the family wealth was exhausted. The mother wept day and night, not drinking even a drop of water for three days. *Jing* was moved and repented, coming to his mother's bedside to earnestly speak about correcting his mistakes. He repeated his words several times, but the mother continued to weep uncontrollably. *Jing* then knelt before her bed. *Jing*'s wife, *Shang*, said, "Uncle is kneeling, the virtuous niece-in-law should be a bit more lenient." Upon hearing this, the mother also got up and knelt on the bed, saying, "When my niece-in-law rushed to the funeral, there was no one left to perform ancestral rites in our family."

In hopes of a slim chance, they welcomed the birth of a younger brother. Unfortunately, their grandmother had passed away again, and at that time, the niece-in-law took care of her younger brother, tending to him from infancy, protecting him day and night. Was this out of self-sacrifice? No, it was because the responsibility to bring glory to the ancestors and benefit future generations rested entirely on the younger brother. Now, he suddenly changed his usual conduct, not pursuing scholarly or official careers. The niece-in-law’s expectations for her younger brother had become like a distant dream, and she felt as if she could not rest in peace after death, nor face her ancestors with shame in the afterlife.” After speaking, she wept again. When Shang heard this, he could not help but join in her tears. Shang advised, “The niece-in-law has not eaten for three days, and you have been kneeling with your younger brother for a long time. Please take a break.” The niece-in-law then wiped her tears and had Shang help Jing stand up. Jing instructed Shang to serve food, but the niece-in-law refused to eat. Jing and his wife urged her repeatedly, and she finally ate. Due to her excessive grief, she fell ill and could not recover, and Jing cared for her as if she were his mother, and she recovered after more than a month. Later, when Jing became negligent, the niece-in-law would cry and refuse to eat. Jing was afraid of disappointing her and became determined to advance, achieving repeated success in the imperial examinations and rising to the rank of Censor. He submitted a memorial to the emperor expressing his feelings, and the niece-in-law was posthumously honored with the title of “Shao Shu Ren” and received a tablet inscribed with the virtue of filial piety and chastity. She died peacefully at over seventy years old, and Jing mourned her deeply, as if burying a close relative.

Strict adherence to great principles, profound and poignant emotions. Repeatedly reading it makes one cry. Ma Zhuwu The Cow Demon In the deep mountain village east of the Shanhai Pass, farmers keep oxen for plowing fields. After the farming season ends, they drive the cattle to the mountains, where a hired person watches over them. When the cattle are in the mountains, they are most afraid of being injured by tigers. Whenever a tiger comes, one of the cows steps forward to confront it. This is a common occurrence, nothing unusual, but the cow cannot defeat the tiger. The hired person, employed by the villagers, drives the cattle into the mountains, gradually going deeper. One morning, as he was about to lead the cattle to graze, a tiger suddenly appeared. The hired person was greatly startled and could not help but cry out, "The tiger has come!" Before he finished speaking, a bull leaped out and rushed directly at the tiger. There was a tree near him, so he climbed up to watch. He saw the tiger showing its claws and teeth, while the bull relied on its hooves and horns, fighting fiercely for a long time. Neither side won, and the tiger eventually left. The cattle ate greedily due to hunger. The hired person knew the cow was hungry and feared the tiger might return, so he quickly climbed down and fed the cow dry rations. After feeding the cow, the tiger indeed returned.

The bull possessed extraordinary strength, and when it fought a tiger, it became even more powerful, ultimately defeating the tiger and driving it away. Upon this, the man was filled with joy, sleeping beside the bull at night, feeling secure and fearless thereafter. Whenever a tiger approached again, the bull would charge and drive it off, protecting the herd without any harm. The following spring, as the weather warmed, he began to drive the bull back, telling the owner about the bull's abilities and suggesting that they buy another bull to replace it for farming, which the villagers agreed to. One night, while in the mountains, the man dreamed that the bull called out to him, saying, "Wake up, Eastern man! Wake up! I have eaten the lingzhi mushroom and gained some knowledge; I shall die tonight. After I die, please collect my two horns and keep them, for they will be useful later." The bull then added, "If you encounter danger, call out 'niú guǐ' dozens of times, and I will come to your rescue." Upon waking, the man thought the dream was unreal and did not believe it. The next morning, he saw that the bull had indeed died. It was customary to remove the skin of a bull that died in the mountains and give it to the owner, who would then believe the story. However, the man, recognizing the bull's unusual nature, only collected its two horns and buried them. The villagers, however, doubted the story and refused to believe it.

From then on, the person joined a group that went into the mountains to gather ginseng. They did not know that their dwelling was where tigers roamed. He made a nest in the tree to sleep. Suddenly several tigers came, devoured all his friends, and ate their flesh. He saw it, and both his soul and spirit were lost. After a long time, the tigers finally left. He wanted to descend, but feared the tigers would return. Suddenly remembering what the cow had said, he called out "cow ghost" repeatedly while descending. Hurriedly headed east. After walking several hundred steps, suddenly saw tiger flies flying with the wind, knowing the tigers had returned, hurriedly climbed back up the tree to avoid them. When the tigers finally left again, the sun was already slanting, and he had called out "cow ghost" countless times. Suddenly a person came from the east, with a large body, looking like a green ox, and said to him, "Easterner, hurry down! With me here, you can be assured of no harm." After he descended, the person said, "Follow me!" His pace was very slow, but with great effort, he managed to keep up. The person led him to a mansion, with sturdy houses, and helped him sit down, performing the rites of a host.

From then on, the person joined a group that went into the mountains to gather ginseng. They did not know that their dwelling was where tigers roamed. He made a nest in the tree to sleep. Suddenly several tigers came, devoured all his friends, and ate their flesh. He saw it, and both his soul and spirit were lost. After a long time, the tigers finally left. He wanted to descend, but feared the tigers would return. Suddenly remembering what the cow had said, he called out "cow ghost" repeatedly while descending. Hurriedly headed east. After walking several hundred steps, suddenly saw tiger flies flying with the wind, knowing the tigers had returned, hurriedly climbed back up the tree to avoid them. When the tigers finally left again, the sun was already slanting, and he had called out "cow ghost" countless times. Suddenly a person came from the east, with a large body, looking like a green ox, and said to him, "Easterner, hurry down! With me here, you can be assured of no harm." After he descended, the person said, "Follow me!" His pace was very slow, but with great effort, he managed to keep up. The person led him to a mansion, with sturdy houses, and helped him sit down, performing the rites of a host.

He was deeply suspicious and surprised, thinking to himself, "Is this the cow ghost I called?" He asked about the person's name, and the response was, "There's no need to ask." Soon after, he was fed with wine and food, and all the servants were male. He asked, "Why do you have no wife?" The answer was, "I cannot mate with females, so there is no need for a wife." The person then said, "You must not go out; if you do, do not go more than two hundred steps away." He did not understand the words. One day, feeling very bored, he went out but did not venture far, looking at the green mountains surrounding him, the scenery was very beautiful. Suddenly, a tiger lying on the hillside saw him and suddenly rose, and he was greatly frightened and ran back, but the tiger did not come. After several days, the person said, "You are now calm, and you can resume your livelihood." He asked, "What should I do with my servant?" The person replied, "Didn't you come here to collect ginseng? There is the most ginseng somewhere, and you can follow me to collect it." The road was rugged, and it was very difficult to travel. The person said, "You can ride me!" Then fell to the ground and transformed into a plow ox, and he knew it was a cow ghost, riding it without doubt. Traveling for many moments, he saw many tigers playing and jumping, and the ox carried him straight towards them, and the tigers saw the ox and all fled. He saw that the place had a lot of ginseng and hurriedly dug it up. Day and night, he carried the ginseng back on the ox, and the ox transformed back into a person, and in this way, he obtained several hundred pounds of ginseng.

Previously, his father died, and his mother remarried. After a year, his mother's second husband also died, leaving them poor and without support, which led to their freezing and starving. One day, he chanced to go to the market and saw his mother wearing tattered clothes and carrying a basket to beg for food. He suddenly remembered his mother's virtue and quickly bought cooked food to offer to his mother, begging her to return home. Someone said to him, "Your mother remarried, and affection and righteousness have been severed. You are showing such respect, which cannot help but be excessive." He replied, "Not so. If my mother's second husband had not died and I showed such respect, it would be excessive; if my mother's second husband had died and she depended on clothes and food, and I showed such respect, it would be excessive; if my mother had no clothes or food, and I speculated about supporting her and showed such respect, it would still be excessive. But if she is howling cold in the market, with no one to show pity to, begging for cold food, not eating a full meal in a day, even if I did not know her, seeing her would still move my heart, how much more so for my birth mother? Although my mother's past misdeeds have reached their limit, her great virtue is ultimately unforgettable. This matter cannot be generalized."

Someone heard and said, "I am truly a lowly person!" The character was in a place associated with demons, staying alone every night. Suddenly, he thought that he had not returned home for a long time, and his elderly mother was without support, then he felt fear. Then he thought about getting this unexpected wealth, and it would not be difficult to marry, then he felt joy. Joy and fear alternately overwhelmed him, and he repeatedly spoke to himself, his eyelids could not close. The cow demon said to him, "Since you have something on your mind, you should return quickly." The character said, "Nothing." The cow demon said, "The filial piety of a person, in normal times it is easy, in times of change it is difficult. You can fully express your filial heart in times of change, truly admirable and worthy of respect." The cow demon continued, "The ginseng you obtained does not need to be carried in large quantities. It can be exchanged for 200,000 coins, which is enough. The money, except for honoring your mother, buy all the women's clothing. I will arrange for you to marry." The cow demon warned, "There are tigers on the way. I will accompany you." When the character left the mountain, the cow demon said, "You must return within ten days. I will wait here." The character returned home and told his mother about the matters involving the cow demon and others. When the clothes were ready, it had already been ten days, then he bid farewell to his mother and left. The cow demon indeed waited and accompanied him back.

One night, just before dawn, a sudden and strong wind arose, but it quickly subsided. The ox ghost asked the man to rise and said, "Where are the clothes you have control over?" The man replied, "They are all here." The ox ghost said, "This is useful now! Take the clothes and go to the empty valley behind the house. There is a woman lying naked, and she does not allow intimacy. Do not give her clothes, and the matter will surely harmonize." The man went, and indeed, there was a woman with a hairpin, lying naked and weeping. The man said, "Do not weep; I have come to give you clothes." The woman said, "If you indeed clothe me, you are truly a good person." The man smiled and said, "I am not good, but I really like beauty. If you do not become my wife, I will not clothe you!" The woman remained silent. The man said, "Why did you come here? How did I know you were here and give you clothes? There is indeed divine power in between, it is likely the work of heaven. Why do you disobey?" The woman heard his words and found them reasonable, so she agreed. The woman was clothed, and she went with the man to his home. She said her surname was Fu and mentioned the names of her father and brothers and their place of residence. They became husband and wife. The woman saw the ox ghost and said, "I was brought here by the wind, it must be him who did it." The man also recounted the events in detail. The next day, the ox ghost said, "There is a wasteland here, because there are many tigers, and no one has cultivated it. I have driven away all the tigers. You can cultivate it. If tigers come again in the future, you can tie grass to make a cow, place my two horns on the outside of the main gate, and tigers will naturally avoid it." He spoke and then disappeared.

The Protagonist, missing his/her mother, intended to bring her home to care for her but feared the wife might not agree. He/she therefore told the wife about arranging the mother's remarriage and also expressed his/her own intention, pleading with her to reluctantly comply. The wife said, "That's acceptable. But I can treat her as a mother-in-law, but I cannot call her that directly. Because calling her mother-in-law directly, not only might my feelings be forced, but the mother's heart would also feel ashamed. What is the surname of your maternal grandfather's family?" The protagonist replied, "It's Liu." The wife said, "You should pretend to the mother that I only know she is being taken care of by you and call her Liu Mother. I will do my best to serve her, and she will consider her role. Then we can live together peacefully without any complaints. Otherwise, if I try my utmost to serve her and do not meet her wishes, she will surely say that I do not treat her as a mother-in-law, and my heart will always find it hard to justify myself." The protagonist agreed, "Your words are right." As the protagonist was about to leave, the wife said, "At that time, my parents will certainly think I am dead. Please send a message to me." The protagonist promised. After returning and seeing the mother, the protagonist changed clothes and went to the residence of Mr. Fu. Seeing the family status, he/she knew it was a wealthy and influential family. He/she asked to meet Mr. Fu and told him about the matter. Mr. Fu was pleased and hast

Xubai Taoist said: The ghost rewarded him, indeed treating him very well, yet it was also because he could fully express the heart of a filial son. In a certain town, there were two officials known for their filial piety. Both had fathers who died and mothers who remarried. One, after passing the imperial examination, went to his mother's new husband's house at night, bowed three times and kowtowed, and then returned, because his mother's new husband was still alive and could provide support. The other welcomed and supported his mother at home, because his mother's new husband had died and there was no one to provide for her. Although their actions were different, their principles were the same.

Hua Yue Nian A man from Fujian named Tong Da, handsome and well-proportioned, studied Confucian classics and took the examination for young scholars, becoming well-known both far and near. Pleased with his talent and appearance, he wished to find a beautiful wife. There was a beautiful daughter of a wealthy man named Hua Ruiting who lived next door, named Yue Nian, who was exceptionally beautiful and had been too strict in choosing a spouse, remaining unmarried despite reaching the age of marriage. Hua once discussed matchmaking with a friend over a cup of wine, and the friend said, "Only Tong Da would be a suitable match, how can one consider wealth or poverty? He is not extremely poor." Hua agreed, and the conversation ended there. Yue Nian heard this and was very happy. It turned out that Yue Nian's maternal family had a distant relative relationship with Tong Da, and they had met during celebrations and funerals. Marrying Tong Da was actually in line with her original wish. One day, when Yue Nian visited her maternal family, she passed by the city god temple and met Tong Da again, feeling an attraction and smiling warmly. Tong Da wondered why the girl seemed so affectionate towards him. Soon after, he heard that Hua had decided to arrange a marriage for her, realizing that her smile was due to this news. He then asked a close friend to act as a matchmaker. Believing that the girl had shown interest, he thought the match would be successful. However, the match ultimately failed, and he regretted his initial enthusiasm, thinking it was unnecessary trouble. Just then, a female Taoist priest was begging for food on the street, with a solemn and dignified appearance, exuding an air of otherworldliness. She had been begging for half a day without receiving any donations. Tong Da felt compassion for her and offered her vegetarian food, inviting her to stay at his home. On the verge of leaving, she gave a piece of jewelry to Tong Da and said, "I have something to tell you, but I fear you may not be able to bear it."

### Final Translation Hold this and strike the rock at the southern peak three times. The stone wall will immediately open, and you can obtain a fairy as a match." After speaking, he left. The boy obtained the bracelet and struck the rock. The cliff wall opened widely. He entered and walked about half a li, where he saw a fairy sitting at a desk writing. Seeing the boy, she threw down her pen and asked, "Who are you? How did you get here?" The boy replied, "I am Tong Da, guided by a fairy to come here." The fairy said sternly, "I do not care whether you are a boy or not, or whether you are named Da or not. I possess an object, and if you also have it, then I will obey your commands. Otherwise, please leave; do not have wild thoughts!" She then removed the golden bracelet from her arm and showed it to him. The boy said, "This is my family's old object; how could I not have it?" He compared the bracelet he had with hers, and they matched perfectly. The fairy blushed and was about to stand up, but the boy embraced her. She resisted, saying, "How rash are you! With just a little favor, you have already taken steps forward?" She asked about his family background. He said, "I am the daughter of a fairy. My mother only says my father is Liu, but I do not know the details. She also said that I have a karmic connection with the mortal world and placed me here to wait for you. Counting the days, I knew that you would surely arrive today." She then gave the boy the words written on the desk, which were his name, age, and the time and date of his arrival. The boy also told her about the nun's alms and the message she gave him before departure. The fairy sighed and said, "What you saw is my mother." Suddenly, the cave became dark, and they knew it was evening. They lit a candle and drank together. After drinking, they extinguished the candle and slept. From then on, the boy visited the cave every three to five days.

About half a year later, the child, now back home, heard of Moon Lady's death and could not help but feel deeply moved. Previously, when Moon Lady had heard of the family's intention to marry the child, she thought that her elderly father desired it, and that nothing would prevent the match, so she smiled at the child. Later, upon learning that the child's proposal of marriage had not succeeded, she felt embarrassed by having laughed at him, as if she had lost her virtue. Subsequently, when she learned that her father was planning to arrange a marriage with another family, she took her own life. The child visited the cave and spoke of Moon Lady's death, expressing his grief. The girl said, "Do you have any relation to her?" He replied, "No." She then asked, "Was she exceptionally beautiful?" He answered, "Yes." The girl said, "Even if she was beautiful, what does it matter to you? There are countless good women in the world. If you were to grieve over every one who died, you would never have a moment of joy or peace!" The child then recounted the details of the marriage proposal and the girl's death. The girl said, "She was a chaste woman; one should not betray her virtue."

The boy asked, "How many days has he been dead?" and was told it had been three days. He said, "I have a elixir; if taken, it can revive him, but it will not allow for reproduction." The boy happily accepted the elixir and went directly to the noble's room, claiming he could revive Lady Yue. The noble, grieving for his daughter, urgently asked about the method to revive her. He asked, "What happens after she is revived?" The noble replied, "If indeed she is revived, I will marry my daughter to him." The boy immediately ordered to open her coffin and placed the elixir in her mouth. After a while, Lady Yue sat up, opened her eyes, and saw the boy. The family told her, "You have been dead for three days; now the boy has revived you." She blushed and said, "After meeting at the temple, I thought we would never meet again; I did not expect to meet again today." Her sister-in-law said, "My father has already promised her in marriage!" Lady Yue, upon hearing this, turned red with shame, bowed her head, and remained silent. The noble invited the boy to the guest house, arranged the date for the wedding, and returned.

The young man returned home and went to see the fairy, who had a worried expression on her face. The fairy asked, "Should you be happy but you are worried, why is that?" He replied, "You don't know that money is what the world urgently needs, do you? I also know that it's better to be frugal than extravagant, but even frugality has no means of support, what can I do?" The fairy said, "As you say, your family has silver but no money." The young man said, "We don't even have money, how can we have silver?" The fairy said, "I haven't seen what money looks like, but if it's silver, it's not difficult to obtain." She pointed with her finger and said, "Is that not it?" Where she pointed, there were indeed piles of white silver. From then on, whenever the young man returned home, he could take whatever he wanted. When the time came, the fairy wanted to come and see, but the young man did not want her to. He said, "The Hua family doesn't know about you. If they see you, they will know I have a previous wife, and arguments will start from here!" The fairy said, "I will go, but I won't let them see me." The young man agreed.

The wedding ceremony was completed, and the woman said, "The rituals are indeed numerous, yet the concubine-taking was so hasty!" Moon Lady heard the voice and raised her head to see the woman, asking, "Who are you, sister?" The woman replied, "I am Liu, and I serve a virtuous man with your sister." Moon Lady said, "If that is the case, the proper rites for a wife have not yet been performed." The woman said, "Do not say that. I am not competing with you for the position of wife or concubine." The boy wanted to arrange a place for her, but the woman said, "Do not bother. You are just married and enjoying the festivities tonight, and I find it uninteresting to stay here." She then left. The boy then told Moon Lady about the woman and the matter of being rescued by medicine. Moon Lady said, "Why did you not mention it earlier? How can there be a favor of saving one's life without showing gratitude face-to-face?" The boy said, "This is not a minor favor; it cannot be thanked directly. One should wait for an opportunity to repay it." After several days, the boy visited the cave again. The woman said, "Love and hatred are at your discretion, but the deadline cannot be missed. Please meet once a month." The boy smiled and agreed.

Subsequently, Moon Lady indeed did not conceive, and the boy was very worried. The woman said, "People from the state of Qi who have one wife and one concubine do so not only for sustenance but also to ensure the continuation of the family line. You have two wives; if the blessing of many descendants cannot be obtained from one, it can be sought from the other. Bow to me, and I will bear it for you." The boy immediately bowed to her. The woman laughed and said, "How could there be such a thing! I was just joking with you. If it does not go as wished, I will return it with two bows." The boy replied, "Is it about returning the courtesy? If you do not keep your word, I will bow facing north and refuse it." Before the end of the month, the woman said, "I indeed feel the movement of pregnancy, but I can only bear it for you; I cannot raise it for you. Please discuss with Huamei early." She saw Moon Lady and told her that Liu would give birth to a son. Moon Lady said, "His son is his son; what does it have to do with me?" The woman said, "He wants to entrust it to your care." Moon Lady said, "If it is given to me, it is my son; what does it have to do with him?" Moon Lady gladly took on the responsibility and hired a wet nurse in advance to wait. One night, the boy and Moon Lady were talking and laughing without sleeping when the woman suddenly arrived and said, "I want to repay your kindness; this is the time." Late at night, she gave birth to a son, whom Moon Lady took and gave to the wet nurse, naming him Bao Yuan. After a month, the woman finally left. After several years, the boy also ascended to immortality.

Jiang was the second wife of Duan Cheng from Zhang County. After three years of marriage to Cheng, Cheng passed away. No offspring came from the legitimate wife, and Jiang herself had not yet given birth. Her brother-in-law Shi had two sons, who were fond of drinking and gambling, and the sons inherited these traits, leading to their extreme poverty. Jiang wanted to adopt one of them as her heir but decided that he could not maintain the family. She thought about it day and night but found no good solution. She then bought a beautiful maid and hired an old woman to accompany her, intending to make Shi have a child with the maid to continue the family line. She instructed the maid and the old woman to pretend they did not belong to a household. Shi heard that the maid was beautiful and went to see her when Jiang was out. Seeing the maid, he was pleased and did not want to leave immediately. The old woman followed Jiang's instructions, served wine and food to Shi, and urged the maid to serve him. The maid, being in her prime, was lured by Shi, and they secretly consummated their relationship. When Jiang was about to return, Shi finally left. Subsequently, whenever Jiang was away, Shi would sleep with the maid.

There are times when one takes advantage of gaps to secretly pass through, thinking that the sister-in-law's watchfulness would not reach, yet unaware of the reason for borrowing seeds. After two years, the maid became pregnant. Jiang asked Shi, "Does the maid's pregnancy mean you know about it?" Shi said he did not know. The maid and the old woman both confirmed it, but Shi remained silent. Jiang said, "Since you have had private dealings with the maid, you should give the maid to you, allowing you and the maid to come and go freely, share meals and drinks together, but do not trouble your mental faculties with household matters." Shi complied. Not long after, the maid gave birth to a boy named Xingsheng. Shi also began to abstain from excessive drinking and often visited the sister-in-law's house, sometimes handling minor household tasks, never wasting a single coin. Jiang realized that Shi had changed his ways and gradually entrusted him with household affairs. Shi managed everything neatly and orderly. When Xingsheng grew up, Jiang's husband passed away. All matters were left to Shi to handle, while Jiang remained detached. The Vubai Daoist said: How wise was Jiang! Her foresight surpassed that of ordinary men! In the world, foolish men and women view wealth as their lives. Those who accumulate modest fortunes and adopt unsuitable heirs, drink excessively and gamble recklessly, squander their assets and lose their properties. Even those who accumulate wealth through harsh and stingy means may end up suffering from hunger and cold in old age. Is it all due to fate? It is also due to improper handling.

A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. --- ### Final Translation In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings. --- ### Final Translation From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you. --- ### Final Translation As for writings, their origin lies in the Five Classics: edicts, memorials, and military proclamations arise from the *Shujing* (Book of Documents); expository and argumentative writings arise from the *Yijing* (Book of Changes); songs, odes, and praises arise from the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry); ritual sacrifices, mourning, and elegies arise from the *Liji* (Book of Rites); memorials, admonitions, and inscriptions arise from the *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals). Court regulations, military oaths and decrees, the proclamation of benevolence and righteousness, the elucidation of virtue and merit, governing the people and establishing the state—all are indispensable. As for cultivating the mind and spirit, offering gentle admonition in a relaxed manner, and savoring the flavor of such writings, these too are pleasant pursuits. When one has spare energy, then one may study them. --- ### Final Translation The practices of merchants from Yangzhai are similar. Acting for the sake of ancestral worship, those who plot with others for personal gain have motives and actions that are vastly different. This is called the virtue of wisdom and knowledge. Fang, the historian, courtesy name Anren, from the western part of Sichuan, lost his father at a young age and was filial to his mother. He has always been skilled in divination and numerology, and often predicted his mother's life events, not only that she would not live long but also that she would not die a natural death. A friend from the same examination class introduced him to the capital, where he heard that the friend had met a sage who could foresee the future. Thus, Fang went to see the friend with utmost sincerity and told him what he sought. The friend said, "Please first visit your mother, to observe the signs of good or bad fortune." Fang did so. Upon returning, the friend again used divination. He said, "Indeed! She will not die a natural death." Fang was alarmed and said, "I have always been cautious and respectful. Even if there are sins or wrongdoings, how could it involve the family?" The friend replied, "The sin is not yours. Your mother must have gone too far in killing living things." Fang thought about it and said, "I only heard that when my mother served food to my grandmother, there were sometimes ants in the food, and she was scolded and humiliated. Afterward, my mother would kill ants whenever she saw them and would sit and watch until all were injured before leaving." The friend said, "This is the reason. You should urgently advise her to deeply repent and buy living things to release them in many ways. Perhaps it can win back the heart of Heaven." Fang thus admonished his mother day and night, and she complied. He also performed good deeds, hoping to avoid calamity.

After three years, the official was appointed governor of Wenzhou by imperial decree, and he accompanied his mother to take up his post. As they neared the borders of Wenzhou, they stayed at an inn. He heard that another official, who claimed to be going to Wenzhou, was also staying at the inn, which made him doubt this. He submitted his visiting card and paid respects. After meeting, he asked the other official. The official replied, "We have the same duties, but there is a difference between the visible and the invisible." The official then realized that he was the city deity. With respect, he said, "I have one matter to present to the divine. Astrologers all say that my mother will not have a good end. Is this true?" The deity then opened a book and pointed to a specific date, indicating that his mother would be struck by lightning at the government office. The official's hair stood on end with fear, and he shed tears, pleading for rescue. He said, "If my parents reach a hundred years old, even though I am deeply grieved and shedding tears, I can still barely survive. But if they suffer divine punishment, how can I bear to see it? I beg for mercy in my sincere feelings!" The deity did not respond and said, "If nothing else, there is only one method."

After taking office, the official strove to arrange lavish banquets, encouraging his mother to taste everything, doing so six or seven times a day; as for clothes and bedding, he changed them every two or three days. When his food, clothing, and salary were exhausted, he must die ten days earlier, to avoid the suffering before death. If he feared that after death he would suffer calamity, he should dig a pit one zhang deep in the wilderness, bury the coffin at the bottom of the pit, cover it with soil, stopping at one chi. He should then use another wooden coffin, stuff it with straw to make a human form, write the name and age of the deceased on it, pile earth over it to level with the ground, and not seal or plant trees. There is no other method, this is a great fortune!” The official arrived at the office and followed the god's words exactly, serving for several months after which his mother indeed died without illness. The official was filled with both grief and joy, and quickly prepared the burial. Ten days after the burial, thick clouds gathered, and thunder shook the ground, greatly frightening him. When the rain stopped, he rushed to his mother's grave to see, and found the fake coffin shattered, the straw man burned beside the grave, but the body was spared. Soon after, he heard that the city god was struck by lightning, and the official was shocked and went to see, immediately gathering workers to rebuild the temple, having statues made of what he had seen. When the temple was completed, he prepared incense, paper money, and sacrificial offerings, paying homage and thanking the god for his blessing. He then was able to bring the coffin home and bury it together with his father in the ancestral tomb.

When his food, clothing, and salary were exhausted, he must die ten days earlier, to avoid the suffering before death. The hermit named Xubai said: "Even if one repents and reforms oneself, if heaven still punishes him, it is because the good he does does not outweigh the bad. If one is punished but ultimately forgiven, it is because his repentance is commendable, and there are filial sons who move heaven on his behalf. Could it be that the grave is so deep that the dragon does not know about it? I do not believe it." In a village, there was a poor man named Li Zhi, who formed a bond of life and death with Zhang Yi from the same village. During the day, he went to the streets to earn a living, and at night, they stayed together in one room. Their friendship was as close as brothers. There was a ruffian named Wang who took advantage of them, wanting to use Yi and Zhi as his henchmen, but Yi and Zhi refused. Wang became angry and insulted them, but because the two were united, the insults did not go too far. Not long after, Wang died, and Yi suddenly fell ill, muttering in his delirium, "Wang beat me." Zhi looked at Yi and saw that his body was all bruised and purple. Although Zhi was filled with anger, he knew that the living and the dead had different paths, and there was nothing he could do. Three days later, Yi also died, and Zhi mourned and arranged for Yi's burial. At night, Zhi dreamed that Yi said, "My brother has harmed me! The place where I am buried is not far from Wang's grave. He not only treated me like a servant, but whenever I displeased him, he beat me severely. And he would say, 'Now that you are the only one left, I can insult, humiliate, and kill you as I please!'" Zhi woke up, greatly angered, and early in the morning, he went to Yi's grave, made vows, and hanged himself from a tree. The villagers, moved by his loyalty, buried Zhi beside Yi's grave. At night, neighbors heard loud shouting and banging sounds after midnight. At dawn, Wang's body was dug up and thrown out. Wang's family tried to cover it up, but at night, it happened again. Wang's body was then moved to another place.

The person of the empty white said: To sacrifice oneself to save a dying friend, those who hear speak of it as frivolous and foolish. Indeed, such criticism is correct. But if friends do not help each other in times of hardship, how can one need friends in the five relationships? As for Li Zhizhe, although his actions are not worthy of emulation, they are sufficient to improve shallow customs. This matter can be compared with the legendary friendship of Yang Zhi and Zuo Qiuming. Wang Xuantang. --- ### Notes on Uncertainty - **虚白道人** - The exact identity of this figure is unclear, so it is left as "the person of the empty white" to preserve the ambiguity. - **李智者** - The exact identity of this figure is unclear, so it is left as "Li Zhizhe" to preserve the ambiguity. - **羊、左之传** - The exact details of this legendary friendship are not provided in the source text, so it is left as "the legendary friendship of Yang Zhi and Zuo Qiuming" to preserve the cultural reference.

Foxy Prince Lü Chang betrothed himself to Miss Huai Fang, the daughter of a scholar-official named Xu from Hankou. Xu, however, was unaware that she was a fox spirit. After the wedding, Xu's son, Gongzi Chongde, went to greet his sister-in-law. He saw a fox lying outside the door, called it up, and discovered it was a servant of the Lü family. Upon returning home, Chongde informed his father, who then asked his daughter about the matter. Only then did he learn that the entire Lü family were fox spirits. The father was greatly ashamed and regretful, vowing never to maintain relations with them. Lü had a beautiful daughter named Yulan. During a visit to his sister, Xu's son Chongde fell in love with her and began an affair. When Huai Fang found out, she praised Yulan's virtues upon returning home and suggested that her brother marry her, revealing this to her mother. The mother agreed, but the father firmly refused and told Yulan about it. Yulan laughed and said, "There will be a time when the desire comes, and at that time, don't call me cruel." Not long after, Chongde got into a quarrel with a fellow student named You, who was accidentally killed with a stone. Xu was filled with fear and had no idea what to do. Huai Fang said, "My younger sister-in-law, Yulan, can solve this problem." Xu said, "If she can, then let my brother marry her." Huai Fang and her brother hurried to the Lü family, where they met Yulan and told her the situation. They also said, "We especially want to congratulate you, dear sister!" Yulan quickly wrote a talisman and instructed Chongde to burn it outside the inner chamber. Only then did she ask, "What is the good news, sister?" Huai Fang replied, "My brother deeply admires your virtue and wishes to marry you." Yulan coldly laughed and said, "A family with many difficulties, a gentleman does not pass by their door. Now the Xu family has an unexpected disaster, and he will soon pay for a life. Will you make me remain unmarried and watch over the door?" Huai Fang wept and said, "I hope not only for your guidance but also for you to use your magical skills to save my brother's life."

Yulan said, "My younger sister is indeed capable of it, but the Xu family disdains her. I fear they will harbor resentment towards me. They will either be content or happy, and discard me like a forgotten object." Said, "Do not dwell on past mistakes. If the brother dies, the sister will also be at a disadvantage!" Said, "What disadvantage is there? Swear on the *Bo Zhōu*, save from countless troubles." Yulan was unwilling, and knelt down to beg, saying, "The unworthy son has made his own mistakes, kneeling here to die is better than dying on the execution ground." For a long time, she still did not respond. Yulan said, "When the elder sister and the young master come, the younger sister hurriedly burned the talisman, because I feared that the spirit of the deceased would leave the body, I believe he has already revived. But two months later, he will still die, give him the elixir, and it can delay his death by a few years." Said, "What about it?" Said, "Life and death are predestined; I can only ensure that he does not face retribution from you." Yulan was pleased, and thus married Yulan, and their marriage became even more harmonious.

Wu Li Nian During the late Ming Dynasty, there was a man named Bu Hong, whose courtesy name was Xiaofeng, and he was from Chu. He had a fondness for reading but was quite simple-minded. One afternoon, he strolled leisurely to the eastern hill, where he saw a gap in the distant mountains and the sun partially obscured behind it. He then laughed alone, saying, "What a beautiful scene! Why am I so in high spirits today?" Suddenly, a fragrant carriage with painted walls approached from the opposite direction. He thought that there must be a beautiful woman inside. Upon closer inspection, however, it turned out to be a middle-aged woman. The woman said, "Are you not the young master?" Bu replied, "Yes." The woman continued, "I have specially come to your mansion to respectfully greet you, and we met on the road. The round trip is slightly more than ten miles. There is a matter I wish to discuss with you, so please get into the carriage and come to my residence." Bu hesitated. The woman said, "Do not doubt. The matter we will discuss is something you have been seeking but cannot necessarily obtain." Bu agreed. After getting into the carriage, it sped off like a flying vehicle, and soon they arrived. She invited him inside, where he saw a beautiful young lady in the room, who did not shy away. They sat down, and Bu asked her surname. The woman said, "Do not be startled, I am actually a fox. If we consider family ties, I am the paternal aunt of your cousin, Wu Gong. I served your cousin Wu Gong for over ten years and bore his daughter, Li Nian. Unbeknownst to us, he mistakenly listened to a fortune-teller and confined me in a ritual circle, covering me with fine paper and inscribing a talisman on it. When you were seven years old, you broke the paper with an object, allowing me to escape the danger and take my daughter away. Your daughter and your cousin are of the same age, and you should also remember this event."

Zhu carefully examined the woman and said, "Yes, yes, I still remember my cousin laughing, with dimples on her cheeks." By evening, the old woman made the woman share the same sleeping quarters with him, and they became very harmonious. The next day, Zhu continued reading as usual. The woman listened to him reading the "Xiangdang" chapter of the *Analects* and said, "This is truly what is meant by not understanding the punctuation! 'Shangren hu bu' is one sentence, 'Su shi caigeng gua' is another. A sage loves the people and all things; how could he neglect even a horse? One must always sacrifice before eating, so the sacrifice must be solemn. You adhere to high-level lectures, but I suspect you have not seen the commentaries by Wang Yangming and Xing Yun. Moreover, you are already twenty plus a little, yet you spend the whole day reciting the plain text of the Four Books. Do you think you can govern the world with just half of the *Analects*?" Zhu replied, "Where did you learn? Why do you understand so deeply?" The woman said, "I don't understand deeply, but I have already passed the exams to become a doctoral candidate!" Zhu asked in surprise, "Why?" She said, "I dressed as a man and served as a student to a certain scholar-official for eleven years, not only passing the first level but also about to pass the second level. You can be a juren (a successful candidate in the imperial examinations)." Zhu became more suspicious. She said, "Why be suspicious? My name is actually using your courtesy name. Have you ever heard of anyone named Zhu Xiaofeng being famous in the school?" Zhu realized that the old woman had arranged for this woman to marry him, as it was her long-standing plan. Thus, Zhu stopped reading and spent days talking and feasting with Li Niang. After a long time, he suddenly understood. When he appreciated the beauty of the woman, from superficial to profound, he gradually understood the origins, and his hands were tired and his mouth dry. His focus on studying shifted to the bed.

卜 stayed at the girl's house for about half a month, and the girl wanted to return home together. He said, "My legal wife, Lady Gai, is fierce and jealous, what should we do?" The girl replied, "I am not afraid. If he is not jealous, it still benefits me." Upon returning, he directly told Lady Gai. After several nights, Lady Gai did not allow him to stay in the girl's room. The girl said, "Her jealousy is revealed!" He wanted to go tell Elder Wu, but the girl said, "No, family affairs will arise, it would sadden my old father." The girl moved her bed into Lady Gai's room, and he tried to stop her, but she did not listen. By evening, the girl led him to share her bed, and Lady Gai seemed confused and did not argue. He doubted and asked the girl. She said, "I used a small trick to control her." Lady Gai then feared the girl and gradually treated her with respect, as if she were a guest. The girl also did not consider herself the main wife, and he admired her even more.

In the realm of writing, one achieves a state of profound absorption, skillful without harming elegance, even more so than the criticism of Min Gong in *Liaozhai Zhiyi*, which seems more sincere and honest. Ma Zhugu A Single Ear of Grain Li Jiyun of Lijiang, a wealthy man, had three sons, each of whom he married off. During the autumn harvest, Li returned from his fields in the south, holding three ears of grain to give to his three daughters-in-law. He said, "Each of you should keep them well, and next spring we will use them as seeds to accumulate private savings." When the cuckoo called for plowing, he asked about the leftover ears of grain. Only the youngest daughter-in-law, Zhang, kept hers, while the eldest and middle ones had discarded theirs. He asked the youngest, "How could you value such a small ear of grain so highly?" She replied, "Every thread and grain is a gift from heaven; how could we squander it? Besides, it is a gift from my father, how dare I treat it lightly!" Li was very pleased and used one ear of grain as seed. The following year, he harvested about a sheng of grain; using that sheng as seed, he harvested about two dan; using two dan as seed, he harvested so much grain that it filled carts and baskets, beyond count. Within six or seven years, a thousand acres of land were insufficient to plant the grain they had harvested. Li told Zhang, "This year's harvest from the land is all the offspring of that single ear of grain; the profit can be taken by us." Zhang listened respectfully. The other two sisters-in-law looked ashamed, and Zhang suggested dividing the grain equally among the three of them. Li was delighted and said, "Our family has someone capable of maintaining and preserving it!" He then handed over all household affairs to the youngest son to manage.

### Translation *Wisdom Records Volume Five* **Jadeite** In the eastern province's examination hall, it was said that a group of foxes had temporarily resided there, and specific individuals often witnessed strange phenomena to verify this. A county student found this deeply unbelievable. One evening, as he passed by the place, reaching the Cloud Road Archway, he suddenly remembered what people had said and decided to investigate to dispel everyone's doubts. Passing through the Dragon Gate, the examination rooms were all pitch black, and the slightest movement of the wind caused his hair to stand on end. Upon reaching the main hall, he stood there for a long time, and nothing unusual occurred, making him secretly laugh at the absurdity of the rumors. Suddenly, he heard a recitation coming from behind the hall, so he smoothed his clothes and stepped forward again. As he approached the official's quarters, he saw the light shining through the windows, and the reading voice was clear and loud. Listening in, he realized that what was being read was a *zhiyiwen* (eight-legged essay) by Qi Jia on the theme of "a minister serving his lord with loyalty." The words were well-written, and the voice was powerful, making it impossible to get tired of listening. After listening for a while, he entered and said, "Sir, you are diligent in your studies!" The official, upon seeing him, moved aside and offered a seat with humility.

The guest asked for the person's name, and the host replied, "My name is Xu Yin, and I am preparing for the imperial exams." The bamboo stove boiled water, and tea was served as wine. They had a brief conversation, and the guest's heart and mind suddenly opened up. The guest greatly admired the host and said, "With a deep courtyard and no partner, are you not lonely?" Xu Yin replied, "My humble and cramped dwelling has no place to hang a curtain for study, so I am just borrowing this place." He then smiled and said, "People say there are many foxes here, but I have lived here for more than a month and have seen no activity. This shows that false rumors cannot be relied upon." The guest said, "I also came for this reason. Hearing your words, my doubts were dispelled, and I will spread this to dispel everyone's doubts." After speaking, they both laughed heartily. The guest said, "I would like to accompany you in your studies. Without knowing your intention, how dare I force you?" Xu Yin replied, "It is indeed what I wish. But to study here, one must prepare their own meals, which is quite inconvenient. I will also return to study at home." After some time, the guest took his leave. Xu Yin accompanied him to the public hall and then returned. Subsequently, the guest would often meet Xu Yin in the market, and they would simply nod in acknowledgment.

One day, the scholar and his friends went by boat to the Lixia Pavilion for a gathering and drinking. During the drinking session, one of the friends mentioned that the imperial examination hall was haunted by foxes. The scholar strongly denied this claim and recounted in detail his experience of entering and meeting Xu Yin, but the others did not fully believe him.
