The daughter said, "Wealth and poverty are matters of fate. How many have gone from poverty to wealth throughout history?" After hearing her words, Yi told Chen that, "Marriage is a major matter, and I dare not act on my own. I will write a letter to inform my father. If my father allows me to decide, then I will agree to the marriage." But before his letter reached home, a message arrived from home, saying that the daughter had already been promised in marriage to Mr. Lin from the same county as a second wife. Mr. Lin had been appointed as a county clerk and would be sent to Shandong; he would come next year to complete the marriage ceremony. Yi showed the letter to Chen, and Chen replied to him. There was nothing to be done; only despair and gloom remained. One day, a female servant suddenly arrived and gave a pair of sachets to him, saying, "These were made by the lady who caught your eye. Please give them to you, so that seeing the sachets is like seeing her, and they will help alleviate our longing." He took the sachets and wept; the servant laughed and said, "Do not weep, and there is more to come. Lady Jiao sent me to say: if I can marry you, I will endure and live; if not, I will hang myself and die." He said, "Please convey to Lady Jiao that I hope she does not die. After all, Lady Jiao and I have not had the approval of our parents or the matchmaker's words. How could we allow a slight acquaintance to ruin our lifelong plans?" He urged the servant to leave.

After hearing that Zhang had been appointed as the magistrate of Haifeng and had taken his family with him, I also learned that Zhang's son-in-law was coming to the east and would be getting married within a few months. Aqiao, with her beautiful face, now had a husband, and I believed that I would never see her again. At that time, I was living in a rented house outside the city, with only my elderly mother at home. One night, after the second watch, I was about to go to bed when I suddenly heard a knocking sound. I opened the door and saw several carriages. An old woman asked, "Are you Mr. Yu?" I replied, "Yes." The old woman then turned around and helped a young woman down from the carriage, offering her shelter. I was about to ask questions, but the old woman said, "This is the person to your liking." I was overwhelmed with joy and surprise, and led her inside. The old woman helped the young woman to first bow to my mother and then to me. I asked, "Why has Jiaoniang come here?" The young woman blushed and remained silent. The old woman answered on her behalf, "Jiaoniang heard that Lin's wedding was imminent and repeatedly tried to take her own life, vowing to marry no one but you. The family had no choice but to pretend that Jiaoniang had died suddenly to inform Lin, and they sent me to deliver her to you to fulfill her wish. However, since Lin is waiting for an appointment in the provincial capital, where there are many eyes and ears, it might be inconvenient, so moving far away to another town would be better." I said, "I have old relatives in Jimo, registered there for generations, but the journey is long and beyond my means." The old woman replied, "There is enough money and resources in the carriage for the journey." I was poor, with few possessions, so I immediately packed up, asked my mother to board the carriage, and left at night. We arrived in Jimo and performed the wedding ceremony. I looked at the young woman, and her beauty was still the same, but she was less charming, and I did not realize that it was all a deception.

After three years, coinciding with the mother's birthday, Yu respectfully set up wine and drink. After the mother's meal was removed, Yu and the girl drank together in a private room. The girl said, "If I had not served you until now, I am now beginning to serve you. Should I be嫌弃 because I am older, and be discarded like an old shoe?" Yu replied, "You are over thirty, but I still see you as a young woman." The girl remained silent. Yu said, "If you do not believe me, let it be as clear as the sun." The girl said, "Indeed, I am not Aqiao." Yu asked in surprise why, and the girl said, "Aqiao heard that I was about to return home and hanged herself. I saved her and told her, 'Do not die, those who love you will eventually wait for you, and your initial wish will be fulfilled.' After Lin's father died, and after the mourning period ended, Lin died again from illness. Now Zhang, the prefect, was dismissed from office due to sea bandits and is living in peace in Feng, planning to arrange a marriage for Aqiao with someone of a different surname. You should go quickly, otherwise it will be too late." Yu asked, "Who are you?" The girl replied, "I am actually a fox."

Admiring the deep affection between the two of you, I will assist you with great care and delicacy. If you go, I wish to accompany you. Yu asked, "Might there be any violation or obstruction?" The fox replied, "I will go, but I won't let anyone else see me." Yu followed her, and quickly arranged for a carriage to go. He presented himself to Zhang, and upon returning, immediately sent a matchmaker to propose marriage. Zhang was pleased and asked for a report back at another time. After Lin's death, someone proposed marriage for Ajiao. Zhang discussed it with the girl, who said, "Lin was not my husband, let alone anyone else?" Her mother asked her, and the girl directly said, "I will not marry anyone but Yu." Zhang said, "Yu knows you are surnamed Lin, and it has been three years since then, he must have already married." The girl replied, "If that is the case, then I am willing to spend my life with him." Her mother became angry and said, "I will not raise you for life." The girl said, "If you do not raise me to the end, I will shave my head and become a nun, and I will never betray Yu in this life." Having said this, she could not stop crying. Zhang had no choice but to send someone to inquire about Yu, who had moved far away three years ago, and his whereabouts were unknown. Upon hearing this, the girl cried at times.

After the matchmaker left, Zhang called the daughter, who had tear marks on her face, and said, "Do not be sad, Yu Lang has come. I will trouble someone to seek marriage for you now." The daughter lowered her head and remained silent. After returning to her embroidered chamber, she reflected to herself, "As soon as I heard that Yu Lang had moved far away, there was immediately a matchmaker proposing marriage—clearly a false deception. The previous words of Yu Lang cannot be trusted." By night, she again hanged herself. At that time, Yu was talking and feasting with a fox under a lamp. The fox suddenly exclaimed in surprise, "Your beloved person has hung herself again." Yu said, "What should we do now?" The fox replied, "Didn't she give you a sachet as a gift? You can quickly bring it here, for without this, it is not enough to gain trust." The fox took the sachet and flew away on the wind. When Jiao began to hang herself, she revived when she was released and saw the fox, saying, "Sister, you have saved me twice. I wish to know your divine name so that I can burn incense in gratitude in the future." The fox said, "I am not an outsider." She then gave the sachet to the girl. The girl was startled and asked about the origin of the sachet. The fox said, "Who did you give this thing to, and who gave it to me?"

Yu was indeed here, please do not doubt it. The woman, initially filled with sorrow, now turned to joy and asked for details. The fox said, "The story is long, I will tell it to you in person another day." After saying this, the fox disappeared. After the matchmaker had arranged the marriage, they chose a good day to send the betrothal gifts. On the night of the wedding, the fox suddenly vanished. After the wedding ceremony, Yu and the woman exchanged tender glances, each fulfilling their long-held wishes. Later that evening, Yu helped the woman onto the bed, loosening her collar and sash, when suddenly someone laughed and said, "Is A娇 not embarrassed?" The woman quickly pushed Yu away and looked around, but there was no one else in the room, greatly puzzling her. Yu explained, "This is my fox wife, the very one who saved you twice." Hearing this, the woman bowed twice and thanked him, respectfully asking the fox to appear so they could talk and laugh together. She repeated her request several times, but there was no response. Later, the couple slept happily, while the fox laughed quietly in the dark, causing the woman to feel ashamed and unable to bear the embarrassment. The next night, fearing further disturbances, the room remained silent all night, and she realized the fox had gone far away. With the fox's help, Yu had become a wealthy man and contributed funds to restore Zhang's position. His property was located in the region of Mo, and he settled there. Xu Bai Dao Ren said: Those who are deeply attached to emotions often harm righteousness; if one harms righteousness, even if their emotions are sincere, they are not worthy of praise. If Yu loved beauty but was not confined by it, as seen in his advice to Zhang not to make a mistake that would affect her life; if Zhang valued chastity and did not change it, as seen in her marrying someone of a different surname twice and committing suicide. Their situation was admired not only by people but also by foxes, and the foxes admired it more than people did.

**Translation:** Where emotion is truly expressed, it is also where righteousness is deeply conveyed. The *Odes of Zhou* and *Songs of Chu* should all be viewed in this light. — Ma Zhuwu In moments of tension, suddenly relaxing; in places of connection, suddenly severing—this is a masterful writing technique. — He Ziying Empress A’Jiao of the Han dynasty, initially housed in a golden chamber, was ultimately abandoned in Changmen Palace—how unfortunate! If she had lived, she could be said to have been deeply righteous. — Shangyuan Li Yu, respectfully annotated **The Righteous Ghost** Liu Sheqing was a man from Dongjun. He ran a tavern at a canal crossing, which had been in operation for many years. One day, while counting money, he found paper money ash in his cash drawer, which greatly puzzled him. He began to pay close attention to it. Every evening, after closing the door and extinguishing the lights, a person would come to buy wine. Liu would put the money into the drawer and pour the wine in the dark. One day, he placed the money on the table, and in the morning, it had turned into paper ash. The next day, the same thing happened again, and Liu realized that the wine buyer was a ghost. That night, the ghost returned to buy wine. Liu asked for a fire to light a lamp, and once the lamp was lit, he invited the guest in, but the guest only reluctantly agreed. Liu saw that the guest had no shadow and believed him to be a true ghost. Liu was bold and fearless. He said to the ghost, "Drinking alone is boring. I wish to share a drink with you." The ghost remained silent. Liu then filled a pot with wine, heated it with fire, and poured two cups on the table. Before they could drink, the ghost’s cup was already empty. Liu was extremely pleased. The ghost was an excellent drinker, and Liu also enjoyed it. They shared the same interests and tastes, and their spirits were in harmony. After several weeks, all suspicions were dispelled, and they became like old friends.

Liu smiled and said to the ghost, "From now on, I wish to drink with you every night. If there's no wine, buy me some; don't rely on what you have in your bag. What you urgently need is actually my worthless stuff." He then pointed to the paper ash on the table and said, "What use is this in the human world?" The ghost was greatly embarrassed and got up to leave. Liu held him back and said, "We share similar spirits; whether human or ghost, there's no reason to be suspicious." The ghost stopped and smiled, "I've benefited greatly from you, but I haven't done anything in return." Liu asked, "Where is the benefit?" The ghost replied, "Have you noticed that my business has been thriving recently?" Liu said, "Yes." The ghost asked, "Are there customers who come from elsewhere instead of your regular patrons?" Liu said, "Indeed, there are." The ghost said, "That's my contribution." Liu was very grateful and asked for the reason. The ghost said, "I am a ghost who drowned in the canal." Liu asked, "Why don't you reincarnate?" The ghost replied, "Ghosts who drown or hang must have someone to take their place. I am waiting for two years." From then on, the ghost came every night; there were also times when they sat facing each other, conversed, and left without drinking.

In about a month, the ghost suddenly said, "Tomorrow I will bid you farewell forever." Liu asked her, "Is there someone to take your place?" The ghost replied, "There is a woman who drowned at the place where I drowned, and she will take my place." The next night, the ghost returned, and Liu was suspicious. The ghost said, "This woman was holding a child in her arms and was whispering to herself, 'Your grandmother misses you, but the boat is not here on this side, how unfortunate to be like this.' When she reached the water's edge, she put the child down, lost her footing, and fell into the water. The child, being unaware, also crawled towards the water. I could not bear to see both the mother and child die at the same time, so I rescued them again." Liu said, "This is truly a righteous deed. We can meet again and talk and dine together, which is an extra blessing." After several months, the ghost again said, "Tomorrow night I will definitely leave." Liu asked, "Is there someone to take your place?" The ghost said, "Yes." The next night, the ghost returned. Liu asked, "Has the person to take your place arrived?" The ghost said, "Yes."

The person who substitutes for me is my brother, who has been engaged in trade for two years without returning. At the command of my mother, I went to look for my brother and return home, arriving at the riverbank. It was already evening, and the boatman happened to be on the opposite bank, called out to him but he did not respond. Hearing that the water in this place is not very deep, the younger brother will cross the river first to report to my mother. Mother will know sooner, and will think less of my brother for a moment. My elder brother can walk slowly after crossing the river, and I will return to meet you when I get home. Feeling it, he shouted to his brother, "When the elder brother gets home, he will take care of our parents without waiting for me, I will become a drowning ghost here." The elder brother heard this and jumped into the water to save him. Such brothers are rare in the world. How could I harm them? Therefore, I let them cross the river on their own. The King of the Underworld summoned me and blamed me for privately allowing the substitution.

The envoy stopped him and said, "Though the water here is not very deep, there are many pits and uneven ground; what if something unexpected happens?" The man replied, "Life and death are determined by fate; how can one fear death at the expense of endangering one's parents?" The envoy tried to stop him, but the man said, "Even if it means death, I have no regrets, so there's no need to hold me back." He forced his hand to cross. The envoy was deeply concerned for him, but he finally crossed safely without harm. The ghost said, "Since there is no successor, why did you let him go?" The man replied, "He is a filial son; even if he were a ghost for a thousand years, he would never take a filial son's place." The ghost said, "Your virtuous deeds are well-known; heaven will surely recognize them, and your position cannot be measured." The next day, the ghost smiled and said, "Indeed as you said, now we part forever." The man asked, "Is there someone else who will take your place?" The ghost replied, "No, the King of the Underworld praised my good deeds and specially appointed me as the land deity of a certain village in Guan. If you miss me, you can come to Guan for a visit." Soon, the ghost's attendants came to escort him. The man waved goodbye with lingering affection, as if parting from a close friend, and stood watching him ride away on horseback.

A Year Later, Liu traveled to Guantao for some reason, returned, and took a detour through Guan to visit a village. Upon arriving, the elders of the village received him with great enthusiasm, which made Liu suspicious. It turned out that the night before, the elders had all dreamed of the land god saying, "Tomorrow, a guest named Liu will come. He is my friend, so treat him with respect." Thus, the elders received him in such a manner. Liu bought incense and paper money, presented offerings before the god. That evening, he stayed at the village and dreamed of his ghostly friend saying, "I am honored by your visit and am extremely grateful. However, if you had arrived two days later, I would have greatly disappointed your visit, as I have already been promoted to the position of city god in a certain county in Guizhou. Here are a few ounces of gold, a small gift to thank you for our past affection." The ghost then placed the gold on the bed and left. Liu woke up and saw that there were indeed more than fifty ounces of gold. The next morning, he went to the temple to express his gratitude and bid farewell to the villagers before returning home. The author, Xubai Taoist, said: When one encounters a pitiable situation and feels compassion, it shows that one's heart holds benevolence; when one admires someone who respects their elder brother, it shows that one values fraternal duty; when one respects a son who is filial to his parents, it shows that one is deeply devoted to filial piety. A drowned ghost who possesses these three virtues and was specially promoted by the gods demonstrates that the heavens reward virtue with utmost fairness. Some people believe that a drowned ghost being replaced three times is an act of ghostly righteousness. I believe: the first time, a woman held her child and thought of her parents; the second time, a younger brother called out to his elder brother to care for their parents; the third time, a son gave up his life to save his parents. These acts are all due to filial piety. Filial piety can move heaven and earth, even bringing the dead back to life. Even if a ghost is not righteous, it cannot do anything about it.

Reading this biography, one learns that even drowned ghosts can cultivate merit, how much more so humans! — Elaborated by Yang Zihou This matter is recorded in the *Liaozhai Zhiyi*, and it is also mentioned in the *Xie Duo* and a certain book, though the wording is slightly different. — Commented by Fisherman and Woodcutter This ghost is the City God, who must be able to bestow blessings and protect the people, far surpassing earthly officials. — Annotated by Li Yu of Shangyuan Biyu Long Peiduo, whose name is unknown, was a scholar from Datong, Shanxi. He was of a generous and cheerful nature, upright and self-respecting. One night under the bright moon, an old man with a gray beard came to visit. He had a refined and elegant appearance and splendid attire. When asked, he said, "I am Mr. Zhang, courtesy name Shengzhou." His speech was elegant, and the gentleman was pleased with him. He then said, "Do you know Mr. Xia San, the chancellor?" "I am a younger scholar from the same year," he replied, "how could you not know?" Mr. Zhang said, "Currently, the price of deer skins is high. Mr. Xia will go hunting tomorrow and will definitely return by this path. There will be deer hunted. Why don't you ask for it?" Long said, "I have never taken things without paying, I will not do this." Mr. Zhang said, "Buying it with money is also acceptable." Long replied, "If he buys it without asking for payment, that is taking by cunning, especially not." Mr. Zhang took out several taels of silver from his sleeve and placed it on the table, saying, "I urgently need this, please trouble you to buy it for me."

The dragon was angry and said, "I bought it, but the merchant did not ask for a price. Why did I spend this silver? Moreover, since I am not willing to do it myself, would I do it for others and lose my integrity? Please quickly take back the silver." Zhang took the silver and left. After a short while, he returned, bowed down, and knelt. The dragon was surprised and said, "Why are you doing this? The matter is urgent, please stand up and speak frankly." Zhang said, "I will tell you the truth, the hunted deer is my true form." The official was startled and said, "Why didn't you say it earlier? To see someone die without helping is not the act of a benevolent person. How can we save it?" Zhang replied, "Place my true body in a quiet room, and after a night, I can revive." The official agreed. Zhang bowed and thanked him before leaving. The next day, the official waited for him. At dusk, XIA Guo led his attendants, with eagles and hawks, bows and arrows, and rode a horse to arrive. The dragon invited him to his home, served him wine, and recounted the hunting details. Seeing the deer, he gently stroked it and, under the pretense of a worn fur coat, asked for it. After XIA Guo left, the dragon went to the quiet room and found the deer had transformed into an old man. He helped the old man lie down on the bed, gave him rice porridge, and locked the room door. In the morning, the old man was gone. Although his heart was shocked and amazed, he also set it aside as something outside his concern.

The Long family had been renowned for their wealth for five generations. Although they were said to be in decline, their reputation for wealth still spread far and wide. One night, eighteen large thieves, armed with torches and weapons, broke the outer door with stones and entered. Long was greatly afraid and asked, "Is there any grudge?" The thieves replied, "No. But we eighteen people will each receive one hundred taels of silver, and we will all leave immediately." Long said, "Our family truly does not have such a large amount of silver." The thieves did not listen and were about to smash the door with a large stone. Suddenly, an old man came from outside and told the thieves, "This is a good and virtuous family. Now it has declined, and truly does not have much money to give you." The thieves asked, "Are you defending this family? You are just bringing trouble upon yourself, and you will die without regret." They all rushed at the old man with sharp blades. Long peered through a window and was very worried about the old man, but none of the thieves could harm him. The old man struck the thieves with a stick, and they all fell down. Soon after, the thieves all left with broken heads and bruised foreheads. Long was very happy, quickly opened the door, but the old man had already disappeared. Long's estate was very large, with about five hundred households living there. One night, the estate was hit by a fire. The fire, aided by the wind, was very fierce and could not be approached. Long's residence was in the path of the wind, and the fire spread to several houses, almost reaching Long's house, causing the entire family to be in great fear and at a loss. Suddenly, a person stood on the wall and sprayed water, and the fire immediately went out. The person then disappeared. Long believed that a god was protecting him and sacrificed animals to give thanks.

Lóng Gōngchē took part in the Ministry of Rites examination. His wife, Miáo Shì, suddenly contracted water hydrops, with her navel protruding and her back flat, a condition that was considered untreatable according to medical texts. Suddenly, a beautiful woman appeared, who was about to come of age. She claimed she could treat the lady's condition. When asked about her name, she replied, "Bì Yù." When questioned further, she said, "Do not inquire too deeply, for I did not come here without reason." After taking the medicine, the woman gently massaged her, and then kissed her, blowing her breath onto her. Miáo Shì felt a strange fragrance penetrating her orifices, and a thread of warm air reaching her lower abdomen. Soon after, she urinated several pots. The next morning, when she looked, her abdomen had wrinkles. After a month of treatment, she returned to her original state.

The woman said, "Sir, when you return, do not mention that your esteemed illness was treated by me." The man responded affirmatively. She took her leave, but the wife insisted on keeping her; when the man was about to return, the woman finally departed. Upon his return, the man fell ill again. His illness was caused by suppressed anger, which formed a hard mass, causing severe pain. Due to excessive purgation, he suffered from diarrhea that did not stop. Within a month, his body was emaciated to the bone, and he was almost unable to stand. Family members had already arranged for his afterlife. Suddenly, Biyu arrived, and the man was greatly pleased, receiving her in a separate courtyard. He asked, "Can Miss Biyu also treat my illness?" She replied, "Yes. This condition requires a major warming and tonifying treatment, and it will not be effective without using substances like alum, dragon bone, millet, and horse chestnut root." The man said, "I have used them in great quantities." She responded, "The medicine is not strong enough to fight the disease, but it is not that the medicine is ineffective. I will use the lady's medical skills to treat it, and I can ensure a quick recovery. However, do not let the husband see my face, and do not let him know that the one who treats him is me."

Miao said, "Then what shall we do?" He replied, "Pretending to be a lady would be fine." Miao was pleased and said to Long, "I have heard of a magical technique, specifically for treating your illness. It can only be performed in the quiet of the night, when no one is around, and in the darkness. You should try it." Long agreed. They then covered the windows and doors with felt blankets, extinguished the candles, and invited the woman in. Long felt a strong fragrance in his nose, saying, "Why is there such a sudden fragrance?" Miao pretended to say, "It's because you put the perfumed materials in your bag and wore them." Miao then held a bamboo tube and made Long put it in his mouth, saying, "I will put it in my mouth to help you breathe better." Long said, "We have always kissed. Why do you need the tube?" The woman wanted to leave upon hearing this, but Miao pulled her back and said to Long, "Keep silent, do not let the maids hear it, or they will mock us behind our backs." The woman then put the bamboo tube in her mouth and blew air into it. Long felt a great sense of relief and comfort, beyond words.

Shortly after, he pressed his hands on his abdomen to treat the illness, gently pushing and moving it, doing so three times. The next day, the diarrhea reduced significantly, and the pain was halved; the dragon was greatly pleased. The following night, the treatment was the same as before. As he was massaging, the dragon said with a smile, "Please treat me well. After I recover, when I lie with you under the same quilt and pillow, I will have something to repay you." The woman heard this and withdrew her hand, leaving the room. The man followed her to another room and saw that her face was flushed with embarrassment, she lowered her head and remained silent. He tried to comfort her greatly, saying, "The master thought the one who treated him was his concubine, not knowing it was a guest. He mistook a guest for a concubine, so he spoke in a bold and unrestrained manner. Please forgive me especially." The woman said, "I do not blame the master; I only regret causing trouble for myself and causing such humiliation." After a while, she still followed the man back into the room and continued the treatment as before. Three nights later, she said, "I am fully recovered now. But you should continue to take ten full flavors of medicine for more than a month to fully restore your health." After speaking, she took her leave. The man tried to keep her, but in an instant, she disappeared, and he then realized that the woman was not human.

The daughter of a certain gentleman was renowned for her unparalleled beauty, and those who admired her name vied to offer her a dowry, but all failed. The Dragon heard this and laughed, saying, "Beauty may exist, but unparalleled beauty probably does not. Perhaps those who love her consider her beautiful, and thus they take her as unparalleled." Later, at the Ghost Festival, he met her, and he then believed her name was not unfounded. He stared at her; after the girl left, he still stood like a wooden puppet. Thinking of her beauty, he often neglected food and sleep, and his body gradually wasted away. Miao asked him, and he told the truth. Miao said, "You are also too naive. This is not something that can be obtained through power or money, and thinking about it is also futile." The Dragon said, "I also know this, but I cannot forget it." He then fell ill in bed, gradually becoming seriously ill. Miao was very worried about him. Suddenly, he remembered that Biyu must have a magical technique to cure him, but regretted having no place to invite her. Unwillingly, he prayed devoutly at night, and after several nights, the girl arrived. Miao told the girl about Long's illness. The girl said, "I know this, but this illness is not something I can cure." Miao pleaded with her, and the girl said, "I have only one technique, but it might harm me, and I will regret it." "What is that technique?" the girl whispered in his ear. The girl, dressed in gorgeous attire, was talking and laughing in the courtyard. Miao secretly told Long, "Is that girl really beautiful?" "Yes," Long replied. "If that girl is beautiful, she is not as beautiful as the one talking and laughing in the courtyard." Long heard this, struggled to get up, peered through a crack, and was greatly delighted, suddenly forgetting his serious illness. Miao asked, "How does she compare to that girl?" "She is not as beautiful as this," Long said.
During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

After three years, Long asked, "Have you not given birth?" She replied, "Yes." Long said, "My eldest son is as foolish as a dog or a horse, and you have not given birth to another, so I will be extinct from here on." His son Xuan, who was over ten years old, did not know about men and women. The woman said, "Xuan's illness, I can cure it." Long was pleased and said, "If you can do it, it is better than having you give birth. What kind of medicine do you need?" The woman said, "The ingredients are difficult to obtain, please let me go for two months to gather them in the mountains. When the ingredients are complete, I will come myself. Do you believe me?" Long said, "Yes." The woman then left. After two months, she returned and said, "All the ingredients are ready. Wait for continuous rainy weather, and use the rainwater from the eaves to prepare the medicine." It happened that there was continuous rain during autumn, and the medicine was prepared according to the method and given to Xuan. After a short while, he let out a loud cry, his nose and mouth bled, and he sweated profusely, in a state of unbearable pain. Long urgently called the woman to administer the medicine, but she had already disappeared, and both Long and his wife were greatly alarmed. After a while, Xuan died. Long was deeply grieved by his son's death, and he forgot his foolishness, while his wife cried even more bitterly. Suddenly, Xuan used his hand to swat a fly, and Long hurriedly looked, and Xuan had already revived. He said, "Mother is crying." He got up and sat on the bed, speaking clearly, with no trace of foolishness. The couple were overjoyed and greatly praised the woman, believing that she must be a divine being. Suddenly, they saw a red note on the whitewashed wall, which read, "Biyu is not someone else; she is the woman you saved from a hunting accident. The previous incidents of theft and fire were personally addressed by my father, who then instructed me to protect your household according to the situation, to repay your great kindness."

虚白道人曰：The deer's repayment of kindness can be called the highest. However, observing the honest man's words to the deer, there is no intention of seeking repayment. It can be known that those who repay without seeking repayment do so with greater effort, it is emotion, and it is also reason. If, with a friendship of several years, one faces a ruler who personally bestows grace, one immediately thinks of adding insult to injury, how ashamed is this deer, is it not much? Can people be inferior to other species? Furred animals still know to repay kindness, how much more so humans? However, how can those who do not show kindness receive repayment? Alas, how good is virtue! Huang Qinxuan Please consult a medium. I dare not believe it exists, nor dare I say it does not exist, perhaps it is a matter of sincerity and efficacy. Then I continued with Mr. Yin Jiemo, who was skilled in poetry and prose, promoted through the imperial examination system, and tested and used to instruct and guide. Before holding office, he was a student, serving as a teacher to Mr. Zhai Linjiang and Mr. Zhou Ernan, and was friends with Mr. Chen Jitang, Mr. Liang Yunshan, and Mr. Hu Ancun. One day, they talked about the *Xitang Collection*. It is said that Mr. Xie Wuyi once asked Mr. Pan Dalin, "Have you recently composed new poetry?" He replied, "When lying quietly, I heard the sound of wind and rain in the forest, then got up and wrote on a wall, saying, 'The whole city is shrouded in wind and rain as the Double Ninth Festival approaches.' Suddenly, the tax collector arrived, ruining my mood, and I left only this one line." Someone said, "The opening line is extraordinary like this, there must be fine lines later, but it was finally ruined by the tax collector's arrival and stopped, it is indeed a pity."

All of you are poets, why don’t you continue with pearls and jade, to complete the perfect jade?” None of the scholars present responded. One person said, “Why don’t we ask the Pure Yang Immortal to continue?” All agreed, saying, “Yes.” The gentleman set up the machine, and the scholars lit incense and burned talismans, but the machine remained still for a long time. The scholars then prayed silently. After another period, the machine still did not move. The gentleman then used the machine to continue with the following lines: “The autumn air is bleak, and the earth is cool. Wine is poured at the mountain top for guests to drink, and chrysanthemums, since Tao’s time, for whom do they bloom? The cap of Meng has been blown away in surprise, and the hat of Lin has been worn in imitation for many times. With enthusiasm, the poem will be attached to the wall, but who would remember Liu Lang when inscribing a cake?” When the recording was finished, the scholars were amazed and praised it as extraordinary, all saying that only a divine being could have written it. The gentleman did not claim it himself. Later, those who saw it would often praise it. I once stayed at his home for four years, and we often talked and feasted, and he finally recounted the story.

The Virtuous Hermit said: Observing this, the practice of divination seems to be unreliable in its certainty. It is said that in the Imperial Academy, there was a large tree, and when the wind blew, a branch broke, causing the birds in the nest to fall to the ground along with the branch, forming a couplet: "The wind blows the bird's nest, and the two or three birds fall to the ground in succession." This couplet carries a double meaning and is indeed difficult to match. Thus, they sought divination to find a response. When the deity arrived, the mechanism moved, and it wrote: "Stop the machine; return to this moment in three days." On the appointed day, they set up the machine and waited. When the machine moved, it wrote: "Rain falls on the monkey's den, and the many monkeys come out, covered in dew, looking up to the sky." It then wrote: "I composed this couplet, traveling throughout China, but found no real scene to match it. Later, I came to Fujian, where there are many mountainous regions with monkeys. It happened that after the rain, the monkeys emerged from their dens, covered in dew, and looked up to the sky, and I found the inspiration for it." This couplet is not an absolute match, and it is possible that the person conducting the divination had the couplet prepared beforehand, using elaborate explanations to make it seem magical. Those who hear of it should not be deceived by it.

**Next Life Debt** In the northern village of the county, there was a farmer named Wang. He was diligent and thrifty in managing his household, and in good years, he had enough to support himself and his family. He had a maternal uncle who was a drunkard and gambler, and often borrowed money from Wang without repaying it. One day, the uncle tried to force a loan from Wang, and the amount was many times more than usual. Wang was unable to repay it, so he refused him. The uncle became angry and hit his head with a stone, claiming that Wang had beaten him. He planned to report Wang to the authorities to punish him for disrespecting his superior. Wang was afraid and lowered his voice and smiled, saying to the uncle, "My family doesn't have that much cash on hand. Please give me some first, and the rest can be paid in ten days. Uncle, take this money, and it might not all be spent in five or six days." The uncle then changed from anger to happiness and said, "Out of consideration for our close relationship, I forgive you. The ten-day deadline cannot be missed." Wang agreed. The uncle wrapped his head in cloth, fed him wine and food, and then left. When the time came, Wang paid the amount as agreed.

A short while ago, Wang happened to be napping during the day when someone suddenly appeared. Startled, Wang asked, "Have you spent all the money?" The visitor replied, "Yes. Today, my uncle came, not to borrow, but to repay." Wang was shocked to hear this. The visitor continued, "Excluding the small amounts, you borrowed a total of more than two hundred thousand. I request to serve for twenty years to offset the debt." After speaking, he suddenly disappeared. Wang woke up, only then realizing it was a dream, and found it strange. His wife laughed and said, "A horse gave birth to a mule." Wang replied, "The mule is my uncle." He then told his wife about the dream in detail. After the mule grew up, when Wang wanted to use it, he would say, "There is something that troubles Uncle to do." Whatever he pointed out, it was done as desired. Otherwise, it would definitely be exchanged with someone else. If the neighbor borrowed it, he would also say, "Neighbor so-and-so wants Uncle to work hard." When he took it away, it was obedient and well-behaved. Otherwise, it would become fierce and evil, and definitely not be used by anyone else. One day, there was a person selling pottery. The mule broke the reins and ran to the place where the goods were, trampling the bowls and plates with its hooves, smashing them all to pieces. The person scolded Wang, who said, "Uncle destroyed it, and I am willing to compensate as much." The person was surprised and asked, "Why is the mule your uncle?" Wang explained the name of his uncle and the strange story of the mule's birth and repayment of the debt. The person said, "If that's the case, you don't need to compensate. I owe someone money, and the amount of the damaged goods just matches what I owe. It's good for me to clear this debt." Then, he left with an empty basket.

Wang raised a mule for twenty years when, suddenly, he had a dream in which someone said to him, "The debt you owe has been fully settled. Within the item, there is Liu from East Village who has taken away twenty thousand, and I find it difficult to clear the debt on his behalf. It is reasonable for me to collect it for you. Set this price and sell me to Liu, and that will be acceptable." Wang could not bear to do it. The entity kept appearing in dreams and speaking, the words earnest and sincere, and Wang finally followed his words. Liu paid the price just a few days later, and the mule died without any illness. This story was told to me by my friend, the scholar-official Ji Shuren. It seems he knows fully the place of residence and names of these entities, but he chose to conceal this information, and I also did not feel it appropriate to ask in detail.

虚白道人曰：Debts should be repaid promptly; this is a matter of principle. However, the situations of borrowing and repaying debts can be vastly different: there are those who borrow money for gambling and other immoral purposes, which is unlike those who borrow without capital or are lazy and unreliable; there are those who have the ability to repay but are stingy about doing so, which is unlike those who have lost all their assets and have nothing to repay; there are those who lack documentation and deny the debt, which is unlike those who promise to repay but cannot. For those who genuinely cannot repay their debts, since they owe the debt in reality, it is not excessive to reincarnate as another being to repay it. For those who have the ability to repay but choose not to, their hearts are as cruel as wolves, and their actions are akin to theft. If they do not pay a hundredfold interest and are repeatedly made to reincarnate as cattle or horses to repay the creditor, it is still a lenient punishment by the Creator. There is another matter that can be made into a laughing matter. There are those with multiple creditors and numerous debts. After death, they meet someone who says, "You owe me several hundred coins, which you have not yet repaid." The debtor replies, "I will reincarnate as a chicken to repay you." They then meet another person who says, "You owe me more than a hundred thousand coins, and now you can repay me." The debtor replies, "I will reincarnate as a mule to repay you." Later, they meet someone who says, "You owe me several ten thousand gold coins, and it is only right that you repay me." The debtor, moved with pity, says, "There is no item in the world worth several ten thousand gold coins; even if I wanted to repay it, I could not. If there is no other way, I will reincarnate as your father and find ways to generate wealth to repay you."

Clear and detailed. 何子英 In the next life, debts are repaid without error, even close relatives cannot escape. The way of heaven favors return, is it truly so, it is also sufficient as a mirror. Attached is a story, though called humorous, it is spoken with emotion. 渔樵散人 The Drought-Footed Fish Deng Chengji of Qizhou, a great wealth. He did not get along with his brother Chengwu, due to dividing the family leading to litigation, it lasted three years without resolution, recklessly spending without limit, and the divided property, not giving an inch. Their residences were not far apart, when they met, they did not exchange a word. One day, Chengwu bought a pair of fish, the attendant carried them past Chengji's residence. Chengji saw them, called the attendant to stop, saying: "This is a drought-footed fish, transformed from a long snake, eating it will certainly lead to death with no remedy." The attendant said: "Is there a test for this?" He replied: "Yes. At night, bind its tail with a rope and hang it upside down from the beam, by morning there will surely be proof." The attendant reported this. Chengwu said: "I love this, he deliberately says this to hinder my enjoyment." Then he angrily said: "Turtles are water creatures, falsely calling it a drought, what does he see to say so?" He shouted at the attendant to bring him and scolded him, the attendant tried to mediate and stopped. Quickly ordered the cook to prepare the fish, saying: "If I eat it without harm, then I will blame him, listen to what he has to say."

If Brother is convinced by what Brother says, Brother will not believe what I say. Therefore, I hastened to buy a fish, secretly sending someone to deliver it, and had the cook prepare it to feed Brother. I instructed the cook to make sure to kill and discard the fish that Brother bought, and to warn not to speak to Brother about it. The cook followed the instructions, and prepared the fish as I said, hanging it upside down from the beam to observe the changes. Early in the morning, he looked at it and was greatly surprised, for the turtle had transformed into a white-flowered snake, about six or seven feet long, with its shell on its head nearly touching the ground. The cook was just about to report, but Brother deeply resented and said, "I ate the fish without any harm, so I know my younger brother does not have good intentions." The servants recounted its strangeness. Brother hurried to the place where the fish was hung, looked at it in great surprise, his face turning ashen, and remained silent for a long time. He saw the snake bend its body and look back, as if it wanted to bite the rope but could not. The servants wanted to kill it, but Brother stopped them and said, "Let my younger brother see it." He then had someone urgently invite Brother, and Brother greeted him at the door, saying, "Last night, fortunately, my younger brother bought another fish to replace it; otherwise, my younger brother would have been injured in both hands and feet." Brother said, "I heard that this fish has a faint trace of a coiled snake on its shell, eating it by mistake can hurt people, but I have never seen it with my own eyes." When he saw it, he was also greatly surprised and pale, and had someone chop it into several pieces, digging a deep hole and burying it. Brother kept Brother to eat together. During the drinking, Brother wept and said, "Brother, you were not friendly, and I deeply regret my past mistakes. In the future, I will build a villa and retire there, all household affairs will be entrusted to you, Brother will not be involved." Brother also blamed himself for being disrespectful. Brothers were harmonious, and they stopped arguing and returned home.

The hermit of empty white said: "The poem says, 'The terror of death and loss, the deep affection among brothers. In the lowlands and highlands, brothers seek each other.'" It deeply speaks of the principle of mutual support in times of crisis, even good friends cannot compare. Yet, in times of peace, there are those who value friends more than siblings, how utterly contrary to reason! If we consider the case of Deng and his brother, it is also an example of mutual support in times of crisis. The brothers lived apart, leading to a lawsuit lasting three years. The local magistrate could not resolve it, and relatives and friends could not mediate. Seeing a one-legged fish, the brothers stopped the lawsuit. Officials and relatives and friends are not as effective as a turtle. Like the bell of the clear night, it warns and enlightens. This matter is also recorded in the *Good Deeds Book*, sufficient to advise the world. The narrative is particularly concise. A wanderer and fisherman. Pan Zeyuan, a poor person from Tongcheng, Anqing, lived in the village of Sanjia outside the western gate of Tongcheng. Poor and with nothing to do, he only got up early to pick up lost items along the road, often exchanging them for money or grain. One day, he saw a person tie his horse to a tree and whip it, then unharness and ride away. Pan walked to that place and saw a leather bag, knowing it was left by the rider who was riding fast and could not be called back. Pan took the bag home, opened and looked inside, finding two hundred pieces of silver and one hundred taels of gold. His wife asked where it came from, and Pan told her the truth. She said, "You go quickly to the place where the money was picked up. If anyone comes looking for it, give it to them." Pan agreed. Seeing there was no place to put it, he had no choice but to open the earthen bed, put the bag in the hole, and left. He waited all day, and no one came looking for it.

The hermit of empty white said: "The poem says, 'The terror of death and loss, the deep affection among brothers. In the lowlands and highlands, brothers seek each other.'" It deeply speaks of the principle of mutual support in times of crisis, even good friends cannot compare. Yet, in times of peace, there are those who value friends more than siblings, how utterly contrary to reason! If we consider the case of Deng and his brother, it is also an example of mutual support in times of crisis. The brothers lived apart, leading to a lawsuit lasting three years. The local magistrate could not resolve it, and relatives and friends could not mediate. Seeing a one-legged fish, the brothers stopped the lawsuit. Officials and relatives and friends are not as effective as a turtle. Like the bell of the clear night, it warns and enlightens. This matter is also recorded in the *Good Deeds Book*, sufficient to advise the world. The narrative is particularly concise. A wanderer and fisherman. Pan Zeyuan, a poor person from Tongcheng, Anqing, lived in the village of Sanjia outside the western gate of Tongcheng. Poor and with nothing to do, he only got up early to pick up lost items along the road, often exchanging them for money or grain. One day, he saw a person tie his horse to a tree and whip it, then unharness and ride away. Pan walked to that place and saw a leather bag, knowing it was left by the rider who was riding fast and could not be called back. Pan took the bag home, opened and looked inside, finding two hundred pieces of silver and one hundred taels of gold. His wife asked where it came from, and Pan told her the truth. She said, "You go quickly to the place where the money was picked up. If anyone comes looking for it, give it to them." Pan agreed. Seeing there was no place to put it, he had no choice but to open the earthen bed, put the bag in the hole, and left. He waited all day, and no one came looking for it.

The following year, Pan saw a person who was beating an animal in anger. Pan tried to persuade him, saying, "The animals are unaware, why beat them so fiercely?" The person replied, "Last year, on the same day, I carried silver to the government office. The animal stopped here, and I got off to place the silver pouch on the ground and beat it, causing me to forget and leave it behind. Now, the animal stops here again, how could I not be angry?" Pan smiled and said, "Wasn't it you who lost the silver last year? I found it and left it unopened. Please come to my humble home to hand it over." The person looked up and down at Pan. Pan said, "Why are you looking at me? You must think that because I am poorly dressed and live in poverty, I would not have found the silver and left it for the person who lost it. If so, you truly judge me by common standards." Pan saw that the person did not believe him, so he said, "I will first tell you the amount of money you lost. If it does not match, then my words are false." He then revealed the amount. The person was extremely happy and quickly let go of what he was riding, leading it with him to follow Pan back. Upon arriving, he saw the simple bed and worn-out mat in the room, with nothing of value, and could not help but sigh. Pan said, "I will first hand over the lost silver to dispel your doubts." He opened the bed frame and pulled out the bag. The person opened it and inspected the contents, which were completely untouched, and was extremely surprised.

The person said, "Is there a wine shop in this village?" Pan replied, "No, there isn't. If you walk west for half a li, you will reach the outskirts where there are many wine shops." The person said, "You can buy wine and food, and we can drink together and chat a bit." Upon hearing this, Pan took a set of utensils and went to the wine shop. The person stopped him and asked, "Do you have money?" Pan laughed and said, "No, I don't. I will have to ask someone to buy it on credit." The person said, "It's okay." He then gave several hundred coins to Pan. Pan, having no choice, accepted the money and went to the wine shop. After some time, he returned. While drinking, the person began to inquire about Pan's name. Later, the person introduced himself as Liu, mentioning his achievements and saying he comes from a certain county in Fenshou, Western Province. There are several pawnshops in Anqing and the county, and the leftover money is the interest from the pawnshop in Tong County.

Afterwards, Liu pointed to the lost gold and said, "Since you have kept it to compensate me, I will give it all to you, but you will certainly not accept. Please accept 200 taels of silver as a token of gratitude." Pan refused. Liu said, "Would 100 taels be acceptable?" Pan also refused. While speaking, a young boy entered. Liu asked him, and Pan said, "The little boy is named Yu." Liu looked at him carefully and found that he had exceptionally good looks and uncommonly graceful behavior. He thought to himself, "Pan is so generous with his money; it is fitting that he should have such a blessed son." Then he said, "I have a daughter whose age is similar to your son's. I wish to marry our families." Pan considered the difference in wealth to be significant and firmly declined. Liu repeated his proposal several times, and Pan finally agreed. Liu gave 200 taels of silver to Pan and said, "Use this as a fund for my son's studies." Pan considered the gift from Liu to be for his son and it was not convenient to decline again. Liu said, "I will not be here for long; we will talk more when I come back." He finished speaking and left.

Pan Dekin, having lived comfortably for a while, returned to poverty after six or seven years. He told his wife, "Shall we all go to visit relatives in the western province?" His wife smiled and said, "He gave it to you but you did not accept it, so he pretended to form a marriage alliance to give gold as a gift. Why do you believe it to be true?" Pan replied, "Even if he does not recognize us as relatives, he must have some means to help us, which is better than starving to death here." Thus, he took his household belongings and led his wife and children on their journey. After a short while, they had traveled several tens of miles away from Liu's residence and passed through a certain village. As they passed by the gate of a large house, a drunk person came out and saw Pan. Pointing at Pan's second son, he asked, "Is this your son?" Pan answered that his second son was indeed five years old. The drunk person said, "There is a matter I would like to discuss with you. Please come to my humble abode." Pan agreed to go. It turned out that the person had a young daughter, and after drinking, he told her, "I have a great fortune, and I do not worry about finding a wealthy son-in-law."

The wife was displeased with his incoherent and tasteless drunken speech, and she said, "One person, one life." The man became angry and, upon encountering Pan, invited him to the guesthouse, asking about Pan's family and clan. He introduced himself as Jiao Shu, saying he had a daughter who was born in the same year as the second son of the official and wished to marry into the family. Pan was astonished and said, "I am poor and cannot support myself, having fled here due to disaster. How dare I marry into your family? Moreover, this matter should not be discussed after drinking." He then took his leave. Jiao firmly held onto him, immediately wrote a letter and gave it to Pan, saying, "Please sit for a while, I will prepare a simple drinking table, as a temporary meeting of relatives." Having said this, he staggered inside, entered the inner courtyard, vomited messily, and lay in bed unable to get up. The family told Pan, "The master is drunk, his words cannot be trusted. Please leave immediately, do not delay your journey." Pan then left. When he arrived at the entrance of Liu Gong's house, the gatekeeper said, "Breakfast is already over, you can come again at noon." Pan said, "I am not begging for food, I am actually visiting a relative." The younger one said, "Our master does not have such poor relatives." The older one said, "Do not say this."

Then, he inquired into the matter in detail, and Pan spoke truthfully. The person said, "Please wait a moment, I will report this to the superior." Liu asked about the guest's situation, and the doorkeeper replied, "He brings everything in one load." Liu said, "What does that mean?" The doorkeeper explained, "All his possessions, he brings in one load." Liu said, "If that is the case, there is no need to ask about his attire." First, he instructed his family to invite Pan to a quiet room, and then he sent maidservants to bring male and female clothes. After dressing, Liu emerged to greet him. After the formalities were completed, Liu said, "The road is far, and it is inconvenient for your close relative to come in person. I will choose a good day to send the bride. Your honor in coming today greatly comforts my heart." There was a spare room of a close friend on the same street, so he arranged for Pan to stay there, providing daily provisions and frequent banquets. When they spoke of the wedding, Pan said, "My wife is pregnant and will give birth soon. Please wait until she has delivered before we discuss it further." Liu agreed. Pan's wife gave birth late at night, and Pan buried the placenta. The pit where he dug was full of pebbles, and the same was true in other places.

Heated, it was not stone but white metal. Greatly delighted, he urgently called Ziyu to rise, and they moved it into the room, piling it under the bed, nearly filling the space. They immediately buried the umbilical cord like a treasure. At dawn, he saw Liu and told him about his wife's childbirth. Liu was pleased and said, "Last night, my brother dreamed that the wealth-attracting child came to your house. This child's wealth and destiny are boundless." Pan was convinced and then said, "My brother's residence is not suitable for long-term rental. If he wants to sell it, then I will buy it." Liu replied, "I will definitely buy it, but the price in silver is not yet determined, so there will be a slight delay." Pan said, "No matter how much the price is, I can fully pay it." Liu was surprised and asked, "Where does the silver come from?" Pan said, "Do not inquire in detail. As you say, it is approximately the result of the newborn's wealth and destiny." After buying the house, he then married a wife. The traditional wedding ceremony, the ritual of presenting a goose, was not as richly elaborate as in large households. They bought a large property, and the gold under the bed did not decrease. Within two or three years, they became wealthy and beautiful.

One day, while having a small drink with Liu, I asked him, "Do you know Jiao Shu from a certain village?" Liu replied, "He is a close friend." I said, "I have heard that he has a daughter who was born in the same year as my second son. I wish to arrange a marriage for them. Would this be acceptable?" Liu said, "Yes. I will act as a matchmaker." After getting drunk, Jiao Shu wrote a marriage agreement for Pan, but upon waking up, he deeply regretted it and was ashamed of breaking his word. Subsequently, anyone proposing marriage for the daughter would always be refused by saying that she was already betrothed. As the daughter grew older, her mother was deeply worried and told her, "Your betrothal to Pan is due to your father's drunken mistake, but it was really because I spoke too much and angered him." The daughter said, "Do not say so, it is fate. I also swear to be a member of the Pan family and will never marry into another family." The daughter's mother then informed Jiao about the daughter's words. Jiao planned to send someone to Anqing to investigate, but Liu Gong happened to arrive and spoke about the matter of Pan Zeyuan seeking a marriage.

One day, while having a small drink with Liu, I asked him, "Do you know Jiao Shu from a certain village?" Liu replied, "He is a close friend." I said, "I have heard that he has a daughter who was born in the same year as my second son. I wish to arrange a marriage for them. Would this be acceptable?" Liu said, "Yes. I will act as a matchmaker." After getting drunk, Jiao Shu wrote a marriage agreement for Pan, but upon waking up, he deeply regretted it and was ashamed of breaking his word. Subsequently, anyone proposing marriage for the daughter would always be refused by saying that she was already betrothed. As the daughter grew older, her mother was deeply worried and told her, "Your betrothal to Pan is due to your father's drunken mistake, but it was really because I spoke too much and angered him." The daughter said, "Do not say so, it is fate. I also swear to be a member of the Pan family and will never marry into another family." The daughter's mother then informed Jiao about the daughter's words. Jiao planned to send someone to Anqing to investigate, but Liu Gong happened to arrive and spoke about the matter of Pan Zeyuan seeking a marriage.

Jiao considered Liu to be his closest friend, so he wrote a letter to Master Pan after drinking, detailing his daughter's words as well. He also said, "My daughter has sworn that there is no other intention, and I will also firmly follow my daughter's will." Liu asked, "Do you know the great name of Master Pan?" Jiao replied, "I forgot it after drinking." Liu then told Jiao's words to Master Pan, who laughed and said, "What he called Master Pan is no one else but me. I thought his promise of marriage was a mistake due to his drunken state, but I didn't expect that his drunken words would be kept." After speaking, Master Pan took out Jiao's letter and showed it to Liu. Liu was overwhelmed with surprise and held the letter to see Jiao, narrating the whole story in detail. Jiao was extremely happy and quickly went inside to tell his wife. His wife smiled and said, "My one-word promise has not failed." Jiao laughed in agreement. He then went out and said to Liu, "Please take the original letter back. The marriage is already settled, there is no need to speak further, and you can serve as a silent matchmaker."

The third son of Pan is named Jú. When he grew up, he often went out and would sometimes encounter extremely poor people who would beg him for help. Jú tried to refuse, but he could not, so he pointed to the ground and said, "If you dig half a foot into this spot, you will find several taels of silver." They dug, and each time they found exactly the amount he had said. The Xubai Taoist said: Generosity is easier when one is wealthy, but harder when one is poor. People often suffer from the inner distress caused by poverty. Pan Zeyuan, for example, left behind lost items to repay their original owners, believing that external wealth does not benefit those destined to be poor. From this, we can infer that he was without flattery when in a humble position, without greed when faced with wealth, and acted in the world without harming others for personal gain. He did not engage in oppressive taxation or exploitation. All these qualities can be trusted. Therefore, it is not accidental that extreme poverty allows one to marry into a wealthy family and raise a son who attracts wealth and prosperity.

Qin Li Juan Mao Dashi, a scholar from Yizheng, became acquainted with Qin Deyuan from Jiangdu during his annual examination in Yangzhou. They had a disagreement over wealth and social status, but they became inseparable friends. In a moment of drinking, they made a promise of marriage, with Mao's son and Qin's daughter as the couple. After the decline of Mao's family, Qin wanted to break off the engagement. Qin's wife was unhappy and often scolded him. Qin finally used the lack of a marriage contract as an excuse. Mao knew about it and sued Qin. The judge also found no evidence against Mao and did not receive justice, dying of anger. At the beginning, after Mao and Qin became engaged, Mao once took his son Yu to the prefectural capital and stayed at Qin's house. At that time, the two children were innocent, and the wet nurse sometimes teased them, saying, "Does Master Mao want a wife?" Yu said, "Yes." She said, "How about your beautiful sister?" He said, "Good. But I'm afraid my sister will dislike me." Li said, "Not dislike." Li Juan grew up, and the family still told this story as a joke. When Li heard it, she was very embarrassed, and the family began to warn each other not to say it. This girl heard that her father wanted to break off the engagement and always wept and did not eat. Her mother comforted her, saying, "Do not be sad, your father's decision, I will never follow." Yu was already sixteen and wanted to file a lawsuit but had no money. He had no choice but to think of going to Qin's house to insult and humiliate him to vent his anger. When he arrived, Qin had just left and was not yet back. Qin's mother saw him and quickly called Li to see Mao's son. Li came, and they went into the room while the mother left, and she also told the family not to come forward. After several days, they gave generous gifts and left. When Qin returned, he said that he had already promised his daughter to a certain gentleman. Li knew about it and was worried day and night. Fortunately, the gentleman was wearing heavy mourning clothes, and the wedding would be delayed for some time.

Not long after, Li informed her mother of her pregnancy, and her mother was also distressed. A neighboring cloth merchant named Mao, from Hangzhou, returned home every two years. His wife, Yu, loved Li's gentle and quiet nature and adopted her as a foster daughter, often visiting each other. Li then went to Yu's room, weeping while kneeling for a long time. Yu was surprised and asked her in detail. Later, she said, "Sworn to remain faithful to one person is the first virtue of a woman, something that everyone is happy to help with. If a boy is born, please take care of him." When the child was born, the father took him away with him. At this time, Li found it hard to bear emotionally, but it was better than being abandoned in a dream. A certain official would soon complete his mourning period, and when the day for a joyous occasion arrived, Li and her maid Chunlan fled secretly at night. The next day, they stayed overnight at a farmer's house, which turned out to be Qin's tenant. The tenant secretly informed Qin, who was looking for the daughter. Receiving the information, Qin immediately rode away on horseback. While staying at the tenant's house, the tenant's wife was very hospitable but did not mention where to sleep, causing Li to suspect something. Suddenly, Li heard that the owner had arrived, and everyone went out to greet. Soon, a beautiful woman entered and said, "Your father has come. You can follow me to hide temporarily." Li and Chunlan met her father at the gate, saw him enter in a bad mood, and did not understand why he did not see her.

The Qin entered and did not see the girl, and along with the tenant woman, did not understand the reason why, and returned with a sense of disappointment. Lijuan followed the beautiful woman out, Chunlan followed her, and they entered a mountain cave together. Knowing in her heart that the beautiful woman was a fairy, she bowed and asked her. The woman said, "I am not a fairy, but a fox. But if the sister does not come with me, your respected father will carry her away and forcibly match her with a certain gentleman. The sister once shared the same bed with Mola, making her two husbands, and I know the sister has sworn not to do so." Lijuan said, "I only promised him, but we never actually met." The fox laughed and said, "Does the sister still remember the words 'Gentleman, please wait,' 'Gentleman, please wait'?" Lijuan heard this and her face turned red, saying, "Where was my sister at that time?" The fox replied, "The fox realm exists between the realms of yin and yang, so humans cannot see it. And while there are chaste women in the world, there are no chaste foxes. But we fear heavenly retribution and dare not act recklessly without a predestined bond. However, in matters of marriage between men and women, we often observe their voices and appearances to broaden our knowledge, thinking this is a great gathering in the human world. Alas! Humans are not as good as foxes." Chunlan said, "Since the fairy has bestowed kindness, why not allow her to see Mola to fulfill her unfulfilled wish?"

The fox said, "Not yet. The slow and fast separations and unions are also predestined. If what the maid says is true, then there would be no resentful women or unmarried men in the world. Wait until the gentleman dies, and both Mao Lang and his father become noble; that will be the time." The girl, upon hearing these words, realized that the matter of having a son with the fox sister was also thoroughly understood, and thus wanted to follow her to learn the true cultivation techniques. The fox said, "Since ancient times, there are no loyal and filial immortals. You may escape here, but Mao Lang and his mother are now eating sparse food and are not full; what to do? I have accumulated wealth; you should not personally present it as an introduction." Then she took out a pair of embroidered shoes and told the girl to wear them, saying, "This will not worry about traveling." After taking a few steps, she felt as if walking in the air like clouds. Not long after, they arrived at a large gate, where they saw the walls and fences in ruins, an old house with a dimly lit lamp still burning, and Mao Lang's mother sitting alone while Mao Lang stood by her side. The girl hurriedly entered and knelt down, and the mother was startled and asked, "Who are you?" The girl replied, "My daughter-in-law Qin." The mother asked, "How did you get here?" The girl answered, "No time to explain in detail." She then placed the silver on the table and wanted to leave. Mao Lang said, "Your family is looking for you urgently." The girl said, "That is why I am in such a hurry, for this reason." After speaking, she left, and Mao Lang also did not know why.

Ten years later, Mao entered school and, after another examination cycle, was again recommended as a scholar. The son of the Qin woman was raised by the Yu family and had already passed the imperial examination. After the announcement of the results, he was appointed as a county magistrate and assigned to Jiangsu. Before he left, the Yu family instructed him, saying, "Since you will be serving in this province, you can return home to visit your family." When he was near his hometown gate, the fox, Li Juan, and Chunlan had already arrived. Li Juan told her mother in detail about the kindness of the fox sister, and the mother also wept with gratitude. She ordered Yu to marry her daughter and also took Chunlan as a concubine. His son was named Baosheng and served as a county magistrate in Jiangdu for several years. The Qin family had fallen on hard times, and Baosheng remembered his mother's relatives and often provided them with assistance.

The Daoist Master Xubai said: If Qin Shi Huang had a sense of shame, he would not have eaten the crops of the people. In my town, there is someone like Qin Shi Huang, and someone joked with him, saying, "You have achieved the success of raising a daughter!" Seeing his face turn red with embarrassment. Jian Ruxian Jian Yudi, a scholar during the Ming dynasty, was from a noble family in Yunnan. He had a daughter named Ruxian, whom he named because her natural beauty was like that of a fairy. At the age of fifteen, a Daoist nun stayed at his home and saw the daughter. She was greatly delighted and said, "This is a celestial being, not an ordinary person of the mortal world." The daughter heard this and then took the Daoist nun as her teacher, learning Daoist practices for immortality. Jian was afraid that the daughter would be led astray and strongly refused the Daoist nun, sending her away. Coincidentally, there was a historian named Ye who wanted to marry the daughter to his son Changqing. Jian asked his wife to discuss the matter with the daughter, but she did not want to marry Ye, so he forced the marriage. The daughter had a pair of jade lions that she usually played with. The Ye family gave a dowry, and the jade lions were included in the return gifts. Since then, the daughter often cried and gradually reduced her food and drink. Jian was concerned and told her, "Ye has already proposed, and it cannot be changed. Moreover, his family is the most prestigious in the county. Why are you so depressed?" The daughter said, "I do not envy their wealth and status. I have a bold statement, and I hope you will accept it. My servant girl Wu is also from an official family. I hope you will adopt her as your own daughter and let her marry into the Ye family instead. I am determined to pursue Daoist practices and cannot obey orders blindly." After she finished speaking, she continued to cry. Jian thought the daughter was ill and pretended to agree, saying, "It is also possible. If you recover, we can discuss it further."

After her recovery, the girl lived with her maid, sharing meals and sleeping in the same room. Their affection for each other was no less than that of siblings. She often spoke to Wu about the matter of substitution, but Wu always dismissed it as nonsense and paid no heed. Three days before the wedding, as they were about to go to sleep, the girl told Wu, "My brother Ye has now passed the examination and entered the ranks of scholars. My sister marrying such a man would not be a disgrace." Wu was startled and asked, "Is what you said earlier true?" The girl replied, "What is there to be deceitful about? I have had a dream every night that my teacher called me. Parting is tonight. Do not disobey your father's command when you present the pair of geese during the wedding ceremony, for it would be a grave act of filial impiety." Wu asked, "Who is your teacher?" The girl answered, "She is He Xian Gu, one of the Eight Immortals." Wu sighed and said, "If you ascend to the ranks of immortals, do not forget your sister!" The girl responded, "What are you saying? My feelings for you are always on my mind. Although I have not yet become Ye's wife, my parents' command is not a trivial matter. I cannot treat Ye as a stranger!" As they spoke, the night grew late, and Wu fell asleep. Upon waking, he found the girl gone and quickly informed Zhan. Zhan knew something was unusual but did not search for her. When the groom came to fetch the bride, Wu's wife dressed up and left with the new groom. Ye and Wu had a harmonious relationship, unaware that the girl had left her family like a fish leaving its net.

Ye was upright and resolute, hating unjust matters. One day, while riding a horse to visit a friend, he passed through a pine forest and heard a very pitiful cry coming from inside. He dismounted and went to investigate. He saw one person sitting on the ground drinking, and another bound to a tree, naked and severely injured, with a very pitiful situation. He approached and asked, "What is going on here?" The bound man replied, "I am Zhang Zifang. I have a daughter, and the prominent official Su wanted to take her as a concubine. I refused at first, so he had his younger brother force me to accept the daughter in a deep part of the forest. They beat me mercilessly. Even now, I am willing to give her up, but they still won't let me go. Please help me, and plead for my mercy." Ye knew Su was a prominent official who liked to do cruel deeds, and his younger brother relied on his elder brother's power, being even more rebellious and defiant. He did not want to get involved, but feeling sorry for Zhang's innocence, he said, "If he has already repented, it seems reasonable to forgive him." Su's younger brother retorted, "What business is it of yours?" and ordered his followers to drag Ye away. Ye tried to argue, but the followers beat him with sticks. Ye had practiced martial arts and was skilled in combat. He became very angry, took the weapons from his followers, and killed Su's younger brother. The followers were frightened and scattered. Ye quickly freed Zhang and rode his horse home. By the time he arrived, it was already dusk. His wife, Wu, greeted him and said, "You should escape quickly, or else you will be in trouble! Hurry to the Tiger Crouching Valley in the deep mountains; there will be a savior." Ye asked, "How do you know this?" She replied, "I had a dream during my daytime sleep, and a god showed me the way." Ye believed her and hurriedly rode away.

Wu lived alone, preoccupied with his thoughts, and could not sleep for a long time. Deep into the night, he suddenly heard a knock on the door. Thinking it was the man returning, he asked who it was, and a woman's voice replied. He opened the door and saw a beauty beyond compare. The woman stood there weeping. When he asked her, she did not respond. After a long while, she said, "I am Zhang, the daughter of Zhang Zifang, whom the gentleman saved. My father returned from the pine forest and recounted the events. I went out and saw two men enter with weapons, claiming to be sent by the Grand Historian to kill my father. I hurriedly hid, and they searched for me but could not find me and left. I have no one to rely on and wish to seek refuge with the gentleman to serve as a concubine, or else serve the lady, dedicating my life as a servant." After speaking, she wept again. Wu said, "I also fear that misfortune will befall me, and I plan to seek refuge with the Zhang family to protect myself. The gentleman is far away on a journey; can you follow him?" She replied, "I can. But I fear that when we meet, the gentleman may not believe me." Wu said, "Do not worry about that." He then took out a leaf robe and had the woman dress as a man. After she was dressed, Wu looked at her and laughed, saying, "With such attire, those who do not know will see and create trouble out of nothing." He then gave her funds and a jade lion, saying, "This is a token of the couple." The woman was startled and asked, "What does this mean?" Wu then told her about the lady's practice of Daoist cultivation, his own substitution as a bride, and everything else. He also said, "The gentleman went out to search for his original wife, Lady Zhan." The temple bell announced the dawn, and the woman bid farewell to Wu and left on her own. ---

Ye Changqing, while fleeing from disaster, changed his name to Zhan Changqing and entered the deep mountains for more than a month. He did not hear of a place called Hu Fu Valley, so he sold his horse to raise funds and searched slowly. Later, he arrived at a small village surrounded by mountains, with steep cliffs reaching up to the sky, like a screen of brocade, which he admired. He then rented a room to live in and enjoyed the scenery freely. Just then, the private teacher of the village had died, so he took over the teaching position. He was in the midst of seeking refuge and had no hope of divine intervention. Zhang's daughter had learned divination from her father and was skilled in the art. After leaving her army, she made a living by selling divination services and had experienced many hardships. She later traveled to the village where Ye Changqing was teaching. Before this, there was a farmer named Jing in the village who had a very beautiful daughter, who was of marriageable age but still unmarried.
