One day, a Taoist priestess stayed at the farmer's home and looked at the girl, saying, "This girl should marry someone from afar."

Jing laughed and said, "I have been a farmer all my life, tilling the land, and I have never traveled more than thirty miles from home. I have no connections outside my village, so how could I marry someone far away?" The nun replied, "Five days later, there will certainly be a person from afar coming. She will be as beautiful as jade, dressed in formal attire, holding a divination board, and she will be a fortune-teller." Jing shook his head without speaking. The nun said, "If there is no one coming five days later, then my words will be seen as false." Jing believed her and began counting the days in anticipation. When the time came, Zhang arrived at the village. As she passed Jing's door, Jing observed her appearance, clothing, and the divination board she held, and everything was exactly as the nun had said. He was greatly pleased. He invited her to his home, served her wine, and asked her many questions. The woman lied and said her name was Ye, that her parents had both passed away, and that she had no other family. When asked about her age, she claimed to be seventeen. Jing said, "She is the same age as my daughter." This was a slip of the tongue by Jing, and the woman did not investigate further.

Jing said, "Are you skilled in fortune-telling?" The other replied, "I have a rough understanding." After the meal, Jing led him to inspect the house and called out his daughter, entrusting her with household affairs afterward. The girl pointed to Jing's daughter and asked, "Who is this for you?" He replied that it was his younger daughter. Glancing at her, she was beautiful, graceful, and elegant, both beautiful and charming. He thought to himself, "How beautiful! Even I find her lovely, how much more so for men?" Jing then asked, "How about my humble abode?" The other responded, "The door faces southeast (Xun) and the main room is the entrance hall (Kan). The kitchen is located in the east (Zhen). It is one of the four auspicious houses. However, the kitchen is slightly northward, partially belonging to the northeast (Gen). The northeast (Gen) receives the southeast (Xun), and the east (Zhen) wood overcomes the northeast (Gen), indicating a younger son, which suggests that the younger children will not thrive." Jing said, "That is true, my wife has given birth to four sons, but only one survived."

"Like this, what do you think?" the girl said. "The kitchen has three bays; if we move the door to the south bay, it will be in harmony with the principle of water nourishing wood, and there will be no hindrance." At that time, the whole family was watching the girl, and Jing Girl in particular. The girl secretly smiled and thought, "He must think I am a man; it's a pity that my thoughts are misused." She then bid farewell. Jing insisted on keeping her. The next day, there were people from the village who came to discuss marriage, saying that Jing wanted to marry her to a woman. The girl declined, saying, "I am a traveler, away from home by thousands of miles, and I will return soon. How can I marry here?" The matchmaker said, "We have already discussed this. When you return, you will follow through." The girl replied, "I am in mourning attire. Only after the completion of the major mourning period can marriage be discussed. How can I wait?" The matchmaker said, "This is a major ritual. How can you not wait for it?" The girl thought, "My heart's desires have not been fulfilled, and my travel funds are nearly exhausted. It might be better to agree temporarily and wait until finding the right person before making further plans." So she stayed with Jing, secretly seeking out Ye Sheng, and no longer wandered around.

Jing's son also studied under Ye. One day, Jing invited Ye to drink wine, with Zhang accompanying them. Zhang heard Ye speak in a way that suggested they were from the same hometown. She asked him about his hometown, and upon learning that it was far away, she became suspicious. When Jing was not around, she tested Ye by saying, "Are you from the Zhan family? Or are you a son-in-law of the Zhan family?" Ye's face changed, and he remained silent. The woman concluded that he was Ye, and then said, "In our county, there is someone named Ye Changqing. Do you know who that is?" Ye suddenly turned pale. The woman said, "I specially came here to find my elder brother." Ye was startled and asked, "Are you acting on behalf of Susu?" She replied, "No, I came here at the command of Lady Zhan Wu." Ye smiled and said, "You're mistaken! How can one person have two surnames?" She said, "The reason for the two surnames, you already know, why ask?" After speaking, she took out a jade lion and showed it to him, asking, "Do you recognize this?" Ye was greatly startled and thought to himself, "How could a family treasure end up in the hands of an outsider?" Just as he was about to take it to examine it closely, the woman put it back in her sleeve. Ye asked, "Where did this come from?" While they were talking, Jing returned. The woman said, "I will tell you in detail when we have free time." After the meal, Ye invited the woman to move the jade. She replied that she would come later. Ye waited until dusk and personally invited her. The woman asked to meet the next day, but Ye did not agree and grabbed her wrist, urging her to hurry. The woman had no choice but to follow him. Upon arrival, she gave the jade lion to Ye, who looked at it and confirmed it was indeed a family treasure.

When asked about the origin, the girl smiled but did not speak. Ye said, "Are you and I of the same surname? The surname Ye is very rare in this town, with only a few families, all of which are my relatives. By inference, your not being surnamed Ye is like my not being surnamed Zhan." The girl said, "I wanted to take the surname Ye, so I adopted it. I am actually surnamed Zhang, the son of Zhang Zifang whom you released in the pine forest before." Upon hearing this, Ye looked up and down at the girl. He laughed and said, "Why are you looking at me like that?" Ye said, "It's hard to turn falsehood into truth, and it's hard to turn truth into falsehood. You are not the son of the Zhang family, but you are almost half of the Zhang family. With such beauty, do not blame me for not being able to obtain what I desire and thus not being able to let go." The girl said, "I first met you, why did you tease me so much?" Ye said, "It was not me teasing you, but you deceiving me." He immediately took off the girl's cloth boots and said, "Do you still want to deny it?" The girl blushed and remained silent, and they became intimate. They recounted past events, filled with a mix of sorrow and joy. The girl asked to continue wearing male clothing and pretend to be friends, and Ye laughed and agreed. One day, during the celebration of Jing's initial birthday, a banquet was set up in the inner courtyard. The girl took the opportunity to say, "I am not unwilling to be a son-in-law, but I am not the type to be a son-in-law." She then recounted the reason for escaping from trouble, visiting Ye, and meeting by chance, and took off her shoes to show her lower body. Jing's grandparents saw this and were greatly surprised, not knowing what to do. The girl said, "Ye is a scholar, a descendant of a prominent family, in the prime of youth, with no fixed return date. If he marries here as a son-in-law, it would be beneficial for both parties."

If you do not consider my words as high as the river Han, I wish to marry your eldest daughter and then your second daughter. The old man and the old woman were both pleased, so they chose a good day to hold the wedding ceremony. After the wedding, the woman said, "This is what is meant by the鹬蚌相持,渔人得利!" She then told the new husband, "This man is not just your husband; he is also my second husband. You should return to me the next night and do not be greedy and keep him here." The man laughed and scolded her. The man wanted to keep the woman with him, but the woman said, "I am not used to seeing live action pornography." One day, the three of them were playing under the moon and had not yet gone to sleep when they suddenly heard a loud cry from the village. The man went out to ask what was happening. It turned out that a villager who made a living by cutting wood was killed by a tiger in the valley, so his family was crying. The man asked which mountain the valley was in and how far it was from there. The villagers said, "It is southeast of here, about dozens of miles away, in the middle of Mount Mo Tian." The man went back and told the two women about his plan to go and investigate the next day. The woman wanted to go with him, and the man agreed, so they both went there on horseback. After traveling about fifty miles, the horse could not go any further, so they wanted to leave the horse and walk. The villagers said, "From here on, there is only danger, no benefit!" The man heard this and asked where the valley was. The villager pointed it out from a distance. The man asked, "Is there a cave in the valley?" The villager said, "It is said that there is a cave in the dense forest of pine and cypress. The place is deep and dangerous, and no one dares to go there. Two years ago, someone saw two women holding hands enter the valley, but no one has heard of them coming out." The woman said, "One of the two women must be Jizhen!" The man said, "Yes."

After lingering for a long time, they returned home with a heavy heart. Leaf said, "Not knowing about the blessed land and having no place to escape to is a matter of fate; knowing about the place and waiting for death is not a matter of fate." Zhang said, "I will try to investigate it." Leaf said, "Is there anyone who would willingly seek death knowing it is certain? Why are you so willing to sacrifice yourself?" Zhang said, "Not so. A wife is to her husband as a subject is to her ruler. Ancient loyal ministers and filial sons helped their rulers and fathers in times of difficulty. When they went, did they expect to return safely? They simply did not fear. Moreover, I have heard that the valley is full of tigers, but I have never seen one. How can I believe such rumors? Even if there are tigers, perhaps I will encounter them at other times and manage to enter the cave. If I meet harm, it will be for my husband's sake. How can you judge me so lightly?" Leaf said, "Although so, you must not go. I am burdening you, and I feel deeply uneasy." Zhang said, "I can do without myself, but not without you. My mind is made up." The next morning, she took the jade lion and set off. Leaf said, "What use is this?" Zhang said, "This is the property of the Zhan family, and it is necessary to prove my identity." Leaf saw Zhang walk two or three miles and then disappear behind trees and rocks, sighing and returning home.

Zhang, entering the valley alone, walked several miles beyond, where the ground was covered with animal tracks and bird prints, and there were no human paths. Upon entering further, he heard the roars of tigers and the cries of monkeys, causing his hair to stand on end like a hedgehog. After about twenty miles, suddenly two tigers leaped up and rushed directly towards Zhang. Seeing this, Zhang, who was unarmed, could not help but raise the jade lion to make a remote attack gesture. The tigers, seeing the jade lion, passed by him and returned to lie down on the path he had come from, not daring to advance. Zhang did not understand why this was happening and wanted to turn back, but the two tigers blocked his way. He then held the jade lion and continued forward. Subsequently, he encountered more tigers, all of which avoided him. Before sunset, he reached the cave entrance, where the green cypress and dark pine trees were mostly two or three times the size of a person. Looking back, he saw two tigers lying at the cave entrance, one on each side. Zhang exclaimed, "I have come to this place and no longer fear you!" and raised the jade lion and entered the cave confidently. Inside, a Taoist nun was sitting upright, with closed eyes as if sleeping.

Seeing Zhang, she was startled and said, "How did you come to be here?" Seeing Zhang holding the jade lion, she asked again, "Are you of the Ye family?" Zhang replied, "My surname is Ye, but I am not of the Ye family." She then asked, "Why have you come?" Zhang then detailed the reasons for his arrival, explaining that he had fled due to an incident involving Ye Gong, a nobleman who had caused trouble. The nun heard this and was suddenly filled with alternating emotions of sorrow and joy. Zhang, realizing that the nun must be Zhan, asked, "Are you Nuns Zhan?" Zhan remained silent. Zhang continued, "Is it not you who was substituted in marriage for the sister? We were mistakenly caught in a situation and cannot escape. It would be better to place yourself outside the situation and enjoy freedom!" Upon hearing this, Zhan helped Zhang up and sighed, "My body is free, but my mind remains entangled. Being separated in this way, the suffering is especially severe. Otherwise, how could my sister have come here safely and unharmed?" Zhang was confused and did not understand. Zhan explained, "I usually use other treasures to subdue tigers, but I showed the jade lion to them, and they all fled in fear. The jade lion is a plaything of Ye Yur, a nobleman, and he must always wear it. If he comes here, I can borrow it to avoid tiger attacks and use it to enter the cave temple."

My heart is like this, still considering my sisters as outsiders?" Zhang suddenly understood and continuously expressed his gratitude. He asked, "What should I do about the matter of the jade person?" Zhan replied, "Do not call it the same as me when referring to my sister's jade person." Zhang smiled and said, "If you just said it a moment ago, why should others not say it?" Zhan blushed and said, "Within three to five days, my teacher will surely come. When he comes, you can urgently ask him for help." Soon after, the fairy arrived. Seeing Zhang, she asked, "Is this Zhang who bears a strange injustice?" Zhang wept and recounted his story. The fairy said, "Do not do that! I have my own way to deal with it." When mentioning Ye Sheng, she said, "I have my own way to save him." When speaking of Wu and Jing, she said, "I have my own way to comfort them." She told Zhang, "My disciple's parents betrothed a daughter to Ye. If we violate this, there will be divine retribution. You can guide Ye here to complete the marriage." Zhang was very happy but dared not act. The fairy handed him a sword and said, "If you encounter any danger, draw the sword and throw it. It will turn misfortune into good fortune."

Zhang bowed and received the instructions before departing, not knowing why he traveled as swiftly as a flying arrow, arriving in an instant. He spoke with Ye and Jing, and Ye was eager to go, while Jing was reluctant. Ye promised to return by the middle of the month, and Jing finally agreed. As they were about to depart, three men suddenly entered from outside, each holding a weapon, saying they were official agents sent to arrest the wanted criminal Ye. Ye was in a state of panic and had no idea what to do. Zhang quickly drew his sword and threw it into the air, and suddenly three heads fell to the ground. In an instant, the bodies turned into blue smoke, which was blown away by the wind. Ye was greatly relieved and bid farewell to Jing before setting out. Upon reaching the cave, he first paid his respects to the immortal lady, and then married Jiang. As he was about to sleep, Zhang told Jiang, "What happened tonight, Wu cannot replace it." The next day, the immortal lady said, "All three of you have a connection to immortality. Even though you are in the cave, you will not be lonely. However, you should not be idle. The methods of spiritual cultivation, Jiang can teach you. In your spare time, you can ride the clouds and travel through the lower realms, bringing blessings to the virtuous and misfortune to the wicked, acting on behalf of heaven. Your merit and virtue will be immeasurable. Three years later, I will come to evaluate your spiritual progress." She then taught them the magical techniques of the immortals, and only after they had mastered them did she depart.

For more than a year, Ye lived in a cave. One day, he told Zhan, "I often dream of meeting people from the Wu and Jing families, and I feel as though I am managing household affairs with them, yet my body is still in the cave. What could be the reason for this?" Zhan replied, "What is false can become true, and what is true should not be thought of as false. You are enjoying blessings in three places at once, and the master has meticulously planned for you. Why do you view this as a dream?" Ye then understood. Three years later, the goddess indeed arrived. She saw that Ye and others had completed their spiritual cultivation and was overjoyed. One day, she went down the mountain with Ye and others to tour and observe. As they were passing, two officials were escorting a criminal official who was passing by the foot of the mountain. Suddenly, a tiger appeared in the ravine and went directly toward the criminal official. Zhang wanted to go and save him, but the goddess stopped him. Zhang watched as the tiger bit the criminal official, satisfied itself with his flesh, and then left. Zhang said, "In the past, the master had compassion as his heart. Why does he now sit and watch without saving?" The goddess said, "The criminal official is not someone else; he is your enemy, Su." Zhang was shocked upon hearing this. The goddess pointed to a tool that was carried on the back and said, "This is your meritorious person. It transformed into your true form, used a false name to file a complaint in the capital, caused Su to be dismissed from office, and therefore incurred punishment and was sent to military service. The road passed through this place." Zhang prostrated on the ground and bowed to thank, saying, "I had long wanted to take revenge, but I did not receive the master's command and therefore dared not go privately. Now, I am able to see my enemy die in the tiger's belly, no less satisfying than killing with my own hands. How much more satisfying than that!"

Ye Changqing had a close friend named Peng Lian, who had spent over forty years in official service. After retiring, Peng returned to his hometown and sailed on Lake Dongting. Suddenly, two ornate boats appeared in front of him, decorated with luxurious and intricate designs. Inside one of the boats, a young man was seated prominently, with two beautiful women beside him. They had bright eyes, white teeth, and a cloud-like appearance, which was rare in the world. Peng recognized the young man as Ye Changqing and called out, "Ye, are you well?" Ye stopped his boat and invited Peng to join him. They reorganized the wine and food, and the two friends drank together. Peng said, "It has been over forty years since we last saw each other. Counting your age, you should be approaching seventy. How is it that you still look so youthful?" Ye replied, "I have learned the secret of preserving youth from immortals, so I do not feel the approach of old age." Peng asked, "Who was the woman who drank with you just now?" Ye answered, "She is my wife."

" Pengya said, "I heard that you live in the mountains and still cannot often return to your old residence, how can you remarry in the two lakes?" Ye replied, "The story is very long, I did not have time to explain in detail. I have not returned to my mountain retreat for several years, and I have a family letter that I dare to trouble you to deliver on your way." Pengya agreed. Ye helped him with travel expenses and gave him pearls and jade, worth several thousand coins. Pengya was extremely pleased, received the letter, and set out. Upon arrival, he saw Ye's gate bustling like a market and his house magnificent, something he had never seen before. After meeting, Pengya realized that Ye was an elderly person with white hair, which surprised him. Pengya asked, "I met you at Dongting Lake yesterday, why did you return so quickly?" Ye said, "Not so. I stayed in Tiger Rest Valley for more than a month and did not go far away, how could I have the ability to be in two places at once and go to Dongting Lake?" Pengya said, "Your family letter, I have already brought it." He wanted to take it out and give it to Ye, but upon searching, it was already gone. Pengya was quite doubtful and surprised. During the conversation, he asked, "How is your legal wife, Wu Lady?" Ye said, "I do not know. Perhaps Wu Lady has remarried someone else and has many descendants."

After speaking, he called out the two sons and three grandsons to meet with Peng, saying, "The etiquette of seniority cannot be neglected." Peng lingered for several days before departing. The son of Jing's family thus came to know about his legitimate mother, Wu. Peng's family lived in a neighboring village to Ye. Upon returning home, he told his old mother about the strange encounter with Ye. She said, "How can you speak falsely? Before, Ye injured someone and fled in difficulty for three months before returning. His wife Wu, because of her unusual appearance, shut the door and did not admit him. In a moment, he regained his true appearance, saying that a celestial being gave him a false face to avoid disaster, and Wu began to believe him. Not long after, she heard that the authorities had captured Ye; not long after, she heard that Ye died in prison. Wu led the body for burial, which turned out to be a walking stick. Later, she heard that Zhang sued in the capital; she also heard that Su was punished and sent to military service, dying on the way. Ye then dared to show his true appearance to people. Now his second son is the magistrate of a county, and this year, his two grandsons were recommended together for the provincial examination. Guests come and go, and the glory is at its peak.

Peng, upon hearing this, became even more perplexed. He hurriedly prepared ceremonial objects and went himself to the Ye family to congratulate them. Upon seeing Ye's demeanor and appearance, which were exactly the same as what he had seen in the mountain retreat, Peng then recounted all that he had encountered. Ye said, "Only I, in the mountain retreat, married Jing as my wife is true; the rest is all fictional." All the descendants of Wu knew that there was a concubine mother in the mountain retreat. The eldest son, Huai De, harbored doubts about the matter and visited Peng to ask in detail. Peng explained it in detail. De said, "If so, my father must be a divine being." He excused himself to go elsewhere and secretly invited Peng to visit the mountain retreat. Jing's sons, Huai Yi and Huai Yu, received him with great enthusiasm. De wanted to pay respects to his father, but Yi said, "A few days ago, father said that Peng's guest and the eldest brother would definitely arrive on a certain day. I cannot stay in the human world anymore to shock the ears of the world. This morning, when I visited father, he was already gone." De stayed for several days and wanted to return. Yi wanted to return together to visit father and also pay respects to the principal mother. De agreed. When they returned home, people said that the grandfather had gone out a few days ago and had not returned. De, Yi, and others all thought that their father might come again, but with no news for several years, they also had no hope anymore.

One day, Wu was conversing with his descendants when suddenly three young men and women entered in fine attire. Upon closer inspection, they were identified as his husband and the ladies Zhan and Zhang. He was greatly pleased. He explained to his family, instructing each to pay their respects in turn. Zhang returned home to bury his parents, while Ye and Zhan went to visit their parents. Zhang, being the adopted son, completed the burial of his parents, and then Ye and the others came to bid farewell to Wu, who disappeared in the blink of an eye. The recluse Master Xubai remarked: "Ye Sheng enjoys blessings of both the divine and earthly realms, yet he possesses such virtue? It is because of his straightforward and upright nature." From this, we can understand that those with small virtues enjoy small blessings, and those with great virtues enjoy great blessings; if one has no virtue, they are inevitably subject to the six calamities, and what blessings could they have? The story of Peng Lian and his mother is used to fill in the details of three locations and events that were not fully explained earlier, and the writing style is intricate and masterful. — Ma Zhuwu This piece has many threads, and the interplay and connections between them all reveal the author's ingenuity. After reading it, one can imagine the effort and planning that must have gone into creating such a fine composition. Some say that even with *Strange Tales from a Chinese Studio*, this book cannot be passed down to future generations, but I say that with this book, *Strange Tales from a Chinese Studio* cannot excel before it. Readers of the future may not consider my words mistaken! — Yin Yishan

The entire narrative is structured around a Daoist nun, with many threads intertwining but none becoming disordered, demonstrating exhaustive and meticulous planning and execution. Cheng BoFu Feng Qing Feng Qing, courtesy name ShengYu, was from Daming. He was generous with his wealth and valued friendship, but as a result, his family's fortunes gradually declined. In the past, his guests were as sparse as morning stars and waning crescent moons, and he began to realize the coldness of the world and the futility of superficial friendships. One day, while out in the countryside, he saw a person lying on the ground, knowing that the person was a sick traveler. Feng said to the guest, "My humble home is not far away. Would it be an inconvenience for you to stay for two nights? After you recover, you can continue your journey. Would that be acceptable?" The guest replied, "If so, I would be grateful as if reborn." Feng then helped the guest to his home. The guest introduced himself as Bai from the Zhou dynasty, originally from Dongchang. Feng also introduced himself to the guest and quickly arranged a bed for him to rest. He thought the guest would certainly leave the next morning despite his illness. However, the guest's condition worsened, and he could not get out of bed. Feng sought a doctor to treat him, and his wife even pawned her jewelry to help. After twelve days, the guest's illness began to improve. Previously, there was a wealthy family in the village whose daughter had contracted a ghostly illness and was near death. She had repeatedly mentioned that someone could cure her and offered a certain amount of gold as a reward. Feng and Bai were drinking together when the topic came up. Bai happily said, "If that is true, there is no need to worry about the unscrupulous. I am skilled in talismans and spells, but after the illness, my vital energy has not yet recovered, so it is not advisable for me to do this. I will teach you to perform it instead." Feng went to the girl's house, and indeed, her illness was cured. Five days later, the wealthy man indeed brought gold to the door to express his gratitude. Bai then fully taught Feng his skills. When Bai was about to leave, Feng's wife had just passed away; after the funeral, they parted ways with tears. They asked about their future meeting. Bai said to Feng, "I travel without a fixed place, and we will meet again in the future, but I cannot set a date in advance."

After Cui Zibai left, his magical abilities became widely known and sought after. Those who presented gifts or credentials came one after another to his door, and Cui Zibai's financial situation improved as a result of the demand for his services. Suddenly, an elderly man of the So family presented himself to Cui Zibai, claiming to be troubled by a ghost and requesting his assistance. Cui Zibai followed him and performed a ritual, using magical symbols on water, before returning home. That evening, a beautiful woman pulled back the curtain and entered. Cui Zibai was surprised to see her and asked who she was. She replied, "I am the ghost that was troubling the So family. The entire family of the So family are fox spirits, and I love the So family member for her pleasant demeanor and enjoy her companionship. The ghost harms fox spirits, not humans, so what does this have to do with you?" Cui Zibai, being newly widowed and attracted to her beauty, could not help but embrace her. She said, "Don't do that. The ghost that harms the So family's fox spirit, you can drive away, but can you drive away the ghost that harms the fox spirit? You are truly not one who drives birds into a thicket, but one who is skilled at driving birds away from the thicket."

Although I am a ghost, I am still not favorable to foxes, how much less so humans? If I were a human, I would willingly serve them for life." Cui was extremely happy and they then became intimate. The ghost said, "This matter can happen once but not again. The woman Wan'er from the Suo family is even more beautiful than I am, and he is currently using you. If he marries her to you, then he will treat you, otherwise, he will make an excuse not to come. His love for his son is stronger than his love for his daughter, and he cannot help but marry her to you." Cui agreed to this. The Suo family, after Cui's talismanic water, saw the ghostly disturbances cease, and the father was greatly pleased. However, after five days, the ghost returned, and the disturbances were even more severe. The ghost then said to the sick person, "Cui is not coming anymore, he wants to marry your sister as his second wife. If you do not agree, you will not be able to live again!" The sick person told his father. The Suo family did not believe him, and quickly sent someone with heavy gifts to invite Cui, but Cui indeed did not come. The Suo family, having no choice, agreed to his words and consented. Cui then stayed in the Suo household, every day pretending to set up altars and burn talismans.

After half a month, the ghost suddenly said, "All the family has gone out, leaving only Wan'er alone. Sir, enter her room, and your long-held wish can now be fulfilled." Cui then walked directly into the girl's bedroom. Wan'er was startled and asked, "Why have you come?" Cui replied, "My respected parents have betrothed you to me; do you not know?" Wan'er said, "I know. But it is proper to meet during the wedding; now is not the time." Cui said, "Now truly the heavens have granted our wishes; I earnestly hope you will show mercy." Wan'er said, "I am a lowly woman, but I know some etiquette. Even though I have my parents' permission, to act prematurely and disrupt it, similar to peeping through holes, I truly dare not comply." Cui persisted in his request. Wan'er tried to resist but found her limbs bound, because the ghost of the girl secretly bound her, and she then allowed Cui to do as he wished. Wan'er said, "You used your magic to compel me; if circumstances change, even if I cannot die, I will never be unfaithful to two husbands; you should wait for me."

"Please hurry," the ghost said. "If you meet them, you will feel ashamed and embarrassed." Cui then left. The ghost continued, "I will leave, and your matter will certainly succeed. You can agree on a date to send her and return." Cui followed the ghost's advice. By the agreed date, there was no news, so the ghost went to investigate and returned saying, "The whole family has disappeared!" Despite being angry, Cui could do nothing about it. After more than a year, suddenly Wan'er came with a child. Cui was startled and asked, "Who is the child she is holding?" The woman replied, "It is your illegitimate child, born four months ago. The family disliked him because he did not resemble them and wanted to harm him. I feared that our precautions were not tight enough, so I thought it better to take the opportunity to bring him." She left the child and wanted to go, but Cui held her arm and tried to persuade her to stay. She said, "We can spend three days together. My father has an urgent matter and has gone elsewhere. He will return in four days." Cui asked, "A woman should follow her husband. Why are you so afraid of your father?" She replied, "My father obtained the paired lotus and lily bracelet, which is skilled at controlling foxes. Both near and far, old foxes are afraid of him. How much more so for me?" Three days later, she left.

One day, Cui and the female ghost were having fun with a child by the lamp, when Wan'er suddenly arrived, her clothes tattered and her face pale and gaunt. Cui was greatly startled and was about to ask her what had happened. She said, "You have killed me! My father did not see your child, knew to send it back to you, and became very angry, forcing me to remarry. I refused, and he locked me in a cold room, feeding me meager meals that were not filling. I beg for quick rescue; if delayed, I will die in prison!" She then tearfully left. Cui thought that the only one he could discuss this with was Mr. Bai, but after they parted, he never came once. Cui was hesitating when he heard a knocking at the door. He opened it to look, and there was Bai. Cui was overjoyed beyond expectation and told him about what happened to Wan'er. Bai said, "My younger brother can also subdue foxes, but he is not very skilled, and I fear he will be humiliated. You can find my teacher to learn the skill." And then he hurried away. Shortly afterward, Wan'er returned, crying and saying, "Your friend Mr. Bai wrote three talismans consecutively, and took my father away!" Cui said, "What my younger brother did is exactly what I wanted to do but couldn't; he is truly my good friend!" Wan'er angrily replied, "My original intention was truly not to go this far! When I asked you for help, I was asking for help for myself, not to control my father."

Although I have not yet served you, I have already given you a child. The relationship between husband and wife has been established, and the distinction between father-in-law and son-in-law is now set. How unkind can you be like this?" After saying this, she angrily wanted to leave. Cui tried to persuade her, saying, "I listened to Brother Bai act on his own without encouraging him, which is repaying a grudge with kindness." The woman replied, "What grudge do I have? You forced my father to marry, and forced me to sleep with you to conceive. If you hadn't killed me to remove the disgrace, that would have been a great kindness. You don't consider this kindness, but instead see it as a grudge?" Cui said, "At this time, you might as well stay a little longer to enjoy the joy of being husband, wife, and mother-in-law." She said, "If my father is indeed unharmed, I will not leave you." Cui was pleased and agreed to see Bai. As they were about to leave, the woman said, "My father's twin-leaf萱flower belt, I think it must be in Bai's hands. Please make sure to retrieve it. If the fox gets this, it will easily become a spirit." Cui nodded and left. When he arrived at Bai's place, he saw a fox tied to the side of the trough. Bai said, "Your coming here, is it still because of this old beast?" The woman replied, "Yes. But this thing is my uncle, my wife's father, and my son's great-grandfather." Bai said, "When talking about this, I cannot help but forgive you." Looking back, the fox was no longer there, and he took the belt back.

After the woman obtained the belt, she suddenly changed her previous conduct. Her son was raised by a female ghost, and she sat alone in a quiet room, meditating and reflecting. Cui did not call her four times, and she did not go to the bedroom. She then told Cui, "A ghost cannot be a suitable partner, and I am also not suitable to be in the position of a lady. The daughter of someone named Si from the neighboring village is very beautiful, and she has just recently died. You should ask the matchmaker to say that she can be brought back to life." Si was delighted and immediately asked the matchmaker to invite Cui to revive the girl. Wan'er accompanied the female ghost to the place, pushed the ghost to merge with the body, and the girl of Si immediately revived. On that day, Si immediately treated Cui with the formalities of a son-in-law and set a date for the wedding. From then on, Wan'er could focus on quiet修养 and no longer sang like a rooster. She still made clothes and shoes for her son occasionally, often claiming to go into the mountains to gather herbs and always returning once a month. As the son grew older, his visits became less frequent, and eventually he stopped coming altogether.

This piece can be read alongside "Changting Zhuan" from *Liaozhai Zhiyi*. —尹亦山 It writes about ghosts and fox spirits with deep emotion, touching the heart. —马竹吾 Ox Repaying a Debt (the manuscript reads "Changshan Someone") A person from Changshan, who had always been morally corrupt and only understood profit, was asked by a man surnamed Fu to borrow a hundred gold coins. The person took the promissory note and used the loaned money for himself. When Fu found out, he asked him. The person pretended to be angry, thinking he had already handed the money to Fu. The lender used the promissory note as evidence and was going to sue Fu. Fu, fearing legal action, paid back the principal and interest on time. After repaying the money, a domesticated ox in his household died suddenly. Fu sighed that misfortunes never come alone, feeling depressed and covering his head to sleep. He dreamed that the person came to see him, looking sad and different from usual. He asked what the person came for, and the person said, "I came to work off the debt." Upon waking, the herder told him the ox had died and then revived. Fu doubted and went to check, and indeed the ox was lying as usual. He said, "Did you really come?" The ox heard this and suddenly stood up, raising its head and opening its mouth as if to speak. The herder asked, and Fu told him about the dream. From then on, the entire family beat and scolded the ox to resolve their old grudge. This continued for eight years, and the person also fell ill and could not get up, with marks of beatings on his body. One day, Fu dreamed that the person said, "I have worked for a long time, but the repayment has no end. Please go sell your property to repay me." Fu woke up, knowing the ox would die, and indeed it did. He felt very sorry for it and no longer expected repayment.

Upon hearing that someone had recovered from illness, he hastily sold his good farmland and sent an invitation to invite a teacher. When the teacher arrived, the host treated him with great courtesy as if he were a family member and said with a sigh, "The silver I borrowed in the past was actually misappropriated by me. I humbly hope you will accept it." The teacher replied, "Why bother? The matter is past, and it can be forgiven." Suddenly, the host started wheezing like a cow and said, "If you truly do not show compassion, you are indeed cruel and harmful to me." Seeing the host's distressed state, the teacher said, "Can we settle the interest and return the principal based on eight years of labor?" The host was pleased and, at the end of the meal, sent someone to deliver the silver to the teacher's home. Later, this person conducted transactions fairly and was praised throughout the village.

The Daoist Master Xubai said: Debts should be repaid promptly, for it is the right thing to do, especially in such unjust matters. Those who borrow under threat or deceit for gambling and debauchery are far worse than those who trade without capital or idle without shame. In the northern outskirts of my hometown, there was a farmer's uncle named Mr. Someone, who was a drunkard and gambler of disreputable character. He repeatedly demanded loans from his nephew. When told he lacked the means to repay, he angrily struck himself with a stone, breaking his own head, and cried out that he had been beaten by his nephew, insisting he would not stop until he received money. One day, while the nephew was napping, he suddenly saw his uncle appear and asked in shock, "Have you spent all the money?" The uncle replied, "Yes. But today I am not here to borrow; I am here to repay the debt." The nephew was confused and startled, but by the time he realized it, the uncle had already disappeared. Upon waking, he knew it was a dream and found it strange. His wife laughed and said, "A horse gave birth to a mule." He replied, "The mule is my uncle!" Then he told his wife about the dream. Thereafter, whenever he used the mule, the family continued to call it "uncle," and it was extremely well-behaved. Once, when there was a seller of pottery, the mule saw it, broke its reins, ran and kicked, trampling it to pieces, leaving no intact pieces. The seller reproached him, and he said, "Uncle destroyed it; I am willing to compensate." The seller was surprised and asked, "Why is the mule your uncle?" He recounted his uncle's name and the strange story of his reincarnation to repay the debt. The seller said, "If so, you need not compensate. I owe him money, and the destroyed pottery matches the amount. I can use this to settle the debt," and thought it was a good thing.

There is another thing that can be a laughing stock: a great debtor with accumulated debts beyond calculation. After death, he met someone who said, "You still have debts to repay." He replied, "I will be reborn as a chicken or a dog to repay them." Then he met another person who said, "Why don't you repay your debts?" He answered, "I will be reborn as a cow or a horse to repay them." Later, he met a third person who said, "You owe tens of thousands of gold pieces, and you should repay them." The debtor sighed and said, "There are no items in the world worth tens of thousands of gold pieces. Even if I wanted to repay them, I couldn't. If there is no other way, please be reborn as my father, and I will generate wealth in many ways to repay you."

Feng Guantun (drafted as "Tian Sheng") In Feng Guantun, a village in Ciping, there lived a man named Tian Sheng, whose courtesy name was Siyi. He was known for his filial piety. His family was extremely poor, and he lost his father at a young age. He married a virtuous woman named Li, but just two years later, she died suddenly. His mother, who served meals day and night, could not bear the hardship, and Tian was deeply concerned. Suddenly, an old man came, leaning on a cane, and said to Tian, "I hear you have lost your wife, and this has caused your mother great distress. Is it not filial to quickly remarry and comfort your mother's heart?" Tian asked the old man his surname. The old man replied, "My surname is Su, and I live a little far away from here." Tian said, "You may not know, but I am not only poor and unable to afford a proper wedding, but who would be willing to give their beloved daughter to a man in such poverty?" The old man said, "I have a daughter who can help with household affairs. If you do not reject me, I will send her to a certain bridge westward tomorrow evening. You should follow the customs of Qi and prepare a sedan chair to come and take her. We will meet at the beginning of the night, and neither too early nor too late." Tian agreed. After the old man left, Tian told the villagers about the encounter, and they all thought it was absurd. However, there was a man in the village who rented out goods, and he said, "I can lend you a sedan chair for the wedding. Why don't you all go together? If this matter turns out to be true, it would surely be a good deed!" The villagers were pleased and agreed to accompany him.

On the appointed day, they arrived at the western side of the bridge, but there was no sign of any activity. The group felt disappointed and was about to return when suddenly a carriage pulled by two mules came from the west. Someone called out from afar, "Are you all here to escort Tian's bride?" Everyone replied, "Yes." The person said, "I am Su." They then helped the woman out of the carriage and into the sedan chair, and Su followed them home before leaving. The mother saw that the new wife was extremely beautiful, unlike a woman from a poor family, and feared she would be difficult to manage. However, the new wife fully fulfilled her duties as a wife and served the mother with humility and respect, even more so than Li's wife. After entering the household, the family gradually became prosperous, and all the plans and strategies she made were as if she had known them beforehand. Tian suspected that she was not human and often asked her about it. She said, "I am indeed not human, but my actions are no different from those of a human, so why investigate further? Let me tell you the truth: I am a fox spirit. My father admired your filial piety and pitied your poverty, so he sent me to serve you. Now, your mother will reach the age of a hundred, and she will die peacefully in a few months. The coffin, burial clothes, and other preparations are already made. I have also arranged for you to have a child through a servant, and you will not even realize it."

Previously, the woman bought a maid named You Shi and tried to persuade her husband to take her as a concubine, but he refused and was also repulsed by her appearance. The woman used a trick to deceive her husband, making him believe he was having relations with her while actually having relations with the maid, so the child was thought to be hers. At this point, she openly stated her intention. She said, "I will return to my father and will not come back." Her husband tried to keep her. She replied, "I cannot do it now, but I will leave in a year." She also said, "I have a piece of advice: Fengguantun is not a place to stay long. On a certain date, you must remember! Sell all your land quickly and move to another place." Her husband did not respond. She repeated, "My words are not empty; do not dismiss a woman's words as inappropriate to listen to." Her husband then became concerned and asked where they should move. She said, "Qufu. This matter cannot be told to others. In the village, the only person you can trust is Zhang Zhong, but he will not ask or speak about it."

In one night, they drank together and became drunk, falling asleep. When they woke up, the person had already disappeared. Within two years, in the year of Xianfeng Yi Mao (1855), chaos broke out in Fengguantun. No one was able to escape, except for the families of Tian and Zhang, who were spared. I heard that they were loyal, filial, and friendly people. A man from Jiangsu named Xu Sheng, with the courtesy name Xiaofeng, returned from visiting friends and waited at the riverbank to cross. He saw a fisherman catch two fresh carp, which were vivid and lovely. Suddenly, he noticed the fish turning its eyes and looking at him. He approached to examine it closely, and the fish turned its eyes several times repeatedly. Finding it strange, he bought the fish and released it into the river. When he arrived home, he told his wife about the fish. His wife said, "The fish turning its eyes must be something extraordinary. If we let it live, we will surely receive its reward." The husband smiled and said, "It might be true."

The Xu Family's Decline The Xu family had fallen from prosperity. One son, named Pu, was betrothed at a young age to a girl from the same village, Fang Xuefu's daughter, as his wife. They wished to complete the marriage but lacked the means. Not long after, he fell ill and died. Pu exhausted his resources to bury him, and the family became even poorer. The time for the peach blossom ode had come, but his life was like a wandering duckweed, broken like a floating twig. A girl in the inner chambers, unable to bear the waste of her youth, and a poor man outside, with no means to bring in a red curtain—Fang's family repeatedly urged the marriage, secretly harboring ulterior motives. Pu had no way to respond, and was forced to flee. One day, crossing the river, he lost his footing and fell into the water. At the critical moment, a piece of wood floated to the surface, and he grabbed it with his hand. He drifted and sank through the night, the wood stopping at the shore, and he was helped up by someone. It was late autumn, the weather cold and the water chilly. Though he was fortunate to survive, he became ill from exhaustion and could not walk. Crawling into a village by the river, seeking shelter, he saw an old man open the door. Pu told him the reason, pleading for his mercy. The old man helped Pu into a side room, took off his wet clothes, covered him with a quilt, and gave him food. Pu's illness worsened, and he could not swallow the food. He said, "I, Xu Pu, will surely become a ghost in a foreign land!" As he spoke, an old woman passed by the room. She asked, "Are you from the Xu family? Where do you live?" Pu truthfully answered. The old woman went away and returned, saying, "Are you related to Xu Xiaofeng's son?" Pu replied, "He is my father." The old woman, upon hearing this, hurried away. Suddenly, the old man appeared with clothing, saying, "Please dress quickly; my daughter will come out." "Who is your daughter?" "There's no need to ask."

Just as Par had finished dressing, a beautiful woman, accompanied by two maids and an old woman, emerged. She said to Par, "I knew that the son of your benefactor's time of good fortune had not yet come, but I did not expect him to be in such a state of hardship." She quickly ordered the maids and the old woman to help Par walk, and the lady followed behind. The old woman asked, "Should we take the guest to the guesthouse?" The lady replied, "There is no one to attend to the guest in the guesthouse." The old woman then asked, "Should we take him to the inner room?" The lady said, "Yes, we can." Soon, they entered a hall, and Par was laid down in a silken canopy. He saw that the house was splendid and beautifully decorated, with fragrant and elegant furnishings. Near a corner pillow, there was a pair of new boots with five patterns. The old woman quickly took them and covered them, saying, "Is this thing still left here?" Par realized that he was lying on the lady's bed and felt very surprised. In a short while, the lady entered and asked Par why he was there and how he had become ill. Par recounted it all. The lady said, "Do not blame others; it is because your fate is unlucky. Is your mother healthy?" Par replied, "She is healthy. But we have no means to support ourselves, and I often worry about it." The lady said, "Do not worry about this. Are you hungry?" Par answered that he was not hungry. The lady said, "How can there be no hunger after several days without food?" She then left. A little while later, she brought food.

Pu originally did not want to eat, fearing he would disappoint the girl's intentions, but forced himself to eat. He did not know what it was, but found its taste very sweet, and finished it in a moment, still feeling unsatisfied. The girl said, "The sick body should moderate food and drink; it can be eaten again after a short delay." Pu looked at the girl, whose appearance was like a fairy, hardly comparable. He said, "I personally received great favor, as if reborn. I wish to hear your beautiful name and await to repay with the grass-knotting gesture." The girl replied, "My mother practices Daoism in the mountains, and only my sister and I live here. I am named Yue Xian, and Shui Xian is my sister's name." Pu said, "My illness has worsened; what shall I do?" Yue Xian said, "Do not worry. There is joy without medicine." Soon after, another beautiful woman arrived, equally as lovely as Yue Xian. Seeing Pu, she exclaimed in surprise, "Who is on the bed?" Yue Xian said, "He is the son of Xu Xiaofeng." The woman said, "The son of Xu Xiaofeng, is he to sleep in my sister's bed? Did my sister perhaps want to sleep with him last night? Shame! Shame!" After saying this, she left in a huff. Yue Xian blushed and remained silent. Pu asked who she was, and the girl said, "That is my sister, Shui Xian." Pu could not praise her enough. By evening, Yue Xian and Pu slept on opposite beds.

Pú said, "Do I have any close relatives or acquaintances with you?" The moon replied, "No close relatives, but acquaintances." Pú asked further, and the moon said, "The reason is beyond your knowledge." That night, Pú dreamed of the moon fairy urging him to wake up and leading him away. Soon they arrived at a pond, not very wide, with water so clear that the bottom could be seen. The moon said, "Bathe in it, and your illness will be cured." Pú hesitated slightly, and the moon pushed him into the water. He felt the water warm and his body very relaxed. After a short while, the heat became unbearable, and he urgently called for the moon fairy to help, but the moon fairy had vanished. Soon after, he stumbled into a pit, was submerged, and woke up in a panic, drenched in sweat, with his single blanket completely soaked. The woman said, "Do not move when you sweat, or it will stop." Pú agreed, and pondered, "How did she know I was sweating? Could there be a reason behind my dream?" After a short time, the sweat subsided, and the illness seemed to disappear. It was already early morning, and he asked the woman for food. She quickly got up to serve him, showing great attentiveness and care. By evening, Pú took the woman to sleep with him. She said, "When you were ill, you should not have had such desires." Pú replied, "Yes. But seeing that the place where I slept is the same as where you sleep, I know you had intentions from the beginning." The woman smiled, and they became intimate.

The next day, Water Lily arrived and said, "At noon, we have prepared a modest feast, respectfully inviting you to join us in celebration. I humbly hope you will grace us with your presence." Pu asked where they would be, and she replied that it was on a boat. Pu was overjoyed. Water Lily said, "This place is near the river. Shall we walk together hand in hand?" Soon after, the three of them went on a laughing and talking journey. Upon reaching the riverside, they saw a brightly decorated boat with fresh curtains. Once aboard, the boat moved swiftly like an arrow. Pu was amazed. After some time, they arrived at the sea, where the two women jumped into the water, and the boat also submerged. Pu was greatly startled. He saw the water standing like a wall, several feet away from the boat, and his shock turned to joy. Soon, the boat sank to the bottom of the sea, and suddenly, a large gate appeared, resembling an official building. Moon Lily and Water Lily were both dressed in court attire, standing outside the gate awaiting them. Pu disembarked, and the two women led him inside. The buildings were grand and magnificent, and the palaces stood tall and imposing.

Pú asked, "Where is this place?" The Water Deity replied, "This is the second chamber of the Dragon Lord, temporarily borrowed to congratulate the new groom." Soon, a feast was laid out, and it was extremely abundant and lavish. Suddenly, eighteen beautiful women appeared, each holding a musical instrument, arranged on both sides. When the wooden clapper was struck once, the sound filled the ears. The two women said, "If there is music, there must be dance. Please offer a small skill to accompany the wine." Then they all rose together, dancing facing each other in front of the seat. Their movements matched the rhythm of the music, and the fragrance of orchids and musk filled the tables. After a long time, it ended. Pú bowed and thanked them, saying, "A humble being from the lower realms, to hear the music of the heavens is a great fortune in three lifetimes. Please stop the music." The women followed his request. The day had already turned to evening, and the Water Deity asked Pú to leave, saying, "This place cannot be stayed in for long." The two women escorted Pú to the gate, where there was a dragon with a red beard wrapped in grass. They ordered Pú to close his eyes and ride it, warning him not to look. Knowing it was supernatural, Pú followed their instructions. He felt the dragon rising and secretly touched it with his hand, the scales and armor feeling real. He thought about opening his eyes to see the wonder, and slightly opened them. He saw that what he was riding was a real dragon. Looking around, he saw clouds connected with the sea's atmosphere, and the wind carried the sound of the tide. He was both surprised and happy, thinking this was a rare and wonderful sight in life. Suddenly, he felt the dragon descend, and he became greatly afraid. He closed his eyes again, but the dragon continued to descend. When they reached the sea, he felt someone catching him. He looked at it, and it was the Moon Deity. They held hands and stood on the surface of the water, as if walking on flat ground. The woman quickly pulled out a jade hairpin and threw it into the sea. Soon, a boat emerged from the water. The woman took Pú and boarded it, and they reached the shore. The boat suddenly flew up, and looking down, he saw famous mountains and rivers, like small mounds and threads. Not long after, it landed in front of the courtyard. As soon as he stepped down, the boat had already disappeared, only the jade hairpin was on the ground. The woman picked it up and put it back in her hair.

A water fairy emerged from the courtyard and said, "My lord, are you startled? I thought a distinguished guest would have no other concerns, but my younger sister feared an unpredictable event, and indeed she suffered this perilous situation. How deep is the concern between husband and wife!" Pu replied, "What deities or spirits are you? Please clearly show yourselves, to dispel my doubts." Yue said, "Considered as deities, how dare we accept. Essentially, we are not human." One day, Yue and Pu drank together at the pavilion by the river. Pu saw the river water with waves piled up in thousands of layers, rolling for ten thousand miles, and then quoted the words of Liu Dinggong: "Without Yu, would we be fish?" Yue said, "You were born at an unlucky time, and your desire to be like a fish is unattainable." Afterward, Pu became drunk and hit the table while singing, "The delicious dishes are fully prepared, but there is no fish to eat." The fairy Yue was not pleased and said, "Why can't you forget your attachment to fish? I am inconvenient to eat fish. If I clearly state the inconvenience, I fear you will also be unwilling to eat."

Yue knew that Pu's fate was very bad, so she did not allow him to go out, and he only found joy in wine and chess. When Shui Xian occasionally came, she only chatted and laughed with her sister and did not speak to Pu. Pu hated her in his heart and told Yue about it. Yue said, "I will wait for an opportunity to mediate for you." One night, Yue said, "My sister is good at sleeping, and now she has forgotten to close the door. You should go quickly, and there will be a one-night encounter. The feelings will naturally not be thin." Pu was extremely happy and got up to leave, but Yue stopped him and said, "My sister is lying down, and she always sets a precious sword by her side for self-defense. You fear being injured by her, so you should first take it." Pu took the sword and handed it over to Yue, then went again. Shui Xian felt and woke up, hurriedly taking the sword. Pu laughed and said, "I took it early and sent it elsewhere." Shui Xian said, "My sister harms me." In the morning, she especially blamed Yue, and anger appeared on her face. Yue said, "My sister forgot to close the door, and he saw the room door half open and did it. What fault does it have to her sister?" Shui Xian said, "Whether the plan to take the sword is also something he thought of himself?" Yue could not answer. Since then, Shui Xian and Pu were close like Yue, but at dusk, they immediately left. One night, Pu visited Shui Xian's bedroom, but the room door was firmly closed, and calling her, there was no response. The next night, it was the same. Yue laughed and said, "Do not go again. A pleasant thing can happen once but not twice."

One day, the sisters Yuexian and Shuixian prepared provisions for a meal with Pu, and the dishes were more elaborate than usual, which surprised Pu. Shuixian asked, "Your birthday is approaching soon, have you forgotten?" Upon hearing this, Pu was deeply moved and sighed, furrowing his brow. Shuixian said, "Should one be happy yet worried, or worried yet happy?" Pu replied, "No, I am worried because I have been away from my elderly mother for several months, and there is no one to take care of her. Moreover, since childhood, I have been engaged to a woman from the Fang family, who has now reached the age of marriage, but I lack the means to complete the wedding, which is also a regrettable matter." He mentioned that the woman's father had expressed disdain for poverty and might consider breaking off the engagement. Yuexian said, "Since you have already been away from your parents for a long time, it is proper to return quickly. Here, we can take care of her. If the Fang family looks down on poverty, they can let her choose a wealthier man to marry." Pu was displeased and said, "The path of a virtuous person begins with marriage. Once a promise has been made, how can it be changed?" Yuexian smiled and said, "I spoke in the opposite way to test you. If you truly believe my words, I am also disgusted by your lack of virtue and deeply disappointed. When you return, will you go by boat or by carriage?" Pu replied, "Traveling by carriage is tiring, while traveling by boat is easy. I prefer the boat." Both sisters laughed. They held candles and went out, drawing a colorful boat on the ground in front of the courtyard and piling useful items inside it.

As Pu was slightly intoxicated, two women helped him out. Pu asked, "Where is the boat?" The two women replied, "It is drawn on the ground." Pu rode it without hesitation, just about to ask for the meeting time, but the boat was already in the air. Soon, it arrived at home, landing in front of the room, and then disappeared. Upon examining the main door, which was already closed, he heard his mother crying, missing him. Pu said, "I have come." His mother opened the door and saw Pu, dressed in a new cotton robe, and her sadness turned to joy. Pu felt ashamed that he had nothing to offer his mother, but soon noticed there was food and money in the boat, and he was greatly pleased. He respectfully carried it to present to his mother. The mother said, "I am not hungry. After you left a month ago, our firewood and rice were almost gone, and I was deeply worried. Early in the morning, suddenly there were some money and rice in the courtyard, and this happened several times. Otherwise, I would have starved to death long ago." Pu said, "This money and rice must be a gift from the fairy I met." He then told his mother in detail. The mother said, "The one you met is probably a water fairy, who avoids fish, and must be a fish spirit." Pu said, "Mother, you are right. What deep kindness could our family have done for the fish?" The mother said, "Your father, when he was alive, used to buy two carp at the riverbank and release them. Could that be the reason?" Pu and his mother were overwhelmed with gratitude. They then decided on the wedding date and sent someone to inform Fang.

Before Pu fled, there was a local powerful person named Ding who wanted to marry Fang's daughter to his son. Fang feared Ding and told the matchmaker, "My daughter was betrothed to Xu Pu when she was young, but now Pu is poor and cannot marry. The betrothal will certainly be annulled. If it is annulled, I will follow your command." Ding heard that Pu had returned and called Pu to his house, intending to force Pu to annul the betrothal. Just then, Ding had another matter and had no time to deal with this, so he had someone lead Pu to a secluded room in a separate courtyard and guard him. The guard said to Pu, "Do you want to break off the betrothal with Fang's family? If you do not want to, you can escape quickly. If you delay, your life will be in danger." Pu was greatly afraid and fled through the back door. At that time, the moon was low in the sky, and the path was barely discernible. After about a mile, he had already arrived at the riverbank. Looking back, he saw the pursuers shouting and coming. Suddenly, he saw a fishing boat by the shore and intended to board it, pleading with the fisherman for rescue. After boarding the boat, the boat moved very fast on its own. Looking around, he realized it was not a boat but a giant turtle, moving with its head raised. He only heard the sound of water, and his socks and shoes were not wet. Pu knew in his heart that it was the Moon Fairy who sent the turtle to welcome him, and he turned fear into joy. After a long time, he suddenly saw two stationary lights on the shore, as if waiting for a guest. When he reached the place of the lights, the turtle climbed onto the shore, and Pu got off. The turtle clumsily went back into the water.

The Lamp-Lighter said, "Why are you coming so late, sir?" Upon looking, he saw that it was the two maids of Shuixian, and greatly rejoiced, following them. The sisters Moon and Shuixian welcomed him at the gate. Upon reaching the courtyard, Pu thanked them for their silent protection and benevolence, and also recounted the entire course of events in detail. Shuixian said, "I know about it. Such a villain should not be allowed to leave descendants. However, since he was unable to catch up with you, he will certainly vent his anger on your mother." Pu was startled and asked, "What can we do about it?" The two women replied, "Do not worry." They then gave him wine to drink to calm his fears. The one who was pursuing Pu was the son of a local bully, who led his family members to do it. By the time they reached the river, Pu was already on a boat. There was a small boat by the riverbank, and they intended to use it to pursue him. The son of the bully boarded first, and once he got on, it was not a boat but a rotten plank floating on the water. Greatly alarmed, he hurried to get on shore, but his body followed the wood and sank, drowning him. Dīng mourned the death of his son and intended to harm Pu's mother to avenge his son's death. Hastily, he sent someone to bring Pu's mother to him. Dīng held a stick and struck her. In a moment of intense rage, his hand became twice as heavy, and with one strike, she died. Upon seeing it, it was not Pu's mother but a neighborly old woman. Dīng intended to make amends, but in the end, he did not know how he had injured the neighborly old woman. When Pu's mother arrived at Dīng's door, she suddenly saw a young woman take her by the hand and say, "He intends to harm your mother, do not enter." Pu's mother saw someone else helping another old woman enter. She followed the woman, but no one saw her, and she was greatly suspicious.

Not long after, the woman carried him through the clouds, and suddenly they descended into a grand mansion. They heard someone say, "The old mother has arrived." They saw a young man and woman come out to greet them. Upon seeing them, the young man was identified as Pu, and he was very happy. After entering the courtyard, the two women bowed and stood up. The mother asked them, and Pu replied, "These are all my daughters-in-law. They are indeed the fairy that my son met." The mother asked, "Which celestial register does she belong to?" Pu said, "Her background is known to you, so there is no need to ask further." The two women competed to offer delicious food and fulfill the duties of filial piety. The mother was delighted and forgot about the matter of marrying off her daughter. After two weeks, the fairy said, "The time for the wedding ceremony has arrived." Pu replied, "I know. But everything is not yet prepared, what can we do?" The fairy said, "I will go with you, and I will take care of everything." She agreed to meet the mother the next morning to go together. Pu was very happy and told his mother. The mother said, "The journey is far, it will not be easy." Pu said, "The next morning, she has her own wonderful method." By evening, they went to bed, and when they woke up, they had already returned. The mother was extremely surprised.

A woman cleaned a spare room and built a cabinet house, saying, "All valuables are stored in one room, making it very convenient to use." Pu looked inside and found the room empty, yet he managed to retrieve the wedding gifts and other items. On the day of the wedding, the woman said, "All the money and goods are prepared; there is no need for me to do anything. Please let me go." Suddenly, she disappeared. Soon after, she returned and said, "I nearly caused a major mistake. There is a great calamity whose timing cannot be predetermined. The boat you rode when you first arrived is still in the courtyard. If you see the boat leaving, quickly board it with the whole family, do not be attached to possessions." She then disappeared again. After Pu married his wife, he mentioned the instructions given by the water deity. His wife deeply believed them to be nonsense. One evening, suddenly the boat appeared in front of the courtyard, causing great alarm. He quickly helped his family and elderly mother onto the boat. A dozen or so armed men suddenly entered from outside, cursing and swearing. Upon hearing their words, it became clear they were part of a gang led by someone named Ding, who intended to humiliate Pu to vent their anger. Just as Pu was about to calm them with gentle words, a loud sound like thunder erupted from the ground, very fierce. Soon after, the boat rose and the ground sank, and Pu's house suddenly became an endless deep valley. The dozen or so wicked people were all drowned together.

Xu Pu and his entire family ascended by boat, and soon they arrived at the residence of the Moon Fairy. Female servants welcomed them, but the two daughters were not there. Xu Pu asked about the daughters, and the servants replied, "They were just here." Xu Pu thought that the daughters would return after some time, but they did not come all night, so he began to doubt. In the morning, he saw several empty rooms filled with an abundance of seafood, of inestimable value, and was greatly surprised. He told his mother, "The two daughters are not coming anymore." His mother asked, "Why say that?" Xu Pu replied, "Seeing this room full of sea creatures, I know that those who repay the father's virtue are all here." Indeed, it was as Xu Pu had said. His mother believed that the daughters had also died in the water and often wept. The Fang family also worried that his mother would miss them, but the distance between east and west was over two hundred miles, making it difficult to travel back and forth, and they also feared that the family of the person who drowned might bother them. Xu Pu said, "If we had the boat we rode yesterday, we could leave at night and return in the morning, which would be very convenient." After he said this, the boat suddenly appeared. Xu Pu was greatly pleased and went away with his wife on the boat. Later, they traveled by carriage and horse, and the boat no longer appeared. The Daoist named Xubai said: "The father sows virtue and the son enjoys the benefits, indeed. However, there is also a distinction between those who can enjoy and those who cannot. If one is unrighteous, unwise, uncivilized, and unjust, those who repay them may not bear to see them die immediately, but soon grow tired of them. As for Xu Pu, he received two daughters like immortals and did not forget the betrothed daughter he had arranged for, so in this regard, he is indeed someone who does not let emotions harm righteousness. Even if he enjoys extraordinary blessings, he can truly enjoy them."

**Final Translation:** The grace of benevolence bestowed and the reward of benevolence received is not extraordinary, even in the case of the fish in the pond of Emperor Wu of Han that held a ring in its mouth. — Ma Zhugu Buying fish and releasing them is an act of benevolence; thinking of one's rightful wife upon getting married is an act of righteousness. Broad love is called benevolence, and acting appropriately is called righteousness. Pu Sheng possesses both. The entire text is interspersed with the character for "ship," striking the head and the tail responds, striking the tail and the head responds, striking the middle and both head and tail respond together—this is known as the "three response method" of literary writing. — Gai Fangru **Case of the Bridge in Qihé** The Qihé Bridge lies on the border between Changqing and Qihé counties, with the bridge top serving as the boundary. In the year of Dàoguāng Bǐngwèi, a person died at the bridgehead; however, the actual location was slightly west, within the territory of Qihé. The magistrate of Qihé came to investigate and found that the body had knife wounds, indicating a case of murder for gain. Given the seriousness of the case, he claimed that the body had been moved and requested to re-examine the injuries, relocating the body to the east of the bridge top and stating, "The body is within Changqing's territory." He blamed the local protector for the misreporting and left. The officials of Changqing reported to their superiors, also mentioning the reason for the body's relocation. The magistrate of Changqing sent someone to invite the magistrate of Qihé to jointly inspect the body. The magistrate of Changqing arrived first and found a receipt in the deceased's chest, detailing the purchase of cloth and the amount of money spent, all clearly recorded.

Not long after, the Chief Magistrate of Qi arrived. The Deputy Magistrate requested to discuss the matter at a nearby temple. The Deputy Magistrate told the Chief Magistrate, "The body is on the west side of the bridge, why was it moved to the east side?" The Chief Magistrate replied, "Not yet." The Deputy Magistrate said, "A traveler was killed, there must be bloodstains. Now there are bloodstains on the west side of the bridge, but none under the body on the east side, if not moving the body, what else could it be?" The Chief Magistrate could not answer. The Deputy Magistrate laughed and said, "In this case, I fear you cannot investigate it, let me handle it." At that time, many villagers entered the temple to watch. The Deputy Magistrate ordered the servants to close the door and became angry, saying, "You all came here to secretly listen to the officials' words, to inform the murderer, right?" He ordered each person to be beaten with twenty strokes and then released. The Chief Magistrate did not understand his intention and interceded on behalf of the villagers. The Deputy Magistrate said, "If so, each person will be penalized by contributing half a bolt of cloth, regardless of color, with five people guaranteeing each other. It will be settled on the third day here, and those who violate will be severely punished." First, those who are willing to contribute cloth, their names and places of residence were recorded, approximately thirty names. The others said, "We are poor, we really don't have money to buy cloth." The Deputy Magistrate said, "Can three of us contribute half a bolt of cloth together?" All said, "Yes." Approximately thirty more were released. The Deputy Magistrate said, "I do not force you to do what is difficult, since you have no money to contribute cloth, each must encourage those who will contribute cloth, bring the cloth here early for submission, and avoid severe punishment." The crowd nodded and left. The Deputy Magistrate agreed with the Chief Magistrate to come here together on the appointed day to collect the cloth, the Chief Magistrate agreed, and they returned.

By the third day, the two magistrates arrived one after the other. Those who brought cloth held it in their arms awaiting inspection, and the senior magistrate inspected the cloth in order. After the inspection, the cloth was returned to the person, and the magistrate asked whether the cloth was bought from a certain person, noting it down one by one. After the inspection, the cloth was counted, and more than half was bought from a certain person. The magistrate asked where this person was. It turned out that this person was also one who brought cloth, and everyone pointed to him, saying, "This is the person." The magistrate asked the person, "How many years have you been dealing in cloth?" The person replied, "I just started this business." The magistrate said, "How many blue, how many white, and how many in total did you buy, and do you remember?" The person answered with numbers. The magistrate said, "No, it is about this much blue, this much white, and this much in total, I fear you might have miscalculated." The magistrate then said, "To whom did you sell all your cloth?" The person said, "I only sold a few pieces to those who brought cloth, and the rest has not been sold yet." The magistrate said, "How many did you sell to those who brought cloth, and how many are left, do you know?" The person answered with numbers again. The magistrate said, "No, it is not so."

A merchant had probably sold several bolts of cloth, leaving a remainder of several bolts. The local official, Qi Yin, immediately sent a servant to fetch the cloth from A's house. After some time, the servant returned with the cloth. When the cloth was counted, it did not match what A had said, but it matched what Mr. Yin had claimed. Mr. Yin smiled and said to A, "You bought this cloth yourself, yet you don’t know its quantity, while I know everything about it. Do you know why?" A replied, "I don’t." Qi Yin was astonished and asked for the reason. Mr. Yin pointed to A and said, "This is a thief who fears nothing, even death." He then produced an account sheet for Qi Yin to see. Qi Yin asked, "Where did this account sheet come from?" Mr. Yin replied, "I found it in the dead man’s chest. The number of bolts A had and the number on the account sheet are the same, clearly indicating that A coveted the cloth and killed the man without a doubt." Upon hearing this, A was shocked to the core and confessed voluntarily. He stated, "The deceased was a man from a certain village in Qihé, who traded cloth for a living. He was attracted by the value of the goods, befriended the man, and invited him to stay overnight. He brought the cloth to my house. At night, he pretended to go with a companion to meet another friend, but on the bridge, he killed him and took possession of the cloth. The cloth sold by the crowd was actually the deceased’s property."

The magistrate having identified the culprit, he then addressed the cloth sellers, saying, "Your cloth, which was purchased at a price, each person can take it away. Is it truly to punish you to enrich the official's purse? It is because stolen goods are always sold cheaply, and I am using your cloth to find the location of the criminal." After saying this, he allowed each person to take their cloth. He then sent a messenger to notify the family of the deceased, to take the body and the cloth, and to reclaim the price of the cloth sold by someone. The officials and common people of both counties praised the divine official. Unfortunately, I forgot his official title, I will inquire again later.
