The official wrote the document himself, and the creditor finally left. He repaid the debt on schedule and in full, and the remaining amount was spent. 濱 and his mother were truly uneasy.

When the time for the divination came too soon, 濱 responded, "Not yet. Because there is no suitable burial ground, the coffin is temporarily resting in the temple hall." The official then asked a skilled geomancer to choose a place. Once the site was selected, he ordered his family to accompany 濱 to Renshou and move the coffin for burial in Mianzhou. The expenditure of silver and money for this matter was minor; but in completing this significant task, the virtue was great. When 戴公 was waiting to take up his post in the provincial capital, he borrowed several taels of silver from a moneylender. The creditor heard that 濱 had moved the coffin for burial in Mianzhou and came along, sitting and demanding payment without leaving. 濱 pleaded with many excuses for an extension, but the creditor did not agree. The matter involving Jiang Gong could not be resolved at once, so he said to the creditor, "In three installments, I will repay it, with a limit of several months, is that acceptable?" The creditor replied, "If you truly wish to repay, one year is also acceptable. The loan document must be changed to your name." The official wrote the document himself, and the creditor finally left. He repaid the debt on schedule and in full, and the remaining amount was spent. 濱 and his mother were truly uneasy.

As the main character's sister reached the age of marriageable age, a young man named Gu, a minor military official, who had recently lost his wife, proposed marriage. Gu was only three or four years older than the main character's sister. He sent a matchmaker to ask for her hand, and the father encouraged and persuaded the mother to agree. The wedding took place within a few months, and the father's daughter also married in the same month. The gifts given to the main character's sister were not much different from those given to the father's daughter. The father personally taught the main character. At the age of 18, he passed the county-level examination. A local gentry person admired his talent and wished to marry him to his daughter, asking the father to act as a matchmaker. The father consulted the main character's mother and arranged the marriage. The main character's mother remembered that everything, including burying her husband and arranging the marriages of her children, was done by Jiang Gong alone. The mother and son were immensely grateful to Jiang Gong. Suddenly, the main character developed a lazy attitude. The father tried to persuade him with many words, but the main character ignored them as if he had not heard them. He only remembered his father's words: "If your father were still alive, he would not allow you to be idle and untrained." The main character became serious and moved by the words, diligently studying at his desk. After a few days, he returned to his old habit of laziness.

Every time it was tested, the result was the same. Knowing that Chu had filial thoughts, the public pretended to say to Chu, "For several days, I have been dreaming that your late father came here to see how you are doing with your studies. You can bring out your father's spirit tablet and place it on the writing desk." Chu followed the instructions. The public offered incense daily and said, "If the young master does not achieve fame, it is all my fault for not teaching well." Secretly observing Chu, the public noticed that his efforts doubled compared to before, for more than a month without slackening, and there were times when he cried in front of the spirit tablet. The public was secretly delighted. Later, Chu asked to light incense for the public, and the public agreed. From then on, Chu made daily progress and monthly achievements, being humble in heart and quick in action, and without being taught, he voluntarily worked hard. He passed the local examination after three years. The public led Chu to the capital for the metropolitan examination, suffering hardships on the road, making sure to go to bed early, and not minding waking up late due to the many winds and frosts in the countryside. When sending him to the examination hall, the public would wait until Chu entered before returning, and when picking him up, the public would arrive before Chu left. The public treated Chu like a son, and Chu also regarded the public like a father. Chu was granted the title of Jinshi, and the public's heart was comforted; the public's responsibility was also fulfilled.

At that time, Cú's elder brother-in-law, Mr. Gu, who held the position of Wuyi Duiwei, heard of Cú's return and offered a feast to congratulate him. During the banquet, Mr. Gu highly praised Jiang Gong, saying, "The way of dealing with friends like you, can be called the utmost in emotion and righteousness. I do not know, my late father-in-law, how earnestly he wrote the letter and what it said." Jiang Gong smiled and said, "Your younger brother has already gained fame, so the matter can be openly stated." He then took out a letter to show Mr. Gu and said, "This is the letter written by your father-in-law." Cú and Mr. Gu looked at it together and saw that the letter contained only a respectful name, with no other words, which greatly puzzled them. Jiang Gong turned to Cú and said, "Your father and I had no prior acquaintance, so it was inconvenient to entrust my daughter and son to you. Therefore, I sent an empty letter to you, asking you to understand the meaning and act accordingly." Upon hearing this, Cú hurriedly dressed and bowed to the ground to express his thanks. He then said, "Before, I only knew of your righteousness; now I realize your kindness and righteousness are fully realized." Mr. Gu said, "This kindness cannot be repaid with just one expression of thanks." Cú agreed and formally adopted Jiang Gong as his father. After Cú entered official service, he frequently sent silver and gifts to celebrate Jiang Gong's birthday. When Jiang Gong passed away, Cú dressed in mourning and cried like a son.

虚白道人曰：今之托妻子于友，而受冻馁者多矣。即不尽然，而贫友来投，避而不面者有之；视朋友之妻子如路人者有之；面受友人之托，及友死而反之者有之。类此者，其人不足责，而友之之人真为无目矣。江公者，竟以无素之人，伪曰契友，而即以契友视其妻子。公盖以为：人既谓吾为友，必吾可以为友。吾不尽友之道，非欺友也，实自欺也。如是之友，不惟百中无一，千中无一，直万中无一矣！ **Translation:** The Master of Void White said: "Today, many entrust their wives and children to friends and suffer from cold and hunger as a result. Even if not entirely so, there are those who avoid meeting poor friends when they come seeking help; there are those who treat their friends' wives and children as strangers; and there are those who accept a friend's trust face-to-face but return it upon the friend's death. Such people are not worth blaming, but the friend is truly blind. Mr. Jiang, treating someone with no prior connection as a sworn friend, and regarding their wives and children as such, believes that since people call him a friend, he must be able to be a friend. He does not fulfill the duties of friendship, not because he deceives his friend, but because he deceives himself. A friend like this is not only one in a hundred, one in a thousand, but one in ten thousand!" 此与《古今奇观》中刘仁普事仿佛。 上元李瑜谨注 **Translation:** This is similar to the story of Liu Renpu in *The Strange Tales of Ancient and Modern Times*. Dated Shangyuan, Li Yu respectfully annotated. 美 人 图 **Translation:** The Portrait of a Beauty 秋子丰，楚人，善画。一日画一美人，方毕，幼子成目注之。丰戏之曰：“汝长大，即令作汝妇。”丰裱之，挂诸寝室，每食，谓成曰：“饿坏汝媳妇矣。”成即盛食供之。及长，知父戏己，而珍之异他物。嗣悬之床头，不时瞻玩，即从塾师读，亦必携之。 **Translation:** Qiu Zifeng, a man from Chu, was skilled in painting. One day, he painted a beautiful woman, and when he finished, his young son Cheng gazed at it intently. Zifeng teased him, saying, "When you grow up, I will make you marry this woman." He then framed the painting and hung it in his bedroom. Every time he ate, he would say to Cheng, "Don't let your future wife starve." Cheng would then bring out plenty of food for her. When Cheng grew older, he realized his father was joking, but he cherished the painting more than any other object. Later, he hung it by his bedside, often admiring it, and even carried it with him when he studied under a private tutor.

After one year, the protagonist lived away from home for a short distance, eating only breakfast and lunch at home but not returning for dinner. He would bring dry food for dinner. One evening, when he took the food, it was gone. The next evening, the same thing happened, and he became greatly puzzled. Believing that no one else was in the room, as the door was locked from the outside, and thinking no one could steal the food, he began to peer through the window repeatedly. Suddenly, he saw a beautiful woman holding food and eating. Upon closer inspection, he realized it was the person in the painting. He quickly opened the door to look inside, but the beautiful woman had disappeared, and the painting remained hanging on the wall. He still suspected it was a dream or an illusion. For several days, the food remained untouched. After a few more days, the food was missing again. He decided to leave the room door slightly open to catch her. As evening approached, his teacher and study companions returned, and he crept to the room, peering in through the window secretly. He saw the woman just descending from the painting, and as she reached the ground, he pushed the door open suddenly, grabbing her arm and saying, "The thief of food, I have finally caught you." The woman was startled and said, "You almost scared me to death! Please let me go. I may be guilty, but I will never flee out of fear of punishment." He released her. Looking back at the painting, he noticed it remained unchanged and asked, "Just now, I saw you descending from the painting. Why is the beautiful woman in the painting still there?" The woman replied, "I am the spirit of the painting."

"If you are a ghostly figure with vivid features and no vitality, how could you exist outside of paper?" Cheng asked. "Why did you suddenly eat my food?" he continued. The woman replied, "I thought the food you ate was also meant for me, so I ate it." Cheng asked, "What did you eat before?" The woman said, "My explanation is lengthy, but I will tell you later. I ate your food, leaving you with nothing to eat. If you do not blame me for this, I cannot avoid taking responsibility." The room had a wall cabinet, where Cheng stored his food. The woman took out dishes and wine from the cabinet, and the steam rose as if the food was freshly cooked. She took more from it. Soon, the table was full of food. Cheng said, "How can there be so much and so delicious food?" The woman said, "The first wedding banquet should not be hastily prepared, and from now on, it should not be done carelessly." During their meal, the woman said, "Before, my father painted a beautiful woman and said that I would become your wife. After you often shared your food with me, I gained vitality and became a real person after more than a year. Before, when you were not yet fully mature, I dared not violate your rules; now that you are about to become an adult, I am also concerned about my own maturity. So I ate your food to meet you." Cheng was overjoyed, drank until he was drunk, and then went to sleep. From then on, every night the woman prepared food and wine, and they ate together.

Cheng's mother suddenly fell ill and passed away, and his younger brother Shou was only four years old. Feng managed household chores during the day and comforted the young child at night, suffering beyond words. He then married a remarried woman named Xu as his second wife. Xu was also thrifty and hardworking, but she looked at Shou with an unfriendly expression. It was the month of winter, and Shou always cried while eating. Feng scolded him, saying, "Why are you crying? Don't blame your mother for not liking you. I will feed you." The bowl was too hot to hold, and Feng found it strange. It turned out that Xu had steamed an empty bowl in the pot and used the hot bowl to serve food to Shou, so Shou cried when he saw it. Feng, seeing this, deeply hated Xu's heartless and cruel nature and beat Shou without number. Although Xu's grudge was deep, she dare not show it to Shou. Later, when Xu gave birth to a son named Ge, she treated Shou as an enemy. When Shou was nine years old, Cheng had already entered the academy. Feng made Shou follow Cheng in his studies and told Cheng not to let Shou come home without reason, because he was afraid that Xu would vent her anger on Shou. Not long after, Feng died. After the funeral, Cheng took Shou to the temple. One day, Cheng went out elsewhere. When he returned, he did not see Shou. He asked, and the students said, "He was called home." Cheng was greatly frightened. He hurried home, asked his mother about Shou, and his mother replied that she had not seen him. He hurried back and told the woman, who said, "Although Shou has a difficult star, he will not be harmed; wait until nightfall, and I will help you rescue him."

After a certain period, Cheng urged her, and she said, "Wait a little longer." Soon after, she said, "It is done." They then walked together and left. When they reached the house, the door was firmly closed. Cheng said, "What shall we do?" She replied, "Leap over the wall and enter." They then held hands and leaped, feeling their bodies as light as feathers, and they jumped over the wall in one bound. In the cellar, they found Shou, whose tongue was pierced by two needles, tied up and seemingly dead. Cheng quickly removed the needles and untied him, and after a while, Shou came back to life. Cheng asked, "What shall we do now?" She said, "I can save him, but I must leave you temporarily." Cheng agreed. She then said, "I am pregnant and will give birth to a child. I ask you to name him." Cheng replied, "You can name him." She agreed, and then took Shou away. Cheng saw her off at the door, but she suddenly disappeared, and the paintings in the room also vanished. Xu knew that Shou was hidden by Cheng and wanted to harm Cheng. Cheng was cautious and avoided him, not staying at home at night. His mother gave him food, and he pretended not to be hungry. One day, his mother prepared a meal, and he forced himself to eat. He suspected something and secretly threw the food to a dog, pretending it fell to the ground and was eaten by the dog. The dog died immediately. Subsequently, regardless of the quality of the food, Cheng would not eat it because his mother would not eat it. His mother could do nothing about it.

Time passes gradually, and Cheng will not be away for more than five days, but he must return home to check on his mother’s provisions. For the younger and older generations, Cheng wants him to study with him. The person agrees to follow Cheng’s example but dares not follow Cheng’s words. Cheng repeatedly urged him, and the person finally agreed as if in response. The person saw Cheng teaching the younger ones with care, treating them as if they were siblings, and finally accepted his heart and actions. Later, the person became filial to Cheng, not less than a biological son, but the family became poorer, and food and clothing were scarce. Cheng returned from the annual examination in the prefecture, and on the way, he heard that his mother had suddenly fallen ill. He hurried home, but his mother had already passed away. He saw that his mother had no outer garment and no money in hand, so unwillingly, he took off his thin-sleeved cotton robe and dressed his mother in it for a simple burial. He was filled with shame and grief, often weeping until his eyelids swelled. Naked without a thick coat, he not only felt cold but also found it inconvenient to go to the temple. The people of the east heard about it and contributed money to buy him clothes. Later, the household became quite prosperous, and after providing for his younger brother, there was some surplus, and his finances gradually improved.

After the completion of his mourning period, the speaker set out for the provincial examination, accompanied by someone. Upon submitting his examination paper, he was suddenly approached from behind and asked, "Where is your elder brother going?" The speaker turned around and recognized his brother, who was named Shou. Overjoyed, they proceeded together to their lodging. Shou then pointed to the speaker and said, "This is my younger brother. I left home because no one was available to take care of the household, so I came along with him." He mentioned that his mother had passed away more than three years ago. Shou added, "My sister-in-law has spoken of it. I will also enter the school after completing my mourning period this year." The speaker quickly asked, "Where does your younger brother currently reside? Does he live with your sister-in-law?" Shou replied, "He currently resides in a certain county, three hundred li away from me. His success is entirely due to my sister-in-law's efforts in hiring a renowned teacher to educate him. Our family is now financially well-off, and my sister-in-law has a son who is thirteen years old." Upon hearing this, the speaker was greatly pleased. After the three rounds of the examination, the brothers returned home together. When they arrived, the speaker's wife greeted them at the door, as if she had known the exact time of their return. The speaker looked at her and saw that she was a beautiful young woman, no different from the one depicted in a painting. They entered the main hall, sat down, and the wife called their son to bow to his father. The son was extremely handsome, with a striking resemblance to his mother. The speaker said, "You and your mother have achieved such a happy state, why don't you share this joy with me?" The wife replied, "After my mother passed away, I immediately wanted to ask my brother to come home and invite you."

Cheng, upon hearing the news, turned pale as if his face had turned to clay. The object in his hand fell without him noticing, and that is why I delayed until now. Cheng said that his mother died without proper clothing, and he felt ashamed for not dressing her properly. The girl said, "Your success in the exam is due to this." Cheng asked, "What do you mean?" The girl said, "After the results were announced, you will know." It was Cheng's essay that the examiner read and found unsatisfactory, wanting to discard it. He saw a female ghost dressed in men's clothes, kneeling and bowing deeply, and was greatly surprised. He took the scroll and read it again, and the ghost immediately disappeared. He wanted to discard it again, and the female ghost bowed as before. Then he took the scroll and showed it to the chief examiner, stating it directly. The chief examiner smiled and thought it was a case of passing through connections. Afterward, he read it himself and found the text really poor, deciding to discard it. He indeed saw the female ghost bowing as the examiner had said, and was greatly surprised by it. He said to the ghost, "Go away, I will definitely pass." The ghost bowed several times and then left. Cheng passed and respectfully visited the examiner. The examiner said, "Your success is not because of your good writing, but because of your good deeds." Cheng stood up and replied, "No, it's not true." The examiner said, "I saw a female ghost dressed in men's clothes, who is she related to you?" And described in detail the ghost's pleading state. Cheng heard it and shed tears, saying, "She is my stepmother." He explained the reason for his mother dressing in men's clothes. The examiner sighed and said, "Filial piety can move ghosts and spirits, just like this!" Therefore, he treated Cheng with great respect.

Cheng one day inspected his chests and boxes, and found an old painting of a beautiful woman inside. He said to her, "Why not hang it?" The woman replied, "If I am in front of you and show you a painting, it is a form of self-degradation." Cheng said, "Then perhaps it can be burned?" The woman said, "On the day the painting is burned, I will forever part ways with you." Cheng did not understand the reason but remembered it deeply. After several years, he had a grandson. On the hundredth day, a grand offering was set up, and the family offered sacrifices to their ancestors. Cheng saw the woman holding the painting and burning it along with the offerings. He was greatly surprised and tried to take it from her. It had already turned to ashes, and the woman had vanished. Soon, he saw the woman standing in the smoke, ascending with it, and she was gone after some time. The Daoist Master Xubai said: People say that a beautiful wife or concubine who cannot do needlework is just for looking at. It strongly suggests that paintings are only for viewing and have no other use. Could they truly serve as living beings to serve others? But since there is no such thing, there was no one to rescue him, and despite Cheng's deep affection, he could not avoid the sorrow of losing his brother. Moreover, there was no one to lift the tray, and despite Cheng's constant thoughts of filial piety, he could not avoid the worry of having no descendants. However, the actions of the person in the painting seem not to be something the person in the painting could do. Perhaps it was another fairy maiden who pretended to be her, to fulfill Cheng's filial and friendly heart.

The person depicted in the illustration is deeply devoted to friendship, surpassing even Nan Yue Zhenzhen. An annotation by Li Yu of Shangyuan. A certain county reported to the authorities that a person had died on the road. It was already evening, so the official ordered the local official to patrol and guard, and to inspect the next day. The local official had another matter, so he sent someone to watch. It was very cold at night, so the guard went to a nearby village to buy wine and drank it himself. When he returned, the body was already gone. It seems that the deceased revived and left on his own. The guard quickly told the local official. The local official was greatly afraid, thinking that it was a false report to deceive the officials, and severe punishment was inevitable, so he was very angry with the guard. The guard said, "A few miles south, there is a newly buried grave of a person named Jia. It is cold, so the body will not decay. We can dig it up and move it to avoid official responsibility." The local official agreed. After opening the coffin and removing the body, they were about to fill the empty grave with earth, but the east had already become bright (dawn). Fearing being encountered by others, they quickly carried the body to a main road to wait for the official inspection. The official arrived, and the inspectors saw that the body was wearing new clothes and that the soles of the shoes had no dirt, knowing that the person did not die on the road, so they reported to the officials that the body was stolen. The official ordered the inspectors to lock up and detain the local official, preventing him from escaping, and also temporarily not asking about the origin of the body, only instructing the inspectors to carefully inspect and secretly report. The inspectors examined the body and found a burn mark on the mouth, the tongue had no skin, and there was a lump in the abdomen the size of a fist, which was very hard, indicating that it was caused by molten lead being poured into the body. They secretly repeated each detail. The official nodded without speaking, sitting firmly without saying anything about returning. It was because the body had been stolen and moved, so there must be someone who reported it, and they would carefully investigate the true circumstances to compensate for the death of the body.

After some time, indeed a young woman cried out in complaint, stating that her husband had just been buried when someone dug up his grave, opened the coffin, and stole his body. The official asked her about her husband's name, age, date of death and burial, and the cause of his illness, and the young woman stated them clearly and in detail. The official saw that the woman's appearance was not proper and thus concluded she was not a chaste woman, and said, "When a woman brings a lawsuit, there must be someone to support her claim. You have none, why is that?" The young woman replied, "My husband's younger brother does not have a family, and I have no other relatives." The official said, "His friends and neighbors from the village can also serve as witnesses." The young woman said, "There is one person who can support my claim." The official asked, "Do you know his name and age?" The woman said, "He is named B, and he is in his twenties." The official asked, "Is he from the same village as your husband?" She replied, "No, he lives next door to my mother's family." The official gave a subtle smile and immediately ordered a subordinate to bring B. The official said, "Are you willing to support the woman's claim?" B said, "Yes." The official also asked B about the husband's name, age, etc., and B answered as the woman had. The official asked, "How do you know all this so well?" B could not answer.

The magistrate laughed and said to the young woman, "The body at the crime scene was moved by thieves. Do you recognize this as your husband?" The woman ran to look and returned crying, "Yes, it is." The magistrate said, "Do not cry. The person who stole the body has been caught." He ordered the guards to bring the village headman and question him, who then confessed. The magistrate said, "The act of false reporting is minor, but the crime of opening the coffin and stealing the body is grave." He ordered the village headman to be flogged forty times and released. The young woman complained that the punishment was too light, and the magistrate said, "He stole the body for a reason, not for money." The young woman argued further, and the magistrate stated, "His crime can be pardoned, but yours is hard to forgive." The woman angrily asked, "What crime have I committed?" The magistrate said, "Do not be angry. Listen to me. Your husband was killed by pouring molten lead into him, and he must have had someone to help him. As for B, he had a secret relationship with you since childhood, and was he involved in planning to kill your husband with you?" Both B and the woman strongly denied the accusations. The magistrate ordered the guards to cut open the body to find the lead. The young woman saw the lead and became extremely frightened, confessing. When questioned, B saw that the woman had confessed and also admitted, "Indeed, I had a secret relationship with her before we were married. Later, I found out and did not allow her to return home, so I devised this plan, making her get her husband drunk and then kill him together."

The Empty White Hermit said: If the person who was harmed has been buried, then the person who harmed them might escape punishment, but the injustice of the victim cannot be made known. Yet, those who might escape punishment ultimately do not, and those whose injustice cannot be known are made to appear as if it is known. There is indeed a supernatural force at play. Heaven and earth are not truly deaf and mute! Those who die on the road become spirits or ghosts. Otherwise, why would such skillful deception occur? **By Li Yu of Shangyuan, respectfully annotated** **The Auspicious Snow** **Yin Sheng of Tian Shen in Fen Prefecture** Yin Sheng of Tian Shen in Fen Prefecture claimed he did not fear ghosts and fox spirits. When asked why, he replied, "Evil does not overcome righteousness. If one is guiltless, there is no reason to fear ghosts and fox spirits." He would often go out at night, carrying wine to remote hills, saying, "If there are any ghosts or fox spirits, they are welcome to join me for a drink." But there was never any response. He heard that a certain mountain had a place where many people were buried, and spirits often troubled people there, so he went there one night. Upon arrival, he saw a blue phosphorescent light jumping around, but he could not catch up with it. When he returned, the light followed him. Yin Sheng paid it no mind and sat at a stone table in front of a tomb to drink alone. Soon, he saw the blue phosphorescent light jump in front of him and said, "Do you also want to drink?" He poured a cup of wine and offered it to the light, which then stopped. Looking closely, it was a skull. Beside the tomb was a deep pit. He placed the skull in the pit and covered it with earth. Soon after, a young man came, bowed deeply, and thanked him.

The living person asked, "I am the ghost you buried in the skull cap. I have been exposed to the wind and sun each day, and my spirit cannot find peace. You placed me in a pit and buried me, which can be said to bring grace even to the bones of the dead." The living person invited the ghost to drink together, and the ghost did not refuse. After drinking several cups, the ghost said, "I have no way to repay your great kindness, but my sister Liu Ruixue wishes to serve as your concubine. I hope you will accept her." The living person replied, "Can one form a relationship with a ghost?" The ghost answered, "Yes. My sister is not one who can harm people." The living person was pleased, and the ghost stood up and left. Soon after, a beautiful woman arrived, and under the moonlight, she was seen to be exceptionally beautiful. The ghost said, "Do not drink too much at night. You can return home with my sister." The living person followed her advice. Upon arriving home, he stayed with Ruixue in a separate room. The next day, he told his wife, Lady Huai. Lady Huai was kind and good, and she was very pleased to see Ruixue. She instructed the living person to keep the fact that Ruixue was a ghost a secret. Ruixue treated Lady Huai like a mother, and Lady Huai treated her very well.

One day, after drinking, the man returned from the county town. In the evening, he passed through a certain mountain, which was indeed full of foxes. The man said, "I've heard there are many foxes here, but why have I never met one?" As he spoke, two foxes appeared in his path, speaking human language, and said, "We heard that you do not fear us. Now we will make you know our power." The man was good at controlling his body with magic, but he feared he might not be able to subdue the foxes. Reluctantly, he tried, and the two foxes could not move. Greatly pleased, he examined the two foxes, finding one male and one female. He then caressed the female and said, "If you can speak human language, you must be able to transform into a human body. I wish to take you as my wife." The female fox whispered, "My father is coming. Please step back a bit. My body will belong to you, sir." At that moment, the sun rose in the east, and indeed, an old man came from the opposite direction, saying, "Our young ones have offended your honor. Please forgive us." The man released his magic, and the foxes and the old man disappeared. In the village, there was a large house where strange occurrences were often seen, frightening the residents who wanted to sell it cheaply but found no buyer. The woman named Snow said to the man, "There are thousands of gold coins hidden in that house. You can quickly buy it." The man followed her advice, and as promised, he found the treasure. The house had many rooms, and he moved in, leaving most of it empty.

In the idle courtyard, it seemed as though someone lived, yet the person could not be seen. Since there were no other strange phenomena, one grew accustomed to it over time. Rui said to the man, "Do you know the true nature of the fox you played with at a certain mountain?" The man replied, "I do not know." Rui said, "She is the most beautiful woman of her time." The man asked, "How do you know this?" Rui replied, "Before we bought this house, her entire family had already been living in this idle courtyard. Our family set up a Buddha altar in the courtyard, and her daughter, in formal attire, worshiped the Buddha on the first and fifteenth days of each month at the hours of 7-9 AM and 9-11 AM." The man asked, "How can I see her face?" Rui said, "You should set up an invisibility object outside the deity room in advance, and hide inside it at the appointed time to see her." The man followed her advice. Indeed, he saw a young lady of eighteen, dressed in finery, approaching. Peering through a crack, he examined her in detail, and found her beauty as radiant as a lotus flower under the morning sun. She pushed the door open and entered. After the ritual was completed, she came out and pointed to the invisibility object, saying, "Who put this object here? If there is a thief hidden inside, it might go unnoticed." She spoke and then left. After some time, the man emerged and returned. He saw Rui and said, "I am utterly captivated by her! To touch her skin would be worth dying for!" Rui said, "I will plan to bring her to you."

The Fox Spirit Girl Named Sanjie The fox spirit girl named Sanjie slept with her sister at night. At the third watch of the night, Rui called her name and said, "Hu Sanjie, you said you would dedicate yourself to Yin, but why have you forgotten it?" He repeated his question. The fox sister asked, "Are you asleep, my younger sister?" Sanjie replied, "Not yet." The fox sister said, "Since you are not asleep, you must have heard the ghost's words. Such a matter of lifelong importance, why did you say it and not follow through? I will inform your parents on your behalf." After seven days, Rui told Yin, "It is agreed; the old fox will invite you to marry there." Yin did not fully believe him. The next day, indeed, a small servant came to invite him. Yin changed his clothes and followed him. Just as he entered the secluded courtyard gate, an old man put on his clothes and hastened to greet him. When he saw him, it was the same old man he had seen at the foot of a certain mountain. They entered the room and sat down. The old man said, "My daughter has been favored by you; she should be made to serve you, and I ask you to take her as your concubine." Yin stood up and thanked him. The old man added, "This courtyard is also part of your estate. Can we proceed with the wedding here?" Yin agreed. After some time, an old woman came out with Third Sister. The old man said, "This is my wife." Yin greeted her with respect. Third Sister sat beside her mother in a corner, and they all sat together for a banquet. After the banquet, the moon had already set. He ordered a maid to hold a candle and guide Yin and Third Sister to a separate room. Yin looked at her without blinking. The girl said, "When you were hiding in objects, you still couldn't get enough of looking at me? Don't think your tricks can deceive me." Afterward, the girl would visit Yin's courtyard every morning to pay respects to Lady Huai. Lady Huai and the girl's mother visited each other as if they were close relatives.

After more than a year, the man fell ill. The shaman said, "The illness has reached a critical stage, and only a miraculous remedy can cure it." They all left in disgust. Rui told Sanjie, "Why don't you seek the elixir of the immortal to save your husband?" Sanjie replied, "Seeking it will definitely not work." Rui said, "If seeking it doesn't work, then steal it." Sanjie shook her head, "The immortal's magical sword is often hanging in the cave." Rui said, "Even if there is a magical sword, it would not be used to harm someone. How can you be afraid of it and just watch your husband die?" Sanjie could not argue further, "Stealing it will take time, and I fear the husband's illness is too severe to wait." Rui asked for a deadline, Sanjie said, "Please give us five days." Rui said, "If the husband dies within five days, I will take full responsibility." Sanjie then left. Rui knew that when someone dies, a ghost official comes to take them. Therefore, he spent days on the path of the dead, observing. One day, he saw a ghost official rushing by. Rui pretended to be a ghost prostitute and seduced him, saying, "Where are you going, sir?" The ghost official saw Rui and was extremely beautiful, so he said, "I am here to arrest someone."

Wei said, "If there is a close relative who is gravely ill, I will check if there is his name in the ticket. If there is, I will arrange for a generous gift." The official was pleased and handed the ticket to Wei. Wei checked it and found that there was indeed the name of Yin Sheng, but he falsely said there wasn't. Taking advantage of the official's distraction, Wei removed Yin Sheng's name, then folded the ticket and handed it back to the official, who left. Wei returned and told Yin Sheng. Five days later, Sister Three indeed arrived, holding three magical pills in her hand. She swallowed one herself, about to speak, when suddenly a deity appeared and took her away. Sister Three threw the remaining pills on the ground and vanished. Wei quickly picked up the pills and said, "You should take them immediately. Sister Three will surely come to claim them." Then he and Yin Sheng divided the pills and took them. Soon after, Sister Three returned, looking frightened, and asked, "Where are the pills?" Wei told her that he and Yin Sheng had divided and taken them. Sister Three said, "You two have harmed me." Yin Sheng held a pair of jade fish pendants, and Sister Three took one. As Yin Sheng was about to ask her, Sister Three hastily said, "I am already pregnant, and whoever wears this fish pendant in the future will be your son." After she spoke, she disappeared immediately. After taking the pills, Yin Sheng's illness was cured instantly. He greatly appreciated Sister Three, knowing that she must be imprisoned by the willow spirit and could not return. Wei, having obtained the magical pills, had already become a ghost immortal.

Fifteen years later, my uncle, who held office in a county under Xi'an, fell ill, and I went to visit him. Upon my arrival, my uncle's illness had already recovered. Coincidentally, the county examination was taking place, and my uncle ordered me to assist in the examination. There was a young boy carrying a jade fish, and I carefully examined it, finding it no different from what we kept at home, and I was greatly surprised. I saw the name "Yin Li" written on his paper, knew he was born to my third sister, but it was inconvenient to acknowledge him. I then inquired about his residence and family background, and fortunately, it was not far from the county office. The next day, I went to visit him personally, approached the door and knocked gently, and an old woman came out. When I saw her, she was the mother of my third sister. Later, the old woman returned, and the young boy was reading in the room. She told him, "Your father has come." At that time, my uncle lacked an heir, and I was overjoyed beyond expectation. I asked about my third sister, and the old woman said, "Because of the poison, she was imprisoned in a mountain cave and suffered greatly, hoping for rescue every day." I said, "Wait until the rites examination is completed, and we will discuss it after returning together." I returned to the office, reviewed the rites texts, and found them quite knowledgeable, so I told my uncle about the matter. He selected Yin Li as the top candidate, and he was admitted to the school. As we were about to discuss returning, Yin Li wanted to see his mother before proceeding. The old woman said, "It is impossible." Yin Li wept and said, "Even if I knew my mother's situation, it would still be a small comfort to express my resentment and longing."

The elderly woman, out of necessity, led the protagonist to a mountain and pointed to a cliff, saying, "The cave entrance halfway up the cliff is where your mother resides." The protagonist looked at it and found it too high to reach, so he burst into tears, saying, "I have failed to fulfill my duties as a son and cannot rescue my mother from her difficulties. Why should my mother have such a son, and how can I not bring shame upon her?" He cried for a long time, but there was no response. The elderly woman tried to persuade the protagonist to return home, but he leaped up and said, "Is there truly no way to save my mother? Is there truly no one who can save her? If so, I will appeal to Pure Yang at the Temple of Heaven and the Jade Palace, asking him about the crime he committed for stealing the elixir to save his wife." After speaking, he saw a young woman standing at the cave entrance, recognizing her as his mother, he bowed to the sky. After finishing his bow, the young woman said, "Do not speak rashly. Return home and ask your mother-in-law to help save me." She then vanished. The protagonist was filled with both joy and sorrow, returning to inform his father. His father asked the elderly woman to accompany them back to Fenyang, but she said, "My daughter entrusted your son to me, a heavy responsibility. I was afraid I could not bear it. Now that your son has grown up and you are reunited, the burden has been lifted. Please let us part ways from here." The father tried to keep her, but she had already disappeared.

The son and father returned, and Hui was greatly pleased. Li saw the auspicious snow and knelt for a long time without standing up. Ru pulled him up and said, "Your intention, I have thoroughly understood. The willow fairy's anger is not because of the pill, but because your mother stole the pill and pretended to be her teacher, so she harbors hidden anger that cannot be released. Even if you mourn, she will not show pity; only by respectfully seeking her teacher can you hope for a solution. However, Chunyang comes and goes unpredictably. Wait for him to come, and I will point him out." One day, Ru said, "Chunyang has arrived. On a certain mountain, there is a Chunyang Pavilion. Go there quickly. Wait outside the pavilion and follow the lame hermit who carries a precious sword into the pavilion. Follow him closely, close the door, and bow deeply. State your request. If the great immortal speaks, respond to everything. Do not harbor a heart of fear, difficulty, or complacency." Li hurried to the mountain and indeed saw the lame hermit enter the pavilion. Li entered, bowed deeply, and stated his request as Ru had instructed. The hermit said, "You are mistaken. I only know how to transform food to satisfy hunger; I have no other abilities." Li bowed and wept, saying nothing. The hermit said, "As you say, the one who seeks my release is indeed a fox. What does that have to do with you?" Li said, "The fox is my mother."

The Daoist said, "Your mother lies ill in the cave, her condition grave. I shall free her, yet doubt she may return. Her ailment can only be cured by a potion made with the blood of a man's middle finger. Canst thou endure the pain to cut thy finger and offer her aid?" Li answered, "I can." The Daoist drew out a cup of wine, unsheathed his sword, and bade Li close his eyes and extend his hand. Li obeyed. The Daoist said, "I am about to cut, fear not." Li assented. After a while, the Daoist made no move to cut, and Li grew suspicious. He opened his eyes and looked around, but the Daoist had vanished. Li returned home with tears in his eyes. Upon arriving, he saw a beautiful woman conversing with his family in the hall. He inquired, and his father replied, "This is thy birth mother." The mother and son met, their emotions a blend of sorrow and joy. The third sister said, "Though I have returned, I shall not stay long. I will be home for three or five days before departing. Since I took the elixir of immortality, I need not eat and am not hungry. Moreover, I have spent seventeen years in the cave, dedicated to spiritual cultivation, and have attained magical arts. This is both a misfortune and a blessing." Li heard this and wept, saying, "I have just seen my mother's face, and now we must part again. How can I bear this?" The third sister said, "I can stay for thee for three months." Li requested to stay for a longer period. The third sister said, "That is acceptable." She remained for three months before leaving.

The heir of the Huai family passed away, and all household affairs were managed by Rui. He also ordered Li to take the local examination and be admitted to the school. The old man lived to a rare old age and, at the time of his death, spat out a red pill. Rui gave it to his daughter-in-law Quan, saying, "This is the elixir of immortality your father-in-law took in his youth. Make sure to keep it well; it can cure all diseases." Rui then moved his own body from the previous year and was buried together with the old man and the Huai family. Afterward, he left and never returned. Later, Li contracted a seasonal illness, and all medicines were ineffective. He was about to die when Quan suddenly remembered her mother-in-law's words and gave the old man's red pill to Li to take. And he was immediately cured. The Daoist Master Xubai said: "If one fears ghosts and foxes, fearing their lack of righteousness, then one's heart must also be unrighteous; if one does not fear ghosts and foxes, not fearing their evil, then one's heart must also be free from evil. From the fact that one does not fear ghosts and foxes, one can see that the person's character is upright, and their intentions are righteous. Therefore, not only does one not fear ghosts and foxes, but ghosts and foxes also fear him. If they fear him, they will respect him, and therefore, one does not hear of upright and virtuous people dying at the hands of foxes or being tormented by ghosts." This story is similar to the legend of the Leifeng Pagoda, but this is even more pure and righteous. -- Shangyuan Li Yu, respectfully annotated. -- Zhao Cheng

In the county of Ji, there was a man named Zhao Cheng, who had little property but some savings. He had a friend named A, who was extremely poor and repeatedly borrowed money from Zhao Cheng without repaying it. Later, Friend A wanted to start a business and asked Zhao Cheng for capital. Zhao Cheng gave him even more, and he did not let his son know about it. Friend A's business thrived, and his wealth increased, all due to Zhao Cheng's efforts. Zhao Cheng suddenly fell ill and knew he would not recover. He wrote down the names and amounts owed by those who owed him money and gave it to his son Rong, but he did not mention Friend A's debt. He sent someone to summon Friend A, wanting to say goodbye forever. Friend A did not come, and Zhao Cheng excused him by saying he was busy. Soon after, Zhao Cheng died. Friend A came immediately to pay respects, and his grief was so great that he had no tears. He then said to Zhao Cheng's son Rong, "Your father, before he fell ill, borrowed some money from me for business; do you know about it?" Rong was surprised and said, "I don't know. My father did not mention it either." Friend A said, "I understand that this money cannot be repaid now. Let's discuss it after your father's funeral." After saying this, he left. Rong said to himself, "Our family has no use for money, so how could we have borrowed so much from Friend A? If it is true, Friend A and my father were close friends, and he would not lie." He could not help but weep in front of the coffin and said, "If it is true that we borrowed money from Friend A, why not include it in the list of debts?" Rong had not finished weeping when Zhao Cheng sat up in the coffin and said to Rong, "Do not cry. I really did not borrow any money from Friend A. Go and summon Friend A, but do not say I have died or that I have recovered." The messenger went, and Zhao Cheng dressed in his funeral clothes and went to the guesthouse to wait.

Not long after, a group of people, including 甲某, arrived first. Others heard that 诚 had died and then came back to life, saw that 诚's behavior was as usual but his face was ashen and devoid of color, and were greatly astonished. 甲某 arrived next, saw 诚, and felt deeply ashamed, forced himself to interact, and felt very uncomfortable. 诚 said, "甲某, you borrowed money from me without a valid reason, and I won't even mention that. The first time you borrowed from me, how much did you take, and did 甲甲兄 handle the transaction?" 甲, seeing 甲某 present, dared not deny it, and said, "Yes, it did happen." 诚 then asked, "The second time, did 乙某兄 handle the transaction, and have you repaid the loan yet?" 甲 replied, "No, I have not repaid it." 诚 continued, "As for 乙某兄, you not only troubled him to borrow money repeatedly, but 乙某兄 also witnessed me giving you the capital for your business."

Not long after, a group of people, including 甲某, arrived first. Others heard that 诚 had died and then came back to life, saw that 诚's behavior was as usual but his face was ashen and devoid of color, and were greatly astonished. 甲某 arrived next, saw 诚, and felt deeply ashamed, forced himself to interact, and felt very uncomfortable. 诚 said, "甲某, you borrowed money from me without a valid reason, and I won't even mention that. The first time you borrowed from me, how much did you take, and did 甲甲兄 handle the transaction?" 甲, seeing 甲某 present, dared not deny it, and said, "Yes, it did happen." 诚 then asked, "The second time, did 乙某兄 handle the transaction, and have you repaid the loan yet?" 甲 replied, "No, I have not repaid it." 诚 continued, "As for 乙某兄, you not only troubled him to borrow money repeatedly, but 乙某兄 also witnessed me giving you the capital for your business."

I lent you some money, but it is still less than one-fifth of what I am owed. How much more so when there is no such thing? The others asked why, and Zhao Cheng said, "Jia fabricated a story about my death and claimed that I borrowed money from him. He went to my son to demand repayment." The others heard this and were all indignant. Zhao Cheng said to Jia, "With all of you here, did I actually borrow money from you?" Jia remained silent. Zhao Cheng was angry and said, "Do you intend to ruin my family?" Jia blushed and said, "No. You did not actually borrow money from me. Because I feared you would die, and your son would come to demand repayment, I fabricated a debt to settle it partially." Zhao Cheng said, "The loan agreement you wrote up, I burned it long ago." He then told his son, "Accept it, do not allow anyone to demand repayment. If you disobey my command, you will be disobedient to me." The son agreed. Zhao Cheng said again, "All people can be friends, but those without a conscience cannot be friends." After saying this, he spat coldly on Jia's face and left in a huff. The others followed him and saw that Zhao Cheng had lain down on his spirit bed. They looked at him and found that he had already died. The recluse of empty white said: The matter of Zhao Cheng indeed greatly pleases the people. Going freely between the realms of the living and the dead, this is indeed a light retribution. The meticulous annotation by Li Yu of Shangyuan.

**The Evil Dream** A man from a small town, who was a small person dwelling in all forms of evil without limit, lived in modest comfort and enjoyed a relatively affluent life. One day, slightly drunk, he went to sleep and had a dream in which a man invited him to drink together at night. The man excused himself, saying he was too drunk. However, the man insisted and forced him to go. Just as he stepped out the door, he suddenly remembered that the man had died long ago and that he had a grudge against him. The man had once been a servant to his daughter, and he had been persuaded by a nun to make his daughter shave her head and become a female nun. The girl's father heard about this and confronted him on the road. The man, enraged, attacked him in a fit of anger. The man, harboring resentment, fell ill and died. The man in the dream remembered this and wanted to return quickly, but the man grabbed him and said, "Where are you going? Now I can repay my grudge on you!" He then attacked the man, but still could not overpower him. Suddenly, two men appeared, like officials, and said to the man, "You are indeed as arrogant as you seem." They all attacked him. The man called out to his family for help, but heard them laughing and talking, and his cries went unanswered. Soon, three men dragged him away. They brought him to a place that resembled an office for officials, tied him to the ground, and left him under guard. The man thought to himself that he had inflicted such humiliation on others, but had never been humiliated himself, and felt a deep sense of resentment. For more than ten days, he had not eaten, and felt as though a fire was burning in his stomach, yet he did not die. He angrily told the guard, "If I have committed a crime, I should die according to the law, but not starve to death in your office!" The guard replied, "The officials are out on official business and have not returned; all criminals are waiting to be imprisoned. Why can't you wait?" Suddenly, two officials appeared and said, "The officials are about to convene." They quickly bound his neck with ropes and dragged him away.

Upon arriving at an office, the tiles were a beautiful blue-green, and the building was extremely magnificent. Among several people, someone was pushed forward and told, "Wait here temporarily." The person saw several others wearing shackles and realized they were criminals. He then asked, "What is this office?" One of them replied, "The underworld." The person was shocked and said, "I haven't died yet, how did I get here?" Soon, the officer pushed the person inside. Inside, the person saw the official sitting upright and solemnly, with horse-faced and ox-headed beings lined up on both sides. When the official saw the person, he became very angry and said, "This person has lost all sense of right and wrong; quickly cut out his heart and make him be reborn as an animal." A red-bearded official interjected, "His term of life is not yet completed." The official retorted, "Punishment cannot be delayed; deserved punishment, let him suffer in this life!" The official shouted at the officer to drag the person down. The officer led the person to a place where there was a small pond, not very large, with clear water that allowed one to see the bottom. Two people were bathing in it.

Upon arriving at an office, the tiles were a beautiful blue-green, and the building was extremely magnificent. Among several people, someone was pushed forward and told, "Wait here temporarily." The person saw several others wearing shackles and realized they were criminals. He then asked, "What is this office?" One of them replied, "The underworld." The person was shocked and said, "I haven't died yet, how did I get here?" Soon, the officer pushed the person inside. Inside, the person saw the official sitting upright and solemnly, with horse-faced and ox-headed beings lined up on both sides. When the official saw the person, he became very angry and said, "This person has lost all sense of right and wrong; quickly cut out his heart and make him be reborn as an animal." A red-bearded official interjected, "His term of life is not yet completed." The official retorted, "Punishment cannot be delayed; deserved punishment, let him suffer in this life!" The official shouted at the officer to drag the person down. The officer led the person to a place where there was a small pond, not very large, with clear water that allowed one to see the bottom. Two people were bathing in it.

The official said to someone, "Why not also bathe it?" The person wished to do so but hesitated, and the official pushed him into the water. Afterward, the water became as dirty as sewage, with an unusual odor. The two people pressed his head to make him drink, and after a long time, they finally disappeared. The person usually carried fragrant items to avoid foul odors when using the toilet, but he could not help vomiting due to the foul water. After vomiting, the water returned to its clear state. Suddenly, he felt the water was very cold and wanted to get out, but he felt something holding his feet, preventing him from moving an inch. Afterward, the north wind blew, and the water froze completely. His body became numb, and his limbs felt as if they were not part of his body, but he only felt a slight warmth in his chest. With a heavily dressed body to keep warm, enduring such severe cold was unbearable. Suddenly, he woke up, and the sun was already high in the sky. He quickly ordered the family to fetch warm clothes to clothe him, and after a while, he returned to his usual state. When the family asked him, he did not hide anything.

By evening, the protagonist ate until full and went to sleep. Suddenly, they felt extremely hungry and opened their eyes to see themselves in a garden, surrounded by countless hungry ghosts. The ghosts pointed at the protagonist and said, "So fat like this, not knowing how many decades of hunger it took to become as thin as our skin and bones." The ghosts mostly ate bean cakes to satisfy their hunger. The protagonist, being extremely hungry, had no choice but to beg for it. The ghosts said, "You have never begged for leftover food from others; it is certainly not worthy of pity to beg from others. Now that you are begging from others, others will also imitate you. And what we eat is actually the food you feed your dogs and horses, how could we deign to eat it?" They all refused to give it. Suddenly, the ghosts joyfully told each other, "Today the officials came to release the wind, we can each find food." Soon enough, the gate of the garden opened, and the ghosts eagerly rushed out. The protagonist followed them. By the roadside, there was a restaurant, and the protagonist rushed to ask for food. They ate and drank a bit but still felt hungry. Wanting to eat again, they suddenly remembered there was nothing in their bag. Fortunately, they seemed to have met the owner before, so they said, "How much money do I owe, please temporarily record it as an external debt, and I will pay it soon." The owner of the restaurant did not think this was acceptable. The protagonist said, "I truly have no money."

The shopkeeper said, "You have no money, but do you have clothes?" The person replied, "It is extremely cold for two days, and without clothes, I will surely freeze to death." The shopkeeper, angered, said, "I owed you hundreds of interest before, and I forcibly took your clothes as collateral. It was also cold at that time, and I did not freeze to death." He then drove away the person and forced them to remove their clothes. The person emerged, hands on shoulders, and was called out by someone, "Come, I will clothe you!" And they said, "In such cold weather, taking someone's clothes as collateral is extremely unjust." They handed over a blue cotton robe to the person. The person, being extremely cold, did not have time to look closely and quickly put it on. The person who gave the robe had already disappeared, and the person transformed into a pig. There was a familiar butcher who tied and took the pig. The person saw seven pigs tied up, and they were the last one. When butchers slaughter pigs, they first strike the pig's head with a wooden stick to make it unconscious, so as not to feel the pain of death, which is also a kind gesture of the butcher. The person had always seen this and said, "Why kill a pig if it is not necessary?" The person was the butcher's employer, so they dared not disobey. The person saw the butcher slaughter the pigs as they said, and they regretted their own words. The person who transformed into a pig was only awakened when their belly was cut open and their intestines were pulled out. When they woke up, they were in extreme pain.

The person was greatly afraid. Therefore, he thought of taking a nap during the day and waited until night, sitting to avoid the suffering of nightmares. At noon, he immediately lay down, and at that time, it was hot and humid. He ordered his wife to fan him. Suddenly, he felt that the heat was unusually intense, and with closed eyes, he cursed and said, "Beast! Even fanning has no power?" He heard the son say, "Beast, do not curse. In a while, it will get even hotter." He opened his eyes and looked at it, finding himself lying on a bed of iron wires, with the coal under the bed just turning red. He wanted to get up, but two people held him down with iron forks. Soon, the fire turned completely red, and his hair and beard were burning. His skin was scorched, and he was extremely painful, thinking it would be better to die quickly. He said, "It's okay." This one said, "The night is not yet over, and a little more time. Can also slightly vent my anger." He asked, "What anger?" They said, "My son died because of his wife. Actually, he died because of this person." He asked for details. They said, "It is difficult to say." A while later, the fire bed suddenly disappeared, and he found himself lying on a cool ground, extremely thirsty, begging for water. The two people said, "Please wait a moment. When we give you water, do not say that it does not please you. You will greatly anger us." The person saw the two people move a large jar and filled it with money. He said, "Where did this money come from?" They said, "All the money you have collected, but it was obtained unjustly." The two people set up a three-legged pot nearby, cooked with dry wood, and soon the fire was strong and the pot was red. They took the money and put it in the pot, melting it into molten copper. They used an iron ladle to pour the molten copper on the person. If it flowed out of the mouth, the skin peeled off and bubbled up. If it entered the throat, the internal organs were damaged. Usually, he worried about gain, but at this moment, he wanted to lose but could not. When the molten copper was finished, the dream began to wake up.

A certain person has a fondness for eating dogs and chickens. When he eats them, he ties them alive, uses boiling water to scald them to remove their fur, and then kills them. The sounds of the animals' suffering and dying are unbearable to hear, but he finds it pleasurable. Later, he dreams of being naked and falling to the ground, with dogs and chickens surrounding and injuring him. The chickens peck his eyes, and the dogs tear his kidneys. He tries to fend them off, but his hands and feet are unable to move. There are three people sitting on the ground drinking nearby, who call out to him for help, but he ignores them, feeling very resentful. After a while, the three people get up and chase away the dogs and chickens. The person then repents and turns to virtue. The three people then tie him to a post and pour boiling water over his head, causing his entire body to rot, while the chickens crow and the dogs bark, as if they are all content. Around midnight, the people and animals disappear. The person wants to return home but does not recognize the path. Half a mile away, he suddenly sees a village. He approaches it and sees only one house, with the outer door slightly open. He wants to find someone to ask the way, but after knocking ten times, no one answers. He bursts into the house and sees five or six men with hats sitting together drinking.

His wife held a pipa, facing him, playing and singing. A man embraced his daughter, kissing and being intimate to the fullest extent. He became very angry, grabbed his fist and beat his wife, accidentally injuring his daughter's eye. His anger was intense, and his wife and daughter disappeared. Trying to leave, a demon with a large hammer met him and said, "You have defiled other women, others have defiled your wife and daughter, why are you angry?" The demon hit him on the head with the hammer. He woke up, still with a headache as if it were broken. Seeing his daughter's eye bruised, he asked her, and she shyly replied, "I dreamed that my father hurt me, did you forget?" He heard this and felt sad. He then asked his wife, "Tell me the truth, I won't blame you." The wife said, "On the night you had a bad dream, you dreamed of a divine being commanding me to make my daughter a prostitute to counteract your evil deeds." He was filled with shame and regret, unable to do anything. Subsequently, he suffered iron chains through the ribs, copper knives scraping the face, eyes gouged out, knees struck, all suffered together, the unbearable pain of having the shoulders and skin scrubbed, all of which were caused by the dream, lasting for ten days before ending.

The protagonist's spirit was severely weakened, and he had little appetite for food, as if just recovering from a serious illness. Every time he ate, he experienced unbearable dreams caused by hunger. Knowing the suffering of beggars, whenever beggars came to his door, he would surely command his family to give them something, so they could turn around and beg elsewhere. Once remembering the frightening dream, he could not bear the trembling, fearing the dream's condition would become worse, and thus thought of praying to deities to relieve it. He worshipped almost all the nearby temples and shrines. By the tenth day, the dreams remained as before, only the hunger dreams no longer occurred. Every time waking from a dream, it was never the divine, and he said: "Wooden and earthen idols have nothing to do with cause and effect. If my power reaches, I will surely change all temples into shelters for orphans and the poor." He calculated that his dreams had lasted for ten days, another night's pain is also a little rest, still a kind of misfortune. By the time of dawn, someone holding an iron hook was about to pull out his tongue. He was shocked and said: "This is an unprecedented event. What sin did I commit, and why is this being added to me?" The person replied: "Not caused by destroying the sacred." Upon hearing this, he realized that the punishment was self-inflicted, endured the pain and being pulled without making a sound. Upon waking, he bit his own tongue, and blood filled his mouth.

I have been born with the recurring nightmares and the additional punishments of having my tongue pulled out. I have come to understand that one's fortune or misfortune is determined by one's own actions. Therefore, I have turned away from my previous actions and corrected my new mistakes. The punishment of having my tongue pulled out was unexpectedly released. Since then, I have vowed not to be greedy for external wealth, not to consume copper, not to eat meat, not to be exposed to boiling water, not to engage in butchery, not to have inappropriate relationships with women, and not to let my wife dream of becoming a prostitute. I have been cautious in all my actions and have not acted recklessly. After ten nights of suffering, I have experienced relief after several months. I know that my sins are deep and cannot be completely erased in a short time. I do not know what actions I should take to completely eliminate the torment of my dreams. It is said that filial piety can move spirits and turn bad luck into good fortune. However, I have lost both of my parents and cannot practice filial piety. Therefore, I built a simple hut near their graves and performed elaborate rituals to honor them. I could not help but cry out in grief, saying, "I am not filial and could not serve my parents while they were alive. I regret it too late. Now I wish to follow them into the afterlife, to repay their great kindness." After crying, I lay on the ground without getting up. I stayed in the hut at night and performed three rituals every morning, noon, and evening. After two months, I no longer had bad dreams. The local community approved of my repentance and encouraged me to return home. However, I still had a recurring nightmare of being trapped in ice, occurring once every ten days. Suddenly, I remembered a young woman who was a nun and had reached the age of marriage. I redeemed her and arranged her marriage. The recurring nightmare of being in a cold ice prison finally ceased.

The Pure White Hermit said: I have heard that the perfected person has no dreams, for they have no desires. From this, we understand that the good or bad nature of dreams is determined by their causes. Those who experience evil dreams should not think that these are mere illusions unrelated to reality; it is because their actions during the day harbor ill intent, and their thoughts run counter to the proper path. The divine uses these dreams to warn, and also to punish, making them endure silently. It is said: To ward off evil dreams, princes should cultivate virtue, officials should perform their duties, and scholars should cultivate themselves, then calamities and misfortunes will naturally dissipate. Such evil dreams can serve to supplement the laws of the state, which may not reach, so that evildoers may gradually learn to restrain themselves. Yang Zihou There are many in the world who persist in evil without repentance; why do they not receive such evil dreams as warnings? I wish to ask the one with the foot split. Above Yuan Li Yu respectfully annotates.

Xu Cuie In Shanxi Province, there was a man named Liu Xiwén. I have forgotten the name of his county or city, but he knew about talismans, geomancy, and feng shui, as well as divination and fortune-telling, though he was not very skilled in these arts. However, he did not pretend to be an expert when he was not, nor did he extort money from those who believed in his abilities. He was an upright person. Regarding the use of talismans, the outcomes were unpredictable, but the main principle was to be fearless. Even if the talismans did not work, he would still try them, and if he suffered humiliation from ghosts or fox spirits, he would treat it with indifference. After his wife died, he had no one to rely on, so he relied on his skills to make a living in various places. He traveled to Pingyang, where someone being plagued by a fox spirit invited him to go. Liu set up an altar and burned talismans, but they did not work. He burned them three times, but they still did not work, and Liu was helpless. Then a beautiful woman came and said to Liu, "My coming is not because of the power of your talismans, but because I came on my own. Because our families are close relatives, I came at the command of my parents to invite you." Liu asked her, and she said, "My younger sister Xu Cuie was engaged to you when she was young, but you broke the engagement and married someone else. My father wanted to find another match for my sister, but she refused. She has remained chaste until now, waiting for you, so I invited you to come to get married." Liu said, "I have no other marriage." The woman replied, "This is what my father did, and you should remember it." Liu was delighted and did not have time to ask many questions, and immediately wanted to go with her. She said, "Why the hurry? When I leave, the patient you treated will recover, and you can accept their gratitude. Five days later, I will meet you at the east gate at noon." After speaking, she disappeared.

A Fox and a Journey In the beginning, Liu Xiwén's father picked up a small fox on the road, which seemed to be newly born. He brought it home and fed it with rice porridge. After three days, the fox opened its eyes and could walk. After two months, it looked like a small dog, with soft and lovely fur. Liu was five or six years old at the time and often played with the fox, sometimes holding it while sleeping during the day. His father saw this and joked, "This is your wife." Another three years passed, and the fox grew to the size of a large dog. Liu always ate with it. Suddenly, the fox disappeared, and Liu thought about it and cried. His father joked with him, "Are you thinking about your wife?" Liu definitely remembered this, but he did not expect this to happen. When Liu received thanks from a sick family, after five days, he decided to go meet a woman. He left through the east gate and saw a small carriage with a horse waiting on the road. The driver saw Liu and asked, "Are you Mr. Liu, the nobleman?" Liu replied, "I am not a nobleman, I am Liu Xiwén." The driver said, "I am the one who comes to welcome the nobleman." He immediately took clothes and a hat from the carriage and gave them to Liu. Liu dressed and got into the carriage, rang the bell, and started the journey. Not long after, he suddenly saw city walls a few miles away. Liu asked, "Where is this place?" The driver replied, "This is the city of Yueyang." Liu thought to himself, "One hundred and twenty miles, and it seems to have arrived instantly?" Soon, they arrived at the entrance of a village, where several people dressed in fine clothes stood in a row, as if waiting for a guest. The driver said, "They are all here to welcome the nobleman." Liu heard this, got off the carriage, straightened his clothes, and bowed.

Together, they accompanied Liu to the grand gate, where they hung lights and decorated with colorful streamers. Upon entering, they observed the traditional wedding ceremony with red candles, which was as grand as that of a noble family. Liu was overwhelmed with joy upon seeing the new bride, whose beauty surpassed that of his elder sister. Later, when they retired to bed, Liu said, "I have learned about your family members, but I do not know the date of our marriage." Cui E smiled and replied, "When I was young, you would take me to sleep with you; after I left, you would cry thinking of me. Have you forgotten?" Liu suddenly remembered. After half a year, Cui E said, "It is time for you to leave; you cannot stay with my family for too long." Liu agreed, saying, "Yes, but I travel without a fixed destination. If you do not accompany me, it would be unreasonable; if you do accompany me, the constant wandering would be too much for you, and it would greatly obstruct my affairs. What should I do?" Cui E said, "It is not difficult. You go ahead. No matter where you lodge, I will come to you every night." Liu remained silent, and Cui E said, "Please do not doubt me; I will not break my word." Liu left, and every night, Cui E would indeed come to the inn where he stayed, and she would predict that certain events the next day would be fruitless. Liu followed her advice, and indeed, everything went smoothly without difficulty. After more than a month, Cui E wished to return home for a visit and asked to return in ten days. Liu asked, "Have you not returned home even once in more than a month?" Cui E replied, "Yes." Liu then asked, "Where should I wait for you?" Cui E said, "There is no need. You will not travel more than a hundred miles away, and I can find you myself." Liu agreed, and Cui E left.

Liu You arrived at a mountain village and heard the cries of about four or five households. Liu was surprised and asked, "Why are the deaths the same?" The villagers replied, "There are two snake spirits in the eastern south mountain. They transform into human form, with the female deceiving strong men and the male tormenting beautiful women, and they sometimes devour young children. Those who are crying now have all lost their sons, wives, and young boys and girls." Liu felt compassion and asked, "How do you know they are snake spirits?" The villagers said, "At the head of the village, there is a large bay with extremely clear water. These creatures come together every night to drink water." Liu asked, "Can we see them?" They replied, "Yes." That evening, a brave villager accompanied Liu, hiding near the bay to wait. After the second watch, they saw four bright lamps coming swiftly from afar. The villagers said, "The bright lamps are the snake eyes." Upon closer inspection, the snakes were about five zhang long and one chi thick, causing Liu's hair to stand on end. In the morning, they observed the tracks of the snakes' movements. The grass on their coming path was flattened, and the grass on their going path was bent upward. Liu, seeing this, said, "I can eliminate this poison." The villagers were delighted and asked for his method. Liu said, "Urgently buy dozens of sharp knives and prepare firecrackers and noisemakers." The villagers followed his instructions. Liu and the villagers buried the sharp knives in the grass along the path the snakes took, slightly exposing their blades, placing one every half step.

After the burial was completed, Liu told the villagers, "When the snake is about to leave, quickly ignite firecrackers and strike noisy instruments together to frighten it." The crowd collectively agreed. The snake, frightened, hurried away. Upon reaching the place where the knife was buried, the snake's heavy body caused the grass to bend more, and the tip of the knife was prominently exposed, piercing the snake's abdomen. The snake, in pain, moved even faster; the faster it went, the more it hurt, and the more it hurt, the faster it moved. Soon, the snake's abdomen split open. The female snake, which was behind, also had a sense of spirit and quickly returned, going back the way it came. The villagers were greatly afraid, saying, "The two snakes only injured one; the other one will surely seek revenge." Fearing the burden of Liu, they would not tolerate him at all. Liu had no choice but to move to the temple. Knowing that misfortune was self-inflicted, he regretted it was too late, and only thought that Cui would come to bid him farewell forever, death would be without regret. At dusk, Cui hurriedly entered and said, "Your time of death has arrived. I will not let you do it here." Liu explained the situation, and Cui said, "Blame you for causing trouble! Tonight, the demon will surely come to seek revenge. I can hide you for two nights, but not three." She instructed Liu to hide behind the deity, with objects to cover him, and wrote a talisman to pacify it, saying, "Do not cough, do not move unnecessarily, and endure hunger. Life and death are involved, not trivial!" After speaking, she left the temple and looked far away. Soon, the snake arrived with the wind, circling in the air, hissing like thunder. The villagers held their breath and dared not come out, and Cui also trembled with fear. After a long time, unable to find Liu, it finally left.

The next evening, Cui approached Liu where he was hiding and told him, "By avoiding the difficulties of tonight, you can achieve a fortunate survival." Liu asked her about it, and Cui replied, "There's no need to ask. Stay hidden and do not move." When the snake arrived, its ferocity was even more intense, and it did not return until dawn. Cui happily told Liu, "Get up. You have not eaten for two days and should have starved to death." After eating, she led Liu away. They reached the back of the mountain, and she pointed distantly and said, "That is the cave entrance. There is a sage inside the cave. When you get there, prostrate yourself in sincere worship; after the worship, express your sorrow for needing rescue and prostrate yourself while weeping. At dusk, be sure to remain silent and stay hidden. Do not be afraid when the monster arrives." She also taught him the words to use to appeal to the sage: "The venomous creature is not far from the great sage's abode. It is now harming people, and it is likely that the great sage would not tolerate seeing it and would certainly eliminate it. I will eliminate it, no different from the great sage doing so, and I am also removing a harm to people. The harm that affects oneself is an undeserved calamity, and it is also something the great sage would surely pity." Liu asked, "Do you know the name of the sage?" Cui replied, "I do not know the name of the sage, nor whether the sage is male or female." Liu was doubtful but had no choice but to go.
