Upon reaching the cave, he prostrated himself and wept, as instructed. As the moon rose in the east, he suddenly heard the sound of the wind and saw a giant snake arrive with the wind. As it approached, it turned around and flew away. It came and went repeatedly. Liu looked up and saw a female sage standing on the cave with a sword, realizing that the snake fled because it feared the sage and the sword.

Shortly, a snake suddenly came from the side and swallowed Liu. The immortal killed the snake and saved Liu, but Liu was already dead. The immortal tried to treat him with immortal medicine, but Liu's throat was tightly closed, and the medicine could not enter. The immortal could not bear to abandon him, looked around and saw no one, so he kissed him to send saliva. He heard someone laugh and say, "Truly, you are rescuing someone from the well." Lifting his head, he saw a woman standing in front of him. Examining her closely, he said, "The wild fox is despicable. Could not save her husband, and entrusted herself to me. What time is it? What is happening? And laughed at it with ordinary feelings." Cui apologized. Liu sat up on the ground, saw Cui standing next to a woman, knew it was the immortal who saved him, and kneeled and thanked. Cui said, "The great immortal kissed you to treat you, you cannot thank once and stop." The immortal glared at Cui. Cui said, "This is a great favor, cannot be hidden or ignored." She also said, "If you are a guest, it is inconvenient to care for you during the journey. I wish the great immortal would stay for a few days, and then send you away." The woman remained silent. She entered the cave. Cui led Liu to follow her, and the woman did not stop them. Cui acted as a matchmaker, and they became husband and wife. Liu asked about her background, and the woman said, "I am the immortal Peony, after being humiliated by the immortal Lü, I have been hiding and practicing here, vowed never to marry. Because I treated you and lost my composure, provoked laughter, had to follow your request." The villagers saw that both snakes were dead, were extremely happy. Not seeing Liu, thought Liu was also dead, built a temple to worship him.

Liu lived in seclusion with two wives. After four or five years, his wife gave birth to a son. The cave was not suitable for raising a child, so Cui requested to take care of the child in the village, and the wife agreed. Liu and Cui took the child to the village, where the villagers welcomed them with great enthusiasm. Liu pointed to Cui and the child and said, "These are my wife and son. I wish to live here, so I ask for a place to stay." The villagers replied, "We have established a shrine in your honor. You can remove the statue and live here." Liu said, "I can live here, but the statue can remain. However, the merit lies with the fairy; what power do I have?" He then explained in detail. The villagers then erected a statue of the fairy beside Liu's statue and offered sacrifices to it four times a year. While Cui lived in the village, Liu would visit occasionally. After the son married and had a wife, Liu stopped coming, and Cui also disappeared. To this day, there are still descendants of Liu in the village. The monk of emptiness and purity said: Liu Xiwén's desire to control the poisonous insects was moved by the sound of crying, and he had no selfish motive. Acting without selfish intent, he was only focused on eliminating harm and had no fear of harm. Those who promote benefits have benefits, and those who eliminate harm are not harmed. Liu's death and resurrection, although it seems to be a fortunate escape, is not truly a fortunate escape.

The Peony Fairy has accumulated great merit, which is sufficient to achieve enlightenment. —— Shangyuan Li Yu, carefully annotated **Qi Shi** Locust plagues have been a recurring phenomenon throughout history, but none were as severe as those in the sixth year of the Xianfeng era (1856). In April of that year, locusts suddenly appeared, but they were not very numerous. However, they tended to form pairs. The villagers were greatly alarmed, fearing that the locusts would lay eggs and cause further damage. Soon enough, small locusts hatched, initially the size of ants, then the size of flies, and finally the size of large jujube seeds. The areas where locusts appeared, the rice plants were completely eaten. The locusts did not appear at the same time or in the same place. When they jumped, they all moved in the same direction, like a flowing river. When objects were used to block them, the locusts stopped and quickly piled up several inches thick. The places where they stopped became barren within half a day, causing significant damage. By the time the rice plants were about to bear fruit, the locusts had grown wings. When they flew, they covered the sky, blocking out the sun, and even high mountains could not be seen from the other side. When they landed, the rice plants were flattened, and each plant had about twenty to thirty locusts, which quickly devoured all the grains and leaves, making it a strange disaster. In the seventh year, locusts appeared again, but fortunately, the people worked together to catch and kill them, so the damage was not severe. In the eighth year, locusts appeared again, but birds ate them all, and the locusts were completely eradicated. Natural disasters are indeed governed by a fixed destiny.

Hearing of the disaster in Chang County, which was more severe than in previous years, Chang County had a woman named Qi, who was the wife of Le. Her husband had died and her son was young, with only her father-in-law still alive, but he had become disabled and could not walk. At first, when the father-in-law became ill, his legs were numb and painful, and the woman feared it would become paralysis. She used her savings to hire a doctor to treat him. The father-in-law said, "Savings are not easy to come by, and my illness is hard to treat. Many years' worth of personal savings spent in one go, very regrettable." The woman replied, "Savings can be kept but not misused. The daughter-in-law's savings were originally set aside for her father-in-law's funeral, now using them to help in an emergency, for my father-in-law's funeral after a hundred years." But her savings were exhausted and the medicine did not work. The woman wanted to sell her property to treat him, but the father-in-law forbade it and she did not listen. For three years, the illness only removed the numbness and pain, but his feet still could not walk. The woman knew the illness could not be cured, so she followed her father-in-law's orders and stopped treating him.

The remaining property was insufficient for food. Only by being diligent and thrifty could one avoid cold and hunger. The person ate simple food and personally cultivated and harvested. The villagers saw this and often deeply sighed. When the locusts were about to move to the neighboring fields of the person, the person sat on the ground and cried bitterly, crying and saying, "If there were no elderly person, I would starve with my child, it is fate, and I would not blame heaven." When the locusts had already infested the neighboring fields, the person cried even more bitterly, and the peasant woman tried to comfort him but he did not wake up. Later a woman said, "You are not good at comforting him." She then told the person, "Your father is hungry and has called you for a long time." The person heard this, stopped crying immediately, wiped away tears, and returned home. The old man saw the person with red and swollen eyes, knowing it was because of the locusts, and told the person, "This is a natural disaster, crying will not help. From now on, do not go to the fields, just accept it." The person followed the order, and the walking locusts jumped over the person's fields without causing any damage; the flying locusts also did not land on the person's fields. Six or seven people told me this story, but none of them knew the person's hometown or name.

The Master of Empty White said: Qi said, "Can be privately stored but not privately used," meaning that the private storage is for the parents. When the private storage was used to treat the old man's illness, it did not cure him, so they sold property again to hire a doctor, not being stingy with wealth when it came to the parents. From this, it can be inferred that the weeping for the locusts is not for oneself or one's children, but truly for the sake of the parents. If one weeps for the parents, then insufficient to provide the parents with delicious food, there are those who know they weep in secret; insufficient to provide the parents with meals, there are those who know they weep at night. Only by avoiding the harm of locusts, the locust god does not weep for the head of the place, because of their filial piety. Otherwise, those foolish women who wept for the locusts at that time, not seen in large numbers, why did all not escape the disaster of the locusts?

A virtuous governor ensures that locusts do not enter the territory; a filial wife ensures that locusts do not enter the fields. What is reasonable must necessarily occur in reality. This is the case as described by Han Yu. In the story of Jiao Nü: During the Ming Dynasty, Mu Siting from Guangxi and Jin Lu from Hubei served together as county magistrates in Shanxi, both being upright and incorruptible. Mu's daughter and Jin's son were the same age and were married. Soon after, Jin returned home due to mourning duties. As Jin passed through the villages, he encountered Li Ren, a martial arts expert. Li Ren had a daughter but no son and loved her dearly. He dressed his daughter in male clothing and taught her martial arts, naming her Jiao Nü. One day, Jiao Nü was standing outside and saw Jin passing by. She quickly returned home to tell her father, "An official with several attendants is passing by the gate, and they look like bandits. If they leave the village, they will harm the official. Father, why don't you go and help him?" Li Ren replied, "It is not my concern, so I can stay out of it." Jiao Nü said, "That is not right. With martial arts skills at hand, one should rely on them to help others in danger. If one sees someone in peril and does nothing, it is a great waste of one's abilities." Li Ren agreed with her. They quickly prepared and chased after Jin. They indeed saw six or seven bandits patrolling and about to rob a carriage. Li Ren shouted, "How can bandits operate openly on the official road during the day? Where is the law?" The bandits saw Li Ren and attacked him instead of the carriage.

Jiao feared that his father would lose, so he borrowed a carriage to go. Indeed, he saw his father in a helpless situation, and the situation was already doomed. Jiao loudly told his father, "I am here!" Ren was greatly relieved. Jiao had practiced using a slingshot since childhood, and within fifty steps, every shot hit its mark. The bandits could not resist, and they began to flee. Jin returned to Ren. Ren's left thigh was injured, and the bleeding did not stop. Jin wrote a prescription, saying, "This is a secret transmission from a mysterious person, specialized in treating wounds." Ren took the medicine, and not only did the bleeding stop, but the pain also subsided immediately. He treasured the prescription. Jin was grateful for Ren's rescue and saw Jiao's mother, who was wise and beautiful. He then said to Ren, "I have a son who is about the same age as Jin Lang. Can the two of them become sworn brothers?" Ren smiled and agreed, mistakenly thinking that Jin had referred to his own daughter as a son. Jin was fortunate to have no family burdens and hosted Ren for several days. The bandits came twice at night, but both times they were driven away by Ren and his father. Before leaving, Jin generously rewarded Ren. Jin returned to office in the northern province. He wanted to arrange a marriage for his son, but the journey was far, and many things were inconvenient, so he was deeply worried. At that time, Mugong resigned and returned home. Suddenly, a messenger arrived with a letter, which said, "Invite the young man to marry in the lake." Jin was delighted and wrote a reply, agreeing to send his son to the lake on the appointed date.

Young Master Jin Xigeng, following his father's command to go to Lake Hu for marriage, had a servant accompanying him. As they approached the border of Lake Hu, the servant fell ill and died. Jin Xigeng was left alone, wandering and feeling the hardship deeply. As dusk approached, he sought refuge at an inn where another person was already staying. This person asked Jin Xigeng about his journey, and Jin gave a brief account. The person pretended to be happy and said, "I am Zhao Cai, sent by the master of the house to welcome you, a distinguished guest." He flattered Jin Xigeng excessively, speaking with great deference. Being young, Jin did not suspect deceit and believed him. Zhao Cai asked, "Why does the young master have no servants?" Jin replied, "The servant died of illness. The person who rented the carriage could not wait and left on their own. Is there someone here who rents carriages?" Zhao Cai said, "There is no need. Go ahead a few miles, and there is a close relative of the master of the house. You can borrow a carriage to go. If you leave early, you will arrive by evening." Jin was overjoyed. The daughter of the innkeeper overheard their conversation and repeatedly told her father, saying, "Zhao Cai is unpredictable. If he leaves early, he will surely harm the young master. Father, you must save him." Indeed, before midnight, Zhao Cai immediately called the innkeeper to open the door and set off.

The shopkeeper said to his daughter, "Indeed. He has left; I will follow him. You must pack your belongings and leave quickly." The daughter agreed. The father left the village but did not see Cai and others, so he hurriedly pursued them for a few li. The moon was as bright as day, but when he looked ahead, there was still no sign of them. Knowing they had taken a shortcut, he quickly changed his route to follow them. It turned out that Cai had led Geng down a side path. Geng was suspicious and asked him, "This way is only a few li closer." Geng did not doubt him. They reached a pine forest, where Cai stopped. Geng asked him why, and Cai said, "We will settle the accounts later and continue on our way. I have often called you a noble person; now you should call me a noble person. I will impersonate your name to marry into the Mu family. Quickly tell me the details so I can prepare for the encounter." Geng remained silent. Cai then stabbed Geng in the lower body with a sharp blade. Geng had no choice but to speak, saying, "My father's letter of introduction and the tokens of trust from both families are all in the package." After he finished speaking, Cai raised the knife to kill Geng. Geng pleaded for a dignified death, and Cai agreed. He tied Geng to a tree and strangled him with a rope before leaving. Cai was afraid Geng might not be dead, so he looked back and saw that Geng was still breathing.

As he was about to strike him again, the shop owner happened to arrive, and the attacker fled in a hurry. The shop owner wanted to return and help Geng, but the attacker came back to attack him. When the shop owner's daughter arrived, the attacker finally fled. The shop owner carried Geng home, where Geng had already regained consciousness but was still severely injured. The shop owner quickly bought medicine to treat Geng, and the pain ceased instantly. Geng asked, "What kind of medicine is this, so remarkably effective?" The shop owner showed him the prescription. Geng looked at it and recognized his father's handwriting, saying, "Where did you get this prescription from?" The shop owner replied, "Several years ago, I saved a high-ranking official surnamed Jin. I was injured by bandits, and the official recorded this prescription to treat me. Because the prescription has such miraculous effects, I have treasured it." Geng was astonished and said, "Are you Mr. Li Ren?" Li Ren answered, "Yes." Geng addressed him as "uncle" and said, "The official you saved is my father. Both my father and I owe you a great debt of gratitude. Why do you live here?" Li Ren explained, "It is also because of your father. I have a grudge with the bandits, who have been harassing me. I fear that if my defenses are not strong enough, they might harm me, so I have moved three times before settling here."

Geng pointed to Ren's daughter and said, "This is my younger brother's kind elder brother?" Ren smiled and said, "She is not your elder brother but your elder sister, Jiao Niang." Geng was puzzled. Ren instructed the girl to change her makeup and appear. Geng was greatly pleased when he saw her. At that time, Jiao Niang's uncle Qi was present. After Ren and his daughter had both left, Geng told Qi, "I wish to marry Jiao Niang as my secondary wife. Do you think it is possible?" Qi replied, "Yes. I will try to be your matchmaker." Qi informed Ren of Geng's intention, and Ren was very pleased. Qi then told Geng, "The matter should not be delayed; we can get married tomorrow night. Since Zhao Cai has already gone under a false name, he has your father's letter, and Prince Mu also finds it hard to distinguish truth from falsehood. Delaying might ruin the plan. You should leave quickly. When you leave, make my cousin dress as a boy and follow you, pretending to be a servant, to avoid danger." Geng agreed. They followed Qi's advice. Ren had a close friend named Qiu, who was also a master in martial arts and feared by bandits. His residence was about ten miles away from Prince Mu. Before leaving, Jiao asked her father to stay at Qiu's house as a place of refuge. Ren agreed.

Talented and bold, using the name of Jin, he met Duke Mu. The documents were without error, and Duke Mu trusted him. Later, he saw that the talent was unfamiliar with etiquette, and soon, Duke Mu began to doubt him. When Xi Jin arrived, the gatekeeper reported, and Duke Mu was greatly alarmed, asking, "How can there be two Jin princes?" He hurriedly received him in a separate courtyard. Seeing Jin's behavior as graceful and cautious, he was indeed the descendant of a noble family. When he heard that Jin had encountered bandits on the road and had his documents taken, Duke Mu did not believe it and thus did not treat Jin with courtesy. Jin was about to leave when Zao Cai suddenly arrived, accusing Jin of impersonating someone and ordering his attendants to beat Jin. Fortunately, Jiao Niang protected him, and Jiao and Jin went to the home of Qiu. At night, six or seven bandits came, claiming to have a grudge against Jin Xi and wanting to settle it. Qiu and Ren's father and daughter drove them away.

The next day, Ren accompanied Geng and the woman back home. Jiao asked Geng, "What should be done with your matter?" Geng replied, "Abandon it." Jiao said, "No, you cannot. Although she has not yet married, she is still your wife. If you allow her to fall into an evil plot and be taken by a bandit, she will surely suffer a misfortune in her life, and you will have a lifelong regret. The resentment of having one's wife taken away is something that people cannot bear. If you alone can bear it, you are truly a heartless person." Geng said, "I deeply resent Mu Gong's father and daughter for being unable to distinguish between truth and falsehood." Jiao responded, "It is not enough to blame Mu Gong. You and the gentleman do not know each other, and the only basis for trust is my father's letter, which was taken first and shown to the gentleman, but the gentleman arrived later without evidence. If Zhao Cai is false, the gentleman will believe it is true; if the gentleman's words are true, the gentleman will believe it is false. This is bound to happen. It is also not enough to blame Mu's sister."

Marriage matters are solely determined by the parents' commands. If Mu Gong believes it to be true, how dare Mu's elder sister call it false? And if you hear that the lord is coming again, we will neither call the true false nor the false true. The situation is indeed very pitiable," said Geng. "Then what should be done to make it right?" asked Jiao. "I suggest you stay here," she replied, "and I will think of a way to see Mu's elder sister. With my unbreakable tongue, within ten days, I will ensure that you and your wife meet, and together you will go to your father's post." Geng did not agree, but Jiao did not listen. "If the Mu family comes," Geng said, "you should be the one to meet them, and I will be second. Moreover, in matters of the bedroom, half of the duties will be taken away, and you will surely regret it." Jiao responded, "This is using private desires to pry into a virtuous person." So they went together to Qiu's house. She told her father, "Ten days later, prepare a small carriage, and wait every morning at the crossroads outside Mu Gong's estate." Ren agreed.

Cao and his father went directly to the Mu residence. Ren told the gatekeeper, "My daughter wishes to hire a servant to do needlework. Do you need a servant in your house?" The gatekeeper replied, "We definitely need one. It's because we urgently need to prepare a dowry for my daughter." He led the girl to see Lady Mu. Lady Mu was pleased with Cao's intelligence and beauty, and immediately ordered him to accompany Miss Xiu Ying in her needlework. Cao was good at understanding Xiu's intentions. After a few days, Xiu regarded Cao as her trusted confidant, and they slept and ate together, calling each other sisters. Every evening, under the lamp, Xiu's eyebrows seemed to be filled with sorrow, and she sighed softly. When Cao first arrived, due to the distance in their relationship, it was not appropriate to ask, so he asked, "What troubles you, that you are so unhappy?" Xiu did not answer. Later, Cao pretended to say, "The maidservants privately discussed something. They all said that my sister did not know. I told my sister that she already knew." Xiu asked, "What were they discussing?" Cao replied, "It is about your matter. The maidservants said: the noble guest who came earlier was definitely a fake."

The younger sister asked, and they all replied: "The Jin family's descendant is a nobleman from a prestigious family, from a household that values education and scholarly pursuits. How could he be so lacking in politeness and so illiterate? Even if he handles things carefully without error, how do we know he isn't a robber impersonating someone else? The younger sister thinks that if we treat something false as true, it doesn't matter much to others, but the wedding is only a month away. My sister's lifelong matter must not be mistaken!" The speaker, upon hearing this, sighed and said, "My unease is indeed due to this. The first person is not false, even if things don't go as planned, it's fate, what can I complain about? If the second person is true, then finding a way to die early to preserve one's reputation is not difficult. But the first person has evidence, so we cannot treat him as false even though he seems false; the second person has no evidence, so we cannot treat him as true even though he seems true. It is hard to distinguish between true and false, and this is my deep worry." The younger sister said, "It is indeed difficult to distinguish between true and false. But losing oneself to a bandit is absolutely unacceptable." The speaker said, "Your words are true. However, the journey is long, and there is no one to accompany me." The younger sister said, "I am willing to go with you." The speaker said, "You are also a woman, and if there is any unforeseen event, how will you handle it?" The younger sister said, "I have a modest skill that can fend off a dozen or so people." The speaker remained silent. The younger sister said, "Sister does not believe me. Please try it tomorrow."

The next day, Ciao requested to be dressed up in a beautiful manner to enhance the spectacle, and Xiu agreed, providing his own clothes for her to wear. Once dressed, Xiu looked at her and smiled, saying, "If you were to lead an army, you could be compared to a rebirth of Pinglei." Ciao wanted to dance with a sword, but Xiu's mother happened to arrive, likely due to the chatter of maidservants. Seeing Xiu's mother, Ciao put down the sword and stood up. Xiu's mother said, "Dance, I have come especially to see your talent as a young woman." Ciao then took a double-edged sword and began to dance. Her body followed the sword, her waist swayed like willow branches, the sword spun around her body, and the light revealed the beauty of a lotus flower. Initially, the path of the sword was distinguishable, but as the dance became more intense, only the sword was visible, and the cold light seemed to envelop the person, lasting for a long time before stopping. Ciao remained composed and stood calmly. Xiu's mother asked, "Are you good at riding and shooting?" Ciao replied, "I haven't practiced, I have tried using a slingshot, but it's not very good." Xiu's mother then ordered someone to bring a slingshot for Ciao and had them set up a wooden post within fifty steps.

She drew her bow and shot, releasing several arrows that all hit their mark. Xiu’s mother was overjoyed and ordered Xiu Ying to take an oath as sisters. Jiao said, “There’s no need for such a ritual; in no time, our bond will deepen naturally.” Xiu’s mother asked why, and Jiao smiled without answering. By evening, the maids and servants had all retired to bed. Xiu Ying asked, “How can we be sure that the later person is genuine?” Jiao recounted how, in a certain year, he had saved Jin Gong in a particular place, and how he had moved there to save Jin Gong’s son. He explained that the earlier person was the one who had harmed Jin Gong’s son, while the later person was the one whom his mother and father had saved. “That’s how we know the later person is genuine,” he said. Xiu Ying replied, “But how can we be sure that he is truly Jin Gong’s son?” Jiao responded, “The young master has mentioned it himself.” Xiu Ying countered, “Self-declared statements are not reliable.” Jiao said, “There is something to rely on. When I saved Jin Gong, your father was injured, and I wrote a prescription to treat him, which healed him instantly. Yesterday, when the young master was injured, your father used the same prescription to treat him, and the young master recognized it as his father’s handwriting.”

From this we can infer that the one rescued earlier was indeed the father of the young master, and can it be that the one who came later is truly the son of the Jin family?" Upon hearing this, Xiu knew that Jiao's words were true and asked urgently, "Where is the young master of the Jin family now?" Jiao replied, "He is currently at my sister's house." Xiu remained silent, and after a while, she said, "My sister and her daughter saved the lives of the Jin family father and son twice, and now she serves as a persuader, which can be said to be acting with loyalty on behalf of others." Jiao said, "Not only this. In the past, when the young master came, my sister dressed as a man and followed him, otherwise, the young master would surely have been humiliated by Zhao." Xiu was startled and said, "I have heard that the followers of the one who came later are skilled in martial arts, did my sister dress as a man to deceive me?" Jiao replied, "Yes." Xiu said, "If so, my sister has deceived me greatly. Even if she is naturally generous, she certainly cannot allow a young woman to associate with young men. Otherwise, there must be another reason." Jiao smiled and said, "Sister, you can truly discern matters."

From this we can infer that the one rescued earlier was indeed the father of the young master, and can it be that the one who came later is truly the son of the Jin family?" Upon hearing this, Xiu knew that Jiao's words were true and asked urgently, "Where is the young master of the Jin family now?" Jiao replied, "He is currently at my sister's house." Xiu remained silent, and after a while, she said, "My sister and her daughter saved the lives of the Jin family father and son twice, and now she serves as a persuader, which can be said to be acting with loyalty on behalf of others." Jiao said, "Not only this. In the past, when the young master came, my sister dressed as a man and followed him, otherwise, the young master would surely have been humiliated by Zhao." Xiu was startled and said, "I have heard that the followers of the one who came later are skilled in martial arts, did my sister dress as a man to deceive me?" Jiao replied, "Yes." Xiu said, "If so, my sister has deceived me greatly. Even if she is naturally generous, she certainly cannot allow a young woman to associate with young men. Otherwise, there must be another reason." Jiao smiled and said, "Sister, you can truly discern matters."

From this we can infer that the one rescued earlier was indeed the father of the young master, and can it be that the one who came later is truly the son of the Jin family?" Upon hearing this, Xiu knew that Jiao's words were true and asked urgently, "Where is the young master of the Jin family now?" Jiao replied, "He is currently at my sister's house." Xiu remained silent, and after a while, she said, "My sister and her daughter saved the lives of the Jin family father and son twice, and now she serves as a persuader, which can be said to be acting with loyalty on behalf of others." Jiao said, "Not only this. In the past, when the young master came, my sister dressed as a man and followed him, otherwise, the young master would surely have been humiliated by Zhao." Xiu was startled and said, "I have heard that the followers of the one who came later are skilled in martial arts, did my sister dress as a man to deceive me?" Jiao replied, "Yes." Xiu said, "If so, my sister has deceived me greatly. Even if she is naturally generous, she certainly cannot allow a young woman to associate with young men. Otherwise, there must be another reason." Jiao smiled and said, "Sister, you can truly discern matters."

When rescuing Master Jin, the sister dressed as a boy, and Master ordered the sister to swear brotherhood with the young master. The sister's father laughed and agreed. When rescuing the young master, the sister still dressed as a boy, and the young master treated the sister as a benevolent elder brother, to which the father truthfully responded. The young master then asked his maternal uncle to speak... At this point, Xiu said, "What do you want your maternal uncle to do?" Jiao remained silent. Xiu smiled and said, "Is it to ask your maternal uncle to act as a matchmaker?" Jiao, blushing, replied, "My sister has indeed dedicated herself to him." Xiu lowered his head as if thinking deeply, and after a long time, he said, "I will follow my sister's advice. I ask my sister to carefully consider." Jiao said, "Lavishly bribe the gatekeeper, dress as a boy early in the morning and leave, carrying a lot of valuables as funds. Must wait three days before." Xiu asked why, and Jiao said, "Three days later, my sister's father will wait outside the estate." On the appointed day, Xiu and Jiao traveled together to the estate, and Ren indeed waited there. Ren pushed her in a small cart and returned. Jiao said, "There will surely be those searching for her here, so we must travel another two hundred miles before we can discuss further." Thus, they traveled for two days before stopping. Xiu was well-read, and Jiao ordered Xiu to write a letter to Duke Mu, and asked his father to deliver it.

Xiu and Jiao died together. Mu Gong sent people in four directions to track them down, but no one saw the two women traveling together. Zao Cai heard about it, knew that something had changed, and also fled. Mu Gong saw Xiu Ying's letter and heard Li Ren's words, and quickly prepared, loading the wedding gifts of Xiu Ying onto a cart and going with Li Ren. When he saw the young man and the girl, he was greatly pleased, and then went north together. Suddenly, a dozen or so people blocked the road, loudly demanding that Mu Gong return. If he did not comply, they would all be killed. Ren led Mu Gong's followers to attack them. Jiao Niang saw Zao Cai, shot him, and hit his wrist, causing him to drop his weapon and be captured. The remaining bandits fled. Mu Gong sent Zao Cai to the authorities, who beat him to death.

The Daoist Master Xubai said: "How extraordinary! Cao Nü is a woman among men! Her words already indicate her skills, and she should rescue people in peril—this is something a woman would not typically say. Her actions involve serving Jin Lang and planning for him to reconcile with his ex-wife—this is something a young wife would not typically do. Cao Nü speaks of it and acts on it, her heart and mind so broad and open, as if guided by righteous energy, truly inspiring admiration. As for Jin Gong and his son, trouble arose unexpectedly, but they received unexpected rescue—perhaps due to Jin Gong's upright governance?" --- Mr. Niu, named Gui and styled Ziliang, was from Xiaoshan, Zhejiang. At forty, he had no heir. His wife, Sang, bought a young woman as a concubine. When the woman entered the household, Niu happened to be away. Upon his return, Sang greeted him and said, "I have bought a concubine for you. Do you think she is suitable?" Niu laughed and said, "Why didn't you consult me before making this decision? Perhaps you will regret it later." Sang also laughed and said, "Do you think I am jealous? If so, please send her back immediately, and from now on, do not blame me for other matters, saying I harbor jealousy." Niu laughed and said, "Do not be angry. What sudden act of kindness prompted you to do this?" Sang replied, "Her husband was selling her urgently, and three or four families were trying to buy her. If we delay, she will be sold to someone else." Niu looked at the woman's appearance and found her acceptable, though she was bowing her head and weeping.

The man asked, and the woman replied, "My former husband Zhao Jun was weak, and the ruffian Li Qi lured him to gamble together. When my husband lost, Li Qi immediately demanded repayment. My husband asked for a deadline to bring it back, but Li Qi became angry and cursed him, beating him with several of his associates. I had just returned from my mother's house when I met them, and I spoke on behalf of my husband, pleading for more time. Li Qi then forced my husband to sell me. My price was only half of the gambling debt, and Li Qi wouldn't be satisfied. My husband will surely die at the hands of Li Qi. Moreover, when I was sold, I was carrying my one-year-old child. Li Qi snatched it and threw it to the ground. The child's head was broken, and blood flowed uncontrollably. It cried desperately for a few sounds and then stopped crying, must have already died." She wept bitterly and could not continue speaking. The man felt pity for her and immediately ordered his family to drive her home. Sang said, "If you want to send her home, it's not too late tomorrow." The man replied, "This matter cannot be delayed until night; delaying it would inevitably lead to suspicion and criticism."

The protagonist, having married his wife, still harbored deep resentment. Suddenly, the county magistrate arrived. It was said that the magistrate was the protagonist's close friend. The magistrate had gone out and returned, passing by the protagonist's village, and took the opportunity to visit. After meeting, the protagonist's anger was visible on his face. The magistrate asked him about it, and the protagonist said, "Your people are rude and rebellious in an abnormal way; hearing this, I feel deeply unjust." He then recounted the entire matter involving Zhao Jun in detail to the magistrate. The magistrate said, "They are indeed despicable!" He ordered the servants to arrest the person in question. He asked the protagonist, "How do you know the details?" The protagonist replied, "Zhao Jun's wife, Hong Shi, told me. I bought Hong Shi as a concubine, heard her situation, and pitied her, so I sent her back home. During your visit, I had just sent her back home." The magistrate said, "Having such great merit, you will surely have descendants. Considering this matter, even if you do not take another concubine, you will surely have descendants." The magistrate returned to his office, first questioning Zhao Jun, whose words were the same as the protagonist's; he then questioned Li Qi, who, hearing that the protagonist had already told the truth and with Zhao Jun as a witness, had to admit his guilt. The magistrate asked about the body of Zhao Jun's son, and Li Qi said, "I had the workers bury it. The workers abandoned it in a ravine and returned. I was angry with them, and when the workers returned to look for it, the body was no longer there." The magistrate angrily said, "What crime did this innocent child commit, and you killed him! Even if compensated, there would still be more guilt."

Zhao Gui returned home and saw his wife. His wife explained the reason for his return, and Zhao then led his wife to the "Shengmen" (a place of honor) to express his gratitude. Since then, the two families have visited each other frequently. Later, Zhao's wife gave birth to a son, and Zhao's wife also gave birth to a daughter in the same month. Zhao was deeply grateful for the kindness of the other family and arranged a prearranged marriage between their children. Zhao's son, named Sheng, grew up to be exceptionally slow-witted. At fifteen or sixteen years old, he seemed unaware of the differences between men and women. Zhao had him share a bed with a servant girl, but after several nights, there was no intimacy. Zhao and his wife were greatly worried and said, "We have no descendants, which is our fate. To arrange a marriage for our daughter to this slow-witted man would ruin her life. How could we bear it in our hearts!" His wife asked, "Can we break off the engagement?" Zhao replied, "Yes." He then asked his friends and relatives to convey this to Zhao. Zhao consulted with his wife and daughter. His wife was still hesitant, but the daughter firmly said it was not possible. A few days later, the daughter secretly went to the "Niushibu" (likely a place of honor) and declared that she wished to marry the slow-witted man. Zhao's mother said, "My son is as foolish as a dog or a horse, and I fear he will waste his youth. Do not let your momentary resolve lead to lifelong regret." The daughter replied, "Until death, I will not regret." Zhao sent someone to invite Zhao's parents to come and persuade their daughter to return. The daughter refused. Reluctantly, they proceeded with the wedding ceremony. Sheng was unable to dress himself and needed help with his socks and shoes. The daughter supported him day and night without complaint.

One day, Sang Ning's parents took Sheng along with them. The path was somewhat distant, and they arrived only after spending the entire day traveling. They happened to encounter the mother's illness and could not return. Sheng wanted to return alone, and his mother told him, "Wait two more days, and I will go with you to see your aunt." Sheng asked about the aunt's residence, and his mother said, "It is not far to the southeast." Sheng believed her. After two days, Sheng left his mother and went straight to the southeast, traveling. By evening, he did not meet a single woman. Later, he reached a village entrance and saw an old woman and a young woman standing outside the door. Sheng then thought the old woman was his aunt and approached her to greet his aunt. The old woman laughed and said, "Where is the young gentleman from, and why do you call me aunt?" Sheng smiled foolishly without speaking. The old woman turned to the young woman and said, "This must be the foolish young man from the Niu family." She asked Sheng, and he still managed to say his own name. The old woman was pleased and said, "It is indeed my nephew." She led him home and fed him. She said, "My daughter is suitable for marriage. Your coming is a matter of fate." By evening, she ordered Sheng to sleep with the daughter. In the morning, the girl had a worried look, and the old woman asked her, and she wept. The old woman said, "Does he not know the joy of married life? He can truly be called foolish."

The doctor was called in to examine him, and said, "Phlegm is blocking his heart or mental clarity, and he should be unaware of human affairs." They boiled medicine and made him drink it. He fell into a deep sleep and did not wake up until midnight. Upon waking, he saw a beautiful woman weeping by the light of a lamp and realized she was his wife. He then said, "Why are you still awake so late at night, my dear?" The woman smiled through her tears and replied, "Are you not foolish, my lord?" He said, "If I were always foolish, why would you have feelings for me?" They then undressed and went to bed together. He asked the old woman, "Why do you live alone here?" She replied, "I am actually the concubine of your uncle, Han. When your uncle passed away, your aunt-in-law could not tolerate it, so I brought my young daughter here to live with me, and we have been here for sixteen years." Suddenly, a young man came from outside, and the old woman introduced him to him. He asked about him, and the old woman said, "This is the brother of your nephew's legal wife, Zhao." He was surprised and asked why. The old woman explained, "Seventeen years ago, I happened to visit a certain village for other reasons and saw a baby crying in a deep ravine outside the village. I took him back. Later, I heard that he was the son of Zhao Jun and had been thrown away by a notorious ruffian named Li Qi. We could not find his body, and he was sentenced to death, but later received amnesty, which was changed to hanging and then exile. I did not want to send him back, so I raised him as my own son and arranged a marriage for him. I heard that Li Qi died in prison yesterday, and now he will be getting married. After the wedding, you four can return together."

### Final Translation --- **Translation:** --- **Original Text:** 晟母不见晟，意其自归。回家视之，亦乌有；四方踪迹之，月馀无耗。已绝望，忽见晟同一少年二少妇归，问之：一为晟妻，二为媳兄赵某夫妇。晟举止言语无痴意，牛与妻喜不自胜，急驾车转送俊子夫妇归。而问媳姓氏，晟言系姨丈韩翁之女。晟母愕然曰：“吾表姊无女。”女曰：“媳庶出，乳名仙仙。”晟母曰：“亦未之闻。”牛晟之归也，妻赵氏适归省，闻兄言急归。见夫不痴，情犹初婚，晟投以谑语，羞惭无以自容。晟笑曰：“卿虽实为处女，然同寝有日，勿故为笄女态度以欺吾。”及寝，赵氏曰：“妾意终身守有夫之寡，不图尚有今日。”遂问病愈之详。晟曰：“韩氏以仆痴而泣，渠母见之，故为诊治。夫韩氏泣而卿不泣，卿可谓刚毅人。”赵曰：“妾何尝未泣，但于无人时而泣，泣故无人见耳。”韩与赵同室寝，闻之，谓晟曰：“乡也君谓妾泣为淫，今始知嫁痴郎而泣者，不仅妾一人。”各暗笑。 --- **Translation:** --- **Final Translation:** --- **Original Text:** 晟母不见晟，意其自归。回家视之，亦乌有；四方踪迹之，月馀无耗。已绝望，忽见晟同一少年二少妇归，问之：一为晟妻，二为媳兄赵某夫妇。晟举止言语无痴意，牛与妻喜不自胜，急驾车转送俊子夫妇归。而问媳姓氏，晟言系姨丈韩翁之女。晟母愕然曰：“吾表姊无女。”女曰：“媳庶出，乳名仙仙。”晟母曰：“亦未之闻。”牛晟之归也，妻赵氏适归省，闻兄言急归。见夫不痴，情犹初婚，晟投以谑语，羞惭无以自容。晟笑曰：“卿虽实为处女，然同寝有日，勿故为笄女态度以欺吾。”及寝，赵氏曰：“妾意终身守有夫之寡，不图尚有今日。”遂问病愈之详。晟曰：“韩氏以仆痴而泣，渠母见之，故为诊治。夫韩氏泣而卿不泣，卿可谓刚毅人。”赵曰：“妾何尝未泣，但于无人时而泣，泣故无人见耳。”韩与赵同室寝，闻之，谓晟曰：“乡也君谓妾泣为淫，今始知嫁痴郎而泣者，不仅妾一人。”各暗笑。 --- **Translation:** --- **Final Translation:** --- **Original Text:** 晟母不见晟，意其自归。回家视之，亦乌有；四方踪迹之，月馀无耗。已绝望，忽见晟同一少年二少妇归，问之：一为晟妻，二为媳兄赵某夫妇。晟举止言语无痴意，牛与妻喜不自胜，急驾车转送俊子夫妇归。而问媳姓氏，晟言系姨丈韩翁之女。晟母愕然曰：“吾表姊无女。”女曰：“媳庶出，乳名仙仙。”晟母曰：“亦未之闻。”牛晟之归也，妻赵氏适归省，闻兄言急归。见夫不痴，情犹初婚，晟投以谑语，羞惭无以自容。晟笑曰：“卿虽实为处女，然同寝有日，勿故为笄女态度以欺吾。”及寝，赵氏曰：“妾意终身守有夫之寡，不图尚有今日。”遂问病愈之详。晟曰：“韩氏以仆痴而泣，渠母见之，故为诊治。夫韩氏泣而卿不泣，卿可谓刚毅人。”赵曰：“妾何尝未泣，但于无人时而泣，泣故无人见耳。”韩与赵同室寝，闻之，谓晟曰：“乡也君谓妾泣为淫，今始知嫁痴郎而泣者，不仅妾

Mr. Niu's mother doubted whether the words of his wife, Han, were true. She claimed to visit her family and went to her sister-in-law's house, telling the old woman Han about the matter of her son and daughter-in-law. The old woman was astonished and asked, "Is her milk name Xianxian?" Mr. Niu's mother replied, "Yes." The old woman said, "She is my daughter. My late husband had a fox concubine who gave birth to a daughter named Xianxian. After my husband died, the fox took the girl away." They hurriedly returned with Mr. Niu's mother to see her. After several days, Mr. Niu's wife, Han, missed her fox mother, and Mr. Zhao's wife also wanted to visit her, so they went together. Upon arriving, they found the house empty and desolate. They asked the local people, who said the house had been rented out for several days. Mr. Xubai said: Zhao Jun's injustice might be redressed by human effort; but Mr. Niu's madness is truly beyond human power to cure. The fox cured him, no less than a divine physician. To be cured by a divine physician, one knows that the matter is purely a matter of retribution, purely a matter of heavenly principle, purely a matter of hidden merit. If Mr. Niu had not become mad, the implications would not be shallow or insignificant.

Scrupulously decisive in handling affairs, the orders he gives are also pleasing to people. Annotated by Li Yu from Shangyuan. The Ninth Volume of the Record of Wisdom Su Cheng Su Cheng of Zouping County was fond of doing convenient things. His residence was about ten miles west, and beside the road stood a solitary willow tree, which was quite large, and travelers often rested beneath it. During the hot summer, Su returned to the county and hurried to the tree to escape the intense heat. He removed his hat and loosened his robe, standing in the breeze. Suddenly, a blind man came from the east, moving at a speed that even a sighted person might not surpass. When the blind man arrived, he said, "I am fortunate to have come here; I can rest for a while." He added, "My luck is not good; I haven't earned a single coin in half a day. Now that I have the shade of the willow tree, let me try to divine its fate." He then said, "Disheartened! This willow tree is about to die; it's no different from telling the fortune of the dead." With a sigh, he left. Su thought the blind man's words were baseless. Shortly after, several people arrived, each holding tools for cutting wood, and they all stopped under the tree. Su asked them, and one replied, "We are going to cut down this tree." Su was greatly startled and said, "This tree grows beside the road. If it is cut down, travelers passing this way will have no place to rest." They replied, "The owner of the tree is selling it; what can we do?" Su asked the buyer about the price. One person pointed to another and said, "He is the seller." Su then spoke to them, saying, "I would like to add several thousand to the original price and buy it from you, so that it is not cut down, and travelers can still rest under it. Would you be willing to sell it to me?" They answered, "We are willing." Su said, "If so, I will pay the price for the tree at a later date."

Scu thought: "How could a blind man predict this? Isn't he extraordinary? Extraordinary! I must find him quickly." He said, "Master, why was your prediction about the tree incorrect?" The blind man replied, "Indeed. I calculated that someone would buy and plant it. But there are rarely such good people in the world, so I could not make a definitive decision." Upon hearing this, Scu was even more startled and said, "The person who bought and planted the tree is me." The blind man smiled and said, "This is a convenient matter. You will surely turn misfortune into good fortune." Scu said, "I am just seeking your advice. May I trouble you to carefully analyze it?" The blind man pointed to his palm and said, "Let's not talk about other matters. Today, you will face a peculiar disaster. Because of your good deed of buying and planting the tree, you may have a savior, but you must be able to endure what others cannot endure to avoid it." Scu was greatly alarmed and hurried home.

He saw his wife lying with a young man during the day, and he flew into a rage, wanting to find a knife to kill them. Suddenly remembering the blind man's words, he shook his wife awake and scolded her, saying, "Get up! Who is lying with you?" The wife replied, "Why are you angry? I don't know who it is either. If you think that whoever can sleep in this bed is the one, then that is the one." Su said, "The only ones who can sleep in this bed are me, you, and our daughter." The wife said, "If that's the case, you asked this question unnecessarily." Su realized it was his daughter. He laughed and said, "Why is my daughter dressed as a boy?" The wife explained, "Today is my birthday. Since we don't have a son, I jokingly had our daughter dress as a boy to pay respects and wish for blessings, to entertain ourselves." Su said, "Your life and your daughter's life were nearly lost by my hand, fortunately due to my momentary restraint, and truly because of the blind man's guidance." He then recounted the entire incident to his wife. Su eventually had a son and saw his grandson before he died.

The Daoist monk虚白道人 said: "Patience should not be neglected, as in this case." If苏成 encountered what he did, it was something that no ordinary person could endure. He alone was able to endure it because he had the guidance of a divine being; and the reason why the divine being taught him was also because of his single-minded good deed. Otherwise, disasters arise from hasty and careless actions, how many are there? 颜 星 丽 颜映远, courtesy name奎辟, with the pseudonym星丽, was from Xi'an, Shaanxi Province. At twenty, he studied at a local school and received stipends, but stumbled in the difficult examination hall and was trapped in the annual examinations. By the age of forty, he had not been recommended for the provincial examination, and at that time, he was often unhappy. Suddenly, due to his good conduct, he was recommended for the imperial examination and graduated from school. He rejoiced, saying, "Now my long-held wish has been fulfilled." From then on, he planted flowers, played with stones, drank wine, and composed poetry. He fished in the stream and forgot to return, played chess in the woods and forgot hunger. For all famous mountains, rivers, scenic spots, and well-known places, he desired to visit them. For nearby places, he would travel with friends; for distant places, he would ride a donkey alone. One day, he entered a remote place, feeling very hungry. Looking around, he saw vast emptiness, with no villages or towns in sight. He looked up at the hilltop and saw a few wisps of smoke rising from the treetops, as if there were people living there. When he arrived, he found a man living in a cave in the wild. He begged for food and asked for directions. The man gave him vegetables to eat and said his surname was Shan.

A woman peeks from outside, clear and graceful, gentle and lovely. Her eyes gaze intently. The man says, "This is my daughter. If you find her pleasing, I will arrange for her to take care of your household." The woman replies, "I wish to hear more details." He says, "Is it acceptable to have a wild union?" Yan thought it was not the refined and elevated conduct of a gentleman, and upon seeing this behavior, he turned suspicion into fear. He wanted to leave, but the woman suddenly came out from inside, pulled his clothes, and laughed. She said, "In ancient times, when men and women were married, if their ages were not similar, it was called 'wild union.' Sir, you are now fifty, why don't you understand? And in the past, when Confucius went on a trip, he met Zengzi and composed the poem 'Wild Grass in the Field.' If you heard it, you would also consider it as a vulgar expression of promiscuity. Since my father has given the order, I will be your wife, and you will be my husband. Why must we be intimate and perform daily chores to be considered separate?" After saying this, they both disappeared. Yan returned home, feeling somewhat disappointed and no longer went out.

**Translation:** The Courtesan of Taiyuan (Draft Version: "Feng Yun") In Taiyuan, there was a famous courtesan named Cai Lianfang, skilled in poetry and painting. Her seven-character quatrain reads: "Calling companions to the curtained chamber, peeping to see who the new guest is. Fearing being seen first, she looks through the gap in her silk fan. A few degrees of spring color on the flower branches, her cloud-like hair lazily combed after a late sleep. The parrot in front of the eaves vainly imitates speech, the matters of love in dreams are known only to oneself." It was mid-winter, with snow and rain swirling, and after the snow melted, it gathered to a depth of a foot. She then sculpted a snow beauty as a play. A scholar passed by and took up his brush to inscribe: "Who with a light and graceful hand, adorned an extremely charming courtesan. Sitting steadily on the railing without shame, her mouth always open in laughter. Meeting a kindly and affectionate friend, sweat runs all over her body. Alas, when she dissolves, no one will gather her, following the river water." The courtesan thus conceived the wish to become a proper wife. After leaving her profession, she painted willows on a fan to express her feelings for her former friend and inscribed: "Once I danced with a slender waist at Chang'an, allowing you to pluck the tender branches. From now on, I will write my story in the paintings, not allowing the east wind to disturb it again." Soon after, the man she had been with died suddenly, and the courtesan vowed never to enter a brothel again, living by selling her poetry and paintings.

One day, a man who bought paintings came, and upon hearing his accent, it was clear he was from Baode. When asked, he said his name was Feng Yun. It seemed familiar, so the narrator smiled and said to him, "I am also from Baode. Can you stay here tonight?" The man had always known that Pian was a courtesan and replied, "Yes." He thought it was just a case of itching to perform and did not realize there was another reason. Previously, the woman originally surnamed Huai, whose father was registered in Baode and was skilled in painting. When she was young, she was betrothed to her cousin Feng Yun. At the age of fifteen, she followed her father to visit relatives, but before reaching their destination, her father died in Taiyuan. The girl sold herself into servitude to bury her father. Later, the master gave the girl to someone else, and she changed hands three times, becoming a courtesan. Fortunately, she was able to redeem herself and gain freedom. Her name, Cai Lianfang, was taken from the name of her madam. Seeing Feng Yun, she knew he was her betrothed, but the girl knew it but dared not say, while Feng did not expect this. From then on, the girl and Feng became even more affectionate, and Feng also met with the girl frequently. Feng was a regular attendant, but the abbot disliked his wandering and expelled him. Suddenly, Feng contracted a seasonal epidemic, which was very serious. The girl knew about it and went to persuade Feng to come for treatment. She did her best to care for him, serving him with medicine and soup, and did not change clothes for several days. Feng's illness worsened, and he cursed day and night, slapping her face if she displeased him. The girl endured everything without complaint. When Feng slightly recovered, he repented and prostrated himself on the bed to apologize. The girl said, "You are sick, how could I dare to take it to heart?" Feng was even more moved by the girl's virtue and entrusted all his clothes to her for safekeeping. More than a year later, they had a son, and Feng was overjoyed.

One night, Feng held his child in his arms and laughed with the woman. The woman said, "I heard that you were engaged to Huai when you were young, is that true?" He replied, "Yes, it is." She continued, "Huai's father went to visit relatives, but he died on the road. Later, I heard that she ended up in the entertainment quarters. If she wants to return to you now, would you accept her?" He said, "I would accept her. Many prostitutes seek a better life, and many men accept them. If she wants to return to me, it shows that she has not forgotten the promise made through the matchmaker. Compared to accepting a prostitute without any reason, it is better." The woman laughed and said, "If that is the case, I and you have no proof of our engagement. If you accept her, you will surely value her more than me." Feng replied, "What are you saying? I and you are a different matter. I have received great kindness from you, as if you were my second mother. With you here, I should reject Huai and not accept her, let alone look down on you." The woman still did not believe him, so Feng swore by the bright sun. The woman was delighted and said, "I am Huai." She then recounted the events after she was engaged. He said, "Why didn't you tell me earlier?" She replied, "I lost my virtue in the entertainment quarters, and everyone despises me. I dare not hope that you will accept me. However, if you have no intention of accepting me, I will commit suicide to repay you. I will never repeat my mistakes to further disgrace myself." Feng laughed and said, "I have always suspected that you have seen many people, why are you so attached to me?" Huai had a gentle and compliant nature, treated others with humility and respect, and no one knew that she had been a prostitute. Even if someone did know, they would carefully keep it secret.

The Daoist Master Xu Bai said: Huai once was a prostitute. If she happened to see her husband's letter, she would ignore it indifferently. People would also find it hard to judge her as unrighteous or unkind. Her actions were already in the category of unrighteousness and unkindness. Thus, it is known that being a husband without speaking up, secretly following the wife's wishes, and openly serving her, her service to her husband is twice as devoted compared to ordinary feelings, and her following her husband is as chaste and pure as a virtuous woman. It is difficult to find a chaste widow in the secular world, and it is also not easy to find a righteous woman in the brothel. How can one not consider her heart unkind and her actions unrighteous just because she lost her virtue in the brothel?

In the Year of the Water Ox, during the reign of Emperor Xianfeng, on the fourteenth day of the eighth month of the autumn, southern bandits suddenly arrived from the southwest. Women and children fleeing in panic, carrying the elderly and young, filled the roads with their cries. North of Huangtai, fortunately the river overflowed, and the bandits dared not wreak havoc extensively, though the suffering was already unbearable. The bandits stayed in Lüo Town; on the sixteenth day, they fled eastward. At the time, I was taking refuge in Huazhu Mountain. At dusk, I saw smoke signals rising in all directions, especially to the northeast. There was a man named He Mengling, a scholar from another county, who studied at a temple in the mountains, and he was also escaping from the difficulties faced by women. One day, he climbed the mountain to enjoy the view and saw a woman carrying a basket and walking alone, looking around. She seemed to be picking something. He glanced at her and found her extremely beautiful, and she resembled the daughter of a wealthy man in a neighboring village. He thought to himself that she would never do this. He approached her and saw that the basket was empty. He couldn't help himself and said, "Are you picking vegetables?" The woman replied, "I'm picking herbs." He said, "There are plenty of herbs on this mountain, why did you not find any?" The woman replied, "What I'm picking is really rare." He asked, "What herb?" The woman replied, "Dragon Ginseng Grass." He thought she was joking and ignored her. The woman said, "Getting a fierce wife is also a rare thing in life." He asked, "Who are you talking about?" The woman replied, "I'm talking about you." Unreasonably associating with a young woman, it's reasonable to get a fierce wife to punish your frivolous behavior.

The host teased her, saying, "To get a fierce wife is certainly unfortunate, but if you get a beautiful wife like you, how fortunate is that?" The woman replied, "I am not beautiful, whether I get one or not makes no difference." The host ultimately thought it was improper to seduce a girl in love without reason, intending to return quickly. The woman stopped him, asking, "Do you not wish to marry me?" The host said, "Even if I want to, what can I do about having a domineering wife at home?" The woman suggested, "Living in a temple is possible." The host asked, "There are monks in the temple." The woman replied, "I can ensure that the monks do not see me." The host realized that she was not an ordinary woman and asked her about it. The woman said, "Why ask? My appearance is like that of a woman from the Chē family; you can consider me as a woman from the Chē family." So, the host took her to a mountain villa. After living together for a long time, there was no other unusual behavior, except that every night she ordered the host to burn one incense stick. After burning the incense, the woman placed an umbrella on top of it. The host asked what it was for, and the woman said, "It is a preparation for the future, which you do not know." In mid-February, the weather suddenly turned cold. The host had a fox fur coat and put it on. The woman said, "Do not wear this. Fold it up and keep it hidden, do not let me see it, for I am not only afraid but also find it unbearable to look at." The host did not want to go against her wishes and reluctantly followed her advice. Once, when the host went home to visit his family, his wife was displeased that he had not returned for many days and refused to let him in, so he stayed in another room. Upon returning, he told the woman about it, and she said, "After August, her domineering nature will naturally change." The host did not understand what she meant.

The man had a natural sense of filial piety. When the bandits arrived, he was eager to return home. The woman, with a sigh, said, "Having parents at home, one cannot but leave; you should indeed return." She then held up the umbrella she was carrying to show him and said, "If you encounter bandits, choose a quiet spot by the roadside and hold up this umbrella. Regardless of the number, make them sit around the umbrella; the bandits will not be able to see. There are several paper scrolls tied inside the umbrella. If you see someone being robbed and want to save them, burning one scroll will save one person." She then closed the umbrella and gave it to the man. Upon returning, the man found his parents and villagers fleeing in panic. He quickly told his parents what the woman had said and instructed the villagers, "Follow me, and you can avoid the bandits." The man followed the woman's instructions and arranged things, warning them not to make noise. Soon, the bandits arrived and passed by as if they could not see anything. The children who were captured were crying pitifully. The man then burned one scroll, and one person came to him. He then burned them one after another, saving about a hundred people. Later, he saw a bandit carrying a woman on his horse. Upon seeing, it was Lady Che. He quickly burned the scroll, and the bandit released the woman and rode away alone. He then saw two people chasing a young woman, beating her. It was his fierce wife. Thinking of their marital affection, he also burned the scroll to save her. The woman fell to the ground in front of him, motionless, with her body bruised and disfigured. After some time, the bandits passed, and others returned on their own. Only Lady Che stayed with the man's mother. The man sent the woman back, and her family had all fled and not returned. The woman still followed the man.

Upon arriving home, it was already late in the day, and the parents had gone to sleep early. The wife, seriously injured, did not want to get up, leaving only the husband and daughter sitting silently by the lamp. The husband wanted to let the daughter sleep with his wife, so he could sleep alone, and he told his wife. The daughter heard and said, "Do you hate me? The gratitude for saving my life, I want to repay with my body, but I fear that after returning home, my parents will not follow my wishes." The husband did not have time to answer, and the wife struggled to get up, saying, "If the daughter has such intentions, the husband should follow. I will give up the bed." She took her belongings and left. The husband was pleased and said, "My wife's fierceness has indeed changed?" He then slept with the daughter. The daughter asked about the reason for the precious umbrella, and the husband then explained in detail, saying he did not know what kind of immortal it was. The daughter said, "If she doesn't want you to wear a fox fur coat, she must be a fox." The husband suddenly understood and said, "Your guess is correct." The carriage also greatly appreciated the fox girl. The next day, the husband took the umbrella to the mountain temple, where he saw the daughter waiting outside the temple. He greeted and said, "Your whole family is safe, and you have a wife as you wished, congratulations." The husband bowed and thanked, saying, "It is all due to your efforts." The daughter did not hide herself, and the monk saw and asked. The husband lied and said his wife was a relative who was not injured by the bandits and came specially to thank the divine protection. After that, they all entered the husband's room. The husband asked, "Why do you look like the carriage owner?" The daughter said, "It is not my true appearance. I changed my appearance to know that you would definitely marry me. After I left, seeing him is like seeing me, sparing you countless thoughts of me." The monk saw the daughter leave and held the umbrella and went forward. The husband followed but could not catch up, passed through the rugged path of the Huashan tree, and suddenly disappeared. The husband also returned home from here.

The wife, seriously injured, did not want to get up, leaving only the husband and daughter sitting silently by the lamp. The husband wanted to let the daughter sleep with his wife, so he could sleep alone, and he told his wife. The daughter heard and said, "Do you hate me? The husband also returned home from here.

The Daoist Master Xubai said: In the vast and tumultuous calamity, only those who are filial, fraternal, loyal, and righteous can not only avoid misfortune but also turn it into good fortune. The case of Master He, who received the help of a fox, transformed his fierce wife into a virtuous partner, was surely not a matter of chance. Volume Ten of the Enlightening Records Geng Rumei Geng Rumei, a military scholar, lived in the area south of Mount Song. His younger brother, Ruguai, was born to their stepmother. When Ruguai was one year old, their stepmother passed away, and a hired woman took over nursing him. At night, fearing that the wet nurse might not care for the infant properly, the stepmother carried him to sleep with her. Since her wife Ding also had a son, they could not raise both children together. As Ruguai grew older, they hired a teacher to educate him, and he followed the lessons. However, by the time he was fifteen or sixteen, he suddenly stopped studying and began to play with ignorant children. Rumei tried to encourage and guide him, but Ruguai seemed to pay no heed. Later, he gradually started gambling with disreputable people and would often be away for several days without returning home or eating at home. Only when Rumei called for him would he come back. When Rumei wanted to arrange a marriage for Ruguai, Ruguai scolded him harshly, defying his elder brother's wishes.

One day, Mei suffered from a headache, and medicines proved ineffective. Mei was so worried about it that he neglected his meals and sleep. He heard that someone was good at treating this illness, and though they were slightly far apart, he got up early and hurried to reach them, visiting everywhere but finding no one. It was already evening when he saw three people lying on the ground drinking on a hill. He was about to ask them, and the three of them all stood up. One person said, "Good elder brother has come." Mei did not understand what was meant, and the person said, "To transform the stubborn younger brother with virtue, and to travel far to seek a doctor, one who is not deeply committed to friendship cannot do it. However, I recently heard that your younger brother's hands and feet have already grown cold, and it is certainly beyond the ability of ordinary hands to treat." Mei heard this and wept, sorrowfully requesting the method. One person turned to the other two and said, "Brother Hu, this condition cannot be cured without the help of Brother Zhang's sister." Zhang was displeased and said, "What are you saying, Brother Yuan?" Mei then knelt before Zhang and did not stand up. Yuan said, "The two of you often said that you were once verbally favored by Brother Geng. A favor given in words is still a favor. Brother Geng is in urgent trouble, and it seems inappropriate to stand idly by."

"Tru" said to Zhang, "Your younger sister has completed her Daoist practices. Why not trouble her to treat the patient?" Zhang replied, "My younger sister lives alone and practices Daoism, always unseen by others. How could she meet a young scholar-doctor?" Yuan said, "If your elder brother commands it, she might not dare to disobey." Tru urged strongly, and Zhang became angry, saying, "You also have a sister. If she can accompany my sister, then she will follow your command." Tru said, "I can." Geng quickly rose and thanked him. Together they told Mei, "The two sisters will go on their own tomorrow." Mei returned home and told her wife, Ding, who thought it was absurd. The next day, just before noon, indeed two women descended into the courtyard, both exceptionally beautiful. Ding invited the two women into the room and heard Tru's sister say to Zhang's sister, "Sister, your Daoist appearance is like this. Why are you suddenly preparing a wedding for someone else?" Zhang's sister replied, "My elder brother invited me while kneeling. I cannot help but come. What are you doing, sister?" Tru's sister said, "My elder brother said that if I do not come, you will not come either. I think my coming is actually pointless. My elder brother bowed twice to me, and I could not bear to deliberately disobey." Ding admired them continuously and asked Zhang to treat the patient. Zhang said, "Cover the patient's head with red silk." Ding conveyed Zhang's words to Gui, who doubted it. Zhang asked Tru to accompany the doctor. Gui felt the doctor pressing the middle finger on his forehead, a strong breath blowing on his forehead, a strange fragrance penetrating his brain, and the pain stopped immediately.

A woman who knew medicine, suddenly tore off the red silk, revealing herself to be a heavenly being, no more than an arm's length away. Both beauties were extremely embarrassed, and in an instant, she was gone. Soon after, Guì's food and drink consumption gradually decreased, and after a month, he became as black and thin as a ghost. Méi asked him, saying, "I don't feel any pain or itching, what illness do I have?" But his heart felt as if something was happening, and he was anxious every day. Dīng said, "It was the illness of yesterday, fortunately, the female doctor named Zhāng cured it immediately." Before he finished speaking, Guì said, "The illness of the past indeed relied on her to recover; today's illness was actually obtained because of her." Dīng asked in surprise, "Uncle's illness, did you get it because of her? If so, why are you not intelligent, she is probably a fairy." Guì did not speak, and Méi thought deeply worried. The next day, Yuān arrived, Méi thanked him and told him about his worry, also asking about the story of the verbal promise from the previous day, why he did not remember it at all? Yuān said, "You and a certain classmate went hunting in the mountains, that person was good at shooting, you once playfully shouted: 'Everything in the mountains, each must avoid it!' That person indeed did not catch anything all day, did you remember it?" Méi finally realized, and finally knew that Hú was a fox and Zhāng was a deer.

The speaker said, "In that case, would our friendship not be like that of a monkey?" Yuan smiled and said, "You truly have wisdom beyond others. However, your younger brother, though he gained a disease, even if I exert all my efforts to help him recover and wish to alleviate your worries, Zhang's sister, who was humiliated by being stared at, will certainly not agree to the match. What about Tru's sister?" Mei was afraid that Tru's family might not agree, and asked Yuan to speak on his behalf. Yuan said, "I will certainly not fail in my trust." He left and returned shortly, saying, "Tru will send his sister tomorrow." Mei asked, "How quickly is that?" Yuan said, "Your younger brother's illness should not be delayed." Gui was satisfied with Tru's sister, and soon recovered. Mei chose a day to perform the wedding ceremony for his younger brother. On the appointed day, Yuan came again and said, "Unpredictable events in life are indeed true. Zhang's brother said that his sister was humiliated by being stared at when she treated your younger brother, and upon returning, she blamed Zhang, weeping day and night, believing that the shame of the Yangtze and Han rivers could not wash it away. Zhang comforted her, saying that if she could marry her, this humiliation would not be worth mentioning. His sister then stopped crying. Now I am here to propose the match." Mei was concerned that his younger brother was too young to have two wives. Yuan said, "Indeed, but the virtue of treating your younger brother cannot be overlooked." Mei hesitated, so he discussed it with his wife. His wife went to negotiate with Tru's sister, who said, "With my sister in the family, there is no problem." Mei agreed, and the two sisters were married on the same day.

桂因此改变行为，降低身份去读书，但每次考试都不中。梅对他的期望非常迫切。胡、章对桂说：“你不成名，辜负了伯伯的苦心，我们二人也难辞其咎。现在与你约定：如果你获得一衿，可以侍奉我们两个月；如果中式，愿意团聚两年；只有殿试后，白头偕老，没有其他话说。”桂认为是儿戏，院试仍然不录取。等到回家，两位妻子都不见了，非常惭愧。第二年进入郡学，窥视他的房间，胡氏在那里，问：“章为什么不来？”答：“抽签下个月。”随后胡离开，章来，过了六十天，桂对章说：“明天你果然要走吗？”答：“是的。不敢违背胡姊的命令。”桂说：“你们怎么忍心？”女子说：“不是我们忍心，实在是怕辜负伯伯的期望。你务必苦读，明年登贤书，分离的日子不多。否则，三年分离，情何以堪？”次年，桂落榜，读书更加努力。梅想给他另娶，桂不同意。下科考中经魁，两位妻子一起回来。桂开玩笑说：“你们如果在家，生的孩子将多大了。”两位女子笑着说：“幸好没有耽误，我们早生孩子了。”各自叫孩子来，桂更加高兴。捷报联名南宫。两位女子不见老，后来给儿子娶媳妇，不知道的人常把她们当作妯娌。

The Virtuous White Hermit said: Benevolence on the lips is not real benevolence; even other species still talk about it without stopping. Those who now receive real benefits from people, when they are powerful, take advantage of the surplus; when they decline, they wave their arms and leave, even going so far as to wield weapons and throw stones. Their shame is indeed great compared to foxes and hares.

### Translation **Lin Fang** Lin Fang was a distinguished scholar, skilled in painting plum blossoms. Before the Winter Solstice, a beautiful woman suddenly appeared, passing through the wall. When asked about her family name, she replied that it was Hua. They enjoyed each other's company and desired union. In the morning, Hua asked Lin to paint a plum tree with 81 buds, which he then stuck on the wall. Each day, Hua colored one bud with a red brush, and after coloring all 81, the plum tree transformed into a red apricot tree. That year, it was a leap year, and Hua said, "I have heard of a riddle that has never been solved, but I have found the answer now." Lin asked what the riddle was. She replied, "Lily-of-the-valley has a hundred heads, a thousand heads, ten thousand heads, which can be matched with a lotus root having one foot, half a foot, two feet." Lin praised her for her cleverness. After Hua left, Lin achieved success in his exams, and people believed this was a sign of good fortune. **Li Si Xun** Li Xiangrong from Putai became a supervisor through a donation. There were three people in his class, and he was the last. When a position in Pingyuan became vacant, the other two were from Jinan, and thus the selection was given to Li. This was indeed a smooth path in his official career, but there was another reason behind it. Earlier, Li's father was also a scholar, and he had two brothers who were very close. His younger brother committed murder, and Li's father, seeing that his younger brother had no descendants, volunteered to take the blame. The younger brother protested, but Li's father insisted. He spent many years in prison and was eventually released due to a general amnesty. Li's success in his studies, his fame, and his appointment as a supervisor were all thanks to his uncle, and he also used this opportunity to repay his brother's kindness. Mr. Song Yutian from Qidong, who was acquainted with Li, once mentioned this, and it became clear that heaven seemed to have a special intention in this matter.

The Master also recounted: There was a scholar-official named Li Gong. The reason for his high status and wealth was due to his generous treatment of his private tutor, while other factors could be set aside without discussion. He had five sons who studied under Zhang Xianli, a scholar-official from the northern region. One day, Zhang's son Jun came to visit. Zhang Xianli became very angry and said, "Over the years, the fees you have sent as gifts to the teacher should have covered household expenses and left some surplus. Your mother wrote that your home is suffering greatly. If you are not indulging in frivolous behavior, how could things be so bad?" The next morning, he urged Jun to return home, saying, "Seeing your condition, you must be ill. Go back quickly and do not die here." Li Gong did not try to detain him, but pretended to send him away while actually bringing Jun to live in a villa. Upon examination, Jun had a venereal disease that was about to heal. Li Gong immediately sought a doctor to treat him, and after a month, Jun recovered. Li Gong then explained the situation to Zhang Xianli, asking him to allow Jun to continue studying at the school. Zhang Xianli refused, but Li Gong persuaded him, and Zhang Xianli, moved by Li Gong's intentions, finally agreed. Li Gong then arranged for Jun to eat with the other students. During the examination period, Zhang Xianli set the topic "Be cautious of lust" for the essay. Jun's essay included the lines: "Man is originally foolish; if he becomes overly attached to lust, no one remains wise. Man is originally wise; if he becomes overly entangled in lust, no one remains foolish." Zhang Xianli saw this and became furious again, saying, "A person of low morals writes such words." He was about to punish Jun. Li Gong heard about this and told Zhang Xianli, "The writing is lively and spontaneous, with thoughts flowing naturally to the pen. It shows the talent of a literary scholar. With such keen insight, his fortune and destiny are truly beyond measure." Zhang Xianli then calmed down. Later, all five of Li Gong's sons achieved prominence. Jun also passed the imperial examinations and was appointed as a county magistrate.

Opium Smoke Opium smoke, for those who indulge in it, brings endless harm, truly a unique catastrophe without precedent in ancient or modern times. Once there was a couple who slept by the lamp, smoking until midnight. Suddenly, their conscience awoke, and they sighed, saying, "We two are skin and bones, our property and family fortune ruined, our ancestral lineage destroyed—all due to opium. To quit smoking, one becomes addicted; to abstain from smoking, one dies. What can we do about it?" Suddenly, they heard a wheezing sound. Looking up, they saw a person leaning against the bed, with a strange appearance, a sallow and emaciated face, shoulders rising above the head, and a head hanging down to the chest.
