Startled, they asked him. He said, "I am an opium addict. I have deep experience in this path and have words to offer as advice: if not addicted, there is no need to smoke; if addicted, there is no need to abstain. If one says to moderate, only the sage and the wise can do it, and the sage and the wise would not be addicted to this." He blew out the lamp and left.

Record of Wisdom, Volume XI Jiang Lian During the late Ming dynasty, Jiang Lian, the son of Jiang Xiaolian of Fujian, was initially engaged to the daughter of his maternal uncle, Fish Yuan. As the family gradually declined, Fish Yuan regretted the engagement. Jiang Lian, in order to support his mother, began begging in the market. Fish Yuan considered this a disgrace and consulted his wife. The wife suggested, "Why not buy a coffin, keep the daughter unaware, seal it tightly, and falsely claim that she has died. Once the younger sister cries, her heart will be utterly despaired. You can keep it secret and then choose another husband for the daughter." Fish Yuan followed his wife's advice, placed a coffin in the courtyard, had an old woman accompany the daughter, and did not let her go downstairs. One day, the daughter looked out the window and saw her aunt arrive. Soon after, she heard crying in the courtyard and saw her aunt cry and leave. She asked the old woman, who replied that she did not know. Later, the daughter gradually learned of her father's plot and hated it. She later changed her name to Han Zhong. On the eve of her wedding, at dusk, she saw a beggar pass by the楼下 (which could mean "under the eaves" or "in the courtyard"). Upon hearing his voice, she recognized him as her father, Jiang Lian. The daughter quickly went downstairs and secretly met him through the back door.

Liang asked, “Are you not dead?” The woman replied, “Death and life are trivial matters. You should be hungry, so please eat first.” She prepared food to satisfy Liang. There were two new cabinets in the room, which were part of the dowry. The woman gathered her clothes and placed them together, saying, “When the old woman leaves, please return, and ask you to temporarily hide in here.” Liang entered the cabinet, and the old woman and the mother came in, beginning to discuss the matter of marrying off the daughter. The woman, knowing there was someone in the cabinet, pretended to be happy and said, “Tonight, I will allow you to touch my private parts lightly, and there is no need for the old woman to accompany us.” The mother also seemed pleased and left with the old woman. They opened the cabinet and brought Liang out, and then they slept together. At dawn, the woman again hid Liang in the cabinet. In the afternoon, a neighbor’s daughter, using an excuse, strongly invited the woman. The woman hurriedly sent the dowry to Han through someone. When the woman returned, the new cabinet was gone. Though she secretly regretted it, she could do nothing about it, and decided it would be better to go to her husband’s home early. She went to see her mother-in-law and said, “I am indeed not dead. However, my husband is in the cabinet at Han’s house.” She explained the situation in detail, and the mother-in-law asked, “What should we do?” The woman replied, “Just listen to it.”

At Han Zhong's home, there was only his sister. Han Zhong had other matters and could not return home at night. After taking the fish chest, he instructed his sister to guard the room and handed over the key before leaving. By evening, all the maids, servants, and guests had dispersed. The woman opened the door and wanted to open the cabinet to look at the new shoes. Holding the candle and opening the cabinet, she saw someone sitting inside and was greatly frightened, causing the candle to fall and extinguish. Lian thought the woman was Fish Lady and hugged her on the cabinet. The woman asked, "Do you know who I am?" Lian replied, "Are you not Fish Lady?" The woman said, "I am Han Lady." Lian said, "I had always suspected why the cabinet had moved without legs." The woman said, "Are you the ex-husband of my new sister-in-law?" Lian replied, "Yes." The woman said, "I had always expected that this matter would change. I did not expect the fish net and the wild goose to part, but this is truly a match made in heaven." After the matter was done, the woman said, "One day's favor, I have been deeply moved."

When Lian returned, both of us were ashamed, and it would be better to return to your home together. If the new wife comes, she will be my elder sister-in-law, and you can also get a wife; if she does not come, she will be the legitimate wife, and you can even get two wives. At that time, Lian was at a loss for what to do. The woman gathered gold and silk and returned with Lian. When they arrived home, Yu's wife was attending to her mother and speaking, "What news has the person in the cabinet had until now?" Turning around, she saw Lian and Han entering and was surprised and asked, "Why is there still someone who does not嫌弃 my beggar husband?" They became even happier together. Only Han Zhong lost his sister and his wife. When he learned they had both returned to Jiang, he was extremely angry at Yu Yuan and was about to sue. Yu's wife and husband were afraid and agreed to buy two maidservants as compensation. Lian received Han's wealth and could regularly provide for his mother. Moreover, the two women were of like mind, served their mother-in-law gently and respectfully, greatly pleasing her heart. Lian was able to continue his father's business and later passed the imperial examination.

**余母** The County Magistrate Yu Mengqiao, a juren (provincial examination pass) awaiting an appointment in Shandong, was a man of simple and ancient disposition. Even after taking office, he continued to read incessantly, reciting texts as if he were still a scholar. Because of this, he remained on the waiting list for many years, and few in the officialdom knew him. After the autumn metropolitan examination, Vice-Governor Yan Danchu happened to see Yu's draft essays and was pleased with them, and thus Yu was appointed to oversee the prefecture of Licheng. In his governance, he was guided by kindness and benevolence, embodying the virtue of the legendary Lady Du, and the people affectionately called him "Old Mother Yu." Later, he was promoted to the prefecture of Jiaoxi, eventually reaching the rank of governor. **合欢头** In the neighborhoods of Zang and Yue in Luoyang, there lived a woman who was miserly in nature, in all things; even in matters of sexual desire, she was markedly different from others. Her husband, whenever he wished to be intimate with her, had to coax her for over a dozen nights before she would undress. Even when he made great efforts to please her, her frowning and withdrawing expression ultimately prevented him from being able to fully engage, and she could not even find joy in such encounters, leading to repeated miscarriages. One time, however, she carried a child to term at ten months, and was about to give birth. The woman was distressed, thinking that her narrow doorway could not accommodate the baby, and that her body would surely tear apart. Soon after, she felt pain and became greatly distressed. Unable to do otherwise, she also went into labor. The baby's head was just half-exposed when suddenly she exerted force to fit it together. The midwife exclaimed, "Madam, please wait! The baby's head is being crushed!" In a single, whole sound, the newborn's face from the eyes downward and above the nose was narrow, like a wasp's waist. As the child grew, his face still resembled a happy gourd. Once, he went into town, and all who saw him could not help but whisper and laugh.

严三凤 严三凤，字秋泉，是县里的一位著名秀才。他为人玩世不恭，擅长开玩笑，开口总是让人发笑，而他自己却不笑，因此因此而闻名。在他尚未进入仕途之前，习惯代人写文章，因此参加童子试的时间最长。每次院试，搜检的官员都非常讨厌他，甚至会拍打他的衣服和拉扯他的腰带，暗中做手脚。如果有带酒的人，他一定会将酒倒入瓮中，准备痛饮，这让严三凤非常憎恨。正值夏天，他将尿液解入瓶中，用新鲜的荷叶浸泡酒后塞住瓶口，然后带入考场。搜检的官员看到后，闻到香味以为是好酒，急忙打开瓶塞，将酒倒入瓮中，结果泡沫浮起。官员们感到奇怪，严三凤说：“有什么好奇怪的？这只是童子尿！喝了可以安心神，去除恶秽，但病人用来引药，只需少许即可。你们这些人本来就糊涂，是不是越多越好？”官员们非常生气。因此，巡场的官员轮流守着他，使他无暇他顾。最终，他得以在泮池游玩，而偷酒的弊端也因此被消除。

Dong Er Yun Dong Er Yun was not given a name by choice; people called him "Er Yun" because of his indecisive nature and inconsistent behavior. He hailed from Lingui, Guangxi, and lived in extreme poverty, working as a farmhand for a family. One evening, a thief hid in a rolled-up mat in his room. Dong saw him and then bought wine and food, closing the door. He warmed the wine and told the thief, "The wine is hot now. Come, let us drink together." The thief, calculating that there was only Dong in the room, knew that Dong was calling for him and dared not move. After a while, Dong said again, "I thought it was a friend in the mat." The thief had no choice but to come out. Dong recognized the thief, who was named Wang. They drank together for some time before Dong let him go. One night, he heard some noise, secretly got up, opened the door and slipped out, seeing another thief digging through the wall of the owner's house and entering it. Dong grabbed the thief's feet and pulled him into his own room. He lit a fire, and it was still the Wang-named thief. Dong released his hand and said, "Why did you come back to steal here?" Wang replied, "There is a difference in what I steal. Now stealing from your master is because he is stingy in his household." Dong respected this and let him go again.

After the autumn, Dong would wake up early every day to collect stray items. As the weather gradually became colder, the cold became unbearable. His wife, Zhu, was very virtuous and would weave and make cotton clothes for Dong, personally delivering them to him, which made Dong very happy. One day, he got up too early, and a north wind suddenly arose. He therefore went to a gambling den to take a temporary refuge. He saw that the gamblers had plenty of money and were betting with their clothes as collateral, and they lost. Not far from home, he went home and called his wife to get up. His wife saw that there was no cotton coat and asked him about it. He told her the truth. His wife said, "Do not covet other people's money, do not lose your own clothes." She took out money to let her husband redeem his clothes and said, "Do not gamble again." Dong agreed and returned. He saw that the gamblers had not dispersed and wanted to return like the pearls to Hépǔ, so he gambled again and lost again. He felt ashamed and therefore fled. His wife knew about it and had someone redeem his clothes in anticipation, but there was no news. Two months later, she gave birth to a son. Suddenly, someone outside the window called out, "Brother Dong." Zhu heard and said, "My husband has been away for more than two months." The person said, "I know. My name is Wang, and I am a thief. I have met you once before. Today I stole some white gold and am giving half to your wife for daily use. The silver is outside the window, I am leaving now." There was no movement for a long time. Zhu went out to look and indeed found two ingots of silver, about a hundred and several taels. Afterward, Wang frequently gave things to Zhu, all at night. Zhu is naturally thrifty and good at managing the household. With Wang's help, she became prosperous in six or seven years. She was afraid that she would become a burden to Wang for too long. When Wang came to deliver things, she spoke through the window to him, "With your help, my clothing and food are sufficient, please stop." Wang agreed. From then on, Wang did not give anything more.

Dong Erhun fled his home. Young and strong, he helped travelers along the way and assisted the weary, often having leftover money. He then made headwear and sash, and bought a coarse robe. Though not luxurious or splendid, the robe was not worn out or dirty. As he departed from home day by day, he eventually reached Guizhou. At dawn, he saw a man pushing two pieces of wood with a small cart. The man was named Miao, and the load was a bit heavy, causing a slight slope that he couldn't climb. Dong helped him, saying, "I am on the same road as you, please let me help you." He then pulled the cart and went with him. When they arrived at Miao's home, Dong arranged matters and continued on his journey. Miao kept him, saying, "The sun has already set, it is better to start early tomorrow." Miao fed Dong. Miao happened to have an urgent carpentry task, which he did after eating. Dong had once learned carpentry as a child and had a rough idea of the work, so he imitated Miao and did it. Miao was pleased and hired him. After half a year, Miao said to his wife, "I originally came from another town, and I have only one daughter."

董某诚实，吾欲赘之，以为终身之靠。妻亦欲。因赘董，使从苗姓。苗出积蓄制恒产，亦称小康。一日，董赴集买物，过赌场，见赌者似不精熟，因同赌，竟输。思欲得本资而止，竟全输，空手而归。妻问之，以实告，妻未语。董惭悔交深，愤理匠事，失手将右足小指伤去。他日以他事反目，董曰：“日昨吾赌负之事，汝心终不忘，故有今日之事。吾始知仰食裙带失丈夫气，悔当日不宜赘于汝家也。”妻曰：“吾不为往事。君既有悔，从心所欲可也。”董曰：“可。”董早出不归。妻疑之，嗣果无耗。 Translation: Dong was honest, and I wanted to marry him into our family, considering him a lifelong support. My wife also agreed. Because of this, Dong married into the Miao family and took their surname. The Miao family established a permanent property, and they were considered moderately prosperous. One day, Dong went to the market to buy things and passed by a gambling house. He saw that the gamblers seemed inexperienced, so he joined them and ended up losing. He thought he would stop once he got back his principal, but he lost everything and returned empty-handed. His wife asked him about it, and he told her the truth. She did not say anything. Dong felt deeply ashamed and regretful, and in his anger, he lost control while working as a craftsman and injured his right little toe. One day, they fell out over another matter, and Dong said, "Yesterday's gambling loss is something you never forget, so this happened today. I now realize that relying on others for food has made me lose my manhood, and I regret that I should not have married into your family." His wife said, "I do not dwell on past events. Since you have already regretted, do as you please." Dong replied, "Okay." He left early and did not return. His wife suspected something and later found out there was no news of him.

Dong, in a fit of anger, left his home. Two days later, he began to regret his actions but was ashamed to return. He then followed the main road and continued on his way. Soon, he arrived in Xiu Mountain, Sichuan, where he sold his labor to make ends meet. One day, he was hired by a vegetable farmer's family. The farmer was pleased with his strength and kept him for over a month. Dong worked in the fields during the day and returned to the workers' thatched hut in the evening, where he introduced himself as Miao. The farmer had an adopted daughter who had reached the age of marriageability. One day, she told her father, "The short worker from the Miao family seems to be more than just an ordinary laborer." Upon hearing this, the farmer immediately arranged for a matchmaker to propose marriage to Dong. The farmer feared that his adopted daughter might not be happy with her choice of husband, which could lead to resentment. He had heard that his daughter praised Dong, and believing that she had feelings for him, he thought it was fate. After marrying the daughter, Dong continued his work as a carpenter. The daughter's mother, who was a second wife, had no biological children of her own and therefore cherished her adopted daughter, often secretly helping her. Soon after, they had a son. By the time the son was eight or nine years old, the family had become prosperous. Dong, satisfied with his life, focused solely on his son's education, spending generously without counting the costs. His son, Miao Yunxiang, was bright and quick-witted, passed the county-level examination, and married a wife. Dong believed that his life had been extremely fortunate, unaware of future developments. Suddenly, he remembered his legitimate wife, wondering what her life had been like. He pretended to go on a trip and returned home by riding a horse.

Upon arriving home, he saw a grand and imposing mansion, similar to that of a wealthy family. He thought his wife had remarried and the house had been taken over by another family. Shortly after, a young man in fine clothes emerged from the house. The narrator tried to speak to the young man, but he had already passed by. Suddenly, an elderly man addressed the young man, asking, "Where are you going?" Pointing at the narrator, he said, "Your father has come!" Just then, the narrator's wife came out and recognized him, saying, "Indeed, your father." The narrator had been away for twenty-five years, and now they were reunited, how joyful it was! The wife recounted family matters, and the narrator learned that his son Jixiang had entered the school, which made him very happy. He also recounted his own story of being away. His wife and son observed his demeanor and knew he was not poor, but they were hesitant to believe the rest. Just then, a man from a neighboring village who had passed the provincial examination, surnamed Liang, was being congratulated. The narrator and his fellow villagers went to congratulate him and saw a list of successful candidates from Sichuan posted on the wall, with Miao Yunxiang's name listed, which greatly pleased them. Liang asked the narrator, who replied, "This child." Liang secretly smiled, thinking that since the surname was different, how could he be the son? The narrator told his wife and son, and they also believed it. When the narrator returned home, coincidentally, the son born to his wife Cai, named Miao Yunxiang, was taking the provincial examination. He passed the examination and returned, but did not see his father. He asked his mother, who said, "He left on a trip two months ago and has not returned. There was no place to meet him, so we just wait." By the end of the year, there was no news.

By the time spring arrived, Cai Shi told her son, "Your father must have returned to his hometown. You should go to Lingui to visit him." Yunxiang went to Lingui and searched for several days but found no one with the surname Miao. One day, he passed by Liang Xielian's residence and learned that Liang was a newly appointed official. He then paid a visit, and their bond as fellow examinees deepened, becoming much stronger than usual feelings. Yunxiang explained that he had come to Lingui to search for his father and asked Liang for help. Liang replied, "There is no one with your surname in my area." Suddenly, Yunxiang noticed a list of names on the wall and remembered what Dong had said. He said, "There might be someone who knows about it. You can ask him personally." Liang sent his family to invite Dong and said, "You mentioned there is a guest from Xiushan here. Please have him come along, and he will definitely arrive." Liang's family informed Dong, who told his wife, "My son has come. I think he must have come to find me after passing the exam." His wife still had some doubts. Soon after, Dong and Yunxiang arrived. Yunxiang was instructed to dress formally and pay respects to his mother and elder brother. The whole family was overjoyed and held a feast to celebrate. A few days later, Yunxiang asked his father to return to Xiushan together, but Jixiang and her son did not want to. After delaying for more than a month, Yunxiang and his father planned to return secretly at night. Jixiang found out and sent someone to chase them back. From then on, she did not want her father to travel. If he did go out, someone had to accompany him. She also hid Yunxiang's carriages, horses, and belongings elsewhere, so Yunxiang could not return on his own.

### Translation Unavoidably, the petition was presented to the magistrate of Línguì County, Miáo Gong. The magistrate asked, "Dǒng Jíxiáng, are you his son?" Dǒng replied, "Born of my legitimate wife." The magistrate then asked, "Miáo Yúnxiáng, is he also your son?" Dǒng answered, "Born of a wife who remarried after leaving home." The magistrate said, "You bear the surname Dǒng, why do you also bear the surname Miáo?" Dǒng replied, "There is a reason, which I cannot explain in detail." The magistrate asked, "Which son do you wish to support?" Dǒng said, "I wish to support both. The matter is not within my control; I will leave it to fate. If you grant us the freedom to decide for ourselves, I will be deeply grateful." The magistrate then asked Miáo Yúnxiáng, who said, "As a scholar, I am willing to take our father to Xiùshān for a few months of visits and then return him." The magistrate then asked Dǒng Jíxiáng, who said, "After a few months, what if my brother does not return him?" The magistrate replied, "I cannot guarantee that he will return him. However, this is not just one person's father; both of you wish to support him. What do you think?" Dǒng Jíxiáng said, "My brother has supported our father for twenty-five years, and I also wish to support him for twenty-five years before returning him." The magistrate laughed and said, "Your father is now over fifty years old. In ancient times, reaching seventy was rare. If you support him for another twenty-five years, he will be on his deathbed, which is not reasonable. You may go back for now, and I will entrust the local gentry to mediate the matter." After the magistrate returned home, he saw his mother and smiled. His mother asked him about it, and the magistrate said, "Today, I had a very strange case. Two sons were disputing over caring for their father, and the father could not make his own decision. It was difficult for me to pass judgment." He then recounted the entire case.

The mother said, "Your father is from Lingui, but I have forgotten the county where he was registered. How old is the person who now has the surname Dong? How does his appearance resemble yours?" The son replied, "Yes." The mother said, "You should look at his right foot to see if it lacks a little toe; that is your father." The son then had the case brought forward and showed the plan to the servant. Dong was about to ascend the hall, but the servant deliberately stained his two boots with dirty water to falsely claim the mistake. Dong did not have time to change his boots. When he ascended the hall, the magistrate welcomed him and asked why his boots were dirty. Dong explained it. The magistrate then had new boots brought to replace the old ones. He hurried back to report to the mother. The mother said, "I have already peeped through a hole in the window to see clearly; that is your father." The magistrate quickly sent someone to invite the father and elder brother to the inner study. He bowed to the father and said, "The son sits high, and the father kneels facing north. If I had not known before, I would have been guilty of a grave sin."

Dong was greatly surprised and frightened, and was about to inquire in detail when Miao's wife appeared and smiled at Dong, saying, "You abandoned me and fled. I thought you were dead. Are you still alive in this world?" She pointed to the magistrate and said, "You left three months ago and this child was born. He achieved success in consecutive examinations and was immediately employed, initially taking up this position." Dong was overjoyed and then recounted to Yunxiang the story of how he had married into the Miao family, saying, "I changed my surname to Miao for this very reason." He ordered his two sons to resume their original surname, Dong. The magistrate's name was Chengxiang, and the coincidence of the names was also remarkable. At the time, Wang had been arrested for a crime and, upon hearing that the magistrate was Dong's son, said, "I will have a chance to clear my name." When people asked him about it, Wang remained silent. Not long after, he was indeed released and returned home. This was heard and told by my disciple Liu Yüjī. The person who talked about this matter is from Línguì, and is a fellow villager of Dong Gong. The Vǎibái Daoist said: Dong Gong's name was Yun, but he was not really confused. He released the bandit, still treating him as a gentleman; he supported his wife, and whenever she lacked authority, he would leave, showing that he had not lost his masculine spirit; later, he remarried and had children, establishing a family and a career, which could have lasted a lifetime, but he remembered his first wife and left her to return, demonstrating his commitment to the relationship between husband and wife. Such a person, who is called "confused," would make many of those who today claim to be "not confused" feel ashamed. The world often looks down on others, but such people often have much to be looked down upon themselves. The more they look down on others, the more their own shortcomings become apparent. Those who daily engage in slandering and accusing others, thinking they are wise and upright, cannot escape the disapproval of the famous and virtuous.

**Translation:** **Auspicious Site** A certain scholar-official, currently serving as a governor-general in a southern province, came from a family of farmers and merchants. People all said that the site where his grandparents were buried was truly auspicious. Before his grandmother passed away, the family did not allow her to be buried in the ancestral tomb, and relatives and friends were all upset, threatening to bring a lawsuit. His grandfather said, "A good person naturally finds a good place; why must the ancestral tomb be a field of merit? Moreover, to bring a lawsuit over this would not only ruin our own property but also others', greatly harming family sentiment, which is truly something I cannot bear to do." They then moved the burial site, and it turned out to be auspicious. Thus, it is known that even if a site is truly auspicious, one must still seek the truth in one's heart. **Jiang Yun** The scholar-official Song from Anqing, whose name is forgotten by those who talk about him, went out for a stroll one night when the pear blossoms were in full bloom, the orioles perched on the delicate beauty of the flowers, and the butterflies intoxicated by the rich fragrance. He wandered and enjoyed the scenery until the moon rose high before returning. Upon reaching his study, he wrote a short poem: "The gallery is quiet, the moon slants at the beginning of the month; the clear shade outside the window penetrates the green gauze. A thread of idle sentiment cannot be blown away, forming a dreamy vision on the pear blossoms." After writing it, he placed it under the green mat. The next day, a fellow examinee visited and said, "There is someone who is skilled at summoning the spirit of the soot in the divination bowl. Why don't we go and watch?" The official had never believed in such things and did not want to offend him, so they went together. The diviners were all present, and the official asked, "What deities are you inviting?" They replied that there were many. The official pretended to say, "Last year at my old residence, I and my friend invited the fairy Jiang Yun, who was extremely responsive."

Why not ask for her?” The group said, “Do you recognize the symbol?” He replied, “I do.” So he imitated the request for a female fairy's talisman, made slight changes, drew it, and gave it to the group. They began by burning incense and the talisman. After a short while, the writing brush moved, and a poem descended from the altar, which was the pear blossom poem by Song Gong. He was deeply amazed and did not dare to say that the poem was his own work, but he also joined in praising it. Then, four characters were written: “Song Gong spoke too much, causing me to have this one visit. I have something to entrust.” The group asked what it was, and the writing brush wrote: “It's hard to say. But when it must be said, it must be said. I have a predestined relationship with Bai Ruoyu, and I ask all of you to arrange a marriage for us.” It turned out that Bai was present at the altar, a young woman who had recently lost her husband and was not yet remarried. The group spoke to her, and she was also happy to agree. They teasingly asked when the betrothal gift would be presented and where the bride would be escorted; when they asked again, the writing brush had stopped moving. They all talked until late at night before dispersing.

White's family had no relatives, only an elderly woman and a servant. Upon arriving home, the servant awaited him at the door and said, "Just now, an elderly woman brought a young lady here." White recognized her as Jiao Yun and hurried into the room, where he saw the young lady dressed in red sitting in the canopy. The elderly woman helped her down from the bed and sat beside White, and she truly embodied the phrase "Her beauty surpasses jade, and her fragrance is even more exquisite; her divine grace is like a flower, and her words are even more truthful." The elderly woman prepared a feast and made White and the young lady drink from the same cup. White said, "I heard that you and I have a connection. Dare I ask what it is?" The young lady replied, "To speak of it would be shocking and would tarnish the reputation of ancient people. You need not delve deeply into it." Another day, a spirit medium friend told White, "We burned the talisman, but Jiao Xian did not appear. What should we do?" White said, "It's a bother, it's a bother. Duran Xiang has already been married off." Later, on the Double Ninth Festival, the friends from the altar gathered to climb high, and six or seven farmers requested a spirit medium to inquire about the rainy season. The group suggested summoning Lü Xian, but someone said, "Today, White is elsewhere on business. Why not still summon Jiao Yun?" They then burned three talismans consecutively, and she finally arrived. The spirit medium wrote, "Previously, I was a wandering immortal, roaming at will; now I am a wife and should follow my husband's commands."

If they say I should follow the teachings of the hidden path, I should not generally interact with all of you. Now that I have been summoned three times, I am reluctantly responding. Please quickly tell me the matter." The assembled group said, "This autumn has seen little rain, which farmers are eagerly awaiting. When will it rain?" The woman replied, "This is the will of heaven, and I dare not reveal it. Please create a puzzle using the nine-nine multiplication table, and let all of you guess it: Two nines make eighteen, but two nines do not make eighteen; three eights make twenty-four, but three eights do not make twenty-four; four sevens do not make twenty-eight; five sixes do not make thirty. There is a four-character phrase hidden in one sentence." None of them could understand. Suddenly, someone said, "I know it now. One two and one nine together make eleven, two nines do not make eighteen; one three and one eight also make eleven, three eights do not make twenty-four; four and seven, five and six, all make eleven. The hidden phrase actually refers to eleven. Today is the ninth day of the ninth month, and the day after tomorrow will definitely rain, because the day after tomorrow is the eleventh day." All agreed, and so they informed the farmers. On the eleventh day, it indeed rained heavily. Bai heard this and said, "Why are you doing this good and grand thing?" The woman replied, "Yes. From now on, I will never respond to such calls again." After two years, the woman left, and Bai began his life anew.

If they say I should follow the teachings of the hidden path, I should not generally interact with all of you. Now that I have been summoned three times, I am reluctantly responding. Please quickly tell me the matter." The assembled group said, "This autumn has seen little rain, which farmers are eagerly awaiting. When will it rain?" The woman replied, "This is the will of heaven, and I dare not reveal it. Please create a puzzle using the nine-nine multiplication table, and let all of you guess it: Two nines make eighteen, but two nines do not make eighteen; three eights make twenty-four, but three eights do not make twenty-four; four sevens do not make twenty-eight; five sixes do not make thirty. There is a four-character phrase hidden in one sentence." None of them could understand. Suddenly, someone said, "I know it now. One two and one nine together make eleven, two nines do not make eighteen; one three and one eight also make eleven, three eights do not make twenty-four; four and seven, five and six, all make eleven. The hidden phrase actually refers to eleven. Today is the ninth day of the ninth month, and the day after tomorrow will definitely rain, because the day after tomorrow is the eleventh day." All agreed, and so they informed the farmers. On the eleventh day, it indeed rained heavily. Bai heard this and said, "Why are you doing this good and grand thing?" The woman replied, "Yes. From now on, I will never respond to such calls again." After two years, the woman left, and Bai began his life anew.

**Leprosy Opening** In the southern provinces, there is a belief that women can contract a leprosy-like condition due to exposure to mountainous瘴气 (miasma or toxic air). When a woman contracts this condition, local people recognize it, and no one will marry her. If a man marries such a woman, he is believed to die. However, at the time of her first sexual encounter, she is made to look attractive and is allowed to go out freely. She is encouraged to have sexual relations with men from other places, a practice known as "opening the leprosy," which is believed to cure the condition. After having sexual relations, she is allowed to stay with the man for a month or a minimum of 20 days before sending him away to continue his journey. When she sends him away, she gives him a farewell drink and gifts, showing her earnestness and sincerity, and urges him to return quickly, fearing that he might die on the journey. This practice is socially accepted as a way of choosing a suitable match.

There was a woman from the Wan family who contracted this illness. The Wan family instructed her to follow the custom, but she did not wish to comply. She said, "I request death, so as not to harm others for my own benefit." After delaying for two years, she could not repeatedly disobey her parents' commands, so she had no choice but to lure a young man to share her bed. The young man introduced himself as Zhou Zhang, a poor scholar who had come to study here. In reality, he was surnamed Wu and was pretending to be surnamed Zhou. The man and woman were very compatible. After a month, the Wan family ordered her to send him away, but she could not bear to part with him. After another half month, the illness became unbearable. Because of sharing the same space for a longer time, its onset became more severe, and if delayed further, it would be impossible to leave. The woman stole from her father's hidden stash and planned to run away together. Zhou doubted that her words were not sincere. She said, "You cannot live long in this world, and I cannot have two husbands. This is a way to achieve both. Why do you doubt?" They took the opportunity to escape together, but they stopped before leaving the mountain and rented a place to live. Soon after, the illness manifested, and Zhou asked the woman to seek medical treatment. She said, "Your illness cannot be cured." She then explained the reason for her inevitable death. After speaking, she wept, and the tears did not stop day or night. Suddenly, someone outside the window said, "Crying is of no use." The woman knew that the person was unusual and asked, "If crying is of no use, is not crying of use?" The person replied, "Yes. Thirty li southeast of here is Mothai Mountain. Halfway up the mountain is a cave, where the Immortal Machu cultivates tranquility. You can go there to seek help. However, there are many long snakes there, and I fear you may not dare to go." The woman said, "I dare. I just do not know the path." The person said, "I can guide you." She asked, "Who are you?" The person replied, "I am a wandering ghost. I grieve deeply for your situation, so I inform you."

During the dawn, she saw a small whirlwind and said, "That is me." Zhou heard this and feared being harmed by snakes. The woman replied, "If indeed harmed by snakes, you will have no worry of a widow remarrying after her husband's death; if fortunately spared by the snakes, I will obtain the elixir of immortality to cure your illness, thus proving to you that I am not afraid of death for your sake. This will be your fortune and my happiness." They then proceeded. Indeed, there was a spiral wind in front of them, and they followed it. They entered a deep mountain and suddenly saw a snake as big as a carriage wheel coming towards the woman. Thinking there was no way to escape, the woman closed her eyes and waited, but the snake did not devour her. Continuing forward, they found the ground covered with countless snakes of all sizes. However, when the snakes approached the woman, they all turned their heads and did not bite. As the woman entered the snake-infested area, she felt even more refreshed. She then arrived at the cave temple to meet Magu and described her husband's illness. Magu advised that releasing the poison through a prostitute might cure it. The woman returned and informed Zhou.

Zhou said, "You still do not wish to harm others for your own benefit, would I do such a thing?" The woman replied, "The harm is only to one prostitute, what's the harm?" Zhou responded, "Is a prostitute not a person? If harming her would benefit me, I would not do it either,何况未必能愈耶？" The woman urged him repeatedly, but Zhou still refused to listen. The next morning, he went to the cave again and saw a path obscured by swirling clouds, with sheer cliffs reaching up to the sky. He shed tears and returned. Unexpectedly, his husband had turned into a large snake coiled around him, with drool hanging from its mouth. When he entered the house, he saw the snake's mouth holding a herb, which it left behind. At that moment, Zhou was unconscious, and when called, he regained consciousness. The woman then boiled the herb the snake had held and gave it to Zhou to drink. After taking one dose, his illness disappeared. It became clear that Magu's method of saving him was indeed through this herb. Later, Zhou donated his quota to enter the Northern Examination, and they walked together into the Southern Palace, becoming a candidate for the Hanlin Academy. All the official announcements of success were from Wu Zhang.

A Woman with Two Sons and the Inheritance In the eastern village, there were two brothers who lived separately. The younger brother was wealthy, while the elder brother was poor. After the elder brother's death, he left no children, and his wife, Liu, had become blind, leaving them in a precarious situation that would inevitably lead to starvation. The neighbors, feeling pity for their plight, agreed among themselves to visit the younger brother and ask for assistance for Liu. However, the younger brother refused to help. The neighbors said, "When fellow villagers are in need, we should still help them. How much more so for the elder brother's wife?" The younger brother replied, "When we divided the property, the elder brother did not show much kindness to me, giving me only a little. Helping in times of emergency is still acceptable, but helping the elder brother's wife is truly undesirable." The neighbors were angry and left, saying, "The younger brother has two sons, while the elder brother has no children. It is suggested that the elder brother's wife wants to adopt one of the younger brother's sons as her heir. The younger brother does not want his son to be adopted and prefers to help his elder brother's wife instead." The neighbors returned to speak to the younger brother again. He laughed and said, "Adopting my son to support myself, the elder brother's wife's plan is also clever. But this would mean making my son go from having food and warmth to hunger and cold, which I do not want." The neighbors spoke earnestly to him, saying, "According to the law, there is no provision for cutting off the inheritance of the eldest, and the law is set up this way." The younger brother still refused to listen and became even more disrespectful. The neighbors were even more angry and decided to take Liu to court to force an adoption. They collected money and sent a servant to take Liu to court. They filed a complaint against the younger brother, and the court agreed. The younger brother was afraid and begged in the village to settle the lawsuit. He divided the family property equally and made his eldest son adopt the elder brother's wife, thus becoming her heir.

Xubai the Daoist Master said: "Nurturing one's poor sister-in-law and continuing the family line when there is no heir is one's duty; what need is there for others to say it? If one refuses to heed sound advice, such a person is already unworthy of mention. In the end, one cannot avoid nurturing and cannot dare not to continue the family line, setting an example for those who do not nurture or continue the family line, yet remains a sinner to the world." **Formulas left behind by ghosts and foxes:** **Those who swallow salt brine will have their blood congeal and die. If they are given undiluted soy milk, they will recover. The reasoning is easy to understand. If there is no nearby tofu shop, quickly grind soybeans into juice, and it can still help.** **Those who swallow arsenic should quickly use one liang of Fangfeng, grind it into powder, mix with water, and administer it, and they will recover. This is a formula left behind by a fox. Fangfeng is only a wind-dampness remedy, yet it can neutralize arsenic poison, which is puzzling. According to the *Bencao* (Herbal), Fangfeng can neutralize the toxicity of Fuzi (aconite). Fuzi is extremely hot, and arsenic is also extremely hot. Could it be that Fangfeng's ability to neutralize the heat toxicity of arsenic is due to its ability to neutralize the heat toxicity of Fuzi? Many predecessors' writings have recorded these two formulas. I am concerned that these remedies are not widely known, so I am recording them again here."

Chu Xiwen Chu Xiwen was a merchant from Fujian. On his return from a trading journey, he was still thirty or more miles from home when it suddenly started raining. About twenty steps ahead, there was a small temple that could accommodate five or six people. He quickly sought shelter there. There was already a young woman inside, dressed in simple but elegant attire, with a remarkably beautiful demeanor. He placed his belongings inside the temple and instructed the woman to close the door, saying, "If many people seek shelter, it would be very inconvenient." Fortunately, the temple was sheltered from the wind and had a small eave that kept the rain off, so they stayed dry. The rain stopped at midnight. Throughout the night, Chu did not speak to the woman. At dawn, he called out to her to open the door and retrieve his belongings. The woman began her journey at sunrise and found a left-behind cloth bag, which contained about five taels of silver and three hundred coins. She took the items back home. The woman was Shan Shi, the wife of Mu Ruitu. One day, she was talking with her sister-in-law, saying, "People often say there are few good people in the world, but there are indeed good people. They often appear virtuous on the outside but may have hidden malicious intentions. As for my foolish sister-in-law’s encounter yesterday, it can truly be called a gentleman’s act."

The sister asked about it, and the wife then carefully recounted the story of how she had taken shelter from the rain. The sister laughed and said, "That person is a gentleman, but even when he meets his wife, he might not be a gentleman, perhaps the sister-in-law considers herself a gentleman?" The wife knew that once words were spoken, they could not be recalled, but her conscience was clear, so she showed both the silver and the money to her, saying, "These were left by him." Just as the sister was examining them, the husband arrived and asked where the silver and money came from. The sister recounted the story. Mu said, "If this is true, then he truly does not disgrace the title of gentleman." Then he told his wife, "I have heard that your mother suddenly fell ill, why don't we go together to ask about it?" The mother actually had no illness, and the daughter suspected it. When they arrived, he told the father-in-law, "Your daughter returned home yesterday and encountered rain, and her luck was very good, so she found this much silver and money." He left it on the table and went away. The father asked the daughter, and she answered truthfully. The father said, "Though your feelings are true, your husband's mind is not at ease." Fearing that an excessive reaction might cause a change, he had to keep the daughter at home.

Mu returned and saw the cloth, asking his sister. She replied, "It was also picked up by my sister-in-law." Mu pondered and said, "Giving silver and money to someone, there has never been a case where the cloth was also given." Upon reflection, since the woman stayed in the same temple with a man, the matter was still suspicious. Mu usually pulled a cart and sold goods. One day, on a journey, he met fellow travelers who invited him to drink at an inn. Zha was also present, from the same hometown as Mu, and they had known each other for a long time. Suddenly, Zha saw Mu's cloth and said, "It is what I left behind, there are other things in the cloth, have you seen them?" Mu said, "I saw them. Where did you leave them?" Zha replied, "On a certain day, I took shelter from the rain in a temple and left early in the morning, leaving it behind." Mu pretended and said, "It was I who picked it up in that temple." Zha said, "Strange, a young woman is so chaste, why would she not care about this object?" Mu asked the reason, and Zha boasted about staying with the young woman all night without any improper thoughts. Mu said, "Who are you trying to fool? You usually sleep with women, how come you suddenly have the virtue of not being tempted?" Zha replied seriously, "Prostitutes abandon their integrity and use sex to sustain themselves; those who like to play with women indulge in tenderness and spend money to buy smiles."

Consider it like trade, where there is buying and selling, what harm is there? If one takes advantage of a woman who is alone and defiles her, even if the matter does not become public and does not damage one's reputation, it causes the woman to feel ashamed for life, and even leads her to take her own life out of shame. What greater loss of virtue could there be! Moreover, if we consider the situation from the other side, the woman who has been defiled would surely feel deep pain and desire to seek justice. How can one do to others what they would not want done to themselves?” Mu shook his head without speaking. Zha said, “If I had engaged in immoral behavior, I would have been struck by lightning.” Mu then smiled in satisfaction and bowed to Zha, saying, “I must tell you, the young woman I encountered is my wife. Now that I have heard your words, I know she was wronged. I sent someone to fetch her, but she may not come. If you have a long-standing relationship with her father-in-law, I humbly ask you to mediate on my behalf.” Zha said, “I will.” He then went home and told Dan翁, who sent his daughter to Mu, and their broken mirror was mended. Mu then told his wife, “Now I know that Brother Zha is a true gentleman. Your words were not false.” They both admired Zha, and so they wrote a letter inviting Zha over. At the banquet, Mu had his sister-in-law dress up elegantly and come out to offer Zha a cup of wine, saying, “I have heard that my brother has no one to manage his household. I wish to offer my younger sister to continue the family line, to repay my brother for not taking advantage of my wife. What do you think?” Zha was pleased and immediately stood up to drink a toast. Later, Mu's sister-in-law gave birth to two sons, the second of whom became a high-ranking official known for his virtue.

In the spring of the year corresponding to the stem-branch combination of "Geng Shen," after I was dismissed from office, I temporarily resided in Jixia, where I closed the door and rested while sick, entertaining myself with books. Someone mentioned "Yizhi Lu," and I urgently bought and read the book. It is a collection of strange and supernatural stories, and the style is so outstanding that it surpasses the ordinary, far surpassing the recent writers. The author is identified as Jieling Xiaozijing, also known as Jie Jian. I sought him out, but few people in Licheng knew his name. Later, I learned that he taught at Huangtai Mountain, about eight miles north of the city, so I managed to invite him over. Soon after, he knocked on the door to visit me, and he appeared as an old white-haired man. When I inquired about his life, he said: "I took the preliminary examination for the imperial examinations when I was young, but I never succeeded and did not obtain a degree. My family is poor, and I rely on teaching children for a living. I am now sixty years old." He was slender and stood like a crane, with a warm and elegant demeanor, and his speech was slow and hesitant, as if he could not easily express himself. However, he had read widely from the works of various philosophers and scholars, was skilled in writing and poetry, and had a deep interest in ancient culture and history. This compilation was created in his spare time, as a playful imitation of Pu Songling's "Liaozhai Zhiyi." From this, one can see that Jie Jian is but a small part of the grandeur of Mount Tai and a small part of the vastness of the ocean. Those who discuss his work note that it closely imitates "Liaozhai Zhiyi" and suggest that it could compete with "Liaozhai Zhiyi" for the same seat. I, however, do not agree with this view.

*Liaozhai* is a work of extraordinary talent, its author's genius freely expressed, and his learning profound and extensive. How easy is it for later generations to follow in his footsteps? However, *Liaozhai* was created by someone who felt unrecognized for his talent, using it as an outlet for his frustration. Thus, the book curses gods and demons, and encompasses laughter, jest, scolding, and cursing, with nothing left out. It is indeed a work born of frustration. The author of this text, *Jiezi*, was young and endowed with exceptional talent, yet he found no opportunities for success. By the time he reached old age, he still lived in a remote, impoverished area, teaching children to make a living. How does his poverty compare to that of *Liaozhai*? Yet, his writing contains not a single impure or vulgar word, not a single frivolous or disrespectful word. It encourages good and punishes evil, adhering strictly to the right path. Although it shares the same playful writing style as *Liaozhai*, this book uniquely benefits society. Its value lies in the author's intellectual and moral development, which surpasses *Liaozhai* in one aspect. Nevertheless, I am moved by this.

People's nature is inclined toward the strange and weary of the ordinary, impressed by empty fame but seldom appreciating true merit. *Strange Tales from a Chinese Studio*, with its profound, extensive, and uniquely beautiful talent, seeks out the strange and unusual, emerging from obscurity into prominence, and is sufficient to dazzle the scholars. During the same period, there were high-ranking officials with great influence across the land, whose single word could boost someone's reputation a hundredfold. Thus, even though he was poor and destitute, he could still spread his reputation widely and loudly, captivating the moment. Jiezi's talent is not inferior; he was merely a modest and humble person in his hometown, not seeking superficial glory, not boasting of his reputation. This led to his name not spreading beyond the village gate, and few scholars knew who he was. Is this not an unjust situation? When I served as an official in Licheng, Jiezi was still taking his county examination, but I was preoccupied with worldly affairs and did not recognize him. Now that I am resigning from office and returning home, Jiezi is already an elderly man, and only now do we clench our fists and sigh together. Alas, it is too late!

Preface For thirteen years, I have been serving in Qi, though I am merely a pretender. In Lixia, I have heard of and seen renowned scholars, distinguished intellectuals, scholars, and literati, and it seems that there are no hidden talents in the countryside. Yet, I have never heard of a person named Gongzi Jing. In the summer of the fifth month of the year of the Horse (戊辰), his brother presented me with Mr. Gongzi Jing's *Yizhi Lu* in ten volumes. After carefully reading and pondering it, I gained a thorough understanding and realized that Mr. Gongzi Jing is indeed a distinguished scholar from Jinan. How could I not have seen or heard of him? I am truly ashamed of my limited knowledge and experience. I now regret having met him so late. In his entire work, he either explores the strange and unusual or discusses ghosts and searches for spirits, mostly using playful writing to convey the intention to encourage good behavior and discourage bad behavior. His use of strange and ever-changing ideas and poignant and lingering writing, though derived from *Liaozhai Zhiyi*, carries a deep heart for encouraging goodness and correcting mistakes, and the principle of rewarding virtue and punishing vice, which is relevant to the norms of propriety, the order of the world, and the hearts of people, is perhaps even more so than in *Liaozhai Zhiyi*.

With the talent and learning of the Master, one could have secured a high rank in the imperial examinations, attained a prominent official position, and the achievements in governance that are recorded would certainly be more than sufficient to inspire and maintain moral and social norms. Yet, the Master did not pass the highest level of the imperial examinations and spent his life studying. Is it that the way heaven rewards talented people is indeed as it should be? Is it that once serving as an official, being burdened with official paperwork, and daily engaged in the chaotic world of official documents, the Master's talents were not fully displayed? Or is it that the Master's scholarly pursuits were not fully realized due to the distractions of official life? Thus, the Master was placed in a position of inactivity, hindered in his opportunities and thus aging in obscurity; he dedicated himself to refining history and the classics, focusing his mind and refining his learning. The Master's talents and learning were not only prominently displayed in his own time but also had a lasting impact, not only widely proclaimed in his time but also permanently recorded in history. If so, then heaven places them in positions for the wise and noble, and their intentions are indeed very earnest! At first, one feels special regret for the Master, but later, one no longer feels regret, but instead feels fortunate for the Master. The Master can indeed have no regrets.

In the sixth month of the seventh year of the Tongzhi era, Zhang Baoxian, also known as Yu Jie, wrote this preface. Preface Narrative fiction was already established during the Tang and Song dynasties; in recent times, Pu Songling's *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio* pleases the heart and eyes, almost changing one's feelings, and can be read a hundred times without tiring. Its narrative is detailed and intricate without being tedious, its choice of words rich and elegant without being overly ornate, and its writing style graceful and playful without being overly delicate. Its imaginative and creative aspects, its fantastical and unexpected elements, and its ability to make the impossible seem possible, without dismissing it as mere fiction, were once considered a unique and unparalleled style, few of which could match it. In the summer of 1871, while serving in Jinan, I obtained eight volumes of *Yizhi Lu* (益智录) written by Junzi Jing, read it carefully once through, and was surprised to find that *Yizhi Lu* is a worthy successor to *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio*.

A fellow scholar and friend, Ye Yunshi, the imperial inspector, remarked about the book: "It contains not a single vulgar or disrespectful word, not a single frivolous or disrespectful word, and encourages good deeds while punishing immorality, all in line with propriety." This is greatly different from *Liaozhai*’s scolding of gods and ghosts to vent its frustration and discontent. This statement is correct. However, I particularly appreciate how closely it resembles *Liaozhai* without the traces of deliberate imitation. It is truly a good learner who follows the spirit of the ancients rather than their form. It should be quickly published to share with fellow enthusiasts, and also to understand that writing with a brush is to learn from the ancients but not to copy them. Xie, possessing such talent, cannot obtain even a minor official rank, and now teaches in the countryside, on the verge of old age. Yet, he entrusts his heart to the pen, with not a hint of dissatisfaction or complaint in his writing. How profound his learning and cultivation must be! Scholars read and study ancient texts, yet often have talent but are not recognized. Even light-hearted writings can be lasting, as Yunshi said, serving to encourage and admonish, benefiting the world. I know that you prefer not to be known through this collection, yet this collection can still reveal you. This collection is not sufficient to transmit your legacy, but you will indeed be transmitted through this collection. To verify with Yunshi, these words will not be found exaggerated.

On the tenth day after the summer solstice in the Xin You year of the Xianfeng era, in Binzhou, Du Qiaoyu, known as Junchao, wrote this. --- **Preface** From the annals and histories to the various schools of thought and the myriad works of the Hundred Schools, though their statements differ, their overarching purpose is to encourage virtue and punish wickedness. However, direct and straightforward words may not always be effective; it is easier for people to accept advice conveyed through analogy. Similarly, solemn and serious words may not always be listened to, whereas words that seem deceptive or unconventional often have a greater impact. This is why unofficial histories and folk tales sometimes shed light on the truths found in the classics. In the autumn of Xin You, Gentleman Junjing from Jie visited me at my lecture hall in Jinan, presenting his work *The Record of Wisdom* for my perusal. At the time, the atmosphere was unsettled, and I had to return home in haste, leaving me unable to read it thoroughly. In the spring of Ren Xu, I returned to Lixia, where I finally read it in detail, admiring the depth and breadth of its underlying meaning. Scholars and gentlemen who seize the opportunity to attain positions of power often find little expression of their ideas in their writings; they may be honored during their lifetimes, but their legacies fade after their deaths.

Even if one has the will to write, gathering and compiling the sayings of scholars, seeking out outdated scholarly opinions, can make one read no more than a few pages and immediately want to close the book. Those who go even further, searching for strange and unusual things that go against the norm, displaying beautiful words without stopping at propriety and righteousness, hope to touch hearts and maintain social norms, but this is difficult! This collection, far tracing the tradition of strange tales from *Search for the Supernatural*, *Record of the Strange*, and *Qi H*

In the first month of autumn in the first year of the Tongzhi era, Li Zaoxian of Lixian wrote the preface. Preface Beyond the classics and historical records, there are the *Shiji* (Records of the Grand Historian), works by various philosophers, writings by many schools of thought, and the Five Masters along with miscellaneous writings and narratives, all diverse and numerous. Among these, those that are broad and profound are sufficient to enrich one's mind; those that are strange and unusual are sufficient to startle one's senses. Generally speaking, they do not go beyond emotion and righteousness. Those skilled in expressing emotions, all expressions of joy, anger, sorrow, and happiness, either conforming to the proper expression of emotion or being overly indulgent in emotion, all vividly depict their respective events. When following emotion leads to good, and when indulging in emotion leads to evil, all are the result of emotion reaching its limit, and are unchangeable. Therefore, they speak eloquently, and one cannot stop listening until the end; the inherent nature and subsequent changes are not discussed. Those skilled in expressing righteousness, all that pertains to the norms of conduct and ethical principles, internally connected to the family, externally related to the state, all clearly demonstrate their appropriateness. When acting righteously due to wisdom, and when adhering to righteousness despite foolishness, all are compelled by righteousness, and cannot be shirked. Therefore, they speak earnestly, and one cannot stop until the truth is fully understood. The correctness of the truth is not discussed.

A Fellow Scholar from My Hometown Named Xie Zijing A fellow scholar from my hometown, Xie Zijing, has a deep and sincere nature and is honest and simple. He is well-versed in literature, skilled in poetry and prose, and has a strong preference for classical literature over contemporary trends. He uses classical literature to vent deep feelings and寄托远思于奇异之事。 He collects from various classics and draws inspiration from different schools of thought. His emotional expressions are very close, and his logical expressions are clear and decisive. What exists may not exist, and what does not exist may exist, as he expresses his thoughts and desires as they come to mind. All kinds of supernatural beings and creatures, whether they exist or not, are used in his writing. He melts and casts the classics and history, and spreads beauty and fragrance, with some things that cannot be described in words. As for narrative techniques, events come suddenly and leave gracefully, and the skill of writing is just a minor aspect. Xie Zijing lives in a remote area, where people might consider him ignorant and poorly informed. He is hindered in his studies, where people might consider him slow-witted and lacking in knowledge. He writes this book to vent his frustrations and adhere to principles and morals. This record, although inferior to *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio*, is actually similar in style. It is sufficient to enhance people's wisdom and particularly contains moral teachings in every chapter. Anything unrelated to the world and human nature is not included. I am happy to write the preface for it.

Written in the Simple Leisure Pavilion during the small spring of the year Jia Yin in the Xianfeng era, respectfully penned by your humble brother Huang Nanbin, styled Qin Xuan. Preface When it comes to legendary and fictional narratives, authors often conceal their names to vent their anger or subtly include events to express their feelings. This allows later readers to admire the beauty of the words and the grandeur of the writing, while having no relation to the moral and ethical state of society. This is the greatest warning for writers. Subi is an old acquaintance from my village. In his youth, he strove hard in poetry and books, but did not achieve much; in his later years, he paid attention to moral and social norms, often wishing to continue the tradition. Whenever he had spare time from teaching, he wrote several volumes of *The Record of Wisdom*. Everything he saw and heard, he recorded promptly, and he detailed the stories of loyalty, filial piety, righteousness, and integrity, so that readers would be shocked into conscience and their innate goodness would be aroused. This deeply embodies the essence of the moral teachings in poetry, promoting good and punishing evil, and subtly conveys the intention to change the moral and ethical state of society, which is not trivial! If one were to focus on the clarity of the narrative and the uniqueness of the writing style, it would not be to understand Subi. This is the preface.

In the fifth year of the Xianfeng era, the eighth month of autumn, my fellow townsman and younger brother Chunqing, Wu Bingrong, respectfully records. Preface Jie Zijing of Lixia is a high-minded scholar. Well-versed and skilled in poetry, he has a refined taste for classical prose and does not compose contemporary literary works. Throughout his life, he admired the character of Pu Lixuan and therefore imitated his writing style, writing a book called *Yizhi Lu*. His writing is colorful and bizarre, and his words are fresh and elegant. When he writes about emotions, it is tender and poignant; when he narrates stories, it is full of passion and enthusiasm. When he discusses loyalty and filial piety, it easily moves people's hearts; when he talks about chastity and loyalty, it can uphold the world's morals; when he searches for gods and talks about ghosts, it can introduce new knowledge to people; when he discusses the mysterious and records strange things, it can broaden people's minds. With the brush of a phoenix soaring, he writes texts that are mysterious and elusive. It is not just that every page is like brocade and every word is like a pearl, but rather it is not solely for the sake of richness and beauty. Unfortunately, with such talent, he does not enjoy pursuing officialdom and lives in seclusion in the mountains and forests, entertaining himself with writing. His insight, breadth of knowledge, and scholarly ability are beyond the reach of contemporary scholars. In the middle of summer in the year of Yimao, he showed me the *Yizhi Lu* he had written. I read it repeatedly and could not help but applaud and admire it, and respectfully wrote a preface. He also gave me a poem titled *Zhengugutian*: "With talent in his chest, he wishes to express himself. The book can make ghosts and gods amazed. Completely relies on the brush to tell extraordinary tales, half relies on writing to express grievances. Talks about chastity and loyalty, records steadfastness, upholds social norms and writes with deep emotion. Pu Lixuan has passed away, who will continue? Continue and complete *Liaozhai* and *Yizhi*."

Lord of Power Resonating a Hundred Li, I respectfully read. Preface In the spring of Rénzǐ, I set up my teaching in the northern outskirts of the prefectural city and befriended Master Fangru. My solitary study in the village was dull, and during the spare time after lessons, I would cut the candle and cook tea, inviting Master Fangru for a long night of conversation. One day, he showed me the *Record of Wisdom* authored by Gentleman Jingzi. Upon reading it, I learned that Master was a renowned figure from Lixia, a respected scholar of his time, yet his fate was thin and his times were difficult, and he failed in his examinations. Thus, he gave up on official success and taught young students at Huangtai; he devoted himself fully to writing, gathering a rich collection. Though street songs and alley talks passed it on as a beautiful story, what he saw and heard turned into beautiful tales. With a full heart of talented abilities, he brought a local spirit; with a pen that blooms like flowers, he wrote about a cunning person. He mended his house with vines, only knowing contentment with poverty; and with the mournful sounds of geese and insects, he used this to express his aspirations. Alas! The master's talent is so rich, and the master's fate is so stingy. The master's situation is even more bitter, yet the master's book unconsciously becomes even more skillful! Therefore, in his composition, it is long but not redundant, short but not abrupt; in his sentence construction, it is well-organized and polished; in his use of allusions, it is common but not harming elegance. And in his intention to encourage good and punish evil, his benefit to the world's morals and people's hearts is not shallow! I believe, this book can be a tool for entertainment and relieving boredom, and it can also be literature that enlightens the people and awakens the world. If one views this book only as playful writing, one already loses the master's purpose in writing.

On the first day of the eleventh month in the year of Bingchen during the Xianfeng era, the author Yishan Yishufu wrote this at Tanghua Jiuguan in Pingling. Preface After the decline of the *Shijing* (Book of Songs), the *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals) was written. The *Shijing* focuses on praise and criticism, while the *Chunqiu* focuses on commendation and censure; their meanings are one and the same. Since then, only the *Zizhi Tongjian Gangmu* (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government: Outline and Commentary) by Ziyang (referring to Zhu Xi) has solemnly inherited the spirit of the *Chunqiu*, clarifying heavenly principles and supporting human relationships. Its impact on human hearts and the course of the world is indeed profound. I have studied the works of various schools of thought, such as Daoism, Buddhism, Legalism, Mohism, Agrarianism, Military Strategy, Medicine, and Diplomacy. Each of these schools has expressed its own views and formed its own body of knowledge. However, none of them have extensively discussed the principles of encouragement and punishment. Works like *Soushen Ji* (Record of Searching for the Gods), *Shuyi Ji* (Record of Strange Events), *Xu Qihai Ji* (Continuation of the Harmonious Records), *Shenyijing* (Classic of Strange Supernatural Phenomena), *Shizhou Ji* (Record of the Ten Continents), *Gaoshi Zhuan* (Biographies of High-minded Men), *Shenxian Zhuan* (Biographies of Immortals), *Dongming Ji* (Record of the Dark and Mysterious), *Yingxiong Jichao* (Collection of Biographies of Heroes), *Mutianzi Zhuan* (Biography of the Duke of Mu), *Wudi Neizhuan* (Inner Biography of Emperor Wu), *Feiyan Waizhuan* (Outer Biography of Feiyan), *Zashi Mixin* (Secret Affairs of Miscellaneous Events), *Chuogeng Lu* (Record of Pausing to Plow), *Yunxian Sanlu* (Record of Clouds and Immortals), and *Xiangshan Yelu* (Record of Wild Tales from Xiangshan) are all sufficient to broaden one's knowledge, record local customs, supplement historical records, and provide entertainment. However, they often describe strange phenomena with tales of spiral towers and mouse kingdoms, discuss immortals with descriptions of jade elixirs and cinnabar, boast about strategies that make one's heart race and jaw drop, and depict beauty that startles the eyes and stirs the emotions.

As for those who see it feeling admiration, those who hear it feeling shame, those who are harsh recognizing the uselessness of partiality, and those who are cunning regretting the futility of scheming, such things are generally unheard of. Indeed! Writing a book is not difficult, but writing a book that benefits human hearts and the course of the world is difficult. Jiejun Jing, a lover of learning, spent forty years competing in the arena of fame and success without obtaining even a single degree. Later, he abandoned the study of the classics and took pleasure in writing as a pastime. In the spring of the Gengshen year, he presented his work, "Yizhi Lu," which includes loyal ministers, filial sons, famous scholars, and beautiful women, as well as the foolishness of husband and wife, the triviality of birds and insects, the importance of the fundamental principles of society, the minutiae of daily life, strange and wonderful encounters, and ghosts, evil spirits, fox demons, and other supernatural beings. It is all there. Those who read it sometimes feel admiration, sometimes feel shame; sometimes fear being involved in partiality, sometimes fear being close to scheming. Thus, it is known that Jiejun is not just writing for the sake of writing as a pastime, but to use writing as a remedy. Indeed! Writing a book has no inherent value, but a book that benefits human hearts and the course of the world, like Jiejun's, is truly valuable. Some say it imitates "Liaozhai Zhiyi" in style; others say it is not inferior to "Liaozhai Zhiyi"; still others say it is not as good as "Liaozhai Zhiyi." In summary, this record has its own merit for immortality, and there is no need to compare it with "Liaozhai Zhiyi" in terms of length or quality. The humble younger brother, Zhang Xilin, respectfully dedicates this translation.

序 In the year of Gengshen, I returned to my hometown due to a family bereavement. Coincidentally, I became involved in local militia affairs, and through this, I met Xie Zi, a scholar of long standing who had not yet found his fortune. One day, he presented me with his eight-volume work, *Yizhi Lu*. I received and read it. His writing style is entirely imitative of *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio*, but the main purpose is to encourage good deeds and punish evil. In the sections that discuss filial piety, fraternal love, loyalty, and righteousness, the writing is profoundly moving and emotionally touching, evoking both song and tears. It can benefit the heart and improve the world, truly a precious raft to cross the sea of life, not comparable to ordinary works of fiction. I am thus amazed by Xie Zi! Scholars and gentlemen who are not successful in their time often express their frustration and integrity through their writing. Therefore, when they write, they resent the times and are disgusted by the world, scolding gods and cursing ghosts, using the subject matter to vent their grievances and assert their own sense of injustice. Though their writing may be forceful and impressive, it is also self-aggrandizing, but it lacks the moral purpose of encouraging and punishing. Now, Xie Zi's writing is so outstanding and worthy of being passed down, yet he never achieved official recognition. Can this not be considered a matter of fate? When he writes, however, his heart is calm and his tone is even, with a sense of admonition and guidance, free from the bitterness of frustration. His writing is deeply and intricately crafted, penetrating the heart and lungs. His dedication to reading and self-cultivation is truly exceptional. Yet, I have another point to make. If Xie Zi had achieved official success, I fear that once he became a government official, such writing might have ceased, and how could he have leisurely engaged in literature and enjoyed this extraordinary work? Thus, just as Pu Songling's lack of success led to the fame of *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio*, Xie Zi's lack of success led to the fame of *Yizhi Lu*. Xie Zi can have no regrets.

December of the tenth year of the Xianfeng era, Sun Guanyun, the younger brother of Zifeng from the same county, respectfully reads. Preface Scholars and gentlemen who are not successful in their time often write with great effort and determination to express their accumulated thoughts and feelings throughout their lives. Some study and verify the classics and histories, broadly synthesize ancient and modern knowledge, and form one's own unique perspective, thereby broadening knowledge and preparing for selection, which is commendable. Others collect strange stories and compile trivial remarks, expressing the intention of praising good and condemning evil within their criticism of the world and disgust with society. Although many of these stories may be fictional and the language not strictly classical, they are not harmful to oneself but beneficial to others, and are still preferable to ornate and flowery writing, intricate and decorative prose, and the mere waste of ink and paper. Fictional writing originated in the Han and Jin dynasties and flourished in the Tang and Song dynasties. From "Mingtong" and "Soushen" onward, the number of such works is too numerous to count. In recent times, the book "Liaozhai Zhiyi" has been widely praised and enjoyed, and afterward, there were many authors. Although each has its strengths, all are derived from the same source but not necessarily superior to it. I loved strange tales from a young age and sought out all such books, borrowing from many sources when they were not available at home. Generally, each work has its strengths and weaknesses, making them difficult to judge or compare. Later, I obtained the five works of Ji Xiaolan, titled "Yuewei Caotang," and found that they express seriousness through humor, present the unusual in a conventional manner, narrate simply and clearly, and express emotions in a refined and elegant manner.

In the author's view, this is a playful work, but for the reader, it provides great benefit. Only now do I realize that popular historical narratives, when written by a masterful hand, can be truly extraordinary. Mr. Jie Junjing, whom I knew during my youth when I took the preliminary examinations, has not been seen for over twenty years since he entered officialdom. In the year 1859 of the Xianfeng era, I took leave on grounds of illness and stayed at home, when he unexpectedly visited and presented his work, *Yizhi Lu*, asking for a preface. I have privately commented on it: *Liaozhai Zhiyi* excels in depicting scenes, revealing hidden beauty, and portraying every detail, making it the writing of a talented scholar. Within it, there are laughter, jokes, scolding, and criticism, with about one-third serving as moral instruction. *Yuewei Caotang Biji*, on the other hand, skillfully discusses principles, with extreme detail and careful analysis, striving to achieve logical satisfaction and peace of mind, making it the work of a scholarly author. It does not boast of elaborate decoration or forced innovation, yet about seven-tenths of it serves as moral instruction. During my leisure time, I finally read through Mr. Jie's book and found it fully displays all its beauty, much like *Liaozhai Zhiyi*. The comments by the pseudonym "Xubai Dao Ren" do not contradict the principles of *Yuewei Caotang Biji*. Is it merely to boast of strange and mysterious tales to please the common eye? No, it can also be passed down! As for Mr. Jie's talent and unrecognized status, other prefaces have already explained it in detail, so I will not repeat it here.

**Translation:** Respectfully, your younger brother Yang Fugui presents this. Preface Scholars and gentlemen who are unsuccessful in their careers often resent the world and are disgusted with society, expressing their passions and enthusiasm through cries of injustice. They have something deeply felt within them that they cannot apply to the world, and thus often use writing to vent their pent-up and noble talents. Some create strange and bizarre theories, others mix different elements and write lewd and indecent words. While superficial scholars may be shocked and startled, those with discernment admire their great talent but regret that their reasoning is not pure. Therefore, outside of works like *The Search for Gods* and *Records of the Strange*, there are dozens or even hundreds of such works. However, it is rare to find writings that are moderate and harmonious, in line with the purpose of encouraging good and punishing evil. My friend Xie Junjing has read everything, and when he writes, he produces thousands of words at a time, tirelessly reaching back to ancient times.
